> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
> **Question B (14 Marks)**
> **(a)** Explain the **ShiftRows** and **AddRoundKey** transformations in AES. (7 marks)
> **(b)** Describe the **AES Key Expansion** algorithm with a clear flow of computation. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) — ShiftRows and AddRoundKey (7 marks)

**ShiftRows** cyclically shifts the bytes in each row of the State to the left:
- Row 0: no shift
- Row 1: shift left by 1 byte
- Row 2: shift left by 2 bytes
- Row 3: shift left by 3 bytes

This permutation ensures that bytes from different columns are mixed in subsequent rounds. Its inverse, **InvShiftRows**, performs right circular shifts of 0, 1, 2, and 3 bytes for rows 0–3 respectively.

**AddRoundKey** combines the State with a 128-bit round key $K_i$ via a simple bitwise XOR:
$$
\text{State}' = \text{State} \oplus K_i
$$

Because XOR is its own inverse, the same operation is used in decryption with the round keys applied in **reverse order**. The round keys are produced by the **Key Expansion** algorithm from the original cipher key.

The combination of ShiftRows and AddRoundKey ensures that **every byte of the State is influenced by every byte of the round key and every byte of the previous round's output**, achieving rapid diffusion.

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) — Key Expansion (7 marks)

The AES key expansion converts the cipher key of $N_k$ words (where $N_k \in \{4, 6, 8\}$) into $N_b(N_r + 1)$ 32-bit words. For AES, $N_b = 4$ always.

**Algorithm:**
1. The first $N_k$ words $w[0], w[1], \ldots, w[N_k - 1]$ are filled with the cipher key bytes.
2. For $i$ from $N_k$ to $N_b(N_r + 1) - 1$:
   - **Case A:** If $i \equiv 0 \pmod{N_k}$, then
     $$
     w[i] = w[i - N_k] \oplus \text{SubWord}(\text{RotWord}(w[i - 1])) \oplus \text{Rcon}[i / N_k]
     $$
   - **Case B (AES-256 only):** If $N_k = 8$ and $i \equiv 4 \pmod{N_k}$, then
     $$
     w[i] = w[i - N_k] \oplus \text{SubWord}(w[i - 1])
     $$
   - **Case C (otherwise):**
     $$
     w[i] = w[i - N_k] \oplus w[i - 1]
     $$

**Helper functions:**
- **RotWord** rotates a 4-byte word left by 1 byte: $[a, b, c, d] \to [b, c, d, a]$.
- **SubWord** applies the AES S-box to each of the 4 bytes.
- **Rcon** (round constant) array elements are $[\text{rc}[j], 0, 0, 0]$, with $\text{rc}[1] = 1$ and $\text{rc}[j] = \text{xtime}(\text{rc}[j-1])$.

The result is $N_r + 1$ round keys (e.g., 11 round keys for AES-128), each 128 bits (4 words) long. The schedule is **non-linear** due to SubWord, ensuring strong **key avalanche** — flipping a single cipher key bit alters many round-key bits.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Forgetting the final round:** Many students apply MixColumns in the last round, but the **final round omits MixColumns**. This will cost 1–2 marks.
> - **SubBytes vs SubWord confusion:** SubBytes operates on the **State matrix** (16 bytes). SubWord operates on a single **4-byte word** during key expansion. Do not interchange them.
> - **State filling order:** AES fills the State **column-by-column** (top to bottom, then left to right), NOT row-by-row. Marks are lost if a student fills row-by-row.
> - **GF(2⁸) reducing polynomial:** Always use $m(x) = x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x + 1 = \{11B\}_{\text{hex}}$. Forgetting the reduction during xtime leads to wrong results.
> - **Key sizes and rounds:** AES-128 = 10 rounds, AES-192 = 12 rounds, AES-256 = 14 rounds. Examiners explicitly test this mapping.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- AES is a **symmetric block cipher** standardized by NIST in **FIPS 197 (2001)**, based on the **Rijndael** algorithm.
- AES uses a fixed **128-bit block size** and supports **128/192/256-bit keys** with **10/12/14 rounds** respectively.
- AES is a **Substitution-Permutation Network (SPN)**. Every round (except the last) performs **SubBytes → ShiftRows → MixColumns → AddRoundKey**.
- The **final round omits MixColumns**, and an **initial AddRoundKey (Round 0)** is performed before the first regular round.
- **SubBytes** uses a fixed **16×16 S-box** combining a multiplicative inverse in $\text{GF}(2^8)$ and an affine transformation over $\text{GF}(2)$. It provides **confusion** and **non-linearity**.
- **ShiftRows** cyclically shifts rows 1, 2, 3 left by 1, 2, 3 bytes respectively. Row 0 is unchanged.
- **MixColumns** treats each column as a polynomial and multiplies it by $a(x) = \{03\}x^3 + \{01\}x^2 + \{01\}x + \{02\}$ modulo $x^4 + 1$ in $\text{GF}(2^8)$. It provides **diffusion**.
- **AddRoundKey** XORs the State with a 128-bit round key. It is its own inverse.
- The **xtime** function implements efficient byte-doubling in $\text{GF}(2^8)$ using left-shift and conditional XOR with $\{1B\}_{\text{hex}}$.
- The **AES Key Expansion** generates $N_b(N_r + 1)$ 32-bit words using **RotWord, SubWord, and Rcon**. AES-256 has an extra SubWord step at $i \equiv 4 \pmod{8}$.
- AES decryption is the **inverse** of encryption: **InvShiftRows → InvSubBytes → AddRoundKey → InvMixColumns**, with round keys applied in **reverse order**.
- AES is **provably secure** against known practical attacks when correctly implemented; **side-channel attacks** (power analysis, timing) are the primary implementation-level concern.
- AES is the **global standard** for TLS, IPsec, WPA2/WPA3, disk encryption, and government/military encryption.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Public Key Encryption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT753 - INFORMATION SECURITY IN PUBLIC AND PRIVATE SECTORS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Public Key Encryption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Public Key Encryption (PKE)**, also known as **Asymmetric Key Cryptography**, is a cryptographic system that uses a mathematically related pair of distinct keys — a **Public Key** (which may be freely distributed to anyone) and a **Private Key** (which is kept secret by the owner) — to perform encryption, decryption, and digital signature operations. The fundamental security property is that it is computationally infeasible to derive the private key from the public key, and messages encrypted with one key can only be decrypted with the other key of the pair.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Public Key Encryption is the foundational pillar of modern internet security, underpinning protocols such as **SSL/TLS**, **PGP**, **SSH**, and the **PKI (Public Key Infrastructure)** used in HTTPS web traffic.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Mailbox with a Slot and a Lock"

Imagine a **transparent mailbox** with a narrow slot on the front, and a private door at the back that only the owner can open with their unique key.

| Mailbox Feature | Cryptographic Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The slot on the front (anyone can drop a letter in) | **Public Key** (anyone can encrypt a message for you) |
| The private door at the back (only the owner has the key) | **Private Key** (only the owner can decrypt) |
| Letters once dropped cannot be retrieved by passersby | **One-Way Function** (mathematically irreversible without key) |
| The owner opening the back door | **Decryption** |

This analogy captures the essence perfectly: **asymmetric** because the "drop" mechanism and the "retrieve" mechanism are completely different — they use different keys.

### 1.3 Why Public Key Encryption Was Invented — The Key Distribution Problem

In **symmetric encryption** (e.g., AES, DES), both sender and receiver must share the **same secret key**. This creates a critical vulnerability:

- **Problem:** How do two strangers securely share a secret key over an insecure network (like the internet)?
- **Risk:** If a malicious actor intercepts the key during distribution, all communication is compromised.

> [!NOTE]
> **Whitfield Diffie and Martin Hellman** (1976) proposed the concept of public-key cryptography. **Rivest, Shamir, and Adleman (RSA)** published the first practical implementation in **1977**, which remains the most widely used PKE algorithm today.

### 1.4 Core Terminology Glossary

- **Plaintext ($M$):** The original, readable message before encryption.
- **Ciphertext ($C$):** The scrambled, unreadable output after encryption.
- **Public Key ($e$, $n$):** Published openly; used by anyone to encrypt a message intended for the key owner.
- **Private Key ($d$):** Kept secret; used by the owner to decrypt.
- **Key Pair ($e, d$):** The mathematical pair generated together such that one undoes the other's operation.
- **One-Way Function:** A function that is easy to compute in one direction but computationally infeasible to invert.
- **Trapdoor Function:** A special one-way function that is easy to invert *if* you possess a secret piece of information (the "trapdoor").

### 1.5 Symmetric vs. Asymmetric Encryption — A Comparative Snapshot

| Parameter | Symmetric Encryption | Asymmetric (Public Key) Encryption |
|---|---|---|
| Number of Keys | **1** (shared secret) | **2** (public + private pair) |
| Speed | **Very Fast** (1000x faster) | **Slow** (computationally heavy) |
| Key Distribution | **Difficult / Risky** | **Easy** (public key is open) |
| Scalability | Poor (needs $n(n-1)/2$ keys for $n$ users) | Excellent (needs only $n$ key pairs) |
| Algorithms | AES, DES, 3DES, Blowfish | **RSA**, ElGamal, ECC, Diffie-Hellman |
| Primary Use | Bulk data encryption | Key exchange, digital signatures |
| Key Length | 128 / 256 bits sufficient | 1024 / 2048 / 4096 bits typical |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Mathematical Pillars of Public Key Encryption

Public Key Encryption is not magic — it is built upon three deep mathematical structures that possess the **trapdoor property**:

#### Pillar 1: Integer Factorization Problem (Basis of RSA)
- **Easy direction:** Multiply two large primes $p \times q = n$.
- **Hard direction:** Given only $n$, find the original $p$ and $q$.
- For a 2048-bit $n$, classical computers would need approximately **$10^{20}$ years** to factor it.

#### Pillar 2: Discrete Logarithm Problem (Basis of ElGamal, DSA, Diffie-Hellman)
- **Easy direction:** Compute $y = g^x \bmod p$.
- **Hard direction:** Given $y$, $g$, and $p$, find $x$.

#### Pillar 3: Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (Basis of ECC)
- Similar to discrete log but performed on points of an elliptic curve.
- Provides **equivalent security with much shorter keys** (e.g., 256-bit ECC ≈ 3072-bit RSA).

### 2.2 Operational Steps of a Generic PKE System

A complete Public Key Encryption round-trip follows these structured steps:

1. **Key Generation Phase:**
   - The receiver generates a key pair $(e, d)$ using a randomized algorithm.
   - The public key $e$ is published in a digital certificate or directory.
   - The private key $d$ is stored securely (e.g., hardware security module, encrypted disk).

2. **Encryption Phase (Sender side):**
   - Sender obtains the receiver's public key $e$ from a trusted source.
   - Sender computes $C = E_e(M)$ — the ciphertext.
   - Sender transmits $C$ over the insecure channel.

3. **Decryption Phase (Receiver side):**
   - Receiver receives $C$.
   - Receiver applies their private key: $M = D_d(C)$.
   - Only the holder of $d$ can perform this step.

4. **Verification (for signatures):**
   - A signature created with the sender's private key is verified by anyone with the public key.

### 2.3 RSA Algorithm — The Workhorse of PKE

The **Rivest–Shamir–Adleman (RSA)** cryptosystem is the most deployed PKE algorithm in the world. It was published in **1977** and has withstood cryptanalysis for nearly half a century.

#### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Rule |
|---|---|---|
| $p, q$ | Two large distinct prime numbers | $p \neq q$, both $\geq 1024$ bits in modern systems |
| $n$ | Modulus (part of both keys) | $n = p \times q$ |
| $\phi(n)$ | Euler's Totient Function | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ |
| $e$ | Public Exponent | $1 < e < \phi(n)$ and $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ |
| $d$ | Private Exponent | $e \times d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ |
| $M$ | Plaintext (as integer) | $0 \leq M < n$ |
| $C$ | Ciphertext | $C = M^{e} \bmod n$ |
| $M'$ | Decrypted Plaintext | $M' = C^{d} \bmod n$ |
| Correctness | Euler's Theorem | $M^{e \cdot d} \equiv M \pmod{n}$ |
| Key Size | Security Parameter | **2048 bits** is current KTU/professional standard |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Point:** The value $n$ is public, but $p$ and $q$ are SECRET. If an attacker factors $n$, the entire system is broken. This is why $p$ and $q$ must be chosen from a cryptographically secure random number generator and must be of equal bit length.

### 2.4 RSA Encryption & Decryption — The Why Behind the Math

The RSA algorithm works because of **Euler's Theorem**, which states that for any integer $M$ coprime to $n$:

$$M^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$$

When we set $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, it means $e \cdot d = 1 + k \cdot \phi(n)$ for some integer $k$. Therefore:

$$M^{e \cdot d} = M^{1 + k \cdot \phi(n)} = M \cdot (M^{\phi(n)})^{k} \equiv M \cdot 1^{k} \equiv M \pmod{n}$$

This elegant property guarantees that **decryption perfectly recovers the original plaintext**.

### 2.5 ElGamal Encryption — A Quick Comparative View

**ElGamal** is an alternative PKE based on the **Discrete Logarithm Problem**:

| Step | ElGamal Operation |
|---|---|
| Public parameters | Large prime $p$, generator $g$ |
| Private key | Random integer $x$ |
| Public key | $y = g^{x} \bmod p$ |
| Encryption of $M$ | Pick random $k$; send $(g^{k} \bmod p,\ M \cdot y^{k} \bmod p)$ |
| Decryption | Use $x$ to recover $y^{k}$, then divide |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** RSA is based on **Integer Factorization**, while ElGamal is based on **Discrete Logarithms**. They offer different security assumptions — an attacker would need to break a *different* hard problem to compromise each.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **HTTPS / SSL / TLS:** RSA (or ECC) is used to securely exchange a symmetric session key, after which AES encrypts the bulk data (a **hybrid cryptosystem**).
- **Digital Signatures (DSA, RSA-PSS):** Prove authenticity and non-repudiation in legal contracts, software updates.
- **Email Security (PGP / S/MIME):** Encrypt and sign emails end-to-end.
- **SSH Authentication:** Passwordless login to servers using RSA key pairs.
- **Cryptocurrencies (Bitcoin, Ethereum):** Use **ECDSA (Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm)** for transaction signing.
- **PKI / X.509 Certificates:** Web servers prove their identity using RSA-signed certificates.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive RSA Worked Example — KTU Board Style

This is the **canonical KTU exam problem**. We will solve it step-by-step, showing every algebraic transition explicitly.

> **Problem Statement (KTU Style):**
> In an RSA cryptosystem, the receiver selects $p = 11$ and $q = 13$. Choose a valid public exponent $e$, and compute the corresponding private key $d$. Then encrypt the plaintext $M = 7$ and show the decryption step.

#### Step 1: Compute the modulus $n$

$$n = p \times q = 11 \times 13 = 143$$

This is the modulus that will be used in all modular arithmetic operations.

#### Step 2: Compute Euler's Totient $\phi(n)$

Using the multiplicative property of Euler's totient for the product of two distinct primes:

$$\phi(n) = (p - 1)(q - 1) = (11 - 1)(13 - 1) = 10 \times 12 = 120$$

#### Step 3: Choose a valid public exponent $e$

We need an integer $e$ such that $1 < e < 120$ AND $\gcd(e, 120) = 1$.

Let's try $e = 7$:

- $1 < 7 < 120$ ✓
- $\gcd(7, 120) = 1$ ✓ (since 7 is prime and does not divide 120)

So our **public key** is $(e, n) = (7, 143)$.

#### Step 4: Compute the private exponent $d$

We need $d$ such that $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, i.e., $7d \equiv 1 \pmod{120}$.

Using the **Extended Euclidean Algorithm** to solve $7d + 120k = 1$:

$$120 = 7 \times 17 + 1$$
$$7 = 1 \times 7 + 0$$

Back-substituting:

$$1 = 120 - 7 \times 17$$

This gives us $1 = 120(-17) + 7(1)$, so $d \equiv 1 \pmod{120}$. We need a positive representative in the range $[1, 119]$:

$$d = 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> In this tiny example, $d = 1$ works, but in real RSA systems $d$ is a much larger number. The example is intentionally small for pedagogical clarity — real RSA uses primes that are hundreds of digits long.

#### Step 5: Encrypt the plaintext $M = 7$

The encryption formula is:

$$C = M^{e} \bmod n = 7^{7} \bmod 143$$

We compute $7^{7}$ using repeated squaring to avoid arithmetic errors:

$$7^{1} = 7$$
$$7^{2} = 49$$
$$7^{4} = 49^{2} = 2401$$

Now reduce $2401 \bmod 143$:

$$2401 \div 143 \approx 16.79 \Rightarrow 143 \times 16 = 2288$$
$$2401 - 2288 = 113 \Rightarrow 7^{4} \equiv 113 \pmod{143}$$

Next, $7^{7} = 7^{4} \cdot 7^{2} \cdot 7^{1} = 113 \times 49 \times 7 \bmod 143$:

$$113 \times 49 = 5537$$
$$5537 \bmod 143: \quad 143 \times 38 = 5434, \quad 5537 - 5434 = 103$$
$$103 \times 7 = 721$$
$$721 \bmod 143: \quad 143 \times 5 = 715, \quad 721 - 715 = 6$$

Therefore: $\boxed{C = 6}$

#### Step 6: Decrypt the ciphertext $C = 6$

The decryption formula is:

$$M' = C^{d} \bmod n = 6^{1} \bmod 143 = 6$$

Wait — that does not equal our original $M = 7$! This is because our choice of $d = 1$ is degenerate. In a real system, $d$ would be large. Let us re-derive with a **better** $e$ to make this pedagogical.

#### Re-do: Try $e = 13$ instead

Re-verify: $\gcd(13, 120) = 1$ ✓ (13 is prime, does not divide 120).

Solve $13d \equiv 1 \pmod{120}$ via Extended Euclidean Algorithm:

$$120 = 13 \times 9 + 3$$
$$13 = 3 \times 4 + 1$$
$$3 = 1 \times 3 + 0$$

Back-substitution:

$$1 = 13 - 3 \times 4$$
$$1 = 13 - (120 - 13 \times 9) \times 4$$
$$1 = 13 - 4 \times 120 + 36 \times 13$$
$$1 = 37 \times 13 - 4 \times 120$$

So $d \equiv 37 \pmod{120}$. Our **private key** is $d = 37$.

**Encrypt** $M = 7$:

$$C = 7^{13} \bmod 143$$

Using repeated squaring: $7^1 = 7$, $7^2 = 49$, $7^4 = 49^2 = 2401 \equiv 113 \pmod{143}$, $7^8 = 113^2 = 12769 \pmod{143}$.

$$12769 \div 143 \approx 89.29 \Rightarrow 143 \times 89 = 12727, \quad 12769 - 12727 = 42$$

So $7^8 \equiv 42 \pmod{143}$.

Now $7^{13} = 7^8 \cdot 7^4 \cdot 7^1$:

$$42 \times 113 = 4746$$
$$4746 \bmod 143: \quad 143 \times 33 = 4719, \quad 4746 - 4719 = 27$$
$$27 \times 7 = 189$$
$$189 \bmod 143 = 189 - 143 = 46$$

Therefore: $\boxed{C = 46}$

**Decrypt** $C = 46$:

$$M' = 46^{37} \bmod 143$$

We can verify using **Euler's Theorem shortcut**: Since $e \cdot d = 13 \times 37 = 481 = 4 \times 120 + 1$, we have $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, so:

$$M' = C^{d} \bmod n = M^{e \cdot d} \bmod n = M^{1} \bmod n = M = 7 \quad \checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The decryption always recovers $M$ exactly** by Euler's Theorem, even though computing $46^{37} \bmod 143$ directly would be tedious. This is the elegance of RSA.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — RSA Components Summary

| Step | Input | Operation | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Key Gen | Primes $p, q$ | $n = p \times q$ | $n$ (public) |
| 2. Key Gen | $p, q$ | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | $\phi(n)$ (secret) |
| 3. Key Gen | — | Choose $e$ coprime to $\phi(n)$ | Public key $e$ |
| 4. Key Gen | $e, \phi(n)$ | Extended Euclidean: $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ | Private key $d$ |
| 5. Encrypt | $M, e, n$ | $C = M^{e} \bmod n$ | Ciphertext $C$ |
| 6. Decrypt | $C, d, n$ | $M = C^{d} \bmod n$ | Plaintext $M$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Public Key Encryption Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sender Alice] -->|M plaintext| B[Encryption Engine]
    B[Encryption Engine] -->|C = M^e mod n| C[Ciphertext C]
    C[Ciphertext C] -->|Transmit over insecure channel| D[Insecure Network]
    D[Insecure Network] -->|C delivered| E[Receiver Bob]
    E[Receiver Bob] -->|Apply private key d| F[Decryption Engine]
    F[Decryption Engine] -->|M = C^d mod n = Original| G[Plaintext M Recovered]

    PK[Bob's Public Key in Directory] -.->|Lookup e, n| B
    SK[Bob's Private Key in Vault] -.->|Secret d| F
```

### 4.2 RSA Key Generation Pipeline (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Input["Input Stage"]
        P1[Generate large prime p]
        P2[Generate large prime q]
        P1 --> N[n = p times q]
        P2 --> N
    end

    subgraph Math["Mathematical Computation"]
        N --> PHI[phi of n equals p minus 1 times q minus 1]
        PHI --> ECHOOSE[Choose e: gcd e and phi n equals 1]
        ECHOOSE --> ESOLVE[Extended Euclidean Algorithm]
    end

    subgraph Output["Key Pair Output"]
        ESOLVE --> PUB[Public Key = e, n]
        ESOLVE --> PRIV[Private Key = d, n]
    end

    PUB --> X[Published in PKI / Certificate]
    PRIV --> Y[Stored in HSM / Secure Vault]
```

### 4.3 Encryption-Decryption Cycle with Two-Key Reversal

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as Sender
    participant PKD as Public Key Directory
    participant R as Receiver

    R->>PKD: Publish (e, n)
    S->>PKD: Request Bob's public key
    PKD-->>S: Returns (e, n)
    S->>S: Compute C = M^e mod n
    S->>R: Send ciphertext C
    R->>R: Compute M = C^d mod n
    R-->>R: Plaintext M recovered
    Note over S,R: Only Bob with d can decrypt
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Hybrid Cryptosystem (Real-World PKE Use)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SenderSide["Sender Side"]
        SM[Plaintext Message] --> SKG[Generate Random AES Symmetric Key]
        SKG --> SENC[AES Encrypts Message with Key]
        SM --> RSAE[RSA Encrypts AES Key with Receiver Public Key]
        SENC --> CB1[Ciphertext Message]
        RSAE --> CB2[Encrypted Session Key]
    end

    subgraph Net["Network"]
        CB1 --> N1[Send Both]
        CB2 --> N1
    end

    subgraph ReceiverSide["Receiver Side"]
        N1 --> RSAD[RSA Decrypts Session Key with Private Key]
        RSAD --> SAD[AES Decrypts Message with Session Key]
        SAD --> RM[Recovered Plaintext]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Real-world systems (TLS, HTTPS) almost never use RSA to encrypt bulk data directly because RSA is ~1000x slower than AES. Instead, they use the **hybrid approach** shown above — RSA securely exchanges a temporary AES key, which then encrypts the actual data at high speed.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define Public Key Encryption. List any two advantages it has over Symmetric Key Encryption.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Public Key Encryption (PKE), also called Asymmetric Key Cryptography, is a cryptographic system that uses a mathematically related pair of keys — a public key (for encryption, freely distributed) and a private key (for decryption, kept secret by the owner) — such that a message encrypted with the public key can only be decrypted with the corresponding private key.

**Two advantages over symmetric encryption:**
1. **Simplified Key Distribution:** The public key can be openly shared over insecure channels, eliminating the need for a secure key exchange protocol.
2. **Enhanced Scalability:** For $n$ users, only $n$ key pairs are required, whereas symmetric systems need $n(n-1)/2$ distinct shared secrets.
3. *(Bonus third)* Enables **digital signatures** and non-repudiation, which symmetric systems cannot natively provide.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
**What is a one-way trapdoor function? Why is it essential for Public Key Encryption?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **one-way function** is a mathematical function $f(x)$ that is computationally easy to compute but computationally infeasible to invert, i.e., given $y = f(x)$, finding $x$ is hard. A **trapdoor function** is a special one-way function that *becomes* easy to invert if and only if a specific secret piece of information (the "trapdoor") is known.

**Why essential for PKE:**
- The **public key** defines the one-way direction (encryption) — easy for anyone to perform.
- The **private key** is the **trapdoor** — it makes the inverse operation (decryption) easy.
- Without the trapdoor property, anyone could decrypt any message, defeating the entire purpose of asymmetric cryptography.
- Examples: RSA's trapdoor is knowing $p$ and $q$ to factor $n$; ElGamal's trapdoor is the discrete log $x$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Question Paper Pattern Reminder (KTU 2024):** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and is split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks, OR a single integrated problem. Students must answer ONE choice only.

---

#### **Question A — [14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Modified Past Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the RSA algorithm in detail. In a public key cryptosystem using RSA, given $p = 17$ and $q = 11$, compute the modulus $n$, Euler's totient $\phi(n)$, and find suitable values of public exponent $e$ and private exponent $d$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Encrypt the message $M = 5$ using the RSA system you derived in part (a), and demonstrate the decryption process to recover the original plaintext. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Key Generation:**

Step 1: Compute modulus

$$n = p \times q = 17 \times 11 = 187$$

Step 2: Compute Euler's totient

$$\phi(n) = (p - 1)(q - 1) = 16 \times 10 = 160$$

Step 3: Choose public exponent $e$ such that $\gcd(e, 160) = 1$ and $1 < e < 160$.

Trying $e = 7$: $\gcd(7, 160) = 1$ ✓ (since $160 = 2^5 \times 5$, and 7 is coprime).

**Public key** = $(e, n) = (7, 187)$.

Step 4: Compute private exponent $d$ from $7d \equiv 1 \pmod{160}$.

Extended Euclidean Algorithm:

$$160 = 7 \times 22 + 6$$
$$7 = 6 \times 1 + 1$$
$$6 = 1 \times 6 + 0$$

Back-substitute:

$$1 = 7 - 6 \times 1 = 7 - (160 - 7 \times 22) = 7 \times 23 - 160$$

So $d \equiv 23 \pmod{160}$. **Private key** = $(d, n) = (23, 187)$.

**Part (b) — Encrypt and Decrypt:**

**Encryption:** $C = M^{e} \bmod n = 5^{7} \bmod 187$

Compute via repeated squaring:

$$5^{1} = 5$$
$$5^{2} = 25$$
$$5^{4} = 25^{2} = 625$$
$$625 \bmod 187: \quad 187 \times 3 = 561, \quad 625 - 561 = 64 \Rightarrow 5^{4} \equiv 64 \pmod{187}$$

$5^{7} = 5^{4} \cdot 5^{2} \cdot 5^{1} = 64 \times 25 \times 5 \bmod 187$

$$64 \times 25 = 1600$$
$$1600 \bmod 187: \quad 187 \times 8 = 1496, \quad 1600 - 1496 = 104$$
$$104 \times 5 = 520$$
$$520 \bmod 187: \quad 187 \times 2 = 374, \quad 520 - 374 = 146$$

$$\boxed{C = 146}$$

**Decryption:** $M' = C^{d} \bmod n = 146^{23} \bmod 187$

By Euler's Theorem, since $7 \times 23 = 161 = 1 \times 160 + 1$, we have $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, so:

$$M' = 146^{23} \bmod 187 = 5^{7 \times 23} \bmod 187 = 5^{161} \bmod 187 = 5 \bmod 187 = 5 \quad \checkmark$$

**Original plaintext recovered: $M = 5$** ✓

---

#### **Question B — [14 Marks, Alternative Choice]**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1 Pattern]**

**(a)** Differentiate clearly between **Symmetric Key Encryption** and **Public Key Encryption**. Discuss the merits and demerits of each, and explain why modern systems (like TLS) use a **hybrid** approach combining both. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **ElGamal Public Key Encryption** scheme. With a clear diagram, explain the steps of key generation, encryption, and decryption. What hard mathematical problem is its security based on? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution Outline:**

**Part (a) — Symmetric vs. Asymmetric Comparison:**

| Criterion | Symmetric Key | Public Key |
|---|---|---|
| Key count | Single shared key | Key pair (public + private) |
| Speed | Very fast (AES ~ GB/sec) | Slow (RSA ~ MB/sec) |
| Key distribution | Hard, requires secure channel | Easy, public key is open |
| Scalability | $O(n^2)$ keys for $n$ users | $O(n)$ key pairs |
| Confidentiality | Yes | Yes |
| Authentication / Non-repudiation | No | Yes (via digital signatures) |
| Example | AES, DES, 3DES | RSA, ElGamal, ECC |

**Why Hybrid (TLS approach):**
- RSA is too slow for bulk data.
- AES is fast but needs a shared key.
- **Solution:** Use RSA to securely exchange a randomly generated AES session key, then use AES for the actual data transfer. This gives **speed + security + scalability**.

**Part (b) — ElGamal Scheme:**

**Setup:** Choose a large prime $p$ and a primitive root $g \bmod p$.

**Key Generation:**
1. Receiver picks random private key $x$ (where $1 \leq x \leq p-2$).
2. Computes public key $y = g^{x} \bmod p$.
3. Publishes $(p, g, y)$.

**Encryption** (Sender side, to send message $M$):
1. Pick random integer $k$ coprime to $p - 1$.
2. Compute $a = g^{k} \bmod p$.
3. Compute $b = M \cdot y^{k} \bmod p$.
4. Send ciphertext $(a, b)$.

**Decryption** (Receiver side):
1. Compute $a^{x} \bmod p = g^{k \cdot x} \bmod p = y^{k} \bmod p$.
2. Find modular inverse $(a^{x})^{-1} \bmod p$.
3. Recover $M = b \cdot (a^{x})^{-1} \bmod p$.

**Security basis:** The **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)** — given $g, p, y$, it is computationally infeasible to find $x = \log_{g} y \bmod p$.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks (and How to Avoid Them):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to verify $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$** when choosing $e$. If $e$ and $\phi(n)$ share a factor, no modular inverse $d$ exists — **[-2 marks]**.
>
> 2. **Wrong range for $d$:** Students often write $d$ as a negative number from the Extended Euclidean Algorithm. Always reduce to the **positive representative** in $[1, \phi(n)-1]$ — **[-1 mark]**.
>
> 3. **Confusing $n$ with $\phi(n)$:** Some students encrypt using $C = M^e \bmod \phi(n)$ instead of $\bmod n$. This is incorrect — **[-2 marks]**.
>
> 4. **Skipping modular reduction steps in long powers:** KTU examiners award step marks. Always show $a^2 \bmod n$, then $a^4 \bmod n$, etc., not just the final answer.
>
> 5. **Not stating Euler's Theorem as justification** for why decryption works — examiners love this theoretical backing — **[-1 mark]**.
>
> 6. **Mixing up RSA and ElGamal** when asked to compare. RSA = Integer Factorization; ElGamal = Discrete Logarithm. — **[-2 marks]**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Public Key Encryption = Asymmetric Cryptography** using a public + private key pair.
- **Two main mathematical foundations:** Integer Factorization (RSA) and Discrete Logarithm (ElGamal, Diffie-Hellman).
- **RSA Key Generation Steps:** $n = p \cdot q$, $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$, choose $e$ coprime to $\phi(n)$, compute $d$ via Extended Euclidean Algorithm.
- **RSA Encryption:** $C = M^{e} \bmod n$ | **Decryption:** $M = C^{d} \bmod n$.
- **Euler's Theorem** is the theoretical backbone: $M^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$.
- **ElGamal Encryption** sends a ciphertext pair $(a, b) = (g^{k} \bmod p, \ M \cdot y^{k} \bmod p)$.
- **Hybrid cryptosystems** (RSA + AES) are used in TLS/HTTPS for speed and security.
- **Key size:** 2048-bit RSA is the current KTU/professional standard; 1024-bit is considered weak.
- **One-way trapdoor function** is the essential primitive that makes PKE possible.
- **Advantages:** Easy key distribution, scalability, enables digital signatures and non-repudiation.
- **Disadvantages:** Computationally slow, vulnerable to quantum attacks (Shor's algorithm), requires a Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) for trust.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Takeaway:** Always show your work in modular arithmetic problems — partial credit is awarded for each correct intermediate step, even if the final answer is wrong. Master the Extended Euclidean Algorithm; it appears in nearly every RSA question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Uses of Encryption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT753 - INFORMATION SECURITY IN PUBLIC AND PRIVATE SECTORS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Uses of Encryption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Encryption** is the cryptographic process of transforming an intelligible form of data (called *plaintext* or *cleartext*) into an unintelligible, encoded form (called *ciphertext*) using a mathematical algorithm (cipher) and a secret value (key), such that the original data can only be recovered by parties possessing the appropriate decryption key. According to the KTU 2024 Information Security syllabus, encryption constitutes the foundational primitive upon which confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation services of modern public and private sector information systems are built.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept)**
> Encryption is a *mathematical transformation* $E_k(P) = C$ where plaintext $P$ is mapped to ciphertext $C$ under key $k$. Its inverse *decryption* $D_k(C) = P$ recovers the original message. The strength of the encryption scheme rests not on the secrecy of the algorithm, but on the secrecy and length of the cryptographic key (**Kerckhoffs's Principle, 1883**).

### 1.2 The Two Fundamental Branches of Encryption

| Branch | Key Structure | Primary Use | Speed |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Symmetric Encryption** | Single shared secret key $K$ | Bulk data, file, disk encryption | **Fast (100–1000× faster)** |
| **Asymmetric Encryption** | Public key $K_{pub}$ + Private key $K_{priv}$ | Key exchange, digital signatures, certificates | Slow (computationally heavy) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Locked Briefcase" Model

Imagine two business partners, *Alice* (in Kochi) and *Bob* (in Tokyo), who need to exchange a confidential contract:

1. **Plaintext (P)** → the readable contract document.
2. **Key (K)** → a unique physical key that Alice and Bob have agreed upon in advance.
3. **Cipher (E)** → the lock mechanism that scrambles the document once it is placed inside the briefcase.
4. **Ciphertext (C)** → the locked briefcase. Even if intercepted by a customs officer (the *eavesdropper / adversary* $Eve$), the contents remain unreadable without the matching key.
5. **Decryption (D)** → Bob uses his copy of the key to unlock the briefcase and read the contract.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Mapping**
> In modern TLS 1.3 (used by HTTPS websites), the briefcase analogy translates to: Alice's browser generates a *symmetric session key* via asymmetric key exchange (RSA/ECC), then uses that symmetric key to encrypt the actual data stream. This hybrid approach is the **dominant paradigm** in both public (e-governance portals) and private (online banking) sectors.

### 1.4 Historical Anchor

Encryption is **not** a modern invention. The KTU syllabus expects students to recognize its lineage:

- **~1900 BCE** — Non-standard hieroglyphs in Egyptian tombs (proto-encryption).
- **50 BCE** — *Caesar Cipher* (shift cipher, key = 3).
- **1883** — **Kerckhoffs's Principle** formalized in *"La Cryptographie Militaire."*
- **1949** — Shannon's *"Communication Theory of Secrecy Systems"* established mathematical foundations.
- **1976** — **Diffie–Hellman** key exchange (public-key cryptography revolution).
- **1977** — **RSA** algorithm published (Rivest, Shamir, Adleman).
- **2001** — **AES (Advanced Encryption Standard)** selected by NIST.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Information Security Achieved Through Encryption

Encryption is **not** a single-purpose tool. According to the KTU 2024 Module 1 framework, it is the *enabling technology* behind five distinct security services. Understanding *which use maps to which pillar* is the single most tested concept in this topic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5 Security Services (CIA-Triad + Extensions)**
> 1. **Confidentiality** → prevents unauthorized *disclosure* of data.
> 2. **Integrity** → ensures data has not been *altered* in transit/storage.
> 3. **Authentication** → verifies the *identity* of the sender/receiver.
> 4. **Non-Repudiation** → sender *cannot later deny* having sent the message.
> 5. **Access Control** → restricts who may *use* a resource.

### 2.2 Detailed Uses of Encryption — Categorical Breakdown

#### A. Uses for **Confidentiality** (Preventing Eavesdropping)

1. **Data at Rest Encryption (DAR)**
   - **Full Disk Encryption (FDE):** BitLocker (Windows), FileVault (macOS), LUKS (Linux).
   - **File-Level Encryption:** Encrypted ZIP, 7z archives, VeraCrypt volumes.
   - **Database Encryption:** Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) in Oracle, SQL Server, MySQL.
2. **Data in Transit Encryption (DIT)**
   - **TLS/SSL** (HTTPS) — secures web traffic on port 443.
   - **IPsec / VPN** — secures site-to-site and remote-access tunnels.
   - **SSH** — secures remote shell sessions (replaces plaintext Telnet).
3. **End-to-End Encryption (E2EE)**
   - WhatsApp (Signal Protocol), Signal, ProtonMail.
4. **Email Encryption**
   - **PGP / OpenPGP** (RFC 4880) and **S/MIME** (X.509 certificates).
5. **Voice & VoIP Encryption**
   - SRTP (Secure Real-time Transport Protocol), ZRTP (Phil Zimmermann).

#### B. Uses for **Integrity** (Detecting Tampering)

1. **Message Authentication Codes (MAC)**
   - **HMAC-SHA256**: $MAC = H(K \oplus opad \,\|\, H(K \oplus ipad \,\|\, M))$
2. **Authenticated Encryption (AEAD)**
   - **AES-GCM**, **ChaCha20-Poly1305** — provide both confidentiality *and* integrity in a single operation.
3. **Digital Checksums / Hashing** (one-way; no key)
   - SHA-256, SHA-3, BLAKE2.

#### C. Uses for **Authentication** (Proving Identity)

1. **Password Storage** — never store plaintext; store salted **bcrypt / Argon2 / scrypt** hashes.
2. **Challenge–Response Protocols** — proves knowledge of a key without revealing it.
3. **Smart Cards & HSMs** — private keys stored in tamper-resistant hardware.
4. **Kerberos Authentication** — uses symmetric tickets (DES/3DES/AES) within Windows AD domains.

#### D. Uses for **Non-Repudiation** (Binding Sender to Message)

1. **Digital Signatures**
   - RSA-PSS, ECDSA, Ed25519.
   - Used legally under the **Information Technology Act, 2000 (India)** and the **IT (Amendment) Act, 2008**.
2. **Code Signing** — software publishers sign executables (Authenticode, Apple notarization).
3. **Document Signing** — eMudhra, DSC (Digital Signature Certificates) issued by CCA-approved CAs in India.

#### E. Uses for **Access Control & Key Management**

1. **Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)** — hierarchy of Certificate Authorities (CA), Registration Authorities (RA), and X.509 certificates.
2. **Key Escrow & Recovery** — for law-enforcement access (controversial; Clipper Chip case).
3. **Attribute-Based Encryption (ABE)** — fine-grained access control in cloud storage.

### 2.3 Sector-Specific Use Mapping (Public vs Private)

| Sector | Application | Encryption Mechanism | KTU-Relevant Body / Standard |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Public – Defense** | Classified communication, command & control | Suite A / Suite B algorithms (AES-256, ECDH, ECDSA) | **IND/IC cryptographic policy (India)** |
| **Public – E-Governance** | Aadhaar data vault, DigiLocker, eSign | AES-256 (data), RSA-2048 (key exchange) | **UIDAI**, **CCA**, **MeitY** |
| **Public – Tax & Finance** | GSTN portal, Income Tax e-filing | TLS 1.2/1.3 with Indian CCA Root CA | **GSTN**, **CBDT** |
| **Public – Healthcare** | ABHA (Ayushman Bharat Health Account) records | AES + tokenization | **ABDM / NHA** |
| **Private – Banking** | UPI, NEFT, RTGS, card transactions | 3-D Secure (EMV), HSM-based PIN encryption | **RBI Cyber Security Framework** |
| **Private – E-Commerce** | Amazon, Flipkart, Myntra payment flows | TLS 1.3 + tokenization (PCI-DSS compliant) | **PCI-DSS 4.0** |
| **Private – Cloud (AWS/Azure/GCP)** | Customer data, KMS-managed keys | AES-256 envelope encryption | **ISO 27001**, **SOC 2** |
| **Private – Telecom (5G)** | Subscriber authentication, SUPI concealment | **SUCI** computation using ECIES profile A | **3GPP TS 33.501** |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize the following table — it covers ~70% of numerical/derivation questions.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Plain Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Encryption function | $C = E_k(P)$ | Plaintext $P$ becomes ciphertext $C$ under key $k$ | — |
| 2 | Decryption function | $P = D_k(C)$ | Ciphertext $C$ recovers plaintext $P$ under key $k$ | — |
| 3 | RSA modulus | $n = p \times q$ | Product of two large primes $p, q$ | $n \ge 2048$ bits (current standard) |
| 4 | RSA public exponent | $e$ | Public exponent, coprime to $\phi(n)$ | Typically $e = 65537$ |
| 5 | RSA private exponent | $d = e^{-1} \bmod \phi(n)$ | Modular inverse of $e$ | $d$ is secret |
| 6 | Euler's totient | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | Count of integers coprime to $n$ | — |
| 7 | RSA encryption | $C \equiv P^e \bmod n$ | Modular exponentiation | $0 \le P < n$ |
| 8 | RSA decryption | $P \equiv C^d \bmod n$ | Inverse operation | Recovers $P$ |
| 9 | AES key sizes | $K \in \{128, 192, 256\}$ bits | NIST-standardized symmetric key lengths | AES-128, AES-192, AES-256 |
| 10 | AES block size | $B = 128$ bits | Fixed block processed per round | — |
| 11 | AES rounds | $N_r \in \{10, 12, 14\}$ | Rounds for AES-128/192/256 | $N_r = 6 + \lceil K_b / 32 \rceil$ |
| 12 | AES round keys | $N_r + 1$ keys | Key schedule generates $11/13/15$ round keys | Total $= 176/208/240$ bytes |
| 13 | Diffie–Hellman shared secret | $K_{AB} = g^{ab} \bmod p$ | Both parties derive same secret | $p \ge 2048$ bits |
| 14 | Hash output size | $H: \{0,1\}^* \to \{0,1\}^n$ | Compresses arbitrary input to fixed digest | SHA-256 → $n = 256$ bits |
| 15 | HMAC | $HMAC(K, M) = H((K \oplus opad) \,\|\, H((K \oplus ipad) \,\|\, M))$ | Keyed hash for integrity | — |
| 16 | Work Factor | $W$ | Minimum operations attacker must perform | $W = 2^{128}$ for AES-128 |
| 17 | Information entropy | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Uncertainty in bits | $H(X) \le \log_2 n$ |
| 18 | Perfect secrecy (Shannon) | $H(P) = H(P \vert C)$ | Ciphertext reveals no info about plaintext | Requires $K \ge P$ (One-Time Pad) |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **private sector**, encryption underpins every online transaction you make. When you swipe a debit card at a supermarket in Thrissur, the chip performs a **3-D Secure (EMV)** cryptographic handshake with the acquiring bank. The **PIN block** is encrypted using a **Derived Unique Key Per Transaction (DUKPT)** scheme before transmission. In the **public sector**, India's **Aadhaar** system uses AES-256 to encrypt demographic data in the CIDR (Central Identities Data Repository), and the **Virtual ID (VID)** mechanism limits exposure of the actual 12-digit Aadhaar number. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to be able to discuss *at least one* public-sector and *one* private-sector use case with technical depth.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since *"Uses of Encryption"* is fundamentally a conceptual topic, the analytical work here focuses on (a) the **mathematical basis of RSA encryption** (the most-examined algorithm in KTU papers) and (b) the **information-theoretic entropy analysis** justifying why encryption is necessary. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly — no shortcuts.

### 3.1 Worked Derivation: RSA Key Generation, Encryption, and Decryption

#### Step 1 — Choose Two Primes

Let Alice select two large random primes:

$$p = 61, \quad q = 53$$

Both are prime (a teacher can use small primes to demonstrate; real systems use 1024-bit primes).

#### Step 2 — Compute the Modulus $n$

$$n = p \times q = 61 \times 53 = 3233$$

This value $n$ is published as part of the public key.

#### Step 3 — Compute Euler's Totient $\phi(n)$

$$\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = (61-1)(53-1) = 60 \times 52 = 3120$$

#### Step 4 — Choose the Public Exponent $e$

Pick $e$ such that $1 < e < \phi(n)$ and $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$. A common choice:

$$e = 17$$

Verify: $\gcd(17, 3120) = 1$ ✓ (since 17 is prime and does not divide 3120).

#### Step 5 — Compute the Private Exponent $d$

We need the modular inverse of $e$ modulo $\phi(n)$:

$$e \times d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)} \implies 17 \times d \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$$

Apply the **Extended Euclidean Algorithm** to find $d$:

$$3120 = 183 \times 17 + 9$$
$$17 = 1 \times 9 + 8$$
$$9 = 1 \times 8 + 1$$
$$8 = 8 \times 1 + 0$$

Back-substitute:

$$1 = 9 - 1 \times 8$$
$$1 = 9 - 1 \times (17 - 1 \times 9) = 2 \times 9 - 1 \times 17$$
$$1 = 2 \times (3120 - 183 \times 17) - 1 \times 17 = 2 \times 3120 - 367 \times 17$$

Therefore: $d \equiv -367 \pmod{3120} \equiv 3120 - 367 = 2753$.

$$\boxed{d = 2753}$$

#### Step 6 — Public and Private Keys

- **Public key:** $(n, e) = (3233, 17)$
- **Private key:** $(n, d) = (3233, 2753)$

#### Step 7 — Encrypt a Plaintext Message

Let Alice's plaintext (numeric) be the single letter **"H"**, whose ASCII code is $P = 72$.

Encryption formula:

$$C \equiv P^e \bmod n = 72^{17} \bmod 3233$$

Compute via **modular exponentiation (square-and-multiply)**:

$$72^1 \equiv 72 \pmod{3233}$$
$$72^2 \equiv 5184 \equiv 5184 - 3233 = 1951 \pmod{3233}$$
$$72^4 \equiv 1951^2 = 3{,}806{,}401 \equiv 3{,}806{,}401 \bmod 3233$$

Divide: $3{,}806{,}401 \div 3233 \approx 1177.05$; floor $\times 3233 = 3{,}804{,}641$; remainder $= 1760$.

$$72^4 \equiv 1760 \pmod{3233}$$
$$72^8 \equiv 1760^2 = 3{,}097{,}600 \pmod{3233}$$
$$3{,}097{,}600 \div 3233 \approx 958.05$; floor $\times 3233 = 3{,}097{,}789$; remainder $= -189 \equiv 3044$.

$$72^8 \equiv 3044 \pmod{3233}$$
$$72^{16} \equiv 3044^2 = 9{,}265{,}936 \pmod{3233}$$
$$9{,}265{,}936 \div 3233 \approx 2866.04$; floor $\times 3233 = 9{,}265{,}778$; remainder $= 158$.

$$72^{16} \equiv 158 \pmod{3233}$$

Now $17 = 16 + 1$:

$$72^{17} = 72^{16} \times 72^1 \equiv 158 \times 72 \pmod{3233}$$
$$= 11{,}376 \pmod{3233}$$
$$11{,}376 \div 3233 = 3 \text{ remainder } 1677$$

$$\boxed{C = 1677}$$

#### Step 8 — Decrypt the Ciphertext

Bob receives $C = 1677$. He applies the private exponent $d = 2753$:

$$P \equiv C^d \bmod n = 1677^{2753} \bmod 3233$$

(For brevity, we trust the algebraic identity: by Euler's theorem, $P^{ed} \equiv P \pmod n$.)

The result of this giant modular exponentiation yields:

$$\boxed{P = 72}$$

which is the original ASCII code for **"H"**. The message is recovered.

#### Step 9 — Verification of the RSA Identity

The mathematical *why* it works rests on Euler's theorem:

$$P^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n} \quad \text{when } \gcd(P, n) = 1$$

Then:

$$C^d \equiv (P^e)^d = P^{ed} = P^{1 + k \phi(n)} = P \times (P^{\phi(n)})^k \equiv P \times 1^k \equiv P \pmod{n}$$

This is the **core derivation** examiners expect students to reproduce.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Use Case to Mechanism

| Information Security Service | Cryptographic Mechanism | Use Case (Public Sector) | Use Case (Private Sector) | Algorithm(s) Employed |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Confidentiality** | Symmetric encryption of payload | Encrypted Aadhaar vault records | Customer credit-card data in Amazon DBs | **AES-256-GCM** |
| **Confidentiality (key exchange)** | Asymmetric key encapsulation | TLS handshake on income-tax portal | TLS handshake on Flipkart checkout | **RSA-OAEP**, **ECDH (X25519)** |
| **Integrity** | Hash / MAC of message | Software updates on NIC e-Governance apps | Microsoft Windows code signing | **SHA-256**, **HMAC-SHA256** |
| **Authentication** | Challenge–response with digital certs | mTLS between banks on SFMS (Structured Financial Messaging) | Mutual TLS between microservices in banking APIs | **X.509 + ECDSA** |
| **Non-repudiation** | Digital signature on document | eSign on MCA21 (Ministry of Corporate Affairs) filings | DocuSign contracts at TCS / Infosys | **RSA-PSS**, **Ed25519** |
| **Access Control** | Attribute / identity-based encryption | Restricted access to defence data on DRONA network | Encrypted S3 buckets with IAM policies on AWS | **CP-ABE**, **KP-ABE** |
| **Privacy-preserving computation** | Homomorphic encryption | Census data analysis without raw access | Fraud detection on encrypted transactions | **CKKS**, **BGV** schemes |

### 3.3 Entropy Argument: Why Encryption Is Necessary

Consider a message drawn from a 256-symbol alphabet with uniform probability. The entropy is:

$$H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{256} \frac{1}{256} \log_2 \frac{1}{256} = \log_2 256 = 8 \text{ bits per symbol}$$

An **unencrypted** plaintext carries 8 bits of *information leakage* per symbol. After **perfect encryption** (e.g., one-time pad), the ciphertext appears uniformly random, so an attacker's conditional entropy equals the message entropy:

$$H(P \vert C) = H(P) = 8 \text{ bits/symbol}$$

This is **Shannon's definition of perfect secrecy**. Real-world encryption (AES, RSA) aims to *approximate* this ideal: an attacker gains *zero computationally feasible* information about $P$ from $C$.

$$\boxed{\text{Goal of encryption: minimize mutual information } I(P; C) = H(P) - H(P \vert C) \to 0}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Encryption Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Plaintext P] --> B1[Encryption Algorithm E]
    K1[Secret Key K] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Ciphertext C]
    C1 --> D1[Transmission Channel]
    D1 --> E1[Decryption Algorithm D]
    K2[Same Secret Key K] --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Recovered Plaintext P]
    EVE[Adversary Eve] -.->|intercepts but cannot read| C1
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Public-Key (Asymmetric) Use for Confidentiality

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A2[Sender Alice] --> B2[Lookup Bob's Public Key from PKI]
    B2 --> C2[Encrypt M with Bob's Public Key]
    C2 --> D2[Ciphertext travels over insecure channel]
    D2 --> E2[Bob receives C]
    E2 --> F2[Bob decrypts C with his Private Key]
    F2 --> G2[Original Plaintext M recovered]
    CA[Certificate Authority X509] -.->|issues & signs cert| B2
```

### 4.3 Use-Case Taxonomy — Public Sector vs Private Sector

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ROOT[Uses of Encryption]
    ROOT --> P[Public Sector]
    ROOT --> Q[Private Sector]

    P --> P1[Defence and Intelligence]
    P --> P2[E-Governance Portals]
    P --> P3[Digital Identity Aadhaar ABHA]
    P --> P4[Tax and GST Systems]
    P --> P5[Healthcare Records]

    P1 --> P1a[Suite A Algorithms AES256]
    P2 --> P2a[TLS13 with CCA Root CA]
    P3 --> P3a[Tokenization plus AES256]
    P4 --> P4a[End to End TLS plus HSM]
    P5 --> P5a[AES plus Role Based Access]

    Q --> Q1[Banking and Finance]
    Q --> Q2[E-Commerce Platforms]
    Q --> Q3[Cloud Storage Providers]
    Q --> Q4[Telecom 5G SUCI]
    Q --> Q5[Enterprise Corporate Data]

    Q1 --> Q1a[EMV 3D Secure DUKPT]
    Q2 --> Q2a[TLS13 plus PCI DSS Tokenization]
    Q3 --> Q3a[Envelope Encryption with KMS]
    Q4 --> Q4a[ECIES Profile A]
    Q5 --> Q5a[BitLocker plus TPM]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Authenticated Encryption (AEAD) Use

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Plaintext M] --> ENC[AES-GCM Encrypt]
    K1[Key K] --> ENC
    N1[Nonce IV] --> ENC
    A1[Associated Data AD Header] --> ENC
    ENC --> CT[Ciphertext C]
    ENC --> T1[Authentication Tag T]
    T1 --> VER[Receiver verifies T over AD and C]
    VER -->|Valid| OK[Message accepted Integrity plus Confidentiality]
    VER -->|Invalid| REJ[Reject Tampered]
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram — Hybrid Encryption in Real TLS 1.3

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client as Browser Alice
    participant Server as Bank Server Bob
    Client->>Server: ClientHello with supported cipher suites
    Server->>Client: ServerHello plus Certificate Bob public key
    Client->>Client: Verify certificate against trusted CA
    Client->>Server: ECDHE key share plus encrypted premaster
    Server->>Server: Decrypt premaster with private key
    Note over Client,Server: Both derive same symmetric session key SK
    Client->>Server: Application data encrypted with SK using AES GCM
    Server->>Client: Response encrypted with SK
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**  
> **Define encryption. Differentiate between symmetric and asymmetric encryption with one example each.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

Encryption is the cryptographic process of converting readable plaintext $(P)$ into unreadable ciphertext $(C)$ using a mathematical algorithm and a key, expressed as $C = E_k(P)$, such that the original data is unintelligible to unauthorized parties.

| Property | Symmetric | Asymmetric |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Keys | Single shared secret key | Public + Private key pair |
| Speed | Fast (bulk data) | Slow (key exchange only) |
| Example | AES-256 | RSA-2048 |
| Use | File/disk encryption | Digital signatures, TLS handshake |

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**  
> **List any three real-world uses of encryption in the public sector in India.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Aadhaar Data Vault** — demographic and biometric data encrypted with **AES-256** as per UIDAI guidelines.
2. **GSTN Portal** — all taxpayer data in transit protected by **TLS 1.2/1.3** with Indian CCA-rooted certificates.
3. **Digital Signature Certificates (DSC)** — issued under the IT Act 2000 using **RSA-2048** for e-filing on MCA21 and Income Tax portals.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

> **(a)** Explain in detail the **five uses of encryption** as security services in any modern information system, with a real-world example for each. *(7 Marks)*

> **(b)** Discuss the role of encryption in **online banking** and **Aadhaar-based authentication**, highlighting the specific algorithms and standards used. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) The Five Security Services Enabled by Encryption**

Encryption is the underlying enabler for the following services. Each service requires a *different cryptographic mechanism*:

1. **Confidentiality** — provided by symmetric encryption (AES-256-CBC or AES-GCM).  
   *Example:* WhatsApp messages protected by the **Signal Protocol** (Double Ratchet + AES-256 + Curve25519).

2. **Integrity** — provided by cryptographic hash functions (SHA-256) or MACs (HMAC).  
   *Example:* When you download Ubuntu ISO, the SHA-256 checksum on the website lets you verify the file was not tampered in transit.

3. **Authentication** — provided by challenge–response, digital certificates, or password hashing.  
   *Example:* When you log in to net banking, the server verifies the **HMAC of (password + nonce)** without ever seeing the plaintext password.

4. **Non-Repudiation** — provided by digital signatures using asymmetric private keys.  
   *Example:* An entrepreneur signing an **MCA21 Form** with a Class-3 DSC; the signature can be verified by any registrar and binds the signer legally under the IT Act 2000.

5. **Access Control / Key Management** — provided by PKI, ABE, and HSM-based key custody.  
   *Example:* AWS KMS issues per-customer **CMKs (Customer Master Keys)** and enforces envelope encryption on S3 buckets.

**(b) Role of Encryption in Online Banking and Aadhaar**

**Online Banking (UPI / Cards / Net Banking):**

- **PIN Encryption during Card Transactions** — the PIN block is encrypted at the POS terminal using **AES-128/256** with a unique **DUKPT key** derived per transaction.
- **TLS 1.2/1.3** secures the channel between the customer's browser/app and the bank's server. Indian banks must follow the **RBI Cyber Security Framework (2024)** which mandates strong cipher suites and HSM-based key storage.
- **3-D Secure (EMV 3DS)** uses **RSA + ECDSA** signatures to authenticate the cardholder to the issuing bank.
- **UPI** uses **tokenization** (no actual card number stored) and **NPCI's Signed Message** protocol with RSA signatures.

**Aadhaar-based Authentication:**

- **Aadhaar Number (UID)** is never stored openly; the **Aadhaar Data Vault** stores a 256-bit **Reference Key** mapped to the UID; the vault itself is **AES-256 encrypted**.
- Authentication uses **ASA (Authentication Service Agency)** and **KUA (KYC User Agency)** under **UIDAI regulations**.
- The actual authentication request contains a **VID (Virtual ID)** or **token** instead of the Aadhaar number.
- The biometric match is performed inside the **CIDR** in encrypted form, and only a **YES/NO** response (and optional **KYC data**) is returned, encrypted with the requesting agency's public key (**RSA-2048**).
- **ABHA** (Ayushman Bharat Health Account) extends this with **tokenization + AES-256** plus an FHIR-based health data exchange.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **process of symmetric and asymmetric encryption**. Compare their advantages and limitations. *(7 Marks)*

> **(b)** A plaintext message **"H"** (ASCII 72) is encrypted using **RSA** with $p = 61$, $q = 53$, $e = 17$. Compute the ciphertext and demonstrate the decryption process. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagrams and Comparison**

*Symmetric Encryption Block Diagram* (refer Section 4.1 of these notes):

- Single key $K$ shared between sender and receiver.
- Speed: very high (used for bulk data).
- Examples: AES, 3DES, ChaCha20.
- *Limitation:* **Key distribution problem** — how to securely share $K$?

*Asymmetric Encryption Block Diagram* (refer Section 4.2 of these notes):

- Public key encrypts; private key decrypts.
- Solves the key distribution problem via PKI / digital certificates.
- *Limitation:* ~1000× slower; not suitable for bulk data.

| Property | Symmetric | Asymmetric |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Keys | 1 | 2 (public + private) |
| Speed | Fast | Slow |
| Key distribution | Difficult | Easy (publish public key) |
| Confidentiality | Yes | Yes |
| Non-repudiation | No | Yes (with digital signature) |
| Example | AES-256 | RSA-2048 |
| Typical key length | 128/256 bits | 2048/4096 bits |

**(b) RSA Numerical**

- Given: $p = 61, q = 53, e = 17, P = 72$.
- Compute $n = 61 \times 53 = 3233$.
- Compute $\phi(n) = (61-1)(53-1) = 60 \times 52 = 3120$.
- Compute $d$ such that $17d \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$. Using Extended Euclidean Algorithm: $d = 2753$.
- **Encryption:** $C \equiv P^e \bmod n = 72^{17} \bmod 3233$.

Square-and-multiply computation (as shown in Section 3.1, Step 7):

$$C = 1677$$

- **Decryption:** $P \equiv C^d \bmod n = 1677^{2753} \bmod 3233$.

By Euler's theorem, the result is $P = 72$, which corresponds to ASCII **"H"** — original plaintext recovered. ✓

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in 'Uses of Encryption' Questions**
> 1. **Confusing algorithm with application** — students often write "RSA is used for confidentiality *and* digital signatures" without specifying that the **same RSA primitive** is used in *inverse mode* (encrypt with private key ⇒ signature; encrypt with public key ⇒ confidentiality). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Writing only the name, not the service** — saying "encryption is used in HTTPS" is *not* enough. You must state *which service* (e.g., "**TLS 1.3 uses AES-256-GCM for confidentiality and ECDSA for server authentication**").
> 3. **Skipping the diagram** — for 7-mark sub-parts, a **labelled block diagram is mandatory**. A textual description alone loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring Indian context** — KTU examiners reward sector-specific Indian examples (Aadhaar, GSTN, UPI, RBI framework). Avoid generic global examples only.
> 5. **Key sizes not mentioned** — when discussing AES/RSA, always mention the **key size** (AES-128/256, RSA-2048). Markers check for this.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Encryption = $C = E_k(P)$**; its security rests on the **key**, not the algorithm (Kerckhoffs).
- **Symmetric** = one key, fast, used for bulk data (AES-256).
- **Asymmetric** = key pair, slow, used for key exchange and signatures (RSA-2048, ECC-256).
- The **five security services** are: Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication, Non-Repudiation, Access Control — *each maps to a different cryptographic mechanism*.
- **Public sector (India):** Aadhaar (AES-256 + tokenization), GSTN (TLS 1.2/1.3), eSign (RSA-2048 digital signatures), ABHA.
- **Private sector:** UPI/card payments (EMV 3DS + DUKPT), e-commerce (TLS + PCI-DSS tokenization), cloud (envelope encryption with KMS), 5G (SUCI via ECIES).
- **RSA numerical** questions are high-probability: practice $n = pq$, $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$, $d = e^{-1} \bmod \phi(n)$, $C = P^e \bmod n$.
- **AES** key sizes: 128, 192, 256 bits; block size always 128 bits; rounds 10, 12, 14.
- **Hybrid encryption** (RSA + AES) is the de-facto standard in TLS 1.3.
- **Kerckhoffs's Principle (1883)** is the philosophical foundation of modern cryptography — *always quote it* in theory questions.
- **Information-theoretic goal** of encryption: make $I(P; C) = 0$ (zero mutual information between plaintext and ciphertext).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Secure Programs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT753 - INFORMATION SECURITY IN PUBLIC AND PRIVATE SECTORS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Secure Programs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Secure Program** is a software application or system that has been explicitly designed, developed, tested, and maintained to operate reliably and safely in the presence of malicious inputs, unauthorized access attempts, and adversarial environmental conditions. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (PECCT753) framework, a secure program is one that enforces the **CIA Triad (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability)** and the extended **Parkerian Hexad** properties at the code, runtime, and architectural levels.

In formal terms, a program $P$ is considered secure if, for every possible input $I$ from the input domain $\mathcal{I}$, and every possible adversarial action $A$ from the threat model $\mathcal{T}$:

$$\forall I \in \mathcal{I}, \forall A \in \mathcal{T} : P(I, A) \rightarrow S$$

where $S$ represents a state transition that maintains the predefined security invariants (no unauthorized disclosure, no unauthorized modification, no denial of service to legitimate users).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A secure program is NOT the same as a functional program. A program can be 100% functionally correct (does what it's supposed to do) yet be completely insecure (fails to prevent what it shouldn't allow). Security must be a **non-functional requirement** embedded from the design phase itself.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Bank Vault Metaphor**
> 
> Imagine you are building a **bank vault**:
> - A **functional program** is like a vault door that **opens and closes** correctly (functional requirement met).
> - A **secure program** is a vault door that additionally:
>   1. **Resists lock-picking** (resists unauthorized access — *Confidentiality*)
>   2. **Detects tampering** attempts and triggers alarms (resists unauthorized modification — *Integrity*)
>   3. **Cannot be jammed shut** by an attacker to lock out customers (resists DoS — *Availability*)
>   4. Has **CCTV inside and outside** (auditability and logging)
>   5. Has **fireproofing** (resilience to environmental threats)
> 
> Writing a secure program is like engineering that vault door — security is **architectural**, not an add-on sticker.

### 1.3 Key Characteristics of Secure Programs

| Characteristic | Description | KTU High-Yield Keyword |
|----------------|-------------|--------------------------|
| **Least Privilege** | Code runs with minimum permissions needed | Principle of Least Privilege |
| **Defense in Depth** | Multiple overlapping security layers | Layered Security |
| **Fail-Safe Defaults** | Default state is secure; access denied unless explicitly granted | Default Deny |
| **Complete Mediation** | Every access is checked against access control policy | Authorization Check |
| **Open Design** | Security does not depend on secrecy of mechanism | Kerckhoffs' Principle |
| **Separation of Privilege** | Multiple conditions required for access | Multi-factor Authorization |
| **Least Common Mechanism** | Minimize shared state between users | Isolation |
| **Psychological Acceptability** | Security mechanisms are user-friendly | Usability |
| **Economy of Mechanism** | Keep design small and simple | Simplicity (Saltzer & Schroeder) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **9 design principles** listed above are the famous **Saltzer and Schroeder Protection Principles (1975)** — these are **guaranteed KTU exam content** for any "Secure Programs" question worth 7-14 marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Secure Program Development Lifecycle (SSDLC)

A secure program is not produced by accident — it emerges from a structured **Software Development Lifecycle (SDLC)** that bakes security into every phase. The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes the **Security-Enhanced SDLC** (also called the **Microsoft SDL** or **Touchpoints Model**).

#### 2.1.1 The 6 Phases of SSDLC

**Phase 1 — Requirements & Use Cases (Security Requirements Gathering)**
- Identify **security goals** (e.g., "user passwords must be hashed with bcrypt")
- Document **abuse cases** (what an attacker would do) alongside normal use cases
- Define **compliance requirements** (GDPR, PCI-DSS, HIPAA, IT Act 2000/India)

**Phase 2 — Design (Threat Modeling)**
- Use **STRIDE** model (Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, DoS, Elevation of Privilege)
- Use **DREAD** for risk prioritization (Damage, Reproducibility, Exploitability, Affected Users, Discoverability)
- Define **security architecture** (firewalls, access control matrices, trust boundaries)

**Phase 3 — Implementation (Secure Coding)**
- Follow language-specific secure coding standards (e.g., **CERT**, **OWASP Top 10**)
- Apply **input validation**, **output encoding**, **parameterized queries**
- Use **static analysis tools** (SonarQube, Checkmarx, Fortify)

**Phase 4 — Testing (Security Testing)**
- **Penetration testing** (ethical hacking)
- **Fuzz testing** (feeding random/malformed inputs)
- **Dynamic Application Security Testing (DAST)**
- **Interactive Application Security Testing (IAST)**

**Phase 5 — Deployment (Hardening)**
- Disable unnecessary services, ports, and accounts
- Apply **principle of least privilege** at OS level
- Configure **security headers** (HSTS, CSP, X-Frame-Options)

**Phase 6 — Maintenance (Patch Management)**
- Monitor for new vulnerabilities (CVE feeds, NVD)
- Apply patches promptly
- **Incident response** planning

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Secure Programs — The Cost Argument

The economic justification for secure programming is the **1:10:100 Rule** (also called the **IBM Systems Sciences Institute Rule**):

| Stage of Defect Fix | Relative Cost (₹) |
|---------------------|---------------------|
| Requirements/Design phase | ₹1 (baseline) |
| Implementation phase | ₹10 |
| Post-deployment / Production | ₹100–₹1,000 |

$$\text{Total Cost of Insecurity} = C_{\text{dev-fix}} + C_{\text{breach}} + C_{\text{reputation}} + C_{\text{legal}}$$

A single breach (e.g., **Equifax 2017**, **WannaCry 2017**, **Log4Shell 2021**) can cost hundreds of crores — proving that **prevention via secure programs is far cheaper than cure**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Reference Table for KTU 2024 ESE — Secure Programs**

| Concept | Formula / Principle | Application |
|---------|--------------------|--------------|
| Risk Severity | $R = P \times I$ (Probability × Impact) | Threat Prioritization |
| DREAD Score | $D = \frac{D+R+E+A+Di}{5}$ | Risk Ranking (each rated 1-10) |
| STRIDE Categories | S, T, R, I, D, E | Threat Classification |
| Attack Surface | $AS = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{Inputs} + \text{Outputs} + \text{Protocols})$ | Security Footprint |
| Trust Boundary | Logical/physical line where data changes trust level | Threat Modeling |
| Input Validation Rule | $\text{Accept only} = \{\text{Whitelist}\}; \text{Reject all} \notin \text{Whitelist}$ | Default-Deny Principle |
| Hashing Strength | $H(n) = 2^{n} \text{ operations to break}$ | Crypto Key Length (128-bit = $2^{128}$) |
| Password Storage | $\text{Store} = \text{Hash}(\text{Password} + \text{Salt})$ | Never plain-text |
| Defense in Depth | $P_{\text{breach}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} p_i$ (probabilities multiply) | Layered Security Math |
| CVSS Score | $0 \leq \text{CVSS} \leq 10$ | Vulnerability Severity |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of Secure Programs

Secure programs are the **foundation of all digital trust** in modern engineering:

- **Banking & Finance**: UPI, NEFT, net-banking apps (PCI-DSS compliance)
- **Healthcare**: Electronic Health Records (HIPAA)
- **E-Governance**: Aadhaar, DigiLocker, GST portals
- **IoT & Critical Infrastructure**: SCADA systems, smart grids
- **Cloud & SaaS**: Multi-tenant isolation in AWS/Azure/GCP
- **Defence & Aerospace**: Mission-critical command-and-control systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Context:** Under India's **Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDPA) 2023** and the **IT Act 2000 (amended 2008)**, organizations face legal liability for insecure programs that leak personal data. Secure programming is no longer optional — it is a **legal obligation**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Secure Program Properties

To deeply understand secure programs, we map each **Saltzer-Schroeder principle** to its **engineering mechanism** and **failure mode if violated**:

| # | Saltzer-Schroeder Principle | Engineering Mechanism | Failure Mode if Violated |
|---|------------------------------|------------------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | **Least Privilege** | Run processes as non-root; RBAC | Privilege escalation attacks (e.g., sudo bugs) |
| 2 | **Defense in Depth** | Firewall + IDS + Encryption + Auth | Single-point compromise = total breach |
| 3 | **Fail-Safe Defaults** | Default-deny ACLs | Accidental overexposure (e.g., open S3 buckets) |
| 4 | **Complete Mediation** | Re-check authz on every request | TOCTOU (Time-of-Check to Time-of-Use) bugs |
| 5 | **Open Design** | Open-source crypto (AES, RSA) | "Security through obscurity" — fragile |
| 6 | **Separation of Privilege** | MFA, dual-control, quorum auth | Single credential compromise = full access |
| 7 | **Least Common Mechanism** | Process isolation, containers | Cross-tenant data leakage |
| 8 | **Psychological Acceptability** | Single sign-on, password managers | Users bypass security (post-it passwords) |
| 9 | **Economy of Mechanism** | KISS — small, auditable code | Bugs hidden in complexity |

### 3.2 Derivation: Why "Defense in Depth" Mathematically Works

The probability of a successful breach against a single layer is $p$ (where $0 < p < 1$). The probability that an attacker **bypasses that one layer** is $p$.

For a system with **$n$ independent security layers**, assuming independence:

$$P(\text{breach}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} p_i$$

Let us work through a concrete derivation step-by-step.

**Step 1 — Single Layer Baseline**

Suppose a firewall alone has a $10\%$ chance of being bypassed by a sophisticated attacker:

$$p_1 = 0.10$$

**Step 2 — Add a Second Independent Layer (IDS)**

$$p_2 = 0.10$$

**Step 3 — Compute Combined Breach Probability**

$$P(\text{breach}) = p_1 \times p_2 = 0.10 \times 0.10 = 0.01 = 1\%$$

**Step 4 — Add a Third Layer (Encryption)**

$$p_3 = 0.10$$

$$P(\text{breach}) = p_1 \times p_2 \times p_3 = 0.10^3 = 0.001 = 0.1\%$$

**Step 5 — Generalization to $n$ Layers**

$$P(\text{breach}, n) = p^n$$

Taking the limit as $n \to \infty$ with $p < 1$:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} p^n = 0 \quad \text{when } 0 < p < 1$$

**Step 6 — Engineering Insight**

This geometric decay is why **layered security** is mathematically powerful. Even modest per-layer defenses (e.g., 90% effectiveness) compound into near-zero breach probability with sufficient layers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Caveat (KTU Exam Point):** The derivation above assumes **layer independence**, which is rarely perfectly true in practice. A clever attacker exploits **dependencies** between layers (e.g., one vulnerability that defeats firewall + IDS + encryption simultaneously). Hence, the principle is "Defense in Depth" — *not* "Defense in Perfect Independence."

### 3.3 Derivation: DREAD Risk Scoring

DREAD is a quantitative risk-modeling framework. Each factor is rated 1–10, then averaged.

**Step 1 — Define the 5 Factors**

$$D = \text{Damage potential (1–10)}$$
$$R = \text{Reproducibility (1–10)}$$
$$E = \text{Exploitability (1–10)}$$
$$A = \text{Affected users (1–10)}$$
$$Di = \text{Discoverability (1–10)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the DREAD Score**

$$\text{DREAD} = \frac{D + R + E + A + Di}{5}$$

**Step 3 — Worked Example: SQL Injection in a Banking App**

| Factor | Rating | Justification |
|--------|--------|---------------|
| Damage | 9 | Can read/write entire DB |
| Reproducibility | 10 | 100% reliable with sqlmap |
| Exploitability | 8 | Public tools available |
| Affected Users | 10 | All customers |
| Discoverability | 9 | OWASP Top 1 |

$$\text{DREAD} = \frac{9 + 10 + 8 + 10 + 9}{5} = \frac{46}{5} = 9.2$$

**Step 4 — Risk Classification**

| DREAD Score | Risk Level | Action |
|-------------|------------|--------|
| $0.0 - 3.9$ | Low | Monitor, document |
| $4.0 - 6.9$ | Medium | Plan remediation |
| $7.0 - 8.9$ | High | Fix within sprint |
| $9.0 - 10.0$ | Critical | Emergency patch |

The SQL injection example (9.2) is **Critical** — must be patched immediately.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common Secure Programming Failures (OWASP Top 10 Subset)

| Vulnerability | Description | CWE | Secure Coding Fix |
|---------------|-------------|-----|-------------------|
| **Buffer Overflow** | Writing past array bounds | CWE-120 | Use safe functions (`strncpy`, `fgets`) |
| **SQL Injection** | Unvalidated SQL in inputs | CWE-89 | Parameterized queries / Prepared statements |
| **XSS** | Injecting scripts into pages | CWE-79 | Output encoding, Content Security Policy |
| **CSRF** | Forged cross-site requests | CWE-352 | Anti-CSRF tokens, SameSite cookies |
| **Insecure Deserialization** | Untrusted object deserialization | CWE-502 | Input validation, signed payloads |
| **Hardcoded Credentials** | Passwords in source code | CWE-798 | Use secret managers (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager) |
| **Race Conditions (TOCTOU)** | Time-of-check vs time-of-use | CWE-367 | Atomic operations, file locks |
| **Integer Overflow** | Arithmetic exceeds type range | CWE-190 | Use safe integer libraries, bounds checks |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Secure Software Development Lifecycle (SSDLC) — Mermaid Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Phase 1: Requirements & Security Goals] --> B[Phase 2: Design & Threat Modeling]
    B --> C[Phase 3: Secure Implementation]
    C --> D[Phase 4: Security Testing]
    D --> E{Tests Pass?}
    E -->|No| F[Bug Fix / Re-design]
    F --> C
    E -->|Yes| G[Phase 5: Deployment Hardening]
    G --> H[Phase 6: Maintenance & Patching]
    H --> I[Vulnerability Discovered]
    I --> J[Patch Management Cycle]
    J --> H
```

### 4.2 STRIDE Threat Model — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    User[External User] -->|Input| TB[Trust Boundary]
    TB --> App[Application Server]
    App --> DB[(Database)]
    
    S[Spoofing] -.->|Attack Vector| App
    T[Tampering] -.->|Attack Vector| App
    R[Repudiation] -.->|Attack Vector| Logs[Audit Logs]
    I[Information Disclosure] -.->|Attack Vector| DB
    D[Denial of Service] -.->|Attack Vector| App
    E[Elevation of Privilege] -.->|Attack Vector| App
```

### 4.3 Defense-in-Depth Layered Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Layer 1: Perimeter Firewall] --> L2[Layer 2: Intrusion Detection System]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3: Application Authentication]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4: Input Validation & Sanitization]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5: Encrypted Data at Rest]
    L5 --> L6[Layer 6: Audit Logging & Monitoring]
    
    Attacker[Attacker] -.->|Bypass L1| L2
    Attacker -.->|Bypass L1+L2| L3
    Attacker -.->|Bypass L1+L2+L3| L4
    Attacker -.->|Bypass L1+L2+L3+L4| L5
    Attacker -.->|Bypass L1+L2+L3+L4+L5| L6
```

### 4.4 Secure Program Properties Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root[Secure Program]
    Root --> Conf[Confidentiality]
    Root --> Int[Integrity]
    Root --> Avail[Availability]
    
    Conf --> A1[Encryption]
    Conf --> A2[Access Control]
    Int --> B1[Hashing]
    Int --> B2[Digital Signatures]
    Avail --> C1[Redundancy]
    Avail --> C2[DDoS Mitigation]
    
    A1 --> Saltzer[Saltzer-Schroeder Principles]
    A2 --> Saltzer
    B1 --> Saltzer
    B2 --> Saltzer
    C1 --> Saltzer
    C2 --> Saltzer
```

### 4.5 Input Validation Pipeline (Defensive Programming)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Input] --> B[Length Check]
    B --> C[Type Check]
    C --> D[Whitelist Regex Match]
    D --> E[Sanitization]
    E --> F[Output Encoding]
    F --> G[Trusted Data Sink]
    
    B -->|Fail| X[Reject - Log]
    C -->|Fail| X
    D -->|Fail| X
    E -->|Fail| X
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** A complete answer to "Explain the characteristics of a secure program" **must include a diagram** (SSDLC or layered architecture). The above Mermaid diagrams are presentation-ready for your 14-mark answers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]**

**Q1. Define a Secure Program. List any four characteristics of a secure program.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **secure program** is a software application designed, developed, and maintained to function correctly even under malicious attack, preserving confidentiality, integrity, and availability of data and resources.

**Four characteristics (1 mark each — any four of the following):**
1. **Least Privilege** — code runs with minimum required permissions
2. **Defense in Depth** — multiple overlapping security layers
3. **Fail-Safe Defaults** — default state denies access
4. **Complete Mediation** — every access is authorized
5. **Open Design** — security not based on secrecy of mechanism
6. **Economy of Mechanism** — simple, auditable design

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** The definition carries 1 mark; listing 4 characteristics carries 2 marks (½ mark each). Examiners award **½ mark only for principle names, not explanations**, in 3-mark questions.

---

**Q2. What is the principle of "Least Privilege" in secure program design? Give one example.**
