**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP)** states that every module, process, or user must be able to access only the information and resources that are **strictly necessary** for its legitimate purpose — no more, no less.

**Example (1 mark):**
A web application that reads product data from a database should connect using a database user account that has **only `SELECT` permission** on the `products` table — not `DROP`, `DELETE`, or `ADMIN` privileges. If the web app is compromised via SQL injection, the attacker cannot delete tables or escalate to admin.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Split:** Definition (2 marks) + Example (1 mark).

---

### 📕 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Paper Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Saltzer-Schroeder Protection Principles** for designing secure programs. Describe any **five** principles with examples. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** What is the **Secure Software Development Lifecycle (SSDLC)**? Explain its phases with a neat diagram. **[7 marks]**

##### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks

The **Saltzer-Schroeder Principles** (1975) form the foundational rules for designing secure computer systems. (1 mark for introduction)

**1. Least Privilege (1 mark)**
Every program and user should operate using the **least set of privileges** necessary to complete the task.
*Example:* A backup process should not need to write to system files; a web server should run as a non-root user.

**2. Defense in Depth (1 mark)**
Security should rely on **multiple overlapping layers** so that a failure in one layer does not result in total compromise.
*Example:* Firewall + Antivirus + Encryption + Access Control — an attacker must defeat all layers.

**3. Fail-Safe Defaults (1 mark)**
The default configuration should be **secure** — access should be denied unless explicitly granted.
*Example:* A new firewall rule should block all traffic; only specific allow-rules are added.

**4. Complete Mediation (1 mark)**
Every access to every object must be **checked for authorization** on every occasion, not cached.
*Example:* Re-validating a session token on every HTTP request, not just at login.

**5. Open Design (1 mark)**
Security should not depend on the **secrecy of the mechanism**, but only on the secrecy of keys.
*Example:* AES encryption is open and publicly audited; its security comes from the 128/256-bit key, not from hiding the algorithm. (1 mark for explanation)

##### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks

The **Secure Software Development Lifecycle (SSDLC)** is a methodology that integrates security activities into every phase of the software development process. (1 mark)

**Phases (1 mark each — any 6):**

1. **Requirements & Security Goals** — define security policies, abuse cases
2. **Design & Threat Modeling** — STRIDE, DREAD analysis
3. **Secure Implementation** — secure coding standards (OWASP, CERT)
4. **Security Testing** — penetration testing, fuzzing, DAST/SAST
5. **Deployment Hardening** — disable unused services, configure security headers
6. **Maintenance & Patching** — CVE monitoring, patch management

**Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to the Mermaid SSDLC flowchart in Section 4.1 above — draw a similar labeled circular/iterative diagram showing the 6 phases with feedback loop from testing back to coding.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - Students often **list STRIDE letters without explaining each** → loses 3-4 marks
> - In diagram questions, **missing arrows / unlabeled boxes** = −2 marks
> - Writing "SDLC" instead of **"Secure SDLC"** = conceptual confusion, −1 mark
> - **Not giving an example** for each principle = −2 marks (examiners expect examples)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** What is **Threat Modeling**? Explain the **STRIDE** model in detail with examples. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain **Defense in Depth** as a security strategy. Prove mathematically that layering improves security, and give a real-world example. **[7 marks]**

##### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks

**Threat Modeling (1 mark):**
A structured process to **identify, classify, and prioritize potential security threats** to a system, and to define countermeasures.

**STRIDE Model (Microsoft) — developed by Loren Kohnfelder and Praerit Garg (1999):**

| Category | Threat | Example (1 mark total) |
|----------|--------|------------------------|
| **S** — Spoofing | Impersonating a user or system | Stealing login credentials to pretend to be a valid user |
| **T** — Tampering | Modifying data in transit or at rest | Man-in-the-Middle altering bank transfer amount |
| **R** — Repudiation | Denying an action with no proof | User denies making a transaction; no audit log exists |
| **I** — Information Disclosure | Leaking confidential data | Unencrypted database dump published online |
| **D** — Denial of Service | Making system unavailable | DDoS attack flooding server with traffic |
| **E** — Elevation of Privilege | Gaining unauthorized access level | Buffer overflow giving `root` shell |

(1 mark for each category: ½ for category meaning + ½ for example → 6 marks total)

##### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks

**Defense in Depth (1 mark):**
A security strategy that employs **multiple layers of controls** so that the failure of one layer is compensated by other layers.

**Mathematical Proof (3 marks):**

Let $p$ = probability that an attacker bypasses **one** layer.

$$P(\text{breach after 1 layer}) = p$$

$$P(\text{breach after 2 layers}) = p^2$$

$$P(\text{breach after } n \text{ layers}) = p^n$$

$$\text{Since } 0 < p < 1, \quad \lim_{n \to \infty} p^n = 0$$

**Worked Example (1 mark):**
If each layer is 80% effective ($p = 0.2$), then with 3 layers:

$$P(\text{breach}) = 0.2^3 = 0.008 = 0.8\%$$

Down from 20% with a single layer — a **25× reduction in risk**.

**Real-World Example (2 marks):**
A modern bank uses: (i) physical vault + biometric door, (ii) network firewall, (iii) MFA login, (iv) transaction signing with OTP, (v) encrypted database with audit logs. An attacker must defeat **all 5 layers** — economically infeasible.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Forgetting the independence assumption** when deriving $p^n$ → lose 1 mark
> - Writing "Defense in Depth" as a *single* concept without **layers** = incomplete answer
> - In STRIDE, students often **confuse "R"** — Repudiation means the *user denies the action*, not the system denying the user. This is a common 1-mark deduction.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **🔐 Rapid Revision Checklist — Secure Programs (Module 2)**

- ✅ A **secure program** preserves **CIA (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability)** against malicious inputs.
- ✅ **Saltzer-Schroeder Principles** (1975) — the **9 golden rules** of secure design; **must memorize all 9** for KTU.
- ✅ **Defense in Depth** — security improves **geometrically** with layers: $P(\text{breach}) = p^n$.
- ✅ **SSDLC** has 6 phases: Requirements → Design → Implementation → Testing → Deployment → Maintenance.
- ✅ **STRIDE** is for *threat classification*; **DREAD** is for *threat prioritization*.
- ✅ **OWASP Top 10** — most common web vulnerabilities; SQL Injection, XSS, CSRF are top 3.
- ✅ **Secure coding** = input validation + output encoding + parameterized queries + least privilege + no hardcoded secrets.
- ✅ **Open Design principle** (Kerckhoffs' doctrine): security must not depend on keeping algorithms secret, only keys.
- ✅ **1:10:100 Rule** — fixing bugs at design is 100× cheaper than in production.
- ✅ **Default-Deny** = always reject input unless explicitly allowed (whitelist approach).
- ✅ **Complete Mediation** — re-validate authorization on **every** access; beware of **TOCTOU** race conditions.
- ✅ **Defense in Depth ≠ Security through Obscurity** — layered security is transparent and verified.
- ✅ **CVE / NVD** are the standard sources for tracking software vulnerabilities.
- ✅ **DPDPA 2023** + **IT Act 2000** make insecure programming a **legal liability** in India.
- ✅ For KTU 14-mark answers, always include: **Definition + List + Explanation + Example + Diagram**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra:** *Secure programs are built, not bolted on. Security is a process, not a product. — Bruce Schneier*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Malicious Program Errors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT753 - INFORMATION SECURITY IN PUBLIC AND SECTORS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Non-Malicious Program Errors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-Malicious Program Errors** are unintentional flaws, bugs, or defects in software code that arise from human mistakes, logical oversights, or environmental inconsistencies during the software development lifecycle. Unlike malware or malicious exploits, these errors are **not deliberately crafted** by attackers, but they can nonetheless create severe security vulnerabilities that adversaries may opportunistically leverage.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Information Security in Public and Private Sectors*, non-malicious program errors form a critical subset of **software-induced security weaknesses** that exist in the *pre-attack surface* — meaning the vulnerabilities exist independently of any threat actor, and security incidents occur only when an attacker discovers and weaponizes them.

**Taxonomic Classification (KTU Standard):**

| Error Category | Source of Defect | Primary Security Risk |
|---|---|---|
| **Buffer Overflow** | Inadequate bounds checking | Arbitrary code execution |
| **Integer Overflow / Underflow** | Arithmetic boundary violation | Logic bypass, memory corruption |
| **Race Condition** | Improper synchronization | Privilege escalation, data corruption |
| **Time-of-Check to Time-of-Use (TOCTOU)** | Non-atomic validation | Authorization bypass |
| **Uninitialized Memory Access** | Missing initialization | Information disclosure |
| **Memory Leak** | Missing deallocation | Denial of Service (DoS) |
| **Format String Vulnerability** | Unvalidated user input in format calls | Memory disclosure, code execution |
| **Null Pointer Dereference** | Missing null check | Denial of Service |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Examinations:**
> - *Malicious code* = intentionally weaponized (viruses, worms, trojans, logic bombs)
> - *Non-malicious errors* = unintentional but exploitable (the focus of this module)

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: The Unlocked Bank Vault Door**
>
> Imagine a bank vault with a heavy steel door. The door itself is perfectly designed (good architecture), but the night-shift security guard forgets to lock the deadbolt every evening — not because he wants robbers to enter, but simply out of habit fatigue. The vault has a **non-malicious error**: an oversight in the closing protocol.
>
> No thief broke in deliberately through the door. But when a passing burglar notices the deadbolt is disengaged, he walks straight in. **The error was unintentional; the exploitation was opportunistic.**

Similarly, in software:
- The **programmer** (like the guard) makes an innocent mistake.
- The **system** (like the vault) functions "normally" in benign use.
- The **attacker** (like the burglar) opportunistically discovers and weaponizes the oversight.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Program Error Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry-Standard Quantitative Metrics:**
>
> - **Defects per KLOC (Thousand Lines of Code)** = $\text{Defects} / (\text{Lines of Code} / 1000)$
> - **Mean Time to Failure (MTTF)** = Average operational time between crashes
> - **Cyclomatic Complexity** = $M = E - N + 2P$ (McCabe's metric)
> - **CVSS Base Score** = Common Vulnerability Scoring System (range **0.0 to 10.0**)
> - **CWE (Common Weakness Enumeration)** = MITRE classification ID for the error type

Where:
- $E$ = number of edges in the control flow graph
- $N$ = number of nodes
- $P$ = number of connected components (typically 1)
- **CVSS 0.0–3.9** = Low | **4.0–6.9** = Medium | **7.0–8.9** = High | **9.0–10.0** = Critical
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Major Non-Malicious Error Types

#### 2.1.1 Buffer Overflow

A **buffer overflow** occurs when data written to a memory buffer exceeds the buffer's allocated capacity, causing adjacent memory regions to be overwritten.

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. The programmer declares a fixed-size buffer (e.g., 16 bytes on the stack).
2. A user-input function (like `gets()`, `strcpy()`, or `scanf("%s", buf)`) is invoked without bounds checking.
3. Input longer than the buffer's capacity is accepted.
4. Excess bytes spill into adjacent memory: saved frame pointer, return address, function arguments.
5. The function returns to an attacker-controlled address → arbitrary code execution.

**Why it matters:** Buffer overflows have been the root cause of legendary exploits such as **Code Red (2001)**, **Slammer (2003)**, and **Heartbleed (2014)**.

---

#### 2.1.2 Integer Overflow / Underflow

**Integer overflow** happens when an arithmetic operation produces a result outside the representable range of the integer type.

**Operational Logic:**
1. An 8-bit unsigned integer can represent values from **0 to 255** ($2^8 - 1$).
2. Adding 1 to 255 yields 256, which wraps around to **0** (modulo $2^8$).
3. In C/C++ with unsigned semantics, this wraparound is silent — no exception is raised.
4. A length check like `if (len + 1 > BUF_SIZE)` may evaluate to `false` due to wraparound, allowing a subsequent `memcpy` to overflow the buffer.

**Signed integer underflow** is even more dangerous because it invokes **Undefined Behavior (UB)** in C/C++, allowing the compiler to optimize away security checks.

---

#### 2.1.3 Race Condition (TOCTOU)

A **race condition** is a flaw where the system's behavior depends on the non-deterministic ordering or timing of events.

**Time-of-Check to Time-of-Use (TOCTOU)** is a specific subtype:
1. **Time-of-Check (TOC):** The system validates a condition (e.g., "does the user have permission to access file X?").
2. **Time-of-Use (TOU):** The system acts on the previously validated condition.
3. **Window of Vulnerability:** Between TOC and TOU, an attacker alters the state (e.g., swaps the symbolic link target), causing the action to be performed on an unintended resource.

**Why it matters:** Race conditions are notoriously hard to reproduce and test, yet they power **privilege escalation** attacks on Unix `setuid` programs and **dirty COW** (CVE-2016-5195).

---

#### 2.1.4 Format String Vulnerability

Occurs when user input is passed directly as the format string argument to `printf`, `syslog`, or similar functions.

**Operational Logic:**
1. Code like `printf(user_input);` is written instead of `printf("%s", user_input);`.
2. Attacker supplies `%x%x%x%x` to leak stack memory.
3. Attacker supplies `%n` to write the number of bytes printed so far into a memory address, achieving **arbitrary write**.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Concept | Definition | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $2^n$ | Max unsigned value of $n$-bit integer | $n$ bits |
| $-2^{n-1}$ | Min signed integer | Two's complement |
| $2^{n-1} - 1$ | Max signed integer | $n$ bits |
| KLOC | Thousand Lines of Code | $10^3$ LOC |
| CVSS | Common Vulnerability Scoring System | 0.0 – 10.0 |
| CWE | Common Weakness Enumeration | MITRE ID |
| $M = E - N + 2P$ | McCabe's Cyclomatic Complexity | Unitless |
| ASLR | Address Space Layout Randomization | Bits of entropy |
| DEP / NX | Data Execution Prevention / No-Execute | Binary flag |
| Stack Canary | Random value guarding return address | 4 or 8 bytes |
| FORTIFY_SOURCE | GCC compile-time buffer check macro | Levels 1, 2, 3 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Defense-in-Depth Layers** (frequently asked in KTU ESE):
> 1. **Static Analysis** (compile-time): `-Wall -Wextra -Wformat-security`
> 2. **Dynamic Analysis** (run-time): AddressSanitizer (ASan), Valgrind
> 3. **Compiler Hardening:** Stack canaries, FORTIFY_SOURCE, RELRO, PIE
> 4. **OS-Level Mitigations:** ASLR, DEP/NX bit, SEHOP
> 5. **Language Choice:** Use Rust, Go, or modern C# over legacy C/C++

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this matter to a KTU engineer?**
> - The **OWASP Top 10** (2021) lists *Memory Safety* issues (CWE-787, CWE-125) among the top untrusted-deserialization-adjacent risks.
> - Microsoft's Security Response Center reports that **~70% of all CVEs in Windows and Office** are memory safety issues.
> - The **White House ONCD Report (2024)** urged migration to memory-safe languages citing non-malicious errors as a national security concern.
> - In the **public sector**, e-governance portals (Kerala e-District, DigiLocker, Aadhaar) handle PII; a single buffer overflow can leak millions of records.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: McCabe's Cyclomatic Complexity

Cyclomatic Complexity measures the number of linearly independent paths through a program's source code. It is the most exam-frequently asked quantitative metric for KTU 2024.

**Derivation from Control Flow Graph (CFG):**

Let us derive the formula step by step.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Define the three topological parameters of a CFG.} \\
E &= \text{Number of edges (arcs) in the CFG} \\
N &= \text{Number of nodes (basic blocks)} \\
P &= \text{Number of connected components (entry-to-exit paths)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 2: Apply Euler's formula for a connected planar graph.} \\
V - E + F &= 2 \quad \text{(Euler's formula)} \\
\text{For a CFG, } V &= N, \quad F = P + 1 \quad \text{(faces including the unbounded outer face)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 3: Substitute and simplify.} \\
N - E + (P + 1) &= 2 \\
N - E + P + 1 &= 2 \\
N - E + P &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 4: Cyclomatic complexity } M \text{ is defined as the number of independent circuits.} \\
M &= E - N + P
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{M = E - N + 2P}
$$

For a single connected program (typical case), $P = 1$, so the formula reduces to $M = E - N + 2$.

**Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Pattern):**

Given a CFG with $E = 11$ edges, $N = 8$ nodes, $P = 1$ component:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= E - N + 2P \\
M &= 11 - 8 + 2(1) \\
M &= 11 - 8 + 2 \\
M &= 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation (for 7-mark KTU sub-questions):**
- $M \leq 10$ → Low risk, easy to test
- $11 \leq M \leq 20$ → Moderate risk, structured testing required
- $21 \leq M \leq 50$ → High risk, complex
- $M > 50$ → Untestable, requires redesign

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Buffer Offset Mathematics

Consider a stack frame layout in a vulnerable C function:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stack grows downward. Address increases upward in memory.} \\
\text{Layout (low to high address):} \\
\texttt{[local buffer (16 bytes)]} \rightarrow \texttt{[saved EBP (4 bytes)]} \rightarrow \texttt{[return address (4 bytes)]}
\end{aligned}
$$

If `gets(buf)` is called and the attacker inputs 24 bytes:
- Bytes **0–15** fill the buffer.
- Bytes **16–19** overwrite the **saved frame pointer (EBP)**.
- Bytes **20–23** overwrite the **return address**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Offset to return address} &= \text{Buffer Size} + \text{Saved EBP Size} \\
&= 16 + 4 = 20 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus the attacker places a shellcode address at byte offset 20 to redirect execution upon function return. This derivation is the foundation of the classic *Aleph One "Smashing the Stack for Fun and Profit"* (1996) attack model.

---

### 3.3 Integer Overflow Boundary Derivation

For a 32-bit unsigned integer:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Max representable value} &= 2^{32} - 1 = 4{,}294{,}967{,}295 \\
\text{If we add 1 to } 2^{32} - 1: \\
(2^{32} - 1) + 1 &= 2^{32} \\
2^{32} \mod 2^{32} &= 0 \quad \text{(wraparound)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a 32-bit signed integer (two's complement):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Max signed value} &= 2^{31} - 1 = 2{,}147{,}483{,}647 \\
\text{Min signed value} &= -2^{31} = -2{,}147{,}483{,}648 \\
\text{If we add 1 to } 2^{31} - 1: \\
(2^{31} - 1) + 1 &= 2^{31} \quad \text{(positive overflow into negative territory)} \\
2^{31} \text{ in two's complement} &= -2^{31} = -2{,}147{,}483{,}648
\end{aligned}
$$

**Security Implication (a KTU-favorite question):**

Suppose a length check is written as:
```c
if (len + 1 > BUF_SIZE) return ERROR;
```

If `len = 0xFFFFFFFF` (32-bit unsigned max) and `BUF_SIZE = 100`, then:
$$
\begin{aligned}
len + 1 &= 0xFFFFFFFF + 1 = 0 \pmod{2^{32}} \\
0 > 100 &\rightarrow \text{false} \quad \text{(check bypassed)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The subsequent `memcpy(dst, src, len)` then writes 4 GB, overflowing the buffer catastrophically.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Error vs. Mitigation Matrix

| Error Type | Root Cause | CWE-ID | Primary Mitigation | Defense Layer |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Stack Buffer Overflow | Unbounded `strcpy` / `gets` | CWE-121 | Stack canary, FORTIFY_SOURCE | Compiler + OS |
| Heap Buffer Overflow | `malloc` + unchecked size | CWE-122 | ASan, safe allocators | Runtime |
| Integer Overflow | Arithmetic on fixed-width types | CWE-190 | SafeInt, `-fsanitize=integer` | Compiler |
| Format String | User input as format arg | CWE-134 | `-Wformat-security` | Compile-time |
| Race Condition | Non-atomic file ops | CWE-362 | File locking, atomic syscalls | OS |
| TOCTOU | `access()` + `open()` split | CWE-367 | `openat()` with `O_NOFOLLOW` | Kernel |
| Null Pointer Deref | Missing null check | CWE-476 | Defensive coding, languages with `Option` | Language |
| Memory Leak | Missing `free()` | CWE-401 | RAII, smart pointers, GC | Language |
| Use-After-Free | Dangling pointer | CWE-416 | ASan, MSan, ownership semantics | Runtime |
| Uninitialized Read | Missing init | CWE-457 | MSan, static init | Compiler |

---

### 3.5 Derivation: CVSS Base Score (Simplified KTU Version)

The full CVSS 3.1 formula is complex, but KTU examinations typically require the qualitative understanding and a simplified weighted sum:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Impact Sub-Score (ISS)} &= 1 - ((1 - C) \times (1 - I) \times (1 - A)) \\
\text{Where } C, I, A &\in \{0.0, 0.22, 0.56\} \\
\text{Exploitability} &= 8.22 \times AV \times AC \times PR \times UI \\
\text{Base Score} &= \text{roundUp}(\min(\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability}, \; 10))
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example:** Heartbleed (CVE-2014-0160) had CVSS **7.5 (High)** due to network attack vector, low complexity, no privileges required, no user interaction, and high confidentiality impact.

---

### 3.6 Step-by-Step TOCTOU Window Analysis

Consider a privileged `setuid` program that checks file access then opens it:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TOC:} \quad &\text{if (access("/tmp/data", R_OK) == 0)} \\
\text{TOU:} \quad &\quad \text{fd = open("/tmp/data", O_RDONLY);}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Attack Steps:**
1. Attacker plants a symlink `/tmp/data` → `/etc/shadow` (an unreadable file for the attacker).
2. Privileged program calls `access()` → kernel checks **real UID**, not effective UID, on older systems, or returns success on modern ones.
3. Between TOC and TOU, attacker swaps symlink to point elsewhere OR race-conditions with another process.
4. `open()` follows the new symlink → reads attacker-chosen file as the privileged user.

**Mitigation (Linux):** Use `openat()` with `O_NOFOLLOW`, `O_CLOEXEC`, and modern capability checks. Use **kernel-level file descriptors** that survive `chdir()` and `unlink()`.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Memory Layout Diagram: Stack Buffer Overflow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Stack Frame: vulnerable function] --> B[Return Address - 4 bytes]
    B --> C[Saved EBP - 4 bytes]
    C --> D[Buffer - 16 bytes]
    D --> E[User input: AAAA...AAAA]
    E -. Overflow path .-> F[Overwrites Saved EBP]
    F -. continues .-> G[Overwrites Return Address]
    G --> H[Attacker redirects execution to shellcode]
    H --> I[Arbitrary Code Execution achieved]
```

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: TOCTOU Race Window

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Process T0: access check] --> B{Authorized?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Process T1: open file]
    B -- No --> Z[Reject request]
    C --> D[File accessed]
    
    A -. Window of Vulnerability .-> X[Attacker swaps symlink]
    X --> D
    
    style X fill:#ff6b6b,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#4ecdc4,color:#ffffff
```

---

### 4.3 Defense-in-Depth Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    L1[Layer 1: Language Choice - Rust, Go, Python] --> L2[Layer 2: Compiler Flags - Wall, FORTIFY, Sanitizers]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3: Static Analysis - Coverity, SonarQube, Semgrep]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4: Dynamic Analysis - ASan, Valgrind, AFL]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5: OS Hardening - ASLR, NX, Stack Canaries]
    L5 --> L6[Layer 6: Runtime Protection - CFI, Sandboxing, seccomp]
    L6 --> L7[Layer 7: Monitoring and SIEM]
    
    style L1 fill:#95e1d3
    style L2 fill:#f6c453
    style L3 fill:#aa96da
    style L4 fill:#fcbad3
    style L5 fill:#a8d8ea
    style L6 fill:#ffffd2
    style L7 fill:#ff8b94
```

---

### 4.4 Integer Overflow Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start([Arithmetic Operation]) --> Q1{Is operand checked for safe range?}
    Q1 -- No --> A1[Silent overflow - Vulnerability]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is result re-validated before use?}
    Q2 -- No --> A2[Logic bypass possible]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Is checked arithmetic used?}
    Q3 -- No --> A3[Compiler may still UB-optimize]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Safe([Operation is safe])
    
    A1 --> Exploit[Exploit chain initiated]
    A2 --> Exploit
    A3 --> Exploit
    
    style Exploit fill:#e74c3c,color:#ffffff
    style Safe fill:#2ecc71,color:#ffffff
```

---

### 4.5 Causal Chain: Error to Exploitation

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Programmer Oversight] --> B[Non-Malicious Error in Code]
    B --> C[Vulnerability latent in deployed software]
    C --> D[Discovery by attacker or researcher]
    D --> E{Proof of Concept or CVE assigned]
    E --> F[Exploit weaponized in the wild]
    F --> G[Security Incident - data loss, RCE, DoS]
    
    style A fill:#ffe5b4
    style B fill:#ffcc99
    style C fill:#ff9966
    style D fill:#ff6666
    style E fill:#ff3333
    style F fill:#cc0000,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#800000,color:#ffffff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 2]**
> **Differentiate between malicious and non-malicious program errors with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Malicious Program Errors | Non-Malicious Program Errors |
|---|---|---|
| **Intent** | Deliberately crafted by attacker | Unintentional, by developer oversight |
| **Examples** | Viruses, worms, trojans, logic bombs, ransomware | Buffer overflows, race conditions, integer overflows, memory leaks |
| **Detection** | Antivirus, behavior monitoring, sandboxing | Code review, static analysis, fuzzing |
| **Goal** | Confidentiality, integrity, or availability breach | Functional bug, but exploitable |
| **Origin** | External injection or insider threat | In-house development lifecycle |

**Examples:**
- *Malicious:* ILOVEYOU worm (2000), WannaCry ransomware (2017).
- *Non-malicious:* Heartbleed buffer over-read (2014), OpenSSL null pointer dereference (2022).

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark distinction + 1 mark examples)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
> **What is a TOCTOU race condition? Explain with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Time-of-Check to Time-of-Use (TOCTOU)** race condition is a class of non-malicious program error where a security-sensitive decision is made based on a system state that can be altered by an attacker between the **time-of-check (TOC)** and the **time-of-use (TOU)**.

**Example:** A privileged banking application checks whether a customer has sufficient balance *before* debiting the account.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TOC:} \quad &\text{balance} = \text{getBalance(account\_id)} \\
&\text{if (balance} \geq \text{withdraw\_amount)} \\
\text{TOU:} \quad &\quad \text{debit(account\_id, withdraw\_amount)}
\end{aligned}
$$

If two withdrawal requests execute concurrently for the *same account* without synchronization, both may pass the check and both may debit, leading to **double-spend** — a classic race condition.

**Mitigation:** Use database-level **atomic transactions** with `BEGIN ... COMMIT` and row-level locking (`SELECT ... FOR UPDATE`).

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark example + 1 mark mitigation)*

---

### PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Slot B, Q. 5(a)+(b)]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of a **buffer overflow** vulnerability. With a neat diagram, describe the stack layout during a typical stack-smashing attack, and derive the offset calculation needed to overwrite the return address.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss **defense-in-depth** techniques used to mitigate buffer overflow attacks at the compiler, OS, and language levels. Include specific flags and runtime mechanisms.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Buffer Overflow — Conceptual & Diagrammatic Explanation**

A **buffer overflow** is a non-malicious programming error in which a program writes data beyond the allocated boundary of a memory buffer. It is the most common memory-corruption vulnerability in C/C++ programs, accounting for approximately **15–20%** of all CVEs in legacy codebases (per MITRE's CWE Top 25).

**Root Cause:** The C standard library historically provided unsafe functions (`gets`, `strcpy`, `sprintf`, `scanf("%s", ...)`) that perform no bounds checking. Programmers frequently use them without manual length validation.

**Stack Layout During a Stack-Smashing Attack (7 marks answer requires this):**

```mermaid
graph TD
    H[Higher address] --> A[Function arguments]
    A --> B[Return Address - 4 bytes]
    B --> C[Saved EBP - 4 bytes]
    C --> D[Buffer 16 bytes - local array]
    D --> E[Lower address - stack growth direction]
    
    B -. Attacker-controlled overwrite .-> X[Shellcode address 0x41424344]
```

**Offset Derivation (Step-by-Step):**

For a typical 32-bit x86 stack frame with a 16-byte character buffer:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Identify buffer base address.} \\
\text{buf[0]} &\rightarrow \text{offset 0 from buffer base} \\
\text{buf[15]} &\rightarrow \text{offset 15} \\
\text{Step 2: Identify saved EBP location.} \\
\text{saved\_EBP} &\rightarrow \text{offset 16 (immediately after buffer)} \\
\text{Step 3: Identify return address location.} \\
\text{return\_address} &\rightarrow \text{offset 20 (after saved EBP)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Offset to return address} = \text{Buffer Size} + \text{Pointer Size} = 16 + 4 = 20 \text{ bytes}}
$$

The attacker crafts a payload of **20 bytes of padding + 4 bytes of shellcode address** to redirect the function's return flow.

**Mechanism of Exploitation:**

1. Attacker provides input of 24+ bytes.
2. First 16 bytes fill `buf`.
3. Next 4 bytes overwrite `saved_EBP` (corrupting caller's frame pointer).
4. Next 4 bytes overwrite the return address.
5. When the function executes `ret`, the CPU loads the attacker's address into the instruction pointer (EIP).
6. Execution transfers to the shellcode → arbitrary code execution.

---

**(b) Defense-in-Depth Techniques (7 marks)**

**Layer 1 — Compiler-Level Defenses:**

| Technique | GCC/Clang Flag | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Stack Canaries** | `-fstack-protector-all` | Inserts a random 4-byte value between buffer and return address; verified before `ret` |
| **FORTIFY_SOURCE** | `-D_FORTIFY_SOURCE=2` | Replaces unsafe functions with bounded variants (e.g., `strcpy` → `__strcpy_chk`) |
| **RELRO** | `-Wl,-z,relro,-z,now` | Marks GOT read-only after dynamic linking |
| **PIE** | `-fPIE -pie` | Position Independent Executable enables full ASLR |
| **Integer Sanitization** | `-fsanitize=integer` | Detects UB arithmetic at runtime |

**Layer 2 — OS-Level Defenses:**

- **ASLR (Address Space Layout Randomization):** Randomizes base addresses of stack, heap, libraries. On 64-bit Linux, stack entropy is typically **22 bits**, library **28 bits**.
- **DEP / NX Bit:** Marks stack and heap pages as non-executable (No-eXecute). Even if shellcode is written, it cannot be executed.
- **SEHOP (Structured Exception Handler Overwrite Protection):** Windows-specific.
- **seccomp / AppArmor / SELinux:** Sandbox restrictions on syscalls.

**Layer 3 — Language-Level Defenses:**

- **Memory-safe languages:** Rust (ownership + borrow checker), Go (bounds-checked slices), Java, C#, Python — all prevent direct buffer manipulation.
- **Modern C++:** Use `std::array`, `std::vector`, `std::string` instead of raw arrays and C-strings.
- **Smart pointers:** `std::unique_ptr`, `std::shared_ptr` prevent use-after-free and double-free.

**Layer 4 — Detection and Testing:**

- **AddressSanitizer (ASan):** Compile-time instrumentation detects out-of-bounds accesses, use-after-free.
- **MemorySanitizer (MSan):** Detects use of uninitialized memory.
- **Fuzzing:** AFL++, libFuzzer, honggfuzz — input-mutation testing to discover crashes.

**Conclusion:** No single mitigation is sufficient. Modern secure software engineering mandates **defense-in-depth** so that the failure of one layer (e.g., ASLR bypass) does not lead to total compromise.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Slot A, Q. 6(a)+(b)]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **integer overflow** as a non-malicious program error. Derive the boundary conditions for signed and unsigned 32-bit integers, and illustrate how an integer overflow can lead to a buffer overflow.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss **race conditions and TOCTOU vulnerabilities** in detail. Provide a real-world CVE example and explain modern mitigation strategies.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Integer Overflow — Concept, Derivation, and Exploitation Chain**

An **integer overflow** occurs when an arithmetic operation on integer types produces a result outside the range that the type can represent. Unlike floating-point exceptions, integer overflow in C/C++ is **silent** — the CPU simply wraps around modulo $2^n$ (unsigned) or invokes **undefined behavior** (signed).

**Boundary Derivation for 32-bit Unsigned Integer:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of representable values} &= 2^{32} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 \\
\text{Min value} &= 0 \\
\text{Max value} &= 2^{32} - 1 = 4{,}294{,}967{,}295 \\
\text{Overflow on addition:} \quad 0xFFFFFFFF + 1 &= 0 \pmod{2^{32}} \\
\text{Overflow on multiplication:} \quad 65{,}536 \times 65{,}537 &= 2^{32} + 0 \equiv 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Boundary Derivation for 32-bit Signed Integer (Two's Complement):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Min value} &= -2^{31} = -2{,}147{,}483{,}648 \\
\text{Max value} &= 2^{31} - 1 = 2{,}147{,}483{,}647 \\
\text{Signed overflow is undefined behavior (UB).} \\
\text{Example:} \quad 0x7FFFFFFF + 1 &\rightarrow \text{UB; common wraparound: } -2^{31}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Exploitation Chain — Integer Overflow Leading to Buffer Overflow:**

Consider the following vulnerable C code:

```c
void copy_data(unsigned int len, char *src) {
    char buf[100];
    if (len + 1 > sizeof(buf))  // INTENDED safety check
        return;
    memcpy(buf, src, len);
}
```

**Attack Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Attacker chooses} \quad len &= 0xFFFFFFFF \\
len + 1 &= 0xFFFFFFFF + 1 = 0 \pmod{2^{32}} \\
0 > 100 &\rightarrow \text{false} \quad \text{(safety check bypassed)} \\
memcpy(buf, src, 0xFFFFFFFF) &\rightarrow \text{4 GB overflow}
\end{aligned}
$$

The intended bounds check is **arithmetically defeated** by the integer wraparound.

**Real-World CVE:** *CVE-2019-0708 (BlueKeep)* — integer overflow in RDP channel binding led to pre-authentication remote code execution.

---

**(b) Race Conditions and TOCTOU Vulnerabilities (7 marks)**

**Definition:**
A **race condition** is a concurrency defect where the system's behavior depends on the interleaving or timing of threads, processes, or signals. When a race condition affects a security decision, it becomes a **TOCTOU (Time-of-Check to Time-of-Use)** vulnerability.

**Detailed TOCTOU Anatomy:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Thread checks file permissions - access] --> B{Allowed?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Thread opens file - open]
    C --> D[Reads sensitive data]
    
    A -. window of vulnerability .-> X[Attacker swaps symlink in /tmp]
    X --> C
    
    style X fill:#ff4757,color:#ffffff
```

**Real-World CVE Example — Dirty COW (CVE-2016-5195):**

The *Dirty COW* vulnerability in the Linux kernel's memory subsystem allowed a local user to write to read-only memory mappings via a race condition in the `get_user_pages` function. The exploit gained root privileges on millions of Android devices and Linux servers.

**Modern Mitigation Strategies:**

1. **Atomic Syscalls:** Use `openat2()` with `RESOLVE_NO_SYMLINKS` to prevent symlink substitution.
2. **POSIX File Locking:** `flock()` or `fcntl(F_SETLK)` to serialize access.
3. **TOCTOU-Safe APIs:** Linux `openat(dirfd, path, flags)` operates relative to a directory file descriptor that cannot be swapped.
4. **Transactional Memory:** Hardware-supported (Intel TSX) or software emulation.
5. **Language-Level Concurrency Primitives:** Go channels, Rust `Arc<Mutex<T>>`, Java `synchronized` blocks.
6. **Static Analysis:** Tools like **ThreadSanitizer (TSan)** and **Coverity** can detect data races at compile time or runtime.

**Conclusion:** Race conditions and TOCTOU are among the most elusive non-malicious errors. Defense requires careful API selection, formal concurrency models, and runtime sanitizers.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Based on KTU Valuation Key Analysis):**
> 1. **Confusing malicious vs. non-malicious** — examiners deduct up to 2 marks if the distinction is not explicit. Always state: "non-malicious = unintentional, but exploitable."
> 2. **Skipping derivation steps** — KTU 2024 scheme mandates showing *each* algebraic transition. Jumping from $E - N + 2P$ to "cyclomatic complexity = 5" without intermediate steps = -2 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring CWE/CVE references** — naming a specific CWE-ID (e.g., CWE-121) or CVE (e.g., Heartbleed = CVE-2014-0160) demonstrates syllabus alignment and earns +0.5 bonus marks on borderline papers.
> 4. **Drawing stack layout in text only** — examiners expect a proper diagram. Mermaid or ASCII art earns full credit; pure prose loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Not mentioning ASLR/NX in mitigation answers** — this is the *single most* omitted mitigation in student answers. Always include compiler + OS + language layers.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **Module 2 — Non-Malicious Program Errors: Quick-Fire Revision**

- ✅ **Definition:** Unintentional, exploitable software flaws (NOT malware).
- ✅ **Major Types:** Buffer overflow, integer overflow, race condition, TOCTOU, format string, memory leak, use-after-free, null pointer dereference.
- ✅ **Buffer Overflow Offset Formula:** Offset = Buffer Size + Saved Frame Pointer Size.
- ✅ **McCabe's Formula:** $M = E - N + 2P$; for $P=1$, $M = E - N + 2$.
- ✅ **Unsigned 32-bit Integer Range:** 0 to $2^{32} - 1$ (= 4,294,967,295).
- ✅ **Signed 32-bit Integer Range:** $-2^{31}$ to $2^{31} - 1$.
- ✅ **Integer Overflow → Buffer Overflow:** Wraparound defeats length checks.
- ✅ **TOCTOU Window:** Vulnerability exists between `access()` and `open()`.
- ✅ **Defense-in-Depth:** Language + Compiler + OS + Runtime + Monitoring.
- ✅ **Compiler Flags to Remember:** `-fstack-protector-all`, `-D_FORTIFY_SOURCE=2`, `-fPIE -pie`, `-fsanitize=address,integer`.
- ✅ **OS Mitigations:** ASLR, DEP/NX, seccomp, SELinux/AppArmor.
- ✅ **Memory-Safe Languages:** Rust, Go, Java, C#, Python, Swift, Kotlin.
- ✅ **Sanitizers:** ASan (memory), MSan (uninitialized), TSan (races), UBSan (UB).
- ✅ **Famous CVEs:** Heartbleed (2014), Dirty COW (2016), BlueKeep (2019).
- ✅ **CVSS Score Range:** 0.0 – 10.0; ≥ 7.0 = High/Critical.
- ✅ **CWE Top 25** (MITRE) is the authoritative list of weakness IDs.
- ✅ **McCabe Thresholds:** $M \leq 10$ safe, $M > 50$ untestable.
- ✅ **Analogy to Remember:** Bank vault + forgotten deadbolt = non-malicious error exploited by opportunist.
- ✅ **Key Distinction for ESE:** Buffer overflow is a *code-level* error; DoS is a *service-level* impact.
- ✅ **Format String:** Never pass user input directly as first argument to `printf`.
- ✅ **Best Practice:** Use `snprintf`, `strncpy`, `fgets` (with size limit) instead of legacy unsafe functions.
- ✅ **Public Sector Relevance:** E-governance portals must follow OWASP ASVS Level 2 minimum.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Viruses and Other Malicious Code
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT753 - INFORMATION SECURITY IN PUBLIC AND PRIVATE SECTORS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Viruses and Other Malicious Code

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Malicious Code?

> [!IMPORTANT] **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)**
> **Malicious code (malware)** is any program, script, macro, or fragment of code that is intentionally designed to cause **unauthorized damage, disruption, disclosure, or denial of service** to an information system, or to perform any kind of hostile, intrusive, or unwanted activity on a computing device. Malicious code encompasses viruses, worms, trojans, logic bombs, backdoors, spyware, ransomware, and rootkits.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Biological Pathogen" Viewpoint

Think of a **computer** as a **human body**, and a **network of computers** as a **crowded city**:

- A **virus** is like a **biological flu virus** — it cannot survive or reproduce on its own; it needs a *host file* (like a living cell) to attach itself to and spread only when the file is executed or shared.
- A **worm** is like **airborne disease** — it travels *autonomously* through the network, needing no human action to spread from one machine to another.
- A **trojan** is like a **gift box containing a snake** — it looks attractive/legitimate (a free game, a "useful" tool) but hides destructive code inside.
- A **logic bomb** is like a **time bomb** — it stays silent until a specific condition (date, event, file deletion) triggers it.
- A **backdoor** is like a **secret tunnel** built into a house — attackers can sneak in later, bypassing the front-door lock.
- **Ransomware** is like a **kidnapper** — it locks up your "family" (data) and demands a "ransom" (cryptocurrency) for release.

> [!NOTE] **Key Distinction for KTU Exams**
> The single most tested fact: *Viruses require a host program and human action to propagate; Worms propagate autonomously across networks.* Examiners will **definitely** test this difference.

---

## 1.2 The Malware Classification Taxonomy

Malicious code is broadly classified into the following categories, all of which are **high-yield KTU topics**:

| # | Malware Type | Needs Host? | Self-Propagating? | Trigger Needed? |
|---|--------------|-------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Virus** | ✅ Yes (attaches to a file) | ❌ No (needs user action) | Often (some are immediate) |
| 2 | **Worm** | ❌ No (standalone program) | ✅ Yes (network-borne) | ❌ No (autonomous) |
| 3 | **Trojan Horse** | ❌ No (masquerades as legit) | ❌ No | Optional (payload-based) |
| 4 | **Logic Bomb** | ✅ Yes (lives inside a legit program) | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (specific condition) |
| 5 | **Backdoor / Trapdoor** | ❌ No (standalone mechanism) | ❌ No (manual exploitation) | Optional |
| 6 | **Ransomware** | ❌ No | ❌ No (delivered via other vectors) | User opens the carrier |
| 7 | **Spyware / Adware** | ❌ No | ❌ No | User installs it |
| 8 | **Rootkit** | ❌ No | ❌ No | Pre-installation required |
| 9 | **Bot / Botnet Agent** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (via C2 channel) | Awaiting command |

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Malware Analysis

The following metrics are the **standard numerical yardsticks** of the cybersecurity industry and the **KTU syllabus**:

- **Infection Rate (β)** — Number of new hosts compromised per infected host per unit time. Units: **infections/host/day**.
- **Removal / Recovery Rate (γ)** — Rate at which infected hosts are cleaned (antivirus, patching). Units: **1/day**.
- **Basic Reproduction Number R₀** — Average number of secondary infections produced by one infected host. **R₀ > 1 ⇒ epidemic spreads; R₀ < 1 ⇒ dies out.**
- **Mean Time to Compromise (MTTC)** — Average time between a vulnerability's disclosure and its exploitation in the wild. Units: **days**.
- **Mean Time to Detection (MTTD)** — Average time between initial infection and detection. Units: **hours/days**.
- **Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR)** — Average time to clean and restore an infected host. Units: **hours**.
- **False Positive Rate (FPR)** — Probability that a clean file is wrongly flagged as malicious.
- **False Negative Rate (FNR)** — Probability that a malicious file escapes detection.

> [!NOTE] **Industry Benchmarks (2024-2025):** Modern antivirus engines target **FPR < 0.1%** and **FNR < 5%**, with **MTTD** ideally below **24 hours** for zero-day threats.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of a Computer Virus — Four Phases

Every virus, from the simplest boot virus to the most advanced polymorphic one, passes through a standard **lifecycle**. KTU examiners frequently ask students to **draw and label this lifecycle**.

### Phase 1 — Dormant Phase
- The virus lies **inert**.
- It does nothing — may wait for a specific trigger (date, event, file count).
- Purpose: avoid detection.

### Phase 2 — Propagation Phase
- The virus **replicates itself** and attaches copies to other host programs/files.
- Each infected file becomes a new "patient zero" for further spread.

### Phase 3 — Triggering Phase
- The condition that activates the **payload** is met.
- Example: Friday the 13th, a user opening a specific file, a system clock reaching a date, or a counter crossing a threshold.

### Phase 4 — Execution Phase
- The **payload** fires — the destructive function executes.
- Payloads range from harmless (message display) to catastrophic (data wipe, encryption, exfiltration).

---

## 2.2 Virus Types — The Complete KTU Catalogue

> [!IMPORTANT] **The 8 Virus Types Tested in KTU Examinations**
> Memorize all 8 — examiners love asking for the **classification table**.

### (a) File Infector Virus
- Attaches itself to **executable files** (.exe, .com, .sys).
- Activates when the host program is run.
- Example: **CIH (Chernobyl) virus**.

### (b) Boot Sector Virus
- Infects the **Master Boot Record (MBR)** of a hard disk or the boot sector of a floppy.
- Loads *before* the operating system — extremely powerful.
- Example: **Michelangelo, Brain virus**.

### (c) Macro Virus
- Infects documents and templates that use **macro languages** (Microsoft Word, Excel VBA).
- Spreads via shared documents, email attachments.
- Example: **Melissa (1999), ILOVEYOU**.

### (d) Multipartite Virus
- Infects **both** boot sector **and** executable files.
- Uses multiple infection vectors → harder to eradicate.
- Example: **Flip, Tequila**.

### (e) Polymorphic Virus
- **Encrypts its payload** with a randomly changing key each time it infects a new file.
- Each copy looks different to signature-based antivirus.
- Example: **Storm Worm**.

### (f) Metamorphic Virus
- **Rewrites its own code** with each infection — no two copies share the same byte pattern.
- More advanced than polymorphic — uses code obfuscation, register renaming, instruction reordering.
- Example: **Zmist, Simile**.

### (g) Stealth Virus
- **Hides from antivirus** by intercepting system calls.
- When AV reads a file, the virus returns the *original* uninfected version.
- Example: **Brain, Subway**.

### (h) Armored Virus
- **Resists analysis** by antivirus researchers.
- Uses anti-debugging, anti-VM, anti-sandbox techniques.
- Example: **Conficker**.

---

## 2.3 The Worm — Autonomous Network Propagation

A **worm** is a standalone, self-replicating program that spreads over a network **without human intervention** and without attaching itself to a host file.

### Worm Propagation Phases
1. **Probing/Scanning Phase** — Worm searches for vulnerable hosts (random IP, hit-list, topological, or passive).
2. **Exploitation Phase** — Worm exploits a vulnerability (buffer overflow, weak password, open port).
3. **Replication Phase** — Worm copies itself to the new victim.
4. **Payload Phase** — Optional destructive function executes.

### Famous Worms in History (High-Yield for KTU)
| Worm | Year | Vector | Impact |
|------|------|--------|--------|
| **Morris Worm** | 1988 | `sendmail`, `fingerd` (buffer overflow) | Brought early Internet to its knees; creator Robert Tappan Morris convicted under the Computer Fraud and Abuse Act |
| **Code Red** | 2001 | Microsoft IIS buffer overflow | Infected ~360,000 servers in 14 hours |
| **Nimda** | 2001 | Multi-vector (email, IIS, shares) | Most widespread worm of its time |
| **Slammer** | 2003 | SQL Server buffer overflow | Doubled infections every 8.5 seconds |
| **Conficker** | 2008 | Windows SMB vulnerability | 9-15 million infected hosts |
| **Stuxnet** | 2010 | Zero-day + USB | Targeted SCADA systems (Iran nuclear program) |
| **WannaCry** | 2017 | EternalBlue (SMBv1) | $4-8 billion damages worldwide |

---

## 2.4 Trojan Horse — The Deceptive Malware

A **Trojan Horse** is a malicious program disguised as legitimate, useful, or entertaining software. Unlike a worm, it does **not self-replicate**. It relies on **social engineering** to trick the user into installing it.

### Common Trojan Categories
- **Remote Access Trojan (RAT)** — Gives attacker full remote control. Example: **Back Orifice, DarkComet**.
- **Data-Sending Trojan** — Steals credentials, keystrokes, and sends them to attacker.
- **Destructive Trojan** — Deletes or corrupts files.
- **Proxy Trojan** — Turns victim into a proxy server for relaying attacks.
- **Downloader/Dropper** — Downloads additional malware after installation.
- **Banking Trojan** — Targets financial credentials. Example: **Zeus, Emotet**.

---

## 2.5 Logic Bombs, Backdoors, and Trapdoors

### Logic Bomb
- A piece of code **embedded inside a legitimate program** that activates a malicious payload when a **predefined condition** is met.
- Examples of triggers: a specific date (April 1), a programmer's name being removed from the payroll, a specific file being deleted, or a particular number of program executions.
- **KTU Case Study:** A disgruntled employee at a financial firm in 2013 triggered a logic bomb that wiped the firm's servers after his credentials were revoked.

### Backdoor / Trapdoor
- A **secret undocumented entry point** into a system, bypassing normal authentication.
- Can be:
  - **Planted intentionally** by a developer (for maintenance).
  - **Inserted by malware** (e.g., a RAT, a rootkit).
- The 1983 film *WarGames* popularized the concept — a teen "wargames" his way into a military supercomputer via a backdoor left by its creator.

---

## 2.6 Other Modern Malicious Code

| Malware | Description | Real-World Example |
|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Ransomware** | Encrypts victim's data; demands payment (usually cryptocurrency) for decryption key | **WannaCry (2017), NotPetya (2017), LockBit, REvil** |
| **Spyware** | Silently monitors user activity; collects data | **Pegasus, CoolWebSearch** |
| **Adware** | Unwanted advertisements; can redirect browsers | **Gator, Fireball** |
| **Rootkit** | Hides itself and other malware at OS or kernel level | **Sony BMG XCP (2005)** |
| **Bot / Botnet** | Infected host ("zombie") controlled remotely via Command-and-Control (C2) server; used for DDoS, spam, crypto-mining | **Mirai (2016) — 1+ Tbps DDoS** |
| **Fileless Malware** | Resides only in memory (RAM) — no files on disk; extremely hard to detect | **Poweliks, Astaroth** |
| **Polymorphic Malware** | Changes its code/encryption with each infection | **Storm Worm, VirLock** |
| **APT (Advanced Persistent Threat)** | Long-term, stealthy, multi-stage attack by a sophisticated actor | **Stuxnet, SolarWinds, APT29 (Cozy Bear)** |

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **Master the following equations — every KTU Module 2 numerical or analytical question uses one of these.**

### Epidemiological Models for Malware Propagation

The propagation of computer malware on the internet is mathematically modeled using the same **SIR (Susceptible-Infected-Removed) equations** that epidemiologists use for biological diseases. The total host population is denoted as $N$.

### Equation 1 — Susceptible Population Dynamics
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{dS}{dt} &= -\frac{\beta \cdot S \cdot I}{N}
\end{aligned}$$

Where:
- $S(t)$ = number of **Susceptible** (vulnerable, uninfected) hosts at time $t$
- $I(t)$ = number of **Infected** hosts at time $t$
- $R(t)$ = number of **Removed** (recovered/patched) hosts at time $t$
- $\beta$ = **infection rate** (contact rate × transmission probability)
- $N = S + I + R$ = total host population
- Units of $\beta$ = **1/time**

### Equation 2 — Infected Population Dynamics
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{dI}{dt} &= \frac{\beta \cdot S \cdot I}{N} - \gamma \cdot I
\end{aligned}$$

Where:
- $\gamma$ = **removal rate** (rate at which infected hosts are cleaned, patched, or isolated)
- Units of $\gamma$ = **1/time**

### Equation 3 — Removed (Recovered) Population Dynamics
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{dR}{dt} &= \gamma \cdot I
\end{aligned}$$

### Equation 4 — Basic Reproduction Number (Epidemic Threshold)
$$\begin{aligned}
R_0 &= \frac{\beta}{\gamma}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **The Epidemic Threshold Rule**
> - $R_0 > 1 \implies$ the malware **spreads exponentially** (epidemic)
> - $R_0 = 1 \implies$ **endemic equilibrium** (stable, no growth)
> - $R_0 < 1 \implies$ the malware **dies out** (no epidemic)

### Equation 5 — Initial Exponential Growth of Malware
At the start of an outbreak ($S \approx N$, all hosts vulnerable), the infected population grows as:
$$\begin{aligned}
I(t) &\approx I(0) \cdot e^{(\beta - \gamma) \cdot t}
\end{aligned}$$

The growth rate constant is $\alpha = \beta - \gamma$, and the **doubling time** is:
$$\begin{aligned}
T_d &= \frac{\ln 2}{\beta - \gamma} = \frac{0.6931}{\beta - \gamma}
\end{aligned}$$

### Equation 6 — Detection Performance Metrics
For any antivirus engine:
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Detection Rate (Recall)} &= \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \\
\text{False Positive Rate (FPR)} &= \frac{FP}{FP + TN} \\
\text{Precision} &= \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \\
\text{Accuracy} &= \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}
\end{aligned}$$

Where TP = True Positives, TN = True Negatives, FP = False Positives, FN = False Negatives.

### Equation 7 — Mean Time to Compromise (MTTC) Empirically
The empirical relationship between vulnerability age and exploitation risk:
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Exploitation Probability} &\propto \frac{1}{\text{Time since disclosure}}
\end{aligned}$$

Older vulnerabilities (unpatched) attract exponentially more exploit attempts.

---

## 2.8 Summary Table of High-Yield Formulas

| # | Formula | Application |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $\frac{dS}{dt} = -\frac{\beta S I}{N}$ | Rate of susceptible hosts being infected |
| 2 | $\frac{dI}{dt} = \frac{\beta S I}{N} - \gamma I$ | Net change in infected hosts |
| 3 | $\frac{dR}{dt} = \gamma I$ | Rate of hosts being removed (cleaned) |
| 4 | $R_0 = \beta / \gamma$ | Epidemic threshold |
| 5 | $I(t) \approx I(0) e^{(\beta - \gamma) t}$ | Early-time infection growth |
| 6 | $T_d = \ln 2 \,/\, (\beta - \gamma)$ | Infection doubling time |
| 7 | Recall $= TP / (TP+FN)$ | Antivirus detection accuracy |
| 8 | FPR $= FP / (FP+TN)$ | Antivirus false alarm rate |

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Utility**
> These equations are the **backbone of network defense strategy** in industries like banking, defense, telecom, and critical infrastructure. CERT teams use them to predict whether a new zero-day will become an outbreak, when to deploy emergency patches, and how to size network isolation policies.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Basic Reproduction Number $R_0$ for Malware

**Problem Statement:** Given a worm spreading across a network with contact rate $\beta$ and removal rate $\gamma$, show that the threshold condition for an epidemic is $R_0 > 1$.

### Step 1: Start with the Infected-Compartment Differential Equation
The number of infected hosts changes as:
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{dI}{dt} &= \frac{\beta \cdot S \cdot I}{N} - \gamma \cdot I
\end{aligned}$$

This is a direct application of the Law of Mass Action in epidemiology, where the *new infection* term is proportional to the product of susceptible and infected populations, scaled by the contact rate.

### Step 2: Apply the Initial-Outbreak Approximation
At the very beginning of the outbreak, almost every host is susceptible, so $S \approx N$. Substitute:
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{dI}{dt} &= \frac{\beta \cdot N \cdot I}{N} - \gamma \cdot I = (\beta - \gamma) \cdot I
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Solve the Linear First-Order ODE
This is a separable differential equation. Separate and integrate:
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{dI}{I} &= (\beta - \gamma) \cdot dt
\end{aligned}$$

Integrating both sides from $t = 0$ to time $t$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\int_{I(0)}^{I(t)} \frac{dI}{I} &= (\beta - \gamma) \int_{0}^{t} dt
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Evaluate the Integrals
The left integral is a standard logarithm:
$$\begin{aligned}
\ln I(t) - \ln I(0) &= (\beta - \gamma) \cdot t
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Exponentiate to Obtain the Closed-Form Solution
$$\begin{aligned}
I(t) = I(0) \cdot e^{(\beta - \gamma) \cdot t}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6: Identify the Epidemic Threshold
The infection grows **exponentially** if and only if the exponent is positive:
$$\begin{aligned}
\beta - \gamma &> 0 \\
\implies \frac{\beta}{\gamma} &> 1
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 7: Define the Basic Reproduction Number
Define $R_0 = \beta / \gamma$. The threshold is:
$$\begin{aligned}
\boxed{R_0 = \frac{\beta}{\gamma} > 1 \quad \text{(Epidemic Threshold)}}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **fundamental result** of malware epidemiology and is a favorite KTU question. It tells the defender: *if I can increase my removal rate $\gamma$ (faster patching, better AV) so that $\gamma \geq \beta$, the outbreak dies.*

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Doubling Time of a Worm Outbreak

**Problem Statement:** The Slammer worm of 2003 had a doubling time of approximately 8.5 seconds during its initial outbreak. Given that the average removal rate in that era was $\gamma = 0.001$ per second (one cleanup per 1000 seconds), calculate the contact rate $\beta$ of Slammer.

### Step 1: Recall the Doubling-Time Formula
$$\begin{aligned}
T_d = \frac{\ln 2}{\beta - \gamma}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2: Plug in the Known Values
$T_d = 8.5$ s, $\gamma = 0.001 \, s^{-1}$, $\ln 2 \approx 0.6931$:
$$\begin{aligned}
8.5 = \frac{0.6931}{\beta - 0.001}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Solve for the Denominator
$$\begin{aligned}
\beta - 0.001 = \frac{0.6931}{8.5}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Numerically Evaluate the Right-Hand Side
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{0.6931}{8.5} = 0.08154
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Solve for $\beta$
$$\begin{aligned}
\beta = 0.08154 + 0.001 = 0.08254 \, s^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6: Express in Hours for Intuition
$$\begin{aligned}
\beta = 0.08254 \times 3600 = 297.1 \text{ probes/host/hour}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 7: Compute $R_0$
$$\begin{aligned}
R_0 = \frac{0.08254}{0.001} = 82.54
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE] **Interpretation:** Each Slammer-infected host was attempting to compromise 82+ other hosts before being cleaned. This is why Slammer infected 90% of vulnerable hosts within 10 minutes.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Steady-State (Endemic Equilibrium) of the SIR Model

**Problem Statement:** Find the steady-state infected population $I^*$ in a network with $N$ hosts.

### Step 1: Set All Derivatives to Zero
At equilibrium, populations stop changing:
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{dS}{dt} = 0, \quad \frac{dI}{dt} = 0, \quad \frac{dR}{dt} = 0
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2: Analyze the Infected-Compartment Equation
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\beta \cdot S^* \cdot I^*}{N} - \gamma \cdot I^* = 0
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Factor Out $I^*$
$$\begin{aligned}
I^* \left( \frac{\beta \cdot S^*}{N} - \gamma \right) = 0
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Solve for the Two Possible Equilibria
- **Trivial equilibrium:** $I^* = 0$ (no malware present)
- **Endemic equilibrium:** $\frac{\beta \cdot S^*}{N} = \gamma \implies S^* = \frac{\gamma \cdot N}{\beta} = \frac{N}{R_0}$

### Step 5: Compute the Endemic Infected Population
Using $N = S^* + I^* + R^*$ and the conservation law, the endemic infected level is:
$$\begin{aligned}
I^* = N - S^* - R^*
\end{aligned}$$

For the simple case where $R^* = 0$ initially:
$$\begin{aligned}
I^* = N - \frac{N}{R_0} = N \left(1 - \frac{1}{R_0}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6: Final Endemic-Infected Expression
$$\begin{aligned}
\boxed{I^* = N \left(1 - \frac{1}{R_0}\right)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Verification:**
> - If $R_0 = 1 \implies I^* = 0$ (no epidemic).
> - If $R_0 = 2 \implies I^* = N/2$ (half the network remains infected at equilibrium).
> - As $R_0 \to \infty \implies I^* \to N$ (catastrophic outbreak).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Analysis of Malware

| Parameter | Virus | Worm | Trojan | Logic Bomb | Ransomware | Rootkit |
|-----------|-------|------|--------|------------|------------|---------|
| **Self-Replicating** | Yes (needs host) | Yes (autonomous) | No | No | No | No |
| **Network Spreading** | No (needs user sharing) | Yes (automatic) | No | No | Limited (via email/phishing) | No |
| **Needs Human Action** | Yes (execute file) | No | Yes (install) | Optional | Yes (open attachment) | Yes (privilege escalation) |
| **Trigger** | Optional (date, event) | None | Optional | **Required** | User action | Pre-installation |
| **Primary Vector** | Infected files, email | Network vulnerabilities | Social engineering | Insider | Email, RDP exploits | Software vulnerabilities |
| **Detection Difficulty** | Medium | Low–Medium | Medium | High (in plain sight) | Medium | Very High |
| **Payload Severity** | Variable | Variable | Variable | Catastrophic | Catastrophic | Catastrophic |
| **Stealth Level** | Medium | Low | High | Very High | Medium | Very High |
| **Famous Example** | CIH, Melissa | Morris, Slammer | Zeus, Emotet | Insider case (2013) | WannaCry, LockBit | Sony XCP |
| **Estimated Annual Damage** | Billions | Billions | Billions | Variable | $20+ billion (2023) | Billions |
