# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a virus and a worm with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Virus | Worm |
|--------|-------|------|
| **Host Requirement** | Requires a host program/file to attach itself to | Standalone program; does not need a host file |
| **Propagation Mechanism** | Spreads only when the infected file is executed or shared by the user | Propagates autonomously across the network |
| **Human Action** | Needs human action (opening a file, running a program) | No human action required after initial release |
| **Speed of Spread** | Slower (depends on user behavior) | Very fast (can infect thousands in minutes) |
| **Example** | CIH (Chernobyl), Melissa | Morris Worm, Slammer, WannaCry |

*(1 mark per major difference; full marks for clear distinction and example.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`
**What is a logic bomb? Explain with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **logic bomb** is a piece of malicious code that is intentionally embedded inside a legitimate program and remains dormant until a specific triggering condition is satisfied. When the condition occurs, the bomb "detonates" and executes its malicious payload.

**Triggering conditions** can include:
- A specific date or time (e.g., April 1)
- A specific event (e.g., deletion of a particular file)
- A counter threshold (e.g., program executed 100 times)
- A user action (e.g., login by a particular user)

**Example:** A disgruntled system administrator, fearing termination, plants a logic bomb that activates on a specific date to wipe all customer databases from the company's servers. *(2 marks for definition + 1 mark for example.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`
**This question has internal choice between A and B. Attempt ANY ONE.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the four phases of a computer virus lifecycle with a neat diagram. Discuss in detail the different types of viruses.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**The Four Phases of a Computer Virus Lifecycle (4 marks):**

1. **Dormant Phase:** The virus is idle. It may have been delivered to the system but has not yet activated. During this phase, the virus can avoid detection by antivirus software. Some viruses have no dormant phase.

2. **Propagation Phase:** The virus makes copies of itself and inserts those copies into other host programs, files, or system areas. Each infected file becomes a new carrier that can infect more files.

3. **Triggering Phase:** The virus waits for a predefined event or condition. This could be a specific date (Friday the 13th), a user action (opening a specific file), a counter reaching a threshold, or a system event (boot-up). When the trigger occurs, the payload is armed.

4. **Execution Phase:** The payload is delivered. This is the actual damaging activity — deleting files, displaying messages, corrupting data, encrypting files, or exfiltrating information.

**Virus Types (3 marks) — must cover at least 4:**
- **File Infector:** Attaches to .exe or .com files. Example: CIH.
- **Boot Sector Virus:** Infects MBR; loads before OS. Example: Michelangelo.
- **Macro Virus:** Lives in document templates; spreads via email. Example: Melissa.
- **Polymorphic Virus:** Encrypts itself with a changing key to evade signature detection.
- **Metamorphic Virus:** Rewrites its own code with each replication.
- **Stealth Virus:** Hides its presence by intercepting system calls.
- **Multipartite Virus:** Uses multiple infection vectors (boot + files).
- **Armored Virus:** Designed to resist analysis and reverse-engineering.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**With neat diagrams, explain the anatomy of a Trojan Horse. How does it differ from a virus and a worm? Mention any 4 categories of Trojans.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Anatomy of a Trojan Horse (3 marks):**

A Trojan horse is a malicious program that masquerades as a legitimate, often useful or entertaining, application. It is delivered through **social engineering** — the user is tricked into downloading and running it. Once executed, the Trojan silently installs its malicious payload while the user sees the benign-looking front-end application.

**Diagram — Trojan Attack Flow:**

```
Attacker builds Trojan → Disguises as legit software (free game, tool)
   → Uploads to website / emails to victims
   → User downloads and runs
   → Trojan installs silently
   → Opens backdoor / drops payload
   → Attacker gains remote control / steals data
```

**Differences (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Virus | Worm | Trojan |
|--------|-------|------|--------|
| Self-replicates | Yes | Yes | No |
| Autonomous spread | No | Yes | No |
| Requires user action | Yes | No | Yes (install) |
| Disguised as legitimate | No | No | Yes |

**Four Categories of Trojans (2 marks):**
1. **Remote Access Trojan (RAT):** Provides attacker full remote control of the victim machine. Example: Back Orifice.
2. **Banking Trojan:** Steals financial credentials and online banking information. Example: Zeus.
3. **Dropper/Downloader:** Downloads additional malware after installation.
4. **Destructive Trojan:** Deletes or corrupts files on the victim's system.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Define malware. Explain the SIR epidemiological model used to model the spread of computer viruses. Derive the basic reproduction number R₀ and explain its significance.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Definition of Malware (1 mark):**
Malware, short for **malicious software**, is any program, code, or fragment thereof intentionally designed to cause harm to computer systems, networks, or users. It includes viruses, worms, Trojans, ransomware, spyware, and rootkits.

**The SIR Model (3 marks):**

The SIR (Susceptible-Infected-Removed) model is borrowed from classical epidemiology and adapted to model the propagation of computer malware across networks. The total host population $N$ is partitioned into three compartments:

- $S(t)$ — Susceptible hosts (vulnerable, not yet infected)
- $I(t)$ — Infected hosts (currently compromised and spreading the malware)
- $R(t)$ — Removed hosts (cleaned, patched, or isolated)

The system is governed by the three coupled differential equations:
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{dS}{dt} &= -\frac{\beta \cdot S \cdot I}{N} \\
\frac{dI}{dt} &= \frac{\beta \cdot S \cdot I}{N} - \gamma \cdot I \\
\frac{dR}{dt} &= \gamma \cdot I
\end{aligned}$$

Where $\beta$ is the contact (infection) rate and $\gamma$ is the removal (cleaning) rate.

**Derivation of $R_0$ (2 marks):**

At the start of an outbreak, $S \approx N$, so the infected-compartment equation simplifies to:
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{dI}{dt} &= (\beta - \gamma) \cdot I
\end{aligned}$$

Solving this linear ODE gives:
$$\begin{aligned}
I(t) = I(0) \cdot e^{(\beta - \gamma) t}
\end{aligned}$$

The infection grows only if $\beta - \gamma > 0$, i.e., $\beta / \gamma > 1$. Define:
$$\begin{aligned}
R_0 = \frac{\beta}{\gamma}
\end{aligned}$$

**Significance (1 mark):**
- $R_0 > 1$ → malware spreads as an epidemic.
- $R_0 < 1$ → malware dies out.
- Defenders aim to make $\gamma \geq \beta$ (faster patching and AV updates) to control the outbreak.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A new worm begins spreading on a corporate LAN of 1,000,000 hosts with contact rate $\beta = 0.4$ per hour and removal rate $\gamma = 0.1$ per hour. Initially 10 hosts are infected. Calculate: (i) $R_0$, (ii) the doubling time of the outbreak, and (iii) the number of infected hosts after 24 hours.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $N = 1{,}000{,}000$ hosts
- $\beta = 0.4 \, hr^{-1}$
- $\gamma = 0.1 \, hr^{-1}$
- $I(0) = 10$
- $t = 24$ hours

**Part (i) — Basic Reproduction Number (2 marks):**
$$\begin{aligned}
R_0 = \frac{\beta}{\gamma} = \frac{0.4}{0.1} = 4
\end{aligned}$$

Since $R_0 = 4 > 1$, the worm will spread as an epidemic.

**Part (ii) — Doubling Time (2 marks):**
$$\begin{aligned}
T_d = \frac{\ln 2}{\beta - \gamma} = \frac{0.6931}{0.4 - 0.1} = \frac{0.6931}{0.3} = 2.31 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}$$

The infected population doubles every **2.31 hours**.

**Part (iii) — Number of Infected Hosts After 24 Hours (3 marks):**
Using the early-outbreak growth formula:
$$\begin{aligned}
I(t) &= I(0) \cdot e^{(\beta - \gamma) t} \\
I(24) &= 10 \cdot e^{(0.4 - 0.1) \times 24} \\
I(24) &= 10 \cdot e^{0.3 \times 24} \\
I(24) &= 10 \cdot e^{7.2} \\
I(24) &= 10 \times 1339.43 \\
I(24) &\approx 13{,}394 \text{ hosts}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall**
> - **Mark Loss 1:** Students often forget to use the *initial-outbreak approximation* $S \approx N$ and instead try to solve the full nonlinear system. KTU expects the **simplified exponential** answer in 3-mark subparts.
> - **Mark Loss 2:** Forgetting to convert units — if $\beta$ and $\gamma$ are per *day* and $t$ is in *hours*, you must convert consistently. Examiners strictly check unit consistency.
> - **Mark Loss 3:** Writing $T_d = 1/(\beta - \gamma)$ instead of $\ln 2 / (\beta - \gamma)$. The $\ln 2 \approx 0.6931$ factor is **mandatory** and is a common deduction point.
> - **Mark Loss 4:** Not stating the **epidemic threshold** conclusion after computing $R_0$ — examiners expect the qualitative interpretation ("since $R_0 > 1$, the worm will spread").
> - **Mark Loss 5:** Confusing the formulas for $R_0$ and $T_d$ — memorizing the wrong formula costs **3–4 marks** instantly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Viruses and Other Malicious Code**

- 🔹 **Malware** = any code designed to cause harm; umbrella term covering viruses, worms, trojans, ransomware, etc.
- 🔹 **Virus** = needs **host file** + **user action** to spread. Lifecycle: **Dormant → Propagation → Triggering → Execution**.
- 🔹 **8 Virus Types:** File Infector, Boot Sector, Macro, Multipartite, Polymorphic, Metamorphic, Stealth, Armored.
- 🔹 **Worm** = **standalone + autonomous + network-borne** — no host, no human action. Examples: Morris, Slammer, WannaCry.
- 🔹 **Trojan** = **disguised as legitimate software** — no self-replication. Types: RAT, Banking, Dropper, Destructive, Proxy.
- 🔹 **Logic Bomb** = code that **detonates on a specific condition** (date, event, counter).
- 🔹 **Backdoor / Trapdoor** = **undocumented entry point** into a system, bypassing authentication.
- 🔹 **Ransomware** = **encrypts victim data** + demands payment (cryptocurrency). Modern billion-dollar threat.
- 🔹 **Rootkit** = hides itself and other malware at the OS or kernel level. **Extremely hard to detect**.
- 🔹 **Botnet** = network of "zombie" hosts controlled via a C2 server. Used for DDoS, spam, cryptomining. Example: Mirai.
- 🔹 **SIR Model:** $\frac{dS}{dt} = -\beta S I/N$, $\frac{dI}{dt} = \beta S I/N - \gamma I$, $\frac{dR}{dt} = \gamma I$.
- 🔹 **Basic Reproduction Number:** $R_0 = \beta / \gamma$. **Epidemic iff $R_0 > 1$**.
- 🔹 **Doubling Time:** $T_d = \ln 2 / (\beta - \gamma) = 0.6931 / (\beta - \gamma)$.
- 🔹 **Early Infection Growth:** $I(t) \approx I(0) \cdot e^{(\beta - \gamma) t}$.
- 🔹 **Endemic Infected Level:** $I^* = N(1 - 1/R_0)$.
- 🔹 **Detection Metrics:** Recall $= TP / (TP + FN)$; FPR $= FP / (FP + TN)$.
- 🔹 **Morris Worm (1988)** = first major Internet worm; led to creation of CERTs.
- 🔹 **Stuxnet (2010)** = first publicly known cyber-weapon; targeted Iranian nuclear centrifuges.
- 🔹 **WannaCry (2017)** = ransomware worm using the EternalBlue exploit; damages $4–8 billion.
- 🔹 **Mitigation:** Defense in depth, signature-based + behavior-based detection, network segmentation, least privilege, prompt patching, user awareness training.

> **END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — VIRUSES AND OTHER MALICIOUS CODE**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Targeted Malicious Code
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT753 - INFORMATION SECURITY IN PUBLIC AND PRIVATE SECTORS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Targeted Malicious Code # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition** > **Targeted Malicious Code** refers to a specific class of software threats that are deliberately engineered, deployed, and customized to infiltrate, disrupt, damage, or gain unauthorized access to a particular computing system, network, organization, or individual user. Unlike broad-spectrum malware (such as traditional worms or viruses that spread indiscriminately), targeted malicious code is characterized by its **specificity of victim**, **stealth of operation**, and **objectivity of payload**. According to the **NIST SP 800-83 Rev. 1** (Guide to Malware Incident Prevention and Handling) framework adopted in KTU's reference material, targeted malicious code operates with three cardinal properties: 1. **Specificity of Target** — Pre-identified victim (IP ranges, OS versions, application versions, user behavior). 2. **Low Propagation Footprint** — Designed not to spread widely, thereby evading signature-based detection. 3. **Mission-Oriented Payload** — Carries a specific objective: espionage, sabotage, financial fraud, or persistence. ## 1.2 The Targeted Malicious Code Family (Taxonomy) | Family Member | Primary Function | KTU Sub-Categorization | |---|---|---| | **Trojan Horse** | Disguised as legitimate software | Covert entry vector | | **Backdoor / Trapdoor** | Bypasses normal authentication | Persistence mechanism | | **Logic Bomb** | Activates upon a pre-defined condition | Time/event-triggered | | **Spyware / Keylogger** | Silently exfiltrates user data | Confidentiality breach | | **Rootkit** | Hides existence of other malware | Stealth/evasion layer | | **Fileless Malware** | Resides in memory only (no file artifact) | Forensic-resistant | | **APT Implant** | Long-term foothold in target network | Strategic compromise | ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy > [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy — The "Custom-Tailored Spy"** > Imagine a massive medieval castle (your computer/network). A generic virus is like a **plague** released in the surrounding village — it infects everyone indiscriminately and draws immediate attention from the castle physicians (antivirus). > > In contrast, **Targeted Malicious Code** is like a **custom-trained assassin** who has memorized the guard rotation schedule, forged the captain's seal, and knows exactly which window on the third floor is left unlatched at 2:00 AM. The assassin enters silently, accomplishes one specific mission (steal the crown jewels = data exfiltration), and leaves no evidence. The castle's outer walls (firewall/IDS) are useless because the assassin never attacked the front gate. ## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme This module directly maps to **Course Outcome CO2**: *"Analyze various types of malicious software threats and recommend appropriate countermeasures in public and private sector environments."* The topic carries an expected weightage of **8–12 marks** in the End Semester Examination (ESE) under Module 2, often appearing as either a Part A (3-mark) definition or a Part B (14-mark) descriptive question. ## 1.5 Core Characteristics — At a Glance - **Stealth-first design** (low noise, low bandwidth, low CPU) - **Polymorphic/metamorphic** code (changes signature per deployment) - **Use of legitimate system tools** (LOLBins — Living Off the Land Binaries) - **Command-and-Control (C2) channel** for remote direction - **Defense Evasion Depth (DED)** score typically ≥ 7/10 on MITRE ATT&CK scale - **Persistence via scheduled tasks, registry run keys, services** (Windows) or **cron jobs, systemd** (Linux) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Operational Anatomy of Targeted Malicious Code The execution lifecycle of any targeted malicious payload can be deconstructed into **seven sequential phases**, as adopted from the **Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain®** and **MITRE ATT&CK Framework** (both standard KTU references). ### Phase 1 — Reconnaissance The attacker gathers intelligence on the target: - **OSINT** (Open-Source Intelligence) — LinkedIn, Shodan, Whois - **Network scanning** — Nmap, masscan - **Social engineering** — Phishing emails tailored to the target ### Phase 2 — Weaponization The malicious code is crafted to match the target's environment: - Custom exploits for known/unknown (zero-day) vulnerabilities - **Dropper documents** (e.g., macro-enabled Office files, malicious PDFs) ### Phase 3 — Delivery Delivery vectors (ranked by KTU-expected frequency): - **Spear-phishing email attachments** (~62% of incidents) - **Watering-hole attacks** (compromising a frequently visited website) - **Supply chain compromise** (e.g., SolarWinds-style — inject into trusted software update) - **Removable media** (USB drops in restricted facilities) ### Phase 4 — Exploitation The malicious code gains initial code execution through: - Buffer overflow exploits - Macro execution in Office documents - Browser exploit kits - Exploiting misconfigured services (SMB, RDP, WebLogic, etc.) ### Phase 5 — Installation Establishment of **persistence** mechanisms: - Windows Registry `Run`/`RunOnce` keys - Scheduled tasks (`schtasks /create`) - Services (`sc create`) - Bootkits/MBR infectors - WMI Event Subscriptions ### Phase 6 — Command-and-Control (C2) Out-of-band communication channel established: - HTTP/HTTPS over port 443 (blends with web traffic) - DNS tunneling - Tor hidden services - Public cloud services (Dropbox, GitHub, Telegram) as dead-drop resolvers ### Phase 7 — Actions on Objectives The final payload executes: - **Data exfiltration** (DLP bypass via encryption + chunking) - **Lateral movement** (Pass-the-Hash, Kerberoasting) - **Privilege escalation** (UAC bypass, token impersonation) - **Sabotage** (Wiper components like NotPetya's `mbrmain`) - **Ransomware deployment** (modern double/triple extortion) ## 2.2 Detailed Sub-Type Analysis ### 2.2.1 Trojan Horse (Covert Entry Vector) > [!IMPORTANT] **Definition (KTU Standard)** > A Trojan Horse is a malicious program disguised as, or embedded within, a legitimate software application, which performs unauthorized actions when executed by an unsuspecting user. **Mathematical model of Trojan trust exploitation:** $$ P_{\text{infection}} = P_{\text{delivery}} \times P_{\text{user\_execution}} \times P_{\text{bypass\_AV}} $$ Where each $P$ is a probability in $[0, 1]$. In targeted attacks, the attacker maximizes $P_{\text{user\_execution}}$ through **social engineering**, often achieving rates as high as **0.20 to 0.45** (20%–45%) in well-researched spear-phishing campaigns, compared to **0.01 to 0.03** for generic mass-mailing campaigns. ### 2.2.2 Backdoor (Persistence Mechanism) A backdoor is a covert method of bypassing normal authentication to secure remote access to a system. KTU distinguishes two types: - **Symmetric backdoor** — Hard-coded password (easily reverse-engineered). - **Asymmetric backdoor** — Public-key authentication (cryptographically resilient). **Detection time formula (for KTU analytical questions):** $$ T_{\text{detect}} = \frac{V_{\text{traffic}}}{R_{\text{exfil}}} \times \frac{1}{N_{\text{anomalies}}} $$ Where: - $V_{\text{traffic}}$ = Volume of suspicious network traffic (in MB) - $R_{\text{exfil}}$ = Detection rate of the IDS (in anomalies/sec) - $N_{\text{anomalies}}$ = Number of distinct anomaly thresholds triggered ### 2.2.3 Logic Bomb (Conditional Trigger) > [!NOTE] **Definition** > A logic bomb is malicious code that remains dormant until a specific logical condition is satisfied (e.g., a date, a user action, the deletion of an employee record, or a specific keystroke combination). **Trigger condition formalization:** $$ T_{\text{activation}} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } (C_{\text{time}} = t_{\text{trigger}}) \lor (C_{\text{event}} = e_{\text{trigger}}) \lor (C_{\text{state}} = s_{\text{trigger}}) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ The OR ($\lor$) operator is critical: any single satisfied condition triggers execution. Sophisticated logic bombs use nested AND/OR trees. ### 2.2.4 Trapdoor A **trapdoor** is a secret entry point into a program inserted by the developer, allowing bypass of security checks. Often appears in commercial software as a maintenance backdoor that is later weaponized. ### 2.2.5 Rootkit A rootkit is a collection of malicious software designed to enable privileged access while actively hiding its existence. Operates at three levels: | Level | Privilege | Stealth Method | KTU Example | |---|---|---|---| | **User-mode** | Ring 3 | API hooking, DLL injection | Zeus banker trojan | | **Kernel-mode** | Ring 0 | Driver manipulation, SSDT hooking | TDL4 (Alureon) | | **Bootkit/Hypervisor** | Ring -1 | MBR/VBR infection | Mebroot, Stuxnet | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] **Mandatory Memorization Table** | Concept | Formula / Metric | Standard Unit / Range | KTU Application | |---|---|---|---| | Infection Probability | $P_{\text{inf}} = P_{\text{del}} \times P_{\text{exec}} \times P_{\text{bypass}}$ | Dimensionless (0–1) | Risk quantification | | Mean Time to Detection (MTTD) | $\text{MTTD} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ where $\lambda$ = incident rate | Days (industry avg: 204) | SOC KPI | | Mean Time to Response (MTTR) | $\text{MTTR} = T_{\text{detect}} + T_{\text{contain}} + T_{\text{eradicate}}$ | Hours | IR planning | | False Positive Rate (FPR) | $\text{FPR} = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | 0–1 | AV tuning | | True Positive Rate (TPR/Recall) | $\text{TPR} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | 0–1 | Detection efficacy | | F1-Score (Detection) | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | 0–1 | Balanced metric | | Polymorphic Code Length | $L_{\text{poly}} = n \cdot 2^k$ | Bytes | AV signature complexity | | Detection Time (Backdoor) | $T_{\text{detect}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{traffic}}}{R_{\text{exfil}} \cdot N_{\text{anomalies}}}$ | Seconds | Forensic timeline | | CVSS Base Score | $\text{CVSS} = \text{RoundUp}(\min(\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability}, 10))$ | 0.0 – 10.0 | Vulnerability prioritization | | Encryption Overhead | $T_{\text{exfil}} = \dfrac{S_{\text{data}}}{B_{\text{channel}}} + t_{\text{crypt}}$ | Seconds | Data exfil timing | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Targeted malicious code analysis underpins the following KTU-aligned professional roles: - **SOC Analyst (Tier 1/2/3)** — Triage, root-cause analysis - **Incident Responder** — Containment, eradication, recovery - **Threat Intelligence Analyst** — IOC (Indicators of Compromise) curation - **Malware Reverse Engineer** — Static + dynamic analysis - **Red Team Operator** — Adversary simulation, TIBER-EU, CBEST frameworks The same kill-chain is used defensively in **MITRE D3FEND** and offensively in **MITRE ATT&CK Enterprise v15+**. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Probability of Successful Targeted Attack We model a targeted attack as a **multi-stage Bernoulli process** where each stage must succeed for the overall attack to succeed. This is the standard KTU-expected analytical model. ### Step 1 — Define the Event Space Let there be $n$ sequential stages, where stage $i$ has an independent success probability $p_i$. The events are independent because the failure of one stage (e.g., phishing email blocked) does not affect the success probability of a parallel stage (e.g., watering-hole). ### Step 2 — Express the Joint Probability The overall attack succeeds **if and only if** all stages succeed: $$ P(\text{success}) = P(S_1 \cap S_2 \cap \ldots \cap S_n) $$ ### Step 3 — Apply the Independence Multiplication Rule For independent events, the joint probability equals the product of marginal probabilities: $$ P(\text{success}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} p_i $$ ### Step 4 — Expand for the Standard 4-Stage Targeted Attack Using the KTU standard phases — **Delivery, Exploitation, Installation, C2 Establishment** — we have $n = 4$: $$ P(\text{success}) = p_{\text{del}} \times p_{\text{exp}} \times p_{\text{inst}} \times p_{\text{C2}} $$ ### Step 5 — Substitute Typical Industry Values Using empirical ranges: - $p_{\text{del}} = 0.60$ (high for spear-phishing with valid sender domain) - $p_{\text{exp}} = 0.40$ (CVE-2024 patched in 70% of targets) - $p_{\text{inst}} = 0.85$ (persistence mechanisms mature) - $p_{\text{C2}} = 0.75$ (HTTPS-based C2 hard to block) $$ P(\text{success}) = 0.60 \times 0.40 \times 0.85 \times 0.75 $$ ### Step 6 — Compute the Result $$ P(\text{success}) = 0.1530 $$ That is, a **15.30% probability** of full kill-chain success for a well-resourced targeted attacker. This figure is consistent with the **2024 IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report** industry benchmarks. ### Step 7 — Derive the Inverse — Defender's Required Block Rate To reduce $P(\text{success})$ below a tolerable threshold $\tau$ (e.g., 0.05), the defender must ensure that at least one stage blocks. The probability that **all stages are blocked** is: $$ P(\text{all blocked}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - p_i^{\text{after controls}}) $$ This is the foundation of the **Defense-in-Depth** principle required in KTU CO2 evaluation. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Detection Effectiveness We use a standard **Confusion Matrix** mapping for a malware scanner: | | **Actually Malicious** | **Actually Benign** | |---|---|---| | **Flagged Malicious** | TP = 95 | FP = 8 | | **Flagged Benign** | FN = 5 | TN = 892 | ### Step 1 — Compute Total Samples $$ N = TP + FP + FN + TN = 95 + 8 + 5 + 892 = 1000 $$ ### Step 2 — Compute True Positive Rate (Recall) $$ \text{TPR} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{95}{95 + 5} = \frac{95}{100} = 0.95 $$ ### Step 3 — Compute False Positive Rate $$ \text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{8}{8 + 892} = \frac{8}{900} \approx 0.00889 $$ ### Step 4 — Compute Precision $$ P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{95}{95 + 8} = \frac{95}{103} \approx 0.9223 $$ ### Step 5 — Compute F1-Score $$ F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot \text{TPR}}{P + \text{TPR}} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.9223 \times 0.95}{0.9223 + 0.95} $$ ### Step 6 — Evaluate Numerator and Denominator Separately $$ \text{Numerator} = 2 \times 0.8762 = 1.7524 $$ $$ \text{Denominator} = 0.9223 + 0.95 = 1.8723 $$ ### Step 7 — Final F1-Score $$ F_1 = \frac{1.7524}{1.8723} \approx 0.9360 $$ A scanner with $F_1 = 0.936$ is considered **production-grade** by KTU standards (threshold: $F_1 \geq 0.90$). ## 3.3 Comparative Threat Matrix (KTU High-Yield) | Threat Type | Targeted? | Propagation | Detection Difficulty (1–10) | Typical Payload | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Virus** | ❌ Generic | High | 3 | Corruption, replication | | **Worm** | ❌ Generic | Very High | 4 | Botnet recruitment | | **Trojan** | ✅ Often | Low | 6 | Backdoor, ransomware dropper | | **Backdoor** | ✅ Always | None | 7 | Remote access, persistence | | **Logic Bomb** | ✅ Always | None | 8 | Sabotage, data wipe | | **Rootkit** | ✅ Often | None | 9 | Stealth, privilege escalation | | **APT Implant** | ✅ Always | Very Low | 10 | Long-term espionage | | **Fileless Malware** | ✅ Often | Low | 9 | Lateral movement, ransomware | > [!IMPORTANT] **Key Insight for KTU Board Exams** > A question of the form *"Distinguish between a virus and a logic bomb"* expects you to highlight that a **logic bomb does NOT self-replicate**; it is a *targeted* trigger-based payload, whereas a virus is a *generic* self-propagating code. This is a **favourite 7-mark KTU Part B question**. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Targeted Malicious Code — Kill Chain Architecture (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Phase 1: Reconnaissance
OSINT, Nmap, Shodan, Social Engineering] --> B[Phase 2: Weaponization
Custom Exploit, Dropper, Macro Doc] B --> C[Phase 3: Delivery
Spear Phishing, Watering Hole, Supply Chain] C --> D[Phase 4: Exploitation
Buffer Overflow, CVE-2024-XXXX, RCE] D --> E[Phase 5: Installation
Registry Run Key, Scheduled Task, Service] E --> F[Phase 6: Command and Control
HTTPS C2, DNS Tunnel, Tor] F --> G[Phase 7: Actions on Objectives
Exfiltration, Lateral Move, Ransomware] G --> H[Detection by SOC Analyst
EDR Alert, SIEM Correlation] H --> I[Incident Response
Isolate, Eradicate, Recover, Forensicate] I --> J[Post-Incident
Lessons Learned, IOC Sharing] style A fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000 style B fill:#ffe5cc,stroke:#ff6600,color:#000 style C fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000 style D fill:#e5ffcc,stroke:#339900,color:#000 style E fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#009933,color:#000 style F fill:#ccffe5,stroke:#009966,color:#000 style G fill:#ccffff,stroke:#006666,color:#000 style H fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003399,color:#000 style I fill:#ccccff,stroke:#330099,color:#000 style J fill:#e5ccff,stroke:#660099,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Block Diagram of a Targeted Trojan-Backdoor Hybrid ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Dropper Module
Spoofed PDF/EXE] --> B[Loader Module
Decrypts Payload Stage 2] B --> C[Persistence Module
Registry / Cron / Service] C --> D[Stealth Module
Rootkit Hooking / Process Masquerading] D --> E[Trigger Module
Logic Bomb Condition Evaluator] E --> F[Payload Module
RAT / Keylogger / Wiper] F --> G[Exfiltration Module
AES-256 Encrypted HTTPS POST] G --> H[C2 Server
Attacker Infrastructure] H --> I[Attacker Operator
Remote Commands] style A fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000 style B fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#993300,color:#000 style C fill:#ffff99,stroke:#999900,color:#000 style D fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000 style E fill:#99ffcc,stroke:#009966,color:#000 style F fill:#99ffff,stroke:#006666,color:#000 style G fill:#99ccff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style H fill:#cc99ff,stroke:#330066,color:#000 style I fill:#ff99cc,stroke:#660033,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Defense-in-Depth Layering — Countermeasure Mapping ```mermaid flowchart TD K1[Targeted Attack Arrives] --> L1[Layer 1: Perimeter
Firewall, IPS, WAF] L1 --> L2[Layer 2: Network
NDR, Segmentation, Zero Trust] L2 --> L3[Layer 3: Endpoint
EDR, Application Allowlisting] L3 --> L4[Layer 4: Identity
MFA, PAM, Conditional Access] L4 --> L5[Layer 5: Data
DLP, Encryption at Rest] L5 --> L6[Layer 6: Application
RASP, SAST, Code Signing] L6 --> M1[Attack Detected and Blocked] L6 --> M2[Attack Persists - SOC Engaged] style K1 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000 style L1 fill:#ffe5cc,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 style L2 fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000 style L3 fill:#e5ffcc,stroke:#669900,color:#000 style L4 fill:#ccffe5,stroke:#009966,color:#000 style L5 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003399,color:#000 style L6 fill:#e5ccff,stroke:#6600cc,color:#000 style M1 fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 style M2 fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Targeted vs. Generic Malicious Code — Comparative Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Incoming Malicious Code] --> B{Is Target Pre-selected?} B -->|NO| C[Generic Malware
Virus, Worm, Mass Ransomware] B -->|YES| D{Victim-specific Payload?} D -->|NO| E[Semi-Targeted
Spear-Phishing, Watering Hole] D -->|YES| F[Full Targeted Code
APT Implant, Logic Bomb] C --> G[Signature Detection Sufficient] E --> H[Behavioral Analytics Required] F --> I[Threat Hunting + Threat Intel Required] style A fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000,color:#000 style B fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#999900,color:#000 style C fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000 style D fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#999900,color:#000 style E fill:#ffd699,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 style F fill:#ff9999,stroke:#990000,color:#000 style G fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000 style H fill:#ffff99,stroke:#999900,color:#000 style I fill:#ff9999,stroke:#990000,color:#000 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define targeted malicious code. List three characteristics that distinguish it from generic malware.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Targeted malicious code is a class of malicious software specifically engineered to compromise a particular system, network, organization, or individual user, with a predefined mission objective. Three distinguishing characteristics: 1. **Specificity of Target** — Pre-identified victim profile, infrastructure, and software stack. 2. **Low Propagation Footprint** — Designed not to spread widely, evading signature-based detection systems. 3. **Mission-Oriented Payload** — Carries a singular objective (espionage, data theft, sabotage) rather than indiscriminate damage. *(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three characteristics — ½ mark each, or 1 mark each for 3 traits.)* --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between a Trojan Horse and a Logic Bomb.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Parameter | Trojan Horse | Logic Bomb | |---|---|---| | **Trigger** | User execution of disguised program | Pre-defined logical condition (time/event) | | **Replication** | Does not self-replicate | Does not self-replicate | | **Primary Role** | Covert entry vector | Conditional sabotage/execution | | **Target Specificity** | Often generic | Always targeted | | **Example** | Emotet dropper | Insider-deployed payroll bomb | *(Award 1 mark for trigger, 1 mark for replication, 1 mark for example/role distinction.)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` #### (a) With a neat diagram, explain the Cyber Kill Chain phases of a targeted malicious code attack. Discuss the role of Indicators of Compromise (IOCs) at each phase. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Definition (1 Mark)** The Cyber Kill Chain (Lockheed Martin, 2011) is a seven-stage model describing the sequential phases an attacker traverses during a targeted intrusion. Each phase produces specific **Indicators of Compromise (IOCs)** — observable artifacts (IPs, domains, file hashes, registry keys, behavioural patterns) used by defenders to detect, attribute, and respond. **Step 2 — Phase-by-Phase Explanation (5 Marks — ½ mark per phase + IOCs):** | Phase | Description | Typical IOCs | |---|---|---| | **1. Reconnaissance** | Gathering target intel (Shodan, LinkedIn, email harvesting) | Unusual inbound port scans, OSINT traces | | **2. Weaponization** | Coupling exploit with payload into a deliverable | New file hashes on threat intel feeds | | **3. Delivery** | Transmitting the weaponized bundle | Malicious email headers, suspicious URLs | | **4. Exploitation** | Triggering the vulnerability for code execution | Process anomalies, child-of-Office anomalies | | **5. Installation** | Establishing persistence (registry, services) | New Run keys, scheduled tasks, services | | **6. Command & Control** | Beaconing to attacker infrastructure | Outbound C2 IPs, DNS anomalies, JA3 hashes | | **7. Actions on Objectives** | Data theft, lateral move, sabotage | DLP alerts, mass file reads, encryption ops | **Step 3 — Role of IOCs (1 Mark)** IOCs serve as the **defender's early-warning system**: detection at an early phase (1–4) costs an order of magnitude less than detection at the final phase. IOCs are categorized as **atomic** (IP, hash), **computed** (regex, hash), and **behavioural** (TTP-based). --- #### (b) An organization receives 1000 emails in a day, out of which 200 are spear-phishing attempts. 80 of these are opened by users, and 30 lead to malware execution. The EDR successfully blocks 25 of these executions. Compute: **(i) The overall infection rate after EDR. (ii) The probability that a randomly selected email results in successful compromise. (iii) Recommend two countermeasures to reduce the residual risk. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Identify Given Quantities (1 Mark)** - Total emails: $N = 1000$ - Spear-phishing emails: $S = 200$ - Opened: $O = 80$ - Executed malware: $E = 30$ - Blocked by EDR: $B = 25$ **Step 2 — Calculate Successful Compromises (1 Mark)** $$ C_{\text{success}} = E - B = 30 - 25 = 5 $$ **Step 3 — Compute Overall Infection Rate After EDR (2 Marks)** $$ R_{\text{infection}} = \frac{C_{\text{success}}}{N} = \frac{5}{1000} = 0.005 = 0.5\% $$ **Step 4 — Compute Compromise Probability per Email (2 Marks)** $$ P(\text{compromise} \mid \text{email}) = \frac{5}{1000} = 0.005 $$ $$ P(\text{compromise} \mid \text{spear-phish}) = \frac{5}{200} = 0.025 = 2.5\% $$ **Step 5 — Recommend Two Countermeasures (1 Mark)** 1. **Advanced Email Gateway with Sandboxing** — Detonate attachments in isolated VMs to identify zero-day payloads. 2. **Security Awareness Training + Phishing Simulation** — Reduce $P_{\text{user\_execution}}$ by training users to recognize spear-phishing cues. 3. *(Bonus)* **Application Allowlisting** — Prevents unauthorized executables from running even if delivered. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` #### (a) Explain the architecture and operational mechanism of a Rootkit. Compare user-mode, kernel-mode, and bootkit variants with suitable KTU-level examples. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Definition (1 Mark)** A rootkit is malicious software designed to gain privileged (often root/admin) access to a system while actively concealing its presence and the presence of other malicious software. **Step 2 — Architecture Overview (2 Marks)** ``` [User Application] ↓ [User-mode Rootkit] ← API hooking, DLL injection ↓ [Kernel] ← SSDT hooking, driver-level hiding ↓ [Bootkit/Hypervisor] ← MBR infection, virtualization-based hiding ↓ [Hardware] ``` **Step 3 — Three Variants Compared (3 Marks — 1 mark each):** | Variant | Privilege Level | Hiding Technique | KTU-Level Example | |---|---|---|---| | **User-mode (Ring 3)** | Same as applications | IAT hooking, inline hooking of `ntdll.dll` | Zeus GameOver, Carberp | | **Kernel-mode (Ring 0)** | OS kernel | SSDT/EAT patching, DKOM (Direct Kernel Object Manipulation) | TDL4 (Alureon), ZeroAccess | | **Bootkit (Ring -1)** | Below OS, pre-boot | MBR/VBR replacement, signed bootloader abuse | Mebroot, Stuxnet (USB) | **Step 4 — Detection & Removal (1 Mark)** - User-mode: Antivirus, file integrity checkers - Kernel-mode: Kernel-mode AV (GMER, RootkitRevealer) - Bootkit: Offline scanning from bootable rescue media, secure boot enforcement, TPM measurement --- #### (b) A malware analysis lab observes the following 10-day attack log on a honeypot: - Day 1–3: Reconnaissance probes (low-and-slow, 2 anomalies/day) - Day 4: Exploit attempt (12 anomalies) - Day 5: Successful breach (40 anomalies — C2 beacon established) - Day 6–10: Data exfiltration (15 anomalies/day, 1.2 GB exfiltrated over 5 days) **Compute:** **(i) The Mean Time to Detection (MTTD) assuming the SOC detected on Day 7. (ii) The average exfiltration bandwidth used. (iii) Identify the kill-chain phase where detection ideally should have occurred and recommend two specific controls. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute MTTD (2 Marks)** The first successful breach occurred on **Day 5**. The SOC detected on **Day 7**. $$ \text{MTTD} = T_{\text{detect}} - T_{\text{breach}} = 7 - 5 = \mathbf{2 \text{ days}} $$ **Step 2 — Compute Average Exfiltration Bandwidth (2 Marks)** Total exfiltrated data: $S = 1.2\ \text{GB} = 1.2 \times 1024\ \text{MB} = 1228.8\ \text{MB}$ Duration: $T = 5\ \text{days} = 5 \times 86400\ \text{s} = 432000\ \text{s}$ $$ B_{\text{avg}} = \frac{S}{T} = \frac{1228.8\ \text{MB}}{432000\ \text{s}} \approx 0.002844\ \text{MB/s} $$ Converting to Mbps: $$ B_{\text{avg}} = 0.002844\ \text{MB/s} \times 8 \approx 0.02275\ \text{Mbps} \approx 22.75\ \text{kbps} $$ This is a classic **low-and-slow exfiltration** profile, designed to evade threshold-based DLP systems. **Step 3 — Identify Ideal Detection Phase (1 Mark)** Ideal detection should have occurred at **Phase 4 (Exploitation, Day 4)** — the 12 anomalies represented a clear deviation from baseline. This would have been **48 hours earlier** than actual detection. **Step 4 — Recommend Two Specific Controls (2 Marks — 1 mark each)** 1. **Network Detection and Response (NDR) with UEBA** — User & Entity Behaviour Analytics would baseline normal reconnaissance traffic and flag the 12-anomaly spike on Day 4. 2. **Honeypot-driven Threat Intel Integration with SIEM** — Forward honeypot alerts in real time to the SIEM (Splunk/QRadar) for automated correlation and Tier-1 triage. 3. *(Bonus)* **Egress Filtering and TLS Inspection** — Inspect outbound HTTPS traffic to detect C2 beaconing patterns and exfiltration at the perimeter. --- > [!WARNING] **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Confusing Trojan with Virus** — A Trojan does NOT self-replicate. Students who claim "Trojan replicates like a virus" lose 2 marks instantly. > 2. **Skipping IOCs in Kill Chain answers** — A 7-mark question on the Kill Chain that omits IOCs is automatically capped at 4 marks. > 3. **Forgetting Units** — Bandwidth in KB/s vs KBps (lowercase vs uppercase 'b'), MB vs MiB — these are KTU Board pet peeves. > 4. **Using $|$ inside tables** — When a question asks to "tabulate", the | symbol conflicts with markdown. Always use `\vert` or `such that`. > 5. **Omitting numerical units in calculations** — Even a correct answer without "days" or "Mbps" is marked down 0.5 marks. > 6. **Wrong formula for F1-Score** — Many students write $F_1 = (P+R)/2$ instead of the harmonic mean. This is a **fatal error** in any 7-mark question. --- ## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] **🎯 Must-Know Concepts for KTU Module 2 — Targeted Malicious Code** - ✅ **Definition:** Targeted malicious code = pre-identified victim + low propagation + mission-oriented payload. - ✅ **7-Phase Cyber Kill Chain:** Reconnaissance → Weaponization → Delivery → Exploitation → Installation → C2 → Actions. - ✅ **Five Core Types:** Trojan, Backdoor, Logic Bomb, Trapdoor, Rootkit — each with distinct trigger and persistence models. - ✅ **Trigger Formula for Logic Bomb:** $T = 1$ if any of $(C_{\text{time}}, C_{\text{event}}, C_{\text{state}})$ matches; else $0$. - ✅ **Joint Probability of Attack Success:** $P(\text{success}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} p_i$ (multiplicative model). - ✅ **Rootkit Privilege Rings:** Ring 3 (user) → Ring 0 (kernel) → Ring -1 (bootkit). - ✅ **F1-Score:** $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ (harmonic mean of Precision and Recall). - ✅ **CVSS Score Range:** 0.0 (informational) to 10.0 (critical). - ✅ **IOC Categories:** Atomic (IP, hash) | Computed (regex, hash) | Behavioural (TTP). - ✅ **Defense-in-Depth Layers:** Perimeter → Network → Endpoint → Identity → Data → Application. - ✅ **Low-and-Slow Exfiltration Signature:** ~20–25 kbps sustained, encrypted HTTPS, off-peak hours. - ✅ **Industry MTTD Benchmark (2024):** 204 days (IBM Cost of Data Breach Report). - ✅ **Most Common Delivery Vector:** Spear-phishing (~62% of targeted incidents). - ✅ **Polymorphic Code Length:** $L = n \cdot 2^k$ bytes. - ✅ **Supply Chain Attack Exemplar:** SolarWinds SUNBURST (2020) — must be quotable in any APT-related KTU answer. - ✅ **Always cite MITRE ATT&CK Technique IDs** (e.g., T1059.001 for PowerShell) for extra marks in 14-mark answers.

topics.title: Controls Against Program Threats
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT753 - INFORMATION SECURITY IN PUBLIC AND PRIVATE SECTORS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Controls Against Program Threats

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Program Threats** are deliberate, malicious, or accidental manipulations of software programs that compromise the confidentiality, integrity, and availability (the **CIA Triad**) of information systems. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, *Controls Against Program Threats* refer to the comprehensive set of **administrative, logical (technical), and physical safeguards** designed to prevent, detect, and mitigate malicious code execution and unauthorized program manipulation.

According to NIST SP 800-53 (used as a global benchmark for security controls), program threats are countered through layered defensive mechanisms, often conceptualized as **Defense-in-Depth (DiD)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme (PECCT753) classifies program threat controls into three primary categories: **Preventive Controls**, **Detective Controls**, and **Corrective/Recovery Controls**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Bank Vault" Analogy)

Imagine a bank vault protecting gold bars:

| Security Layer | Real-World Analogy | Information Security Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Thick Steel Walls | Physical barrier | **Firewalls, Access Control** |
| Security Camera | Surveillance | **Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS)** |
| Alarm System | Reactive measure | **Incident Response & Recovery** |
| Vault Door Combination | Authentication | **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)** |
| Guard at Door | Verification | **Biometric Authentication** |

Just as a bank does not rely on a single lock, an information system must not rely on a single control. **Layered security** is the cornerstone of KTU-mandated information security.

## 1.3 Categories of Program Threats (Threat Landscape)

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Threat Categories for KTU 2024:**
> 1. **Malware** (Viruses, Worms, Trojans, Spyware, Ransomware)
> 2. **Program Flaws** (Buffer Overflows, Logic Bombs, Backdoors)
> 3. **Execution Threats** (Privilege Escalation, Code Injection)
> 4. **Data-Driven Threats** (SQL Injection, XSS)
> 5. **Surreptitious Threats** (Covert Channels, Rootkits)

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of Controls

$$\text{Total Security Posture} = f(\text{Prevention}, \text{Detection}, \text{Response})$$

- **Preventive Controls:** Stop the threat *before* it occurs (e.g., antivirus, firewalls).
- **Detective Controls:** Identify the threat *during* or *after* execution (e.g., IDS, log monitoring).
- **Corrective Controls:** Restore system integrity *after* a breach (e.g., backups, patching).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Controls

The KTU 2024 framework organizes controls against program threats in a **three-tier hierarchy**:

### Tier 1: Administrative Controls
- **Security Policies & Procedures** (ISO 27001 aligned)
- **Security Awareness Training**
- **Change Management**
- **Incident Response Planning**

### Tier 2: Logical (Technical) Controls
- **Antivirus / Anti-malware Software**
- **Firewalls (Packet Filtering, Stateful, Application-Layer)**
- **Intrusion Detection/Prevention Systems (IDS/IPS)**
- **Access Control Lists (ACLs)**
- **Encryption (AES-256, RSA-2048)**
- **Code Signing & Digital Certificates**
- **Sandboxing & Virtualization**
- **Patch Management Systems**

### Tier 3: Physical Controls
- **Locked Server Rooms**
- **Biometric Access**
- **CCTV Surveillance**
- **Hardware Security Modules (HSM)**

## 2.2 Deep Dive: Logical Controls (High-Yield for KTU)

### 2.2.1 Antivirus Software — Operational Logic

Antivirus software uses three primary detection methods:

1. **Signature-Based Detection:** Matches file hash against a database of known malware signatures (MD5, SHA-256).
2. **Heuristic-Based Detection:** Analyzes program behavior and code structure for *suspicious patterns* (e.g., self-replication, file overwriting).
3. **Behavioral-Based Detection:** Monitors runtime behavior in a sandbox; flags actions like registry modification or network connection to blacklisted IPs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Detection Effectiveness Equation:**
> $$D_{eff} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \times 100\%$$
> Where $TP$ = True Positives (correctly flagged malware) and $FN$ = False Negatives (missed malware).

### 2.2.2 Firewall Architecture (The Five-Tuple Rule)

A firewall filters traffic based on the **5-tuple**:
$$F = \{Source\ IP,\ Destination\ IP,\ Source\ Port,\ Destination\ Port,\ Protocol\}$$

- **Packet-Filtering Firewall:** Layer 3/4, stateless, fast.
- **Stateful Inspection Firewall:** Layer 3/4, tracks connection state.
- **Application-Layer Gateway (Proxy):** Layer 7, deep packet inspection.
- **Next-Generation Firewall (NGFW):** Combines all above + DPI + IDS.

### 2.2.3 Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) vs. IPS

| Feature | IDS | IPS |
|---|---|---|
| Action | Passive (alerts) | Active (blocks) |
| Placement | Out-of-band (SPAN port) | Inline (network path) |
| Response Time | After detection | Real-time |
| False Positive Impact | Log noise | Service disruption |

### 2.2.4 Trusted System Concepts

A **Trusted System** enforces a security policy using four fundamental principles:
1. **Least Privilege** — minimal access rights.
2. **Separation of Duties** — no single user controls entire process.
3. **Defense-in-Depth** — multiple overlapping controls.
4. **Complete Mediation** — every access checked against policy.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Metric | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)** | $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | Monetary units (₹) |
| 2 | **Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)** | $SLE = Asset\ Value \times Exposure\ Factor$ | Monetary units (₹) |
| 3 | **Risk Score (NIST)** | $Risk = Threat \times Vulnerability \times Impact$ | Qualitative scale (Low/Med/High) |
| 4 | **Detection Rate** | $DR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Percentage (0–100%) |
| 5 | **False Positive Rate** | $FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Percentage (0–100%) |
| 6 | **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** | $MTTD = \frac{\sum Detection\ Times}{Number\ of\ Incidents}$ | Hours/Days |
| 7 | **Mean Time to Respond (MTTR)** | $MTTR = \frac{\sum Response\ Times}{Number\ of\ Incidents}$ | Hours/Days |
| 8 | **Encryption Strength (bits)** | $2^n$ possible keys | Bits (e.g., AES-256 = $2^{256}$) |
| 9 | **Hash Collision Probability** | $P_{coll} \approx 1.18\sqrt{N}$ (Birthday Paradox) | For N inputs, probability of collision |
| 10 | **Cost-Benefit of Control** | $CBA = \frac{ALE_{before} - ALE_{after} - Cost\ of\ Control}{Cost\ of\ Control}$ | Ratio (greater than 1 = justified) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The **ALE/SLE/ARO** triplet is universally applied in **Quantitative Risk Analysis** for enterprise security budgeting, cyber insurance, and ROI calculations on security investments.

## 2.4 Real-World Application

In a typical **Banking Information System (BIS)** under RBI Cyber Security Framework:
- **AES-256** encrypts transaction data at rest.
- **TLS 1.3** secures data in transit.
- **HIPS (Host-based IPS)** monitors endpoint behavior.
- **SIEM (Security Information & Event Management)** aggregates logs for correlation.
- **Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA)** verifies every request, even from internal networks.

This multi-layer approach ensures that even if one control fails, others provide residual protection.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE) with Security Control

This is a **high-yield numerical problem** frequently asked in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) for 7-14 marks.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

> A bank has an online transaction server valued at ₹50,00,000. The **Exposure Factor (EF)** for a ransomware attack is **40%**. The **Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO)** is **0.5** (i.e., once every 2 years on average). The bank is considering deploying an advanced EDR (Endpoint Detection & Response) solution that costs **₹3,00,000 per year**, and it is expected to reduce the ARO to **0.1**.
> 
> **Calculate:** (a) The ALE without the control. (b) The ALE with the control. (c) The Cost-Benefit Analysis (CBA).

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify given parameters**

Let $A$ = Asset Value = ₹50,00,000

Let $EF$ = Exposure Factor = 0.40

Let $ARO_1$ = Annualized Rate of Occurrence (without control) = 0.5

Let $ARO_2$ = Annualized Rate of Occurrence (with control) = 0.1

Let $C$ = Annual Cost of Control = ₹3,00,000

**Step 2: Calculate the Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)**

The SLE represents the monetary loss from a **single occurrence** of the threat. It is computed as the product of the Asset Value and the Exposure Factor.

$$SLE = A \times EF$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$SLE = 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.40$$

Performing the multiplication:

$$SLE = 20{,}00{,}000$$

Therefore, the SLE is ₹20,00,000 (Twenty Lakhs).

**Step 3: Calculate the Annual Loss Expectancy without the control (ALE₁)**

The ALE represents the **expected annual monetary loss** from the threat. It is computed as the product of the SLE and the ARO.

$$ALE_1 = SLE \times ARO_1$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$ALE_1 = 20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5$$

Performing the multiplication:

$$ALE_1 = 10{,}00{,}000$$

Therefore, the expected annual loss without the EDR control is ₹10,00,000 (Ten Lakhs).

**Step 4: Calculate the Annual Loss Expectancy with the control (ALE₂)**

$$ALE_2 = SLE \times ARO_2$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$ALE_2 = 20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.1$$

Performing the multiplication:

$$ALE_2 = 2{,}00{,}000$$

Therefore, the expected annual loss with the EDR control is ₹2,00,000 (Two Lakhs).

**Step 5: Calculate the Annual Savings from the Control**

The annual savings (also called *Risk Reduction*) is the difference between the ALE without control and the ALE with control.

$$Savings = ALE_1 - ALE_2$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$Savings = 10{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000$$

Performing the subtraction:

$$Savings = 8{,}00{,}000$$

Therefore, the annual savings from deploying the EDR control is ₹8,00,000 (Eight Lakhs).

**Step 6: Calculate the Net Annual Benefit (NAB)**

The net annual benefit is the annual savings minus the annual cost of the control.

$$NAB = Savings - C$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$NAB = 8{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000$$

Performing the subtraction:

$$NAB = 5{,}00{,}000$$

Therefore, the net annual benefit is ₹5,00,000 (Five Lakhs). Since the NAB is positive, the control is financially justified.

**Step 7: Calculate the Cost-Benefit Ratio (CBR / Return on Security Investment - ROSI)**

The CBR is the ratio of the annual savings to the annual cost of the control.

$$CBR = \frac{Savings}{C}$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$CBR = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000}$$

Performing the division:

$$CBR = 2.667$$

Therefore, for every ₹1 invested in the EDR control, the bank saves ₹2.67 in expected losses. The investment is **highly justified** because the CBR is greater than 1.

### 3.1.3 Final Tabulated Result

| Metric | Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| SLE | ₹20,00,000 | Loss per incident |
| ALE (without EDR) | ₹10,00,000 | Expected annual loss (current) |
| ALE (with EDR) | ₹2,00,000 | Expected annual loss (post-control) |
| Annual Savings | ₹8,00,000 | Loss reduction |
| Net Annual Benefit | ₹5,00,000 | Profit after control cost |
| Cost-Benefit Ratio | 2.67 | Investment is justified (>> 1) |

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Threat-Control Mapping (KTU-Exam Ready)

| Program Threat | Primary Control | Secondary Control | Tertiary Control | Detection Method |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Virus** | Antivirus (signature) | Heuristic scanner | Sandboxing | File integrity checker |
| **Worm** | Network firewall | IDS/IPS | Patch management | Anomaly-based NIDS |
| **Trojan** | Application whitelisting | HIDS | User awareness | Behavioral analysis |
| **Ransomware** | EDR + Backups | Network segmentation | MFA | Honeypot / decoy files |
| **Buffer Overflow** | DEP / ASLR | Stack canaries | Code review (SAST) | Fuzzing |
| **SQL Injection** | Parameterized queries | WAF (Web App Firewall) | Input validation | Log analysis |
| **Logic Bomb** | Code review | HIDS | Change management | Version control audit |
| **Backdoor** | Code signing | Network segmentation | Port scanning | Integrity checkers |
| **Rootkit** | Secure boot (UEFI) | HIDS | Forensic analysis | Memory forensics |
| **Spyware** | Anti-spyware | Browser security settings | User training | Network traffic analysis |
| **Zero-Day** | Behavior-based IDS | Sandboxing | Threat intelligence | Heuristic engine |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** Questions often ask you to **map a threat to its most appropriate control**. Memorize the *first column → second column* mapping; it carries **2–3 marks** instantly.

## 3.3 Derivation: Return on Security Investment (ROSI)

The ROSI formula is derived from the CBA concept:

$$ROSI = \frac{(ALE_1 - ALE_2) - Cost\ of\ Control}{Cost\ of\ Control} \times 100\%$$

Substituting our earlier example values:

$$ROSI = \frac{(10{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000) - 3{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} \times 100\%$$

Performing the subtraction in the numerator:

$$ROSI = \frac{8{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} \times 100\%$$

Performing the second subtraction:

$$ROSI = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} \times 100\%$$

Performing the division:

$$ROSI = 1.6667 \times 100\%$$

Final result:

$$ROSI = 166.67\%$$

This means the EDR control delivers a **166.67% return** over its cost annually.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Defense-in-Depth Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **layered control architecture** used to counter program threats, showing how data and threats traverse multiple defensive checkpoints.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Request] --> B[Layer 1 Perimeter Firewall]
    B --> C[Layer 2 DMZ Proxy Server]
    C --> D[Layer 3 IDS IPS Engine]
    D --> E[Layer 4 Network Segmentation]
    E --> F[Layer 5 Application Firewall WAF]
    F --> G[Layer 6 Authentication and MFA]
    G --> H[Layer 7 Access Control Lists]
    H --> I[Layer 8 Endpoint Protection EDR]
    I --> J[Layer 9 Data Encryption at Rest]
    J --> K[Database and Asset Layer]
    
    L[Threat Actor] --> M[Layer 1 Honeypot Detection]
    M --> N[SIEM Correlation Engine]
    
    O[Log Sources] --> N
    N --> P[Security Operations Center SOC]
    P --> Q[Incident Response Team]
    Q --> R[Recovery and Patching]
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**

- **A → B → C → D → E → F → G → H → I → J → K:** This is the **legitimate request path**. The request must pass through 9 distinct control layers before reaching the database.
- **L → M:** Threats are **diverted to a Honeypot** at the perimeter, which is a decoy system that records attacker behavior.
- **O → N → P → Q → R:** Logs from all 9 layers feed into the **SIEM engine**, which correlates events and alerts the **SOC team** for **Incident Response and Recovery**.

## 4.2 Control Classification Hierarchy (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Controls Against Program Threats] --> B[Administrative Controls]
    A --> C[Logical or Technical Controls]
    A --> D[Physical Controls]
    
    B --> B1[Security Policies]
    B --> B2[Training and Awareness]
    B --> B3[Change Management]
    B --> B4[Incident Response Plan]
    
    C --> C1[Preventive]
    C --> C2[Detective]
    C --> C3[Corrective]
    
    C1 --> C1a[Firewalls]
    C1 --> C1b[Antivirus]
    C1 --> C1c[Access Control]
    C1 --> C1d[Encryption]
    
    C2 --> C2a[IDS and IPS]
    C2 --> C2b[Log Monitoring]
    C2 --> C2c[SIEM]
    C2 --> C2d[Integrity Checkers]
    
    C3 --> C3a[Backups]
    C3 --> C3b[Patching]
    C3 --> C3c[Disaster Recovery]
    C3 --> C3d[Forensic Analysis]
    
    D --> D1[Biometric Locks]
    D --> D2[CCTV Cameras]
    D --> D3[Secure Server Rooms]
    D --> D4[Hardware Security Modules]
```

## 4.3 Threat-to-Control Flow (Detection Workflow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Program Threat Enters] --> B1{Vector Analysis}
    B1 -->|Network| C1[Firewall Filters]
    B1 -->|Email| C2[Mail Gateway Scans]
    B1 -->|Web| C3[WAF Inspects]
    B1 -->|Removable Media| C4[Endpoint AV Scans]
    
    C1 --> D1{Threat Detected}
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D1
    
    D1 -->|Yes| E1[Quarantine and Alert]
    D1 -->|No| F1[Log and Forward to SIEM]
    
    E1 --> G1[Incident Response Team]
    F1 --> G1
    
    G1 --> H1[Forensic Analysis]
    G1 --> I1[Patch and Harden]
    G1 --> J1[Update Signatures]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "draw a diagram" in the ESE, a **labeled block diagram** with arrows showing flow direction is sufficient. You do **not** need artistic sketches; logical flow diagrams are accepted and preferred by KTU examiners.
