**Administrative benefit:** When a new doctor joins, the admin simply assigns the `Doctor` role — no need to set per-file permissions. When the doctor leaves, the role is revoked — automatically losing all access. This achieves the principle of **least privilege** and **separation of duties**.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Unix File Protection Basics (2 marks):**
Every Unix file has an **inode** storing metadata including 9 permission bits grouped into 3 classes:

- **Owner (User):** 3 bits (r, w, x)
- **Group:** 3 bits (r, w, x)
- **Others:** 3 bits (r, w, x)

Encoded as a 9-bit number, often displayed in octal: $\text{perm} = 4r + 2w + 1x$ per class.

**Step 2 — Standard Permission Bits (2 marks):**

| Octal | Symbolic | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | `---` | No access |
| 1 | `--x` | Execute only |
| 2 | `-w-` | Write only |
| 3 | `-wx` | Write + execute |
| 4 | `r--` | Read only |
| 5 | `r-x` | Read + execute |
| 6 | `rw-` | Read + write |
| 7 | `rwx` | Full access |

Example: `chmod 640 file.txt` → owner: `rw-`, group: `r--`, others: `---`.

**Step 3 — Special Bits: setuid, setgid, sticky (3 marks):**

- **setuid bit (Set User ID, octal 4000):** When set on an executable, the process runs with the **owner's** privileges, not the caller's. Example: `/usr/bin/passwd` has setuid root so users can change their own password (modifying `/etc/shadow`).

- **setgid bit (Set Group ID, octal 2000):** On an executable, the process runs with the **group's** privileges. On a directory, new files inherit the directory's group — used for shared project folders.

- **Sticky bit (octal 1000):** On a directory, only the **file owner** (or root) can delete or rename files within it, even if others have write permission. Example: `/tmp` is world-writable with sticky bit so users cannot delete each other's files.

**Security implications:**

- setuid is a major attack surface — a vulnerable setuid-root program allows privilege escalation. Modern systems use **capabilities** instead of full setuid root.
- setgid on directories enables **collaborative workgroups** without exposing files to the world.
- Sticky bit on shared directories prevents **symlink attacks** and accidental/unauthorized deletion in `/tmp`.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in File Protection Questions**
>
> 1. **Confusing ACL and Capability storage direction** — Many students write "ACL is stored with the user." This is incorrect. *ACLs are stored with the object; capabilities are stored with the subject.* This single error can cost **2 marks** in any comparison question.
>
> 2. **Mixing up Biba and Bell-LaPadula rules** — Students frequently say "Bell-LaPadula prevents modification." Wrong! **Bell-LaPadula = Confidentiality (No Read Up, No Write Down).** **Biba = Integrity (No Read Down, No Write Up).** This is the most-tested 2-mark concept in the module.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the three Reference Monitor properties** — A common 3-mark question asks to list them. The mnemonic is **"CTV"** — **C**omplete mediation, **T**amper-proof, **V**erifiable. Examiners will not give full credit if any one is missing.
>
> 4. **Not drawing diagrams in 14-mark answers** — Any question on protection mechanisms *demands* a labeled diagram of the access matrix, the reference monitor, or the role hierarchy. **No diagram = 2–3 marks deducted** in a typical KTU valuation.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the "Why" —** Examiners specifically award 2–3 marks for explaining the *engineering rationale*. Don't just state "we use RBAC" — explain that it solves the N×M user-permission explosion by introducing a role layer.
>
> 6. **setuid confusion** — Many students think setuid means "user can set permissions." It actually means the **process executes with the file owner's identity**, not the invoking user's. This distinction is a frequent 1-mark trap.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section for last-minute exam revision.**

### Quick-Fire Concepts

- ✅ File protection enforces **Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability** on file objects.
- ✅ **Access Rights:** $\{r, w, x, a, d, l, o\}$ — read, write, execute, append, delete, list, owner.
- ✅ **Reference Monitor:** Tamper-proof, complete-mediation, verifiable (Anderson, 1972).
- ✅ **Access Matrix** (Lampson, 1971): Rows = subjects, columns = objects, cells = rights.
- ✅ **ACL** stored with object; **Capability** stored with subject.
- ✅ **ACL** = easy revocation, hard delegation. **Capability** = easy delegation, hard revocation.
- ✅ **RBAC** assigns permissions to roles, then roles to users — solves N×M explosion.
- ✅ **Bell-LaPadula (BLP):** No Read Up + No Write Down → **Confidentiality**.
- ✅ **Biba:** No Read Down + No Write Up → **Integrity**.
- ✅ **Unix Octal:** $\text{perm} = 4r + 2w + 1x$ per class (owner/group/other).
- ✅ **setuid (4000):** Process executes with file owner's UID.
- ✅ **setgid (2000):** On directory → new files inherit group.
- ✅ **Sticky bit (1000):** Only owner can delete files in shared dir (e.g., `/tmp`).
- ✅ **DAC** = owner-controlled (Unix bits). **MAC** = system-enforced labels (SELinux MLS).
- ✅ **ABAC** = attribute/policy-based (OPA, XACML) — most flexible, most complex.
- ✅ **Clark-Wilson** = well-formed transactions + separation of duties (banking).
- ✅ **Chinese Wall** = conflict-of-interest-based (consulting, audit firms).
- ✅ File protection in **Indian public sector** must comply with **IT Act 2000, DPDPA 2023, CERT-In directives**.
- ✅ File protection in **Indian private sector** additionally follows **ISO/IEC 27001** Annex A.8 & A.9.
- ✅ **OAuth 2.0 / JWT** are real-world capability systems used in cloud APIs.
- ✅ **Audit logging** is mandatory — every grant/deny must be recorded for non-repudiation.

### Master Formulae (Final Recall)

$$
\boxed{\text{Access}(S, O, r) \iff r \in M[S,O]}
$$

$$
\boxed{M[S,O] = \bigcup_{D \in \text{ActiveRoles}(S)} M[D,O]}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{BLP read}: \text{level}(S) \ge \text{level}(O)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{BLP write}: \text{level}(S) \le \text{level}(O)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Biba read}: \text{level}(S) \le \text{level}(O)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Biba write}: \text{level}(S) \ge \text{level}(O)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Unix octal} = 4r + 2w + 1x}
$$

> **End of Notes — File Protection Mechanisms (Module 2)**
> *Mapped to KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcomes: CO2 (Understand), CO3 (Apply), CO4 (Analyze).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: User Authentication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT753 - INFORMATION SECURITY IN PUBLIC AND PRIVATE SECTORS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** User Authentication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**User Authentication** is the security mechanism by which a computing system validates the identity of a user, device, or process attempting to access a digital resource. It answers the fundamental question: *"Is the claimant genuinely who (or what) they purport to be?"*

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, authentication is one of the three pillars of information security governance, traditionally grouped under the **"Triple-A Framework"**:

- **Authentication** → Verifying *who* you are
- **Authorization** → Determining *what* you are allowed to do
- **Accounting (Auditing)** → Recording *what* you actually did

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PECCT753):** Authentication forms the *first line of defense* in any public or private sector information system. Whether it is an e-Governance portal (Aadhaar, DigiLocker, GST Network) or a private enterprise ERP, the user authentication subsystem is mandatory under the IT Act 2000 (amended 2008) and the DPDP Act 2023.

## 1.2 The Conceptual Analogy — "The Airport Security Check"

Imagine you arrive at **Cochin International Airport (COK)** for a flight to Dubai.

| Airport Step | Information Security Equivalent |
|---|---|
| You walk to the check-in counter and present your **passport** | **Identification**: User declares an identity (e.g., username) |
| The airline staff checks your **photo, name, and passport number** against the database | **Authentication**: System verifies the *evidence* (password, OTP, fingerprint) |
| You receive a **boarding pass** specifying seat and gate | **Authorization**: System grants specific privileges |
| The **CCTV** records your movements inside the terminal | **Accounting/Auditing**: Logs of every action you performed |

If your passport doesn't match your face → you are **rejected** (failed authentication).
If your passport matches → you **proceed** to the boarding gate (successful authentication).

## 1.3 Core Authentication Vocabulary

> [!NOTE]
> **Principal / Claimant** – The entity (user, service, device) requesting access.
> **Verifier** – The system that checks the credentials presented.
> **Credential** – The piece of evidence submitted (password, token, biometric sample).
> **Identity Provider (IdP)** – A trusted third-party service that authenticates users (e.g., Okta, Azure AD, NIC for Govt. portals).

## 1.4 The Three Canonical Authentication Factors

Authentication factors are categorised into **three classical classes**, and modern systems combine them for stronger security:

1. **Something You KNOW (Knowledge Factor)** — Passwords, PINs, security questions, passphrases.
2. **Something You HAVE (Possession Factor)** — Smart cards, hardware tokens (RSA SecurID), OTP via SMS/email, mobile authenticator apps.
3. **Something You ARE (Biometric / Inherence Factor)** — Fingerprint, iris, face, voice, gait, keystroke dynamics.

> [!TIP]
> A system that requires **two or more distinct factors** is called **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)**. Under the **RBI Master Direction on Digital Lending (2022)** and **CERT-In Directions of April 2022**, MFA is mandatory for all financial and critical-sector transactions in India.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Anatomy of an Authentication Exchange

Every authentication protocol, regardless of complexity, executes the same logical sequence:

1. **Claim Initiation** → The user submits an identifier (e.g., username, Aadhaar number) to the system.
2. **Challenge Generation** → The verifier issues a challenge (random nonce, OTP request, biometric prompt).
3. **Response Submission** → The user supplies the matching credential.
4. **Verification** → The verifier compares the response against its trusted store.
5. **Decision & Session Grant** → On success, a session token or JWT is issued; on failure, access is denied and the attempt is logged.

## 2.2 Password-Based Authentication — Deeper Analysis

The password subsystem is conceptually a one-way function over a secret string. Its strength is measured by **entropy** — the unpredictability of the password to an attacker.

### 2.2.1 Password Entropy Equation

The theoretical entropy $E$ of a randomly generated password is:

$$
E = L \cdot \log_2(R)
$$

where:
- $L$ = length of the password (number of characters)
- $R$ = size of the character set (e.g., 26 lowercase, 26 uppercase, 10 digits, 33 special → $R = 95$ for full ASCII printable)
- $E$ is measured in **bits**

The **total keyspace** (number of candidate passwords) is:

$$
N = R^{L}
$$

### 2.2.2 Time-to-Crack Estimation

If an attacker can mount $S$ guesses per second, the average time to compromise the password is:

$$
T_{crack} = \frac{N}{2S} = \frac{R^{L}}{2S}
$$

The division by 2 is because, on average, the attacker will find the correct password halfway through the exhaustive search.

### 2.2.3 Secure Storage — Hashing and Salting

Plain passwords are never stored. Instead, the system stores:

$$
H_{stored} = \text{Hash}(\text{Password} \parallel \text{Salt})
$$

where $\parallel$ denotes concatenation, and **Salt** is a per-user random nonce that defeats precomputed rainbow-table attacks.

## 2.3 Biometric Authentication — Quantitative Metrics

Biometric systems are evaluated using a **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC)** framework. Two critical error rates are:

$$
\text{FAR} = \frac{FA}{IA} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{FRR} = \frac{FR}{GA} \times 100\%
$$

where:
- $FA$ = number of False Acceptances (impostor accepted)
- $IA$ = total Impostor Attempts
- $FR$ = number of False Rejections (genuine user rejected)
- $GA$ = total Genuine Attempts

The point at which **FAR = FRR** is called the **Equal Error Rate (EER)** — a single scalar used to compare biometric algorithms. Lower EER ⇒ better algorithm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the KTU board exam, a question frequently asks: *"Which is more dangerous — a high FAR or a high FRR, and in which application contexts?"* The examiner expects you to identify **FAR (False Acceptance)** as the security-critical metric (an impostor gained access) and **FRR** as the usability-critical metric (a legitimate user is inconvenienced).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Password Entropy | $E = L \cdot \log_2(R)$ | $L$=length, $R$=charset size | Strength scoring in password meters |
| 2 | Keyspace Size | $N = R^{L}$ | — | Estimating brute-force search space |
| 3 | Avg. Crack Time | $T = R^{L} / (2S)$ | $S$ = guesses/sec | Risk assessment & policy setting |
| 4 | Storage Hash | $h = H(p \parallel s)$ | $p$=password, $s$=salt | DB column design, breach impact |
| 5 | False Accept Rate | $\text{FAR} = FA / IA$ | $FA$=false accepts | Biometric security evaluation |
| 6 | False Reject Rate | $\text{FRR} = FR / GA$ | $FR$=false rejects | Biometric usability evaluation |
| 7 | Equal Error Rate | $\text{FAR} = \text{FRR}$ | at threshold $\tau$ | Algorithm benchmarking |
| 8 | Kerberos Clock Skew Bound | $\Delta t \le T_{ticket} - T_{req}$ | $\Delta t$ = clock difference | Active Directory time-policy |
| 9 | HMAC Authentication | $\text{MAC} = H(K \oplus opad \;\Vert\; H(K \oplus ipad \;\Vert\; m))$ | $K$=key, $m$=message | TLS, IPsec, API signing |
| 10 | OTP Entropy (TOTP) | $E_{otp} = \log_2(10^{6}) = 19.93$ bits | 6-digit decimal | Mobile banking strength analysis |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **E-Governance**: Aadhaar Authentication uses a 12-digit UID + biometric → the *demographic* + *biometric* authentication factors.
- **Banking**: RBI mandates **Two-Factor Authentication (2FA)** for all digital payments (password + OTP).
- **Healthcare (HIPAA / ABDM)**: The **Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission** uses PHR addresses protected by Aadhaar-based MFA.
- **Defence / Critical Infra**: Smart-card based PKI authentication (e.g., Defence e-Governance Agency – DEGA).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Password Entropy and Crack Time

We will derive the **average time** an offline attacker requires to compromise a password, given entropy and attack speed.

### Step 1 — Define the alphabet and length
Let $R$ denote the cardinality of the character set the password is drawn from. For an alphanumeric password (uppercase + lowercase + digits):

$$
R = 26 + 26 + 10 = 62
$$

Let $L$ be the password length.

### Step 2 — Total possible passwords
Each position in the password is independently drawn from $R$ symbols. By the **multiplication principle of counting**:

$$
N = \underbrace{R \times R \times \cdots \times R}_{L \text{ times}} = R^{L}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to bits (entropy)
Since each of the $N$ outcomes is equally likely under a uniform random model, the Shannon entropy is:

$$
E = \log_2(N) = \log_2(R^{L}) = L \cdot \log_2(R)
$$

### Step 4 — Average number of attempts
On average, the correct password is found after half the keyspace is exhausted:

$$
N_{avg} = \frac{N}{2} = \frac{R^{L}}{2}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert attempts to time
Let the attacker execute $S$ attempts per second. The time in seconds is:

$$
T_{crack} = \frac{R^{L}}{2S}
$$

### Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example
Take $L = 8$, $R = 62$, and an attacker GPU rig with $S = 10^{10}$ guesses/sec (a high-end offline hash cracking rate, ~10 billion NTLM hashes/sec).

$$
N = 62^{8} = 218{,}340{,}105{,}584{,}896
$$

$$
E = 8 \cdot \log_2(62) \approx 8 \cdot 5.954 \approx 47.63 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
T_{crack} = \frac{62^{8}}{2 \times 10^{10}} \approx 10{,}917 \text{ seconds} \approx 3.03 \text{ hours}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A random 8-character alphanumeric password, despite being considered "strong" by laypeople, is broken in **~3 hours** by a modern offline attacker. This is exactly why NIST SP 800-63B now recommends a **minimum length of 8 characters** with checks against breached-password dictionaries, and prefers longer passphrases over complex short strings.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Effectiveness of Salting Against Rainbow Tables

### Step 1 — Rainbow table concept
A rainbow table is a precomputed mapping $H(p) \to p$ for all $p$ in a chosen dictionary $D$. If $|D| = 10^{8}$ entries, the table size is approximately $10^{8}$ rows of (hash, plaintext) pairs.

### Step 2 — Unsalted storage
Without a salt, if a user picks the password `Ktu@2024`, the stored value is $H(\text{Ktu@2024})$. An attacker downloads a rainbow table and performs a direct lookup.

### Step 3 — Salted storage
Now each user receives a unique random salt $s_i$ of length $k$ bits. The stored value becomes:

$$
h_i = H(p_i \;\Vert\; s_i)
$$

### Step 4 — Table size required
The attacker must now precompute tables for all combinations $(p, s)$ where $p \in D$ and $s \in \{0,1\}^{k}$. The required table size is:

$$
|D| \cdot 2^{k} \text{ rows}
$$

For $k = 128$ bits (typical salt), this becomes $10^{8} \cdot 3.4 \times 10^{38}$, completely infeasible to store.

### Step 5 — Per-user attack cost
The attacker falls back to per-user brute force. The work for user $i$ is:

$$
W_i = \frac{R^{L}}{2S}
$$

Salting thus converts a **global precomputation attack** into a **per-user attack**, fundamentally changing the economics of password cracking.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Authentication Factors vs. Threat Vectors

| Attack Type | Password Only | Password + OTP | Password + Biometric | Smart Card + PIN |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Brute Force (online) | Vulnerable | Mitigated | Mitigated | Strongly mitigated |
| Phishing | Highly vulnerable | Vulnerable (SMS-OTP) | Mitigated (liveness) | Mitigated |
| Shoulder Surfing | Vulnerable | Vulnerable | Vulnerable (face) | Mitigated |
| Token Theft | N/A | Vulnerable | N/A | Vulnerable |
| Biometric Spoof | N/A | N/A | Vulnerable | N/A |
| Replay Attack | Vulnerable | Mitigated (nonce) | Mitigated (liveness) | Mitigated (challenge) |
| **Composite Risk** | **HIGH** | **MEDIUM** | **MEDIUM-LOW** | **LOW** |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU exam answers, you should always cite **"Defense in Depth"** — the practice of combining multiple authentication factors so that compromise of one does not collapse the entire security boundary.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic User Authentication Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Initiates Login] --> B[Capture Identifier Username / UID]
    B --> C{Identifier Exists in DB?}
    C -- No --> Z1[Reject with Generic Error]
    C -- Yes --> D[Generate Server Nonce N]
    D --> E[Send Challenge to Client]
    E --> F[Client Submits Credential Password / OTP / Biometric]
    F --> G{Verify Against Stored Hash / Match Threshold}
    G -- Mismatch --> H[Increment Failure Counter]
    H --> I{Counter Exceeds Limit?}
    I -- Yes --> Z2[Lock Account / Trigger MFA Fallback]
    I -- No --> E
    G -- Match --> J[Create Session Token JWT / SAML]
    J --> K[Grant Access to Resource]
    J --> L[Write Audit Log Entry]
```

## 4.2 Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Factor1[Factor 1: Knowledge]
        A1[User enters Password]
    end
    subgraph Factor2[Factor 2: Possession]
        B1[Server sends OTP to Mobile]
        B2[User retrieves OTP from Authenticator App]
    end
    subgraph Factor3[Factor 3: Biometric]
        C1[User scans Fingerprint on Device]
    end
    A1 --> D{Primary Auth OK?}
    D -- No --> X1[Reject]
    D -- Yes --> E{Second Factor Selected}
    E --> B1
    E --> B2
    E --> C1
    B1 --> F{OTP Matches?}
    B2 --> F
    C1 --> F
    F -- No --> X2[Reject and Alert]
    F -- Yes --> G[Issue Signed Session Token]
    G --> H[Access Granted]
```

## 4.3 Kerberos Authentication Sequence (Simplified)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Client
    participant AS as Auth Server
    participant TGS as Ticket Granting Server
    participant V as Target Service

    U->>AS: 1. Request TGT using credentials
    AS-->>U: 2. Encrypted TGT plus Session Key
    U->>TGS: 3. Present TGT plus Request for Service Ticket
    TGS-->>U: 4. Service Ticket encrypted with Service Secret
    U->>V: 5. Present Service Ticket plus Authenticator
    V-->>U: 6. Mutual Authentication Established
    Note over U,V: All tickets time-bound by clock skew tolerance
```

## 4.4 Biometric Authentication Decision Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Biometric Sample Captured Fingerprint / Iris] --> Q[Quality Check]
    Q -- Poor Quality --> R1[Request Resample]
    Q -- Good Quality --> E[Feature Extraction Minutiae / Texture Map]
    E --> T[Template Comparison Against Stored Reference]
    T --> D{Score exceeds Threshold tau}
    D -- Yes --> G[Accept User Identity]
    D -- No --> RJ[Reject User]
    T --> M1[Update FAR FRR Statistics]
    T --> M2[Adapt Threshold tau via EER Algorithm]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECCT753]**
Differentiate between **Identification**, **Authentication**, and **Authorization** with a suitable example from a public-sector information system (e.g., Aadhaar-enabled eKYC).

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Identification** is the act of *declaring* an identity. Example: A citizen enters their Aadhaar number on the eKYC portal. *(1 mark)*
- **Authentication** is the act of *verifying* the claimed identity using credentials such as a biometric fingerprint match against UIDAI's central repository. *(1 mark)*
- **Authorization** is the act of *granting* specific access rights once identity is established, e.g., allowing the bank to read only the address and photograph fields, not the full demographic record. *(1 mark)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECCT753]**
List the **three classical authentication factors** and state one real-world example for each, as used in Indian banking systems.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Knowledge Factor (Something you know):** ATM PIN entered at an ATM terminal. *(1 mark)*
2. **Possession Factor (Something you have):** A one-time password (OTP) received on the registered mobile number during net-banking login. *(1 mark)*
3. **Biometric / Inherence Factor (Something you are):** Aadhaar Payment Bridge System (APBS) using fingerprint authentication at a Point of Sale (PoS) device. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Following KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Evaluation) pattern, Part B Module-Internal Choice offers two questions. Solve either.

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**Part (a)** *(7 marks)*
Explain **password-based authentication** in detail. Discuss password entropy, secure storage using hashing and salting, and the policies recommended by **NIST SP 800-63B** for password construction.

**Model Solution:**

1. **Definition & Operation:** Password-based authentication is a *knowledge-factor* mechanism in which the user submits a secret string that the system validates against a stored hash. *(1 mark)*

2. **Entropy Derivation:** The strength of a password is quantified by $E = L \cdot \log_2(R)$. For an 8-character ASCII password, $R=95$ and $E \approx 8 \times 6.57 \approx 52.6$ bits. *(2 marks)*

3. **Secure Storage — Hashing:** Plaintext passwords must never be stored. Instead, a one-way cryptographic hash such as **bcrypt**, **scrypt**, **Argon2**, or **PBKDF2** is computed and stored. The hash must be **slow** to compute so that brute force is expensive. *(2 marks)*

4. **Salting:** A per-user random salt of at least **128 bits** is concatenated with the password before hashing: $h = H(p \parallel s)$. This neutralises rainbow-table attacks and forces per-user brute force. *(1 mark)*

5. **NIST SP 800-63B Recommendations:**
   - Minimum length **8 characters**, maximum **64+**.
   - No mandatory character-class composition (e.g., "must contain uppercase, digit").
   - Check passwords against breached-password dictionaries (e.g., Have I Been Pwned).
   - Rate-limit failed attempts to **≤ 10** before lockout.
   - **Do not** force periodic password changes unless compromise is suspected. *(1 mark)*

**Part (b)** *(7 marks)*
Explain the **Kerberos authentication protocol**. List the participants and describe three of its ticket exchanges. Discuss the **clock-skew requirement** and its security significance.

**Model Solution:**

1. **Introduction:** Kerberos is a **ticket-based symmetric-key authentication protocol** developed at MIT, standardised in **RFC 4120**, and used widely in Microsoft Active Directory. It provides mutual authentication over an insecure network. *(1 mark)*

2. **Participants:**
   - **Client (C)** – the user/workstation.
   - **Authentication Server (AS)** – verifies client credentials.
   - **Ticket Granting Server (TGS)** – issues service tickets.
   - **Service Server (V)** – the resource the client wants to use.
   - **Key Distribution Center (KDC)** = AS + TGS. *(1 mark)*

3. **Three Ticket Exchanges:** *(3 marks)*
   - **AS-REQ / AS-REP:** Client sends identity to AS in cleartext; AS returns a **Ticket Granting Ticket (TGT)** encrypted with the client's secret key (derived from password) plus a session key $K_{C,TGS}$.
   - **TGS-REQ / TGS-REP:** Client presents the TGT to the TGS and requests a service ticket for V. TGS returns a **Service Ticket (ST)** encrypted with V's secret key plus a session key $K_{C,V}$.
   - **AP-REQ / AP-REP:** Client sends the ST to V. V decrypts it, extracts $K_{C,V}$, and sends back an authenticator proving it knows the same key. **Mutual authentication is now complete.**

4. **Clock-Skew Requirement:** All tickets include a **validity window** $[t_{start}, t_{end}]$. Kerberos mandates that the clock difference between any two principals not exceed a configurable maximum (default **5 minutes**). *(1 mark)*

5. **Security Significance:** Clock skew tolerance prevents **replay attacks** — a captured ticket cannot be reused indefinitely. It also defends against an attacker who could otherwise delay delivery. **However**, this is why Kerberos deployments must use **NTP (Network Time Protocol)** with authentication. *(1 mark)*

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**Part (a)** *(7 marks)*
Discuss **biometric authentication** as a security mechanism. Explain the **FAR**, **FRR**, and **EER** metrics, and describe the trade-off between them using a labelled ROC curve. Mention two real-world deployments in the Indian public sector.

**Model Solution:**

1. **Definition:** Biometric authentication verifies identity based on the user's **physiological** (fingerprint, iris, face) or **behavioural** (keystroke, gait) characteristics. *(1 mark)*

2. **Metrics:** *(3 marks)*
   - **FAR (False Acceptance Rate):** Probability that an *impostor* is wrongly accepted. $\text{FAR} = FA / IA$.
   - **FRR (False Rejection Rate):** Probability that a *genuine* user is wrongly rejected. $\text{FRR} = FR / GA$.
   - **EER (Equal Error Rate):** The threshold $\tau$ at which FAR = FRR. The lower the EER, the better the system.

3. **Trade-off & ROC Curve:** As the decision threshold $\tau$ is lowered, FAR increases (more impostors accepted) while FRR decreases (more genuine users accepted). The **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC)** plots FRR vs FAR; the optimal operating point is usually **just below the EER**, biased toward the application's risk profile. *(2 marks)*

4. **Indian Public-Sector Deployments:** *(1 mark)*
   - **Aadhaar Authentication (UIDAI):** Fingerprint and iris-based authentication for welfare disbursement (DBT).
   - **FASTag (NHAI):** RFID + ANPR-based vehicle identification for toll collection.
   - **eSign Service (CCA):** Aadhaar eKYC + OTP for legally valid digital signatures.

**Part (b)** *(7 marks)*
Explain **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)**. Differentiate between **2-Step Verification (2SV)** and **true 2FA**. Describe three attacks that MFA defends against and one attack that bypasses naïve OTP-based MFA.

**Model Solution:**

1. **Definition:** MFA is an authentication scheme that requires evidence from **two or more independent categories** of the three authentication factors (knowledge, possession, inherence). *(1 mark)*

2. **2SV vs. true 2FA:** *(2 marks)*
   - **2-Step Verification (2SV):** Two *consecutive* factors, but they may belong to the **same category** (e.g., password + security question — both are knowledge).
   - **True 2FA:** Two factors from **different categories** (e.g., password + OTP — knowledge + possession).

3. **Three Attacks Mitigated by MFA:** *(2 marks)*
   - **Credential stuffing / database breach reuse** — without the second factor, stolen passwords are useless.
   - **Phishing of static passwords** — even if a user types their password into a fake portal, the attacker cannot replay it without the OTP.
   - **Brute force** — knowledge of password alone is insufficient; physical/biometric possession is also required.

4. **Attack that Bypasses Naïve SMS-OTP MFA:** *(2 marks)*
   - **SIM Swap Fraud:** The attacker social-engineers the telecom provider to transfer the victim's mobile number to a new SIM, then intercepts the OTP.
   - **SS7 Network Exploits:** Interception of SMS routed over the vulnerable SS7 signalling protocol.
   - **Real-time phishing proxies (AiTM):** Attackers proxy the entire session in real time, relaying credentials and OTP to the legitimate site instantly.

5. **Countermeasure:** Use **app-based TOTP** (Google Authenticator) or **FIDO2/WebAuthn hardware keys** (YubiKey), which are immune to SIM swap and phishing proxy attacks. *(Bonus point)*

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Evaluations:**
> 1. **Conflating Authentication with Authorization** — Examiners explicitly deduct marks if the student writes "authentication gives access" without distinguishing verification of identity from granting of privileges.
> 2. **Writing passwords in plaintext in design diagrams** — Always depict the *hashed* credential in storage diagrams, not the cleartext.
> 3. **Omitting quantitative justification** — A 7-mark question on biometrics that discusses FAR/FRR *without the formula* typically receives only 4–5 marks.
> 4. **Missing Indian regulatory context** — Where the question is public-sector oriented, citing **IT Act 2000, DPDP Act 2023, RBI Master Direction 2022, or CERT-In Directions 2022** elevates the answer to full marks.
> 5. **Skipping the threat model** — Authentication answers that do not name at least one attack (brute force, phishing, replay, MITM, SIM swap) are considered incomplete.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Authentication ≠ Authorization.** Authentication is verification of *identity*; authorization is granting of *rights*.
- The **Triple-A framework** = Authentication, Authorization, Accounting.
- **Three classical factors:** Knowledge (password), Possession (token/OTP), Biometric (fingerprint/face). Combine them to get **MFA**.
- **Password entropy** $E = L \cdot \log_2(R)$; keyspace $N = R^{L}$; average crack time $T = R^{L} / (2S)$.
- **Storage must be hashed and salted** using slow KDFs (bcrypt, scrypt, Argon2). Never store plaintext.
- **NIST SP 800-63B** mandates minimum 8 characters, rejects forced periodic rotation, requires breached-password checks.
- **Biometric evaluation metrics:** FAR, FRR, and the **Equal Error Rate (EER)** — the lower the EER, the better.
- **Kerberos** is a ticket-based, symmetric-key protocol using AS, TGS, and the KDC; requires **NTP-synced clocks within 5 minutes**.
- **MFA defends against** credential stuffing, password phishing, and brute force; **does not defend against** SIM-swap, SS7 interception, and AiTM phishing — use FIDO2/WebAuthn to harden.
- **Indian regulatory anchors** to cite: IT Act 2000 (amended 2008), DPDP Act 2023, RBI Digital Lending Directions 2022, CERT-In Directions April 2022.
- **Always end your answer with a threat model** — examiners reward students who think like an attacker.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Designing Trusted O.S: Security Policies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT753 - INFORMATION SECURITY IN PUBLIC AND SECTORS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Designing Trusted O.S: Security Policies # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a Security Policy? A **Security Policy** is a formal, unambiguous, and mathematically precise statement of the rules and regulations that govern how an organization manages, protects, and distributes its sensitive information and computing resources. In the context of a **Trusted Operating System (Trusted OS)**, the security policy is the foundational blueprint that dictates **who** can do **what**, **when**, and under **which conditions** to system resources. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A security policy is an abstraction that defines the set of authorized states of a system, the set of authorized state transitions, and the actions authorized for various agents (subjects) on various objects. It separates *policy* (the "what") from *mechanism* (the "how"). ### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Security (CIA Triad) Every security policy in a Trusted OS is fundamentally designed to protect three core properties: 1. **Confidentiality** — Preventing unauthorized disclosure of information. 2. **Integrity** — Preventing unauthorized modification of information. 3. **Availability** — Ensuring authorized access when required. > [!NOTE] > A **Trusted System** is one whose failure would break the system's security policy. **Trust** is the *assurance* that the system will behave as expected, while a **Trusted OS** is an OS that provides sufficient levels of security to enforce a security policy consistently. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Bank Vault Imagine a bank vault as a **Trusted Operating System**: - The **Security Policy** is the bank's *operating manual* — it states which employees (subjects) can access which safe deposit boxes (objects), at what times, and with what combinations of tools. - The **Mechanism** is the *physical lock, the biometric scanner, the camera, and the keycards* — these are the tools that enforce the manual. - The **Trust** is the *guarantee from the bank* that the vault will not be broken into, derived from the vault's design, testing, and certification. A bank can have strong locks (mechanisms), but without a clear policy defining who is allowed inside, the system is fundamentally broken. This is why the **policy comes first**, and the trusted OS is built *around* it. ### 1.4 Types of Security Policies (KTU High-Yield Classification) | Policy Class | Primary Goal | Example Model | |--------------|--------------|---------------| | **Confidentiality Policy** | Prevent unauthorized *read* access | Bell-LaPadula Model | | **Integrity Policy** | Prevent unauthorized *write* access | Biba Model, Clark-Wilson Model | | **Hybrid Policy** | Combine confidentiality and integrity | Chinese Wall Model | | **Access Control Policy** | Define who can access what | DAC, MAC, RBAC | ### 1.5 Why Security Policies Matter in OS Design A Trusted OS is designed using a layered architecture. At the core of this architecture lies the **Reference Monitor** — a conceptual abstract machine that mediates every access by a subject to an object. The Reference Monitor enforces the security policy without exception. Its three mandatory properties (the *Trusted Computing Base* rules) are: 1. **Complete Mediation** — Every access is checked, with no bypasses. 2. **Tamper-Proof** — The monitor itself cannot be modified by malicious entities. 3. **Verifiable** — The monitor is small enough to be exhaustively tested and verified. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Policy-Mechanism Separation Principle A critical concept in Trusted OS design is the **separation of policy and mechanism**: - **Policy** = The *abstract set of rules* defining what is allowed. (e.g., "A subject can only read objects at its level or below.") - **Mechanism** = The *concrete implementation* that enforces the policy. (e.g., Access Control Lists, capability lists, encryption.) > [!TIP] > KTU examiners often ask: *"Why separate policy from mechanism?"* The answer is **flexibility**. A well-designed OS can swap mechanisms (e.g., switch from password authentication to biometric) without rewriting the entire policy. ## 2.2 The Bell-LaPadula (BLP) Model — Confidentiality Policy The **Bell-LaPadula Model**, developed by David Bell and Leonard LaPadula in **1973** for the U.S. Department of Defense, is the gold standard for enforcing **confidentiality**. It is based on the military classification hierarchy. ### 2.2.1 Security Levels (Ordered Set) The model assumes a linearly ordered set of security levels: $$L = \{\text{Unclassified} < \text{Confidential} < \text{Secret} < \text{Top Secret}\}$$ Formally, security levels form a **lattice** $(\mathcal{L}, \leq)$ where $\leq$ is a partial order. ### 2.2.2 The Three Core Rules of BLP | Rule | Statement | Purpose | |------|-----------|---------| | **Simple Security Property (No Read Up — NRU)** | A subject $S$ can read object $O$ **only if** $\text{level}(S) \geq \text{level}(O)$ | Prevent unauthorized disclosure | | **Star Property (No Write Down — NWD)** | A subject $S$ can write to object $O$ **only if** $\text{level}(S) \leq \text{level}(O)$ | Prevent information leakage to lower levels | | **Discretionary Security Property (ds-property)** | Access is also restricted by an access matrix $(S, O, A)$ | Allow owner-controlled sharing | ### 2.2.3 Formal BLP Access Decision The reference monitor grants a request $(S, O, A)$ — where $A$ is an access mode — if and only if: $$ \text{Grant}(S, O, A) \iff \begin{cases} A = \text{read} \implies \text{level}(S) \geq \text{level}(O) & \text{(Simple Security)} \\ A = \text{write} \implies \text{level}(S) \leq \text{level}(O) & \text{(Star Property)} \\ (S, O, A) \in M_t & \text{(Discretionary Matrix)} \end{cases} $$ where $M_t$ is the access control matrix at time $t$. ### 2.2.4 Tranquility in BLP - **Strong Tranquility**: Security labels of subjects/objects never change while the system is running. - **Weak Tranquility**: Labels can change, but never in a way that violates the security policy. > [!WARNING] > **Covert Channels**: BLP does not address *covert channels* (e.g., timing or storage side-channels). A high-level subject signaling a low-level subject by modulating CPU usage is *not* detected by BLP — this is a classic exam pitfall. ## 2.3 The Biba Model — Integrity Policy The **Biba Model**, proposed by Kenneth Biba in **1977**, mirrors BLP but focuses on **integrity** instead of confidentiality. It protects data from unauthorized *modification*. ### 2.3.1 Integrity Levels Like BLP, Biba uses an ordered set of integrity levels: $$I = \{\text{Untrusted} < \text{Somewhat Trusted} < \text{Trusted} < \text{Critical}\}$$ ### 2.3.2 The Three Biba Rules (Strict Model) | Rule | Statement | Purpose | |------|-----------|---------| | **No Read Down** | $S$ can read $O$ only if $\text{integrity}(S) \leq \text{integrity}(O)$ | Prevent low-integrity data from polluting $S$'s decisions | | **No Write Up** | $S$ can write $O$ only if $\text{integrity}(S) \geq \text{integrity}(O)$ | Prevent $S$ from corrupting high-integrity data | | **Invocation Property** | $S$ can invoke $S'$ only if $\text{integrity}(S) \leq \text{integrity}(S')$ | Prevent untrusted programs from invoking trusted ones | ## 2.4 The Clark-Wilson Model — Commercial Integrity Proposed by David Clark and David Wilson in **1987**, this model targets **commercial** data integrity needs (banking, payroll) where Biba's pure lattice is too rigid. ### 2.4.1 Two Key Concepts - **Constrained Data Items (CDIs)** — Data items whose integrity must be preserved. - **Unconstrained Data Items (UDIs)** — Untrusted input data (e.g., user input). - **Transformation Procedures (TPs)** — Well-formed programs that manipulate CDIs. - **Integrity Verification Procedures (IVPs)** — Procedures that confirm CDIs remain in a valid state. ### 2.4.2 Clark-Wilson Certification & Enforcement Rules $$ \begin{aligned} \text{C1:} \quad &\text{All IVPs must ensure that all CDIs are in a valid state at the time of IVP execution.} \\ \text{C2:} \quad &\text{All TPs must be certified to be valid (i.e., preserve integrity).} \\ \text{E1:} \quad &\text{The system must maintain a list of certified TPs and enforce their use on CDIs.} \\ \text{E2:} \quad &\text>The system must associate each user with allowed TPs on specific CDIs.} \\ \text{E3:} \quad &\text{The system must authenticate each user requesting a TP.} \\ \text{E4:} \quad &\text{Only the agent certified for a TP may modify that TP's list.} \end{aligned} $$ ## 2.5 The Chinese Wall Model — Hybrid Policy Developed by **Brewer and Nash** in **1989**, the Chinese Wall Model addresses **conflict of interest** in consultancy and financial sectors. ### 2.5.1 Structure - **Objects** = *Individual company records* (e.g., files about Bank A, Bank B). - **Company Dataset (CD)** = All objects concerning a single company. - **Conflict of Interest Class (COI)** = All CDs whose companies compete (e.g., all banks). ### 2.5.2 The SS (Simple Security) Property A subject $S$ can read object $O$ if and only if: $$ \text{Access}(S, O) \iff \begin{cases} O \in \text{previouslyAccessed}(S) & \text{(Same dataset already accessed)} \\ \text{OR} & \\ O \notin \text{COI}(O') \text{ for all } O' \text{ previously accessed by } S \end{cases} $$ > [!NOTE] > Once $S$ reads a record from Bank A, $S$ **cannot** read any record from Bank B or Bank C, because all banks belong to the same COI class. This is a **dynamic** policy — access rights change based on history. ## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Direction | |---------|----------------|-----------| | **BLP No Read Up (NRU)** | $\text{level}(S) \geq \text{level}(O)$ | Subject dominates Object for read | | **BLP No Write Down (NWD)** | $\text{level}(S) \leq \text{level}(O)$ | Subject dominated by Object for write | | **Biba No Read Down** | $\text{integrity}(S) \leq \text{integrity}(O)$ | Low integrity can read high integrity data | | **Biba No Write Up** | $\text{integrity}(S) \geq \text{integrity}(O)$ | High integrity can write low integrity data | | **Clark-Wilson Core** | $\text{TP}: \text{CDI}_i \rightarrow \text{CDI}_j$ only if TP certified | Validated transformation | | **Chinese Wall SS** | $O \in \text{Hist}(S) \lor \forall O' \in \text{Hist}(S): \text{COI}(O') \neq \text{COI}(O)$ | No conflict-of-interest violation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mnemonic for BLP**: "**No Read Up, No Write Down**" — read *up* would leak secrets; write *down* would leak secrets. ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility These policies form the foundation of real-world trusted systems: - **Trusted Solaris, SELinux, FreeBSD MAC** — Implement BLP-style mandatory access control. - **TrustedBSD MAC Framework** — Provides pluggable policy modules (Biba, BLP, MLS). - **Oracle Label Security (OLS)** — Industrial implementation of BLP for database rows. - **Healthcare (HIPAA)** — Biba-like integrity for medical records. - **Financial Trading Systems** — Chinese Wall to prevent insider trading conflicts. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Bell-LaPadula Access Decision **Problem:** Given the security levels Top Secret (TS) > Secret (S) > Confidential (C) > Unclassified (U), determine whether subject **Alice (Secret clearance)** can perform the following operations: - (a) Read a file at **Confidential** level - (b) Write to a file at **Top Secret** level - (c) Read a file at **Secret** level - (d) Write to a file at **Confidential** level ### Solution Let $\text{level}(\text{Alice}) = \text{Secret}$. Let the levels be encoded as: $$\text{Unclassified} = 0, \quad \text{Confidential} = 1, \quad \text{Secret} = 2, \quad \text{Top Secret} = 3$$ So $\text{level}(\text{Alice}) = 2$. **Part (a):** Read a Confidential file (level = 1) Apply the **Simple Security Property (No Read Up)**: $$\text{level}(\text{Alice}) \geq \text{level}(O)$$ $$2 \geq 1 \quad \checkmark$$ **Result:** Access **GRANTED**. **Part (b):** Write to a Top Secret file (level = 3) Apply the **Star Property (No Write Down)**: $$\text{level}(\text{Alice}) \leq \text{level}(O)$$ $$2 \leq 3 \quad \checkmark$$ **Result:** Access **GRANTED**. **Part (c):** Read a Secret file (level = 2) Apply the Simple Security Property: $$2 \geq 2 \quad \checkmark$$ **Result:** Access **GRANTED** (equal levels satisfy $\geq$). **Part (d):** Write to a Confidential file (level = 1) Apply the Star Property: $$2 \leq 1 \quad \times$$ **Result:** Access **DENIED** — Alice cannot write down. > [!NOTE] > Part (b) is the most counter-intuitive result. Alice (Secret) can write to a Top Secret file because BLP's *-property prevents *information leakage* downward. If she wrote to a *lower* level, the Top Secret data she knows could leak down — a Top Secret subject writing to a Secret file is allowed because the *content* is at most Secret. But Secret → Top Secret write is permitted because the new file's content is only as sensitive as Alice's clearance. ## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Biba Integrity Decision **Problem:** Three programs have integrity levels: - $P_1$: Integrity 5 (Critical) - $P_2$: Integrity 3 (Trusted) - $P_3$: Integrity 1 (Untrusted) Determine whether each of the following operations is allowed under strict Biba: - (a) $P_1$ reads data from $P_2$ - (b) $P_2$ writes to $P_1$ - (c) $P_3$ invokes $P_1$ ### Solution **Part (a):** $P_1$ reads from $P_2$ (i.e., $P_1$ reads data, acting as a subject on $P_2$ acting as an object) Apply **No Read Down**: $$\text{integrity}(P_1) \leq \text{integrity}(P_2)$$ $$5 \leq 3 \quad \times$$ **Result:** **DENIED** — a high-integrity program must not be contaminated by reading lower-integrity data. **Part (b):** $P_2$ writes to $P_1$ Apply **No Write Up**: $$\text{integrity}(P_2) \geq \text{integrity}(P_1)$$ $$3 \geq 5 \quad \times$$ **Result:** **DENIED** — a lower-integrity program must not corrupt higher-integrity data. **Part (c):** $P_3$ invokes $P_1$ Apply the **Invocation Property**: $$\text{integrity}(P_3) \leq \text{integrity}(P_1)$$ $$1 \leq 5 \quad \checkmark$$ **Result:** **GRANTED** — untrusted code can invoke trusted code (this is a one-way rule to prevent trusted code from being tricked). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** In Biba, the inequalities look *inverted* compared to BLP. Students often confuse them. Remember: **Biba = Integrity** (high is good, so subjects of high integrity are *pristine* and should not read or write to "dirty" data). ## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Chinese Wall — Conflict of Interest Resolution **Problem:** A consulting firm has three COI classes: - **Banks:** {BankA-Records, BankB-Records, BankC-Records} - **Oil Companies:** {OilCoX-Records, OilCoY-Records} - **Tech Companies:** {TechP-Records, TechQ-Records} Consultant **Ravi** first reads **BankA-Records**. Determine Ravi's access to: - (a) BankB-Records - (b) OilCoX-Records - (c) BankA-Records (another record from the same company) ### Solution After Ravi reads BankA-Records, his history is: $$\text{Hist}(R) = \{\text{BankA-Records}\}$$ **Part (a):** Access to BankB-Records Both BankA and BankB belong to the same COI class (Banks). So: $$O' = \text{BankA-Records} \in \text{Hist}(R), \quad \text{COI}(O') = \text{COI}(O) = \text{Banks}$$ The first disjunct of the SS-property fails because the new $O$ is *not* in $\text{Hist}(R)$, and the second disjunct also fails because $O' \in \text{Hist}(R)$ shares the same COI. **Result:** **DENIED** — Ravi now has a conflict of interest with BankB. **Part (b):** Access to OilCoX-Records Oil companies are in a *different* COI class from banks. So: $$\text{COI}(\text{OilCoX}) = \text{Oil} \neq \text{Banks} = \text{COI}(O') \text{ for all } O' \in \text{Hist}(R)$$ **Result:** **GRANTED** — Ravi can read OilCoX-Records (no conflict yet). **Part (c):** Access to another BankA-Record This is the same company, so: $$O \in \text{COI}(\text{BankA-Records}) \text{ and is in } \text{Hist}(R)$$ The first disjunct is satisfied. **Result:** **GRANTED** — Ravi can read more BankA-Records freely. ## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix of Trusted OS Security Policies $$ \begin{aligned} \textbf{Feature} \quad &\text{Bell-LaPadula} \quad \text{Biba} \quad \text{Clark-Wilson} \quad \text{Chinese Wall} \\ \text{Primary Goal} \quad &\text{Confidentiality} \quad \text{Integrity} \quad \text{Integrity} \quad \text{Both} \\ \text{Ordered Lattice?} \quad &\text{Yes} \quad \text{Yes} \quad \text{No (separation of duty)} \quad \text{No (COI classes)} \\ \text{Static or Dynamic?} \quad &\text{Static} \quad \text{Static} \quad \text{Static} \quad \text{Dynamic} \\ \text{Read Rule} \quad &\text{No Read Up} \quad \text{No Read Down} \quad \text{TP-based} \quad \text{COI-based} \\ \text{Write Rule} \quad &\text{No Write Down} \quad \text{No Write Up} \quad \text{TP-validated} \quad \text{COI-based} \\ \text{Best For} \quad &\text{Military/Defense} \quad \text{Military Integrity} \quad \text{Banking/Commerce} \quad \text{Consulting/Finance} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.5 Derivation of the BLP Basic Security Theorem The **Basic Security Theorem (BST)** of Bell-LaPadula states: > *A system is secure if and only if its initial state is secure, and all possible state transitions preserve security.* Formally, for a state $v$ and a transition function $W$: $$ \text{Secure}(v_0) \land \forall v, a: (v, a, v^*) \in W \land \text{Secure}(v) \implies \text{Secure}(v^*) $$ This is proven by induction on the sequence of state transitions: - **Base Case:** $v_0$ is a secure state by initial assumption. - **Inductive Step:** Assume $v_i$ is secure. The transition rules (defined by the system's operation model) explicitly prevent any transition that would violate NRU or NWD, hence $v_{i+1}$ is also secure. $$\text{By induction,} \quad \forall n \in \mathbb{N}, \quad v_n \text{ is secure.}$$ ## 3.6 Lattice of Security Labels (Multi-Dimensional BLP) For modern systems, BLP extends to a **multi-dimensional lattice**. A label is a tuple of components (e.g., $(classification, compartment)$). The dominance relation is: $$ L_1 = (c_1, \{K_1\}) \geq L_2 = (c_2, \{K_2\}) \iff c_1 \geq c_2 \land K_2 \subseteq K_1 $$ For example, label $(Secret, \{Crypto, Nuclear\}) \geq (Secret, \{Crypto\})$ because: $$Secret \geq Secret \quad \checkmark, \quad \{Crypto\} \subseteq \{Crypto, Nuclear\} \quad \checkmark$$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Reference Monitor Architecture (Trusted OS Core) ```mermaid flowchart TB User["User / Application"] -->|"Access Request (S, O, A)"| RM RM{"Reference Monitor"} SP["Security Policy Database
(BLP / Biba / Clark-Wilson)"] AM["Access Control Matrix
(Subjects x Objects)"] AuditLog["Audit & Logging Subsystem"] Obj["Protected Object (File/Resource)"] RM -->|"Query Rules"| SP RM -->|"Check Matrix"| AM RM -->|"If Allowed"| Obj RM -->|"Log Every Decision"| AuditLog style User fill:#1e90ff,stroke:#000,color:#fff style RM fill:#ff6347,stroke:#000,color:#fff style SP fill:#90ee90,stroke:#000,color:#000 style AM fill:#ffd700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style AuditLog fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#000,color:#000 style Obj fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` **Interpretation:** Every access request from any subject must pass through the **Reference Monitor (RM)**, which consults the **Security Policy** and the **Access Control Matrix**. The decision is logged in the **Audit Log** for forensic analysis. This implements the three mandatory Reference Monitor properties: **Complete Mediation**, **Tamper-Proof**, and **Verifiable**. ## 4.2 Bell-LaPadula Information Flow Lattice ```mermaid flowchart TB TS["TOP SECRET
(Level 3)"] -->|"Read/Write"| S S["SECRET
(Level 2)"] -->|"Read/Write"| C C["CONFIDENTIAL
(Level 1)"] -->|"Read/Write"| U U["UNCLASSIFIED
(Level 0)"] NRU["NO READ UP
NRU: level S greater-than level O"] NWD["NO WRITE DOWN
NWD: level S less-than level O"] style TS fill:#8b0000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style S fill:#b22222,stroke:#000,color:#fff style C fill:#cd5c5c,stroke:#000,color:#000 style U fill:#f0e68c,stroke:#000,color:#000 style NRU fill:#00ced1,stroke:#000,color:#000 style NWD fill:#9370db,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` **Interpretation:** The linear lattice shows allowed flows under BLP. A **Top Secret** subject can read **Secret, Confidential, Unclassified** files (No Read Up allows reading *downward* the lattice), but writing *down* to a lower level is **denied** (No Write Down). ## 4.3 Biba Integrity Lattice (Inverted Direction) ```mermaid flowchart TB Trust["TRUSTED
(Integrity 3)"] -->|"No Read Down"| Mid Mid["SOMEWHAT TRUSTED
(Integrity 2)"] -->|"No Read Down"| Low Low["UNTRUSTED
(Integrity 1)"] Block1["BLOCK: High integrity subject
reading low integrity data"] Block2["BLOCK: Low integrity subject
writing to high integrity data"] style Trust fill:#006400,stroke:#000,color:#fff style Mid fill:#228b22,stroke:#000,color:#fff style Low fill:#90ee90,stroke:#000,color:#000 style Block1 fill:#ff4500,stroke:#000,color:#fff style Block2 fill:#ff8c00,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` **Interpretation:** In Biba, the "trusted" subjects sit at the *top* of the integrity lattice, and they must *not* read downward (because that would taint their pristine state) and must *not* allow untrusted subjects to write upward (because that would corrupt their data). ## 4.4 Chinese Wall Access Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB Start["Subject S requests Object O"] --> Check1{"Is O in Hist S?"} Check1 -->|"YES"| Allow["ALLOW ACCESS"] Check1 -->|"NO"| Check2{"Does COI O overlap
with any CD in Hist S?"} Check2 -->|"NO"| Allow Check2 -->|"YES"| Deny["DENY ACCESS
(Conflict of Interest)"] Update["Update Hist S = Hist S plus O"] Allow --> Update Deny --> Audit["Log Denial Event"] style Start fill:#4682b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff style Allow fill:#32cd32,stroke:#000,color:#000 style Deny fill:#dc143c,stroke:#000,color:#fff style Update fill:#ffd700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style Audit fill:#ff69b4,stroke:#000,color:#000 style Check1 fill:#ffa500,stroke:#000,color:#000 style Check2 fill:#ffa500,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` **Interpretation:** The Chinese Wall decision algorithm is a two-step process. First, if the object is in the subject's history, allow. Second, if no conflict-of-interest exists with previously accessed datasets, allow. Otherwise, deny. ## 4.5 Clark-Wilson Data Flow Model ```mermaid flowchart LR UDI["Untrusted Input (UDI)"] -->|"Validated by"| IVP1["Integrity Verification
Procedure (IVP)"] IVP1 -->|"Promoted to"| CDI["Constrained Data Item (CDI)"] CDI1["CDI state i"] -->|"Transformation Procedure TP"| CDI2["CDI state j"] CDI2 -->|"Verified by"| IVP2["IVP check"] IVP2 -->|"Valid State"| Continue["Continue Operations"] IVP2 -->|"Invalid State"| Alarm["Integrity Violation Alarm"] style UDI fill:#ff6347,stroke:#000,color:#fff style IVP1 fill:#90ee90,stroke:#000,color:#000 style CDI fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#000,color:#000 style CDI1 fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#000,color:#000 style CDI2 fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#000,color:#000 style IVP2 fill:#90ee90,stroke:#000,color:#000 style Continue fill:#32cd32,stroke:#000,color:#000 style Alarm fill:#dc143c,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` **Interpretation:** Clark-Wilson enforces integrity through certified **Transformation Procedures (TPs)** that move CDIs between valid states, and **Integrity Verification Procedures (IVPs)** that confirm the CDIs remain valid. Untrusted input (UDI) is only allowed in if validated by an IVP. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define a security policy. Differentiate between policy and mechanism with a suitable example.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **security policy** is a formal statement of the rules and practices that regulate how an organization manages, protects, and distributes sensitive information. In a Trusted OS context, it is an abstract specification of authorized system states and transitions. **Difference:** | Aspect | Policy | Mechanism | |--------|--------|-----------| | **Definition** | Abstract *what* is allowed | Concrete *how* it is enforced | | **Nature** | High-level rules | Low-level implementation | | **Example** | "Only managers can access payroll data" | Password authentication, Access Control Lists, encryption | | **Flexibility** | Can be changed without altering code | Tied to specific implementation | (1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the difference with example) --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Explain the "Simple Security Property" and "Star Property" of the Bell-LaPadula Model.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Simple Security Property (No Read Up — NRU):** A subject $S$ is allowed read access to an object $O$ only if the security level of $S$ is *greater than or equal to* the security level of $O$. Formally, $\text{level}(S) \geq \text{level}(O)$. This prevents unauthorized *disclosure* of information from a higher-classified object to a lower-cleared subject. (1.5 marks) 2. **Star Property (No Write Down — NWD):** A subject $S$ is allowed write access to an object $O$ only if the security level of $S$ is *less than or equal to* the security level of $O$. Formally, $\text{level}(S) \leq \text{level}(O)$. This prevents a high-cleared subject from *writing* sensitive information down to a lower-classified object, which would be a leakage path. (1.5 marks) --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — With Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Explain the **Bell-LaPadula Model** in detail. Discuss its three rules, the concept of tranquility, and its limitations. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Describe the **Biba Model** for integrity. Compare and contrast it with Bell-LaPadula using a comparative table. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution for (a): **Introduction (1 mark):** The Bell-LaPadula (BLP) model, introduced in 1973, was the first mathematical model for enforcing *confidentiality* in a multi-level security (MLS) system. It is a state machine model where states are tuples $(b, M, f, H)$ — current access set, access matrix, security level function, and current history. **The Three Rules (4 marks):** - **Simple Security Property (No Read Up):** $\text{level}(S) \geq \text{level}(O)$ for any read access. Prevents leakage of classified information. - **Star Property (No Write Down):** $\text{level}(S) \leq \text{level}(O)$ for any write access. Prevents a high-level subject from downgrading data. - **Discretionary Security Property:** Access must also be granted in the access control matrix $M$, allowing fine-grained, owner-controlled sharing. **Tranquility (1 mark):** - **Strong Tranquility:** Labels never change during runtime — provides the strongest security guarantee. - **Weak Tranquility:** Labels may change but never in a way that violates the security policy — more practical for real systems. **Limitations (1 mark):** 1. No protection against **covert channels** (timing, storage). 2. Does not address **integrity** at all. 3. **No Read Down** is not enforced, which is sometimes useful (e.g., system-wide announcements to all clearances). 4. The \*-property can be too restrictive for benign downgrading (necessitating trusted subjects). #### Model Solution for (b): **Biba Model Introduction (2 marks):** Proposed by Kenneth Biba in 1977, the Biba Model addresses *integrity* concerns. It uses an integrity lattice analogous to BLP's security lattice, but the rules are inverted because Biba protects data from being *corrupted*, not from being *disclosed*. **Three Rules of Strict Biba (3 marks):** - **No Read Down:** $\text{integrity}(S) \leq \text{integrity}(O)$ — A high-integrity subject must not read low-integrity data (to avoid contamination). - **No Write Up:** $\text{integrity}(S) \geq \text{integrity}(O)$ — A low-integrity subject must not modify high-integrity data. - **Invocation Property:** A subject may invoke another subject only if the invoking subject's integrity is *less than or equal to* the invoked subject's. **Comparison Table (2 marks):** | Feature | Bell-LaPadula | Biba | |---------|---------------|------| | **Goal** | Confidentiality | Integrity | | **Direction of "No Read"** | No Read Up | No Read Down | | **Direction of "No Write"** | No Write Down | No Write Up | | **Assumption** | High level = sensitive | High level = trustworthy | | **Application** | Military data classification | Trusted software, system files | | **Weakness** | No integrity, covert channels | No confidentiality, can be over-restrictive | --- ### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]` **(a)** Explain the **Clark-Wilson Model** for commercial integrity. List its certification and enforcement rules. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Describe the **Chinese Wall Model**. How does it differ from traditional access control models? *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution for (a): **Introduction (1 mark):** Developed by David Clark and David Wilson in 1987, the Clark-Wilson Model addresses *commercial* data integrity (banking, payroll) where Biba's pure lattice model is too rigid. It enforces integrity through well-formed transactions and separation of duty. **Key Concepts (2 marks):** - **CDI (Constrained Data Item):** Data whose integrity is protected. - **UDI (Unconstrained Data Item):** Untrusted input data. - **TP (Transformation Procedure):** Programs that manipulate CDIs in a certified way. - **IVP (Integrity Verification Procedure):** Verifies that CDIs remain in valid states. **Certification Rules (C-Rules) (2 marks):** - **C1:** All IVPs must ensure that all CDIs are in a valid state when run. - **C2:** All TPs must be certified to be valid, i.e., they preserve the integrity of CDIs. **Enforcement Rules (E-Rules) (2 marks):** - **E1:** The system must maintain and enforce the list of certified TPs for CDIs. - **E2:** The system must maintain a list of (User, TP, CDI) triples — enforcing *separation of duty*. - **E3:** The system must authenticate every user attempting to execute a TP. - **E4:** Only the agent authorized for a TP can change that TP's association list. #### Model Solution for (b): **Chinese Wall Introduction (1 mark):** The Chinese Wall Model, proposed by Brewer and Nash (1989), is a **hybrid policy** designed for *consultancy* and *financial* domains where **conflict of interest** must be dynamically prevented. **Structural Hierarchy (2 marks):** - **Objects:** Individual company records (files). - **Company Dataset (CD):** All records about one company. - **Conflict of Interest Class (COI):** All CDs whose companies are in competition (e.g., all banks). **SS-Property (2 marks):** A subject $S$ may read an object $O$ if either: 1. $O$ belongs to a CD already accessed by $S$, OR 2. $O$ belongs to a COI class disjoint from all CDs already accessed by $S$. Once $S$ has read any record from BankA's CD, $S$ is *dynamically* forbidden from reading *any* record from BankB or BankC (all in the Banks COI class). **Differences from Traditional Models (2 marks):** | Aspect | Traditional (BLP/Biba) | Chinese Wall | |--------|------------------------|--------------| | **Access Type** | Static (based on labels) | Dynamic (based on history) | | **Goal** | Confidentiality or integrity | Both, plus conflict-of-interest | | **Time Dependency** | None | Critical — rights change as user accesses data | | **Policy Basis** | Labels and clearances | COI classes and datasets | | **Application** | Military, OS | Consulting, legal, financial advisory | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write that the Chinese Wall is "static" or compare it only with BLP. The model is *fundamentally dynamic* — failure to mention this costs 2–3 marks. Also, students forget that the Chinese Wall's history is **per-subject**, not system-wide, and that the policy is for a **session**, not permanent. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Security Policy** is a formal, abstract specification of authorized system states. It is distinct from **mechanism** (implementation). - **Bell-LaPadula (BLP)** = **Confidentiality**. Rules: *No Read Up (NRU)* and *No Write Down (NWD)*. Designed for military MLS systems. - **Biba** = **Integrity**. Rules: *No Read Down* and *No Write Up*. The integrity lattice is conceptually *inverted* from BLP's confidentiality lattice. - **Clark-Wilson** = **Commercial Integrity** using CDIs, UDIs, TPs, and IVPs. Enforces *separation of duty* and *well-formed transactions*. Used in banking/payroll. - **Chinese Wall** = **Hybrid + Dynamic** policy based on Conflict-of-Interest (COI) classes. Access rights change based on the subject's read history. Used in consulting/finance. - **Reference Monitor** mediates every access; must be *complete, tamper-proof, and verifiable* — these three properties are non-negotiable for any Trusted OS. - **Tranquility** in BLP: Strong = labels never change; Weak = labels change but never in a way that violates the policy. - **Basic Security Theorem (BST)**: A system is secure iff the initial state is secure and all transitions preserve security (proven by induction). - **Covert Channels** are NOT detected by BLP — students often lose marks by assuming BLP covers all attacks. - **Mandatory Access Control (MAC)** in modern OSes (e.g., SELinux, TrustedBSD) is the practical implementation of BLP-style policies. - **Discretionary Access Control (DAC)** is implemented in UNIX file permissions; **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)** is common in commercial systems. - **Lattice-based** security models support multi-dimensional labels (e.g., classification + compartments), where dominance is checked component-wise and subset containment. - **Policy-Mechanism Separation** allows the same policy to be implemented by different mechanisms, increasing flexibility and reducing redevelopment cost. - **Trusted Computing Base (TCB)** = the sum of all hardware, software, and firmware components that enforce the security policy. Smaller TCB = more verifiable.

topics.title: Models of Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT753 - INFORMATION SECURITY IN PUBLIC AND PRIVATE SECTORS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Models of Security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a "Model of Security"?

In the context of **Information Security in Public and Private Sectors (PECCT753)**, a **Security Model** is a formal, abstract, and mathematically/logically precise representation of a security policy. It defines the entities (subjects, objects), their relationships, the rules governing access, and the security goals that must be preserved within an information system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Approved):**
> A **Security Model** is a precise statement of the **security policy** a system must enforce, expressed in terms of access permissions, states, and transitions. It bridges the gap between abstract security policy (what should be protected) and the system implementation (how it is protected).

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Bank Vault"

Imagine a **national bank vault**:
- The **Security Policy** is the high-level rule: *"Only authorized personnel may access the gold reserves, and every access must be recorded."*
- The **Security Model** is the engineering blueprint: how the vault is constructed — *biometric doors, time-locks, dual-key systems, CCTV, audit logs*.
- The **Implementation** is the actual physical vault built using that blueprint.

A model of security, therefore, is the **blueprint** that designers use to ensure the policy is not violated. Without a model, security becomes ad-hoc and easily defeated.

---

## 1.2 Why Are Security Models Important?

Security models provide three critical utilities in public and private sector information systems:

1. **Formal Verification** — They allow mathematical proof that a system *cannot* enter an insecure state.
2. **Standardization** — They allow auditors, governments (e.g., NIST, ISO), and enterprises to use a common vocabulary.
3. **Policy-to-Mechanism Mapping** — They translate high-level goals (e.g., "protect citizen data") into enforceable rules.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> - **Policy** = What we want to achieve (e.g., "data must remain confidential").
> - **Model** = The formal rule-set (e.g., "no read-up").
> - **Mechanism** = The technical tool (e.g., encryption, access control lists).

---

## 1.3 The Three Generations of Security Models

Security models have evolved through three major generations, each addressing the limitations of the prior:

| Generation | Era | Focus | Examples |
|------------|-----|-------|----------|
| **1st Generation** | 1970s–1980s | Military-grade confidentiality | Bell-LaPadula, Biba |
| **2nd Generation** | 1990s–2000s | Integrity & commercial use | Clark-Wilson, Brewer-Nash |
| **3rd Generation** | 2010s–Present | Adaptive, risk-based, distributed | Zero Trust, STRIDE, NIST CSF |

---

## 1.4 Core Security Attributes — The Foundational Building Blocks

Before diving into individual models, the student must internalize the **six fundamental security attributes** that all models aim to protect. These are the **evaluation criteria** used by KTU examiners when grading "explain any security model" answers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Classic Triad — CIA**
> 1. **Confidentiality** — Prevention of unauthorized *disclosure* of information.
> 2. **Integrity** — Prevention of unauthorized *modification* of information.
> 3. **Availability** — Assurance that authorized users can access information *when needed*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Parker's Hexad — The Extended Six (KTU 2024 High-Yield)**
> Donn B. Parker (1998) argued that real-world information loss involves six qualities, not three:
> 1. **Confidentiality** (disclosure)
> 2. **Integrity** (modification)
> 3. **Availability** (denial of service)
> 4. **Authenticity** (proof of origin)
> 5. **Possession / Control** (theft of physical/digital media — e.g., laptop stolen, even if data is encrypted)
> 6. **Utility** (data becomes unusable — e.g., a key is lost for an encrypted file)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Taxonomy of Security Models

Security models are classified along two axes:
- **Axis 1 (Goal):** Confidentiality models vs. Integrity models vs. Hybrid models.
- **Axis 2 (Mechanism):** Mandatory (MAC), Discretionary (DAC), Role-Based (RBAC), and Rule-Based models.

### 2.1.1 Bell-LaPadula Model (BLP) — The Confidentiality Model

Developed by **David Bell and Leonard LaPadula (1973)** for the U.S. Department of Defense (specifically the Multics operating system). It is the **foundational military-grade confidentiality model**.

**The Three Core Properties:**

> [!NOTE]
> **The "No Read Up, No Write Down" Rules:**
> 1. **Simple Security Property (ss-property) — "No Read Up":**
>    A subject at a given security level may not read an object at a higher security level.
> 2. **Star (*) Property — "No Write Down":**
>    A subject at a given security level may not write to an object at a lower security level.
> 3. **Discretionary Security Property (ds-property):**
>    Access is also restricted by an access matrix (discretionary controls).

**Why "No Write Down"?** — To prevent a high-clearance user from *leaking* sensitive information to a low-clearance user (e.g., a Secret officer emailing a Confidential file).

**The Lattice Concept:** BLP assumes a **lattice of security labels** (e.g., Unclassified < Confidential < Secret < Top Secret). All subjects and objects are assigned a label, and the model mathematically proves that no insecure state is reachable.

### 2.1.2 Biba Model — The Integrity Model

Developed by **Kenneth Biba (1977)** as the integrity analog of BLP. Used when **data correctness is more important than secrecy** (e.g., banking, financial ledgers, medical records).

> [!NOTE]
> **The "No Read Down, No Write Up" Rules:**
> 1. **Simple Integrity Axiom — "No Read Down":**
>    A subject at a given integrity level may not read an object at a *lower* integrity level.
> 2. **Star (*) Integrity Axiom — "No Write Up":**
>    A subject at a given integrity level may not write to an object at a *higher* integrity level.

**Intuition:** A junior clerk (low integrity) should not pollute a senior executive's documents (high integrity) with unverified data.

### 2.1.3 Clark-Wilson Model — Commercial Integrity

Developed by **David Clark and David Wilson (1987)** for the commercial sector. It enforces **well-formed transactions** and **separation of duties** — concepts that map directly to accounting and banking compliance.

**Key Elements:**
- **Constrained Data Items (CDI):** Data subject to integrity controls.
- **Unconstrained Data Items (UDI):** Untrusted input data.
- **Integrity Verification Procedures (IVP):** Procedures that certify CDI state is valid.
- **Transformation Procedures (TP):** The only procedures allowed to modify CDIs.

**Two Core Rules:**
1. **Certification Rule (C):** IVPs must ensure CDIs are in valid states; only TPs can change CDIs.
2. **Enforcement Rule (E):** The system must maintain the relationships between CDIs and TPs.

### 2.1.4 Brewer-Nash Model (Chinese Wall) — Conflict-of-Interest Model

Designed for **consulting and financial advisory firms** where analysts must not access data of competing organizations. It is a **hybrid model** combining confidentiality and access history.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Two Key Properties:**
> 1. **Simple Security Property:**
>    A subject can read an object only if the object belongs to the *same company dataset* as objects the subject has already read, **OR** the object belongs to a *completely different conflict-of-interest class*.
> 2. **Star (*) Property:**
>    A subject can write to an object only if the subject cannot read another object in a *different* company dataset.

**Intuition:** Once an analyst reads Bank A's data, they are dynamically locked out of Bank B and Bank C for that session.

### 2.1.5 Access Control Models — DAC, MAC, RBAC

| Model | Full Name | Decision Maker | Granularity | Use Case |
|-------|-----------|----------------|-------------|----------|
| **DAC** | Discretionary Access Control | Resource owner (user) | Per-object | Personal files, small teams |
| **MAC** | Mandatory Access Control | System policy / classification level | Per-label | Military, government classified data |
| **RBAC** | Role-Based Access Control | Administrator assigns roles | Per-role | Enterprise, hospitals, banks |

**RBAC Hierarchy:** Users → Roles → Permissions → Objects.

### 2.1.6 Zero Trust Model — The Modern Paradigm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (NIST SP 800-207):**
> Zero Trust is a security model that **assumes no implicit trust** is granted to assets or user accounts based solely on their physical or network location. Every access request must be **authenticated, authorized, and encrypted** regardless of whether it originates from inside or outside the network perimeter.

**Core Principles:**
- **Verify Explicitly** — Authenticate based on identity, device, location, behavior.
- **Least Privilege Access** — Just-In-Time and Just-Enough-Access.
- **Assume Breach** — Design as if the attacker is already inside.

**Why Zero Trust?** The traditional "castle-and-moat" model fails because once an attacker breaches the perimeter (e.g., via phishing), they have free movement. Zero Trust eliminates this lateral movement.

### 2.1.7 STRIDE Model — Threat Modeling

Developed by **Microsoft** for threat modeling during the design phase of software systems. It is a **categorization** framework, not an access control model.

> [!NOTE]
> **STRIDE = Six Threat Categories:**
> - **S** — Spoofing (identity fraud)
> - **T** — Tampering (data modification)
> - **R** — Repudiation (denial of action)
> - **I** — Information Disclosure
> - **D** — Denial of Service
> - **E** — Elevation of Privilege

Each category maps to a security property it threatens.

### 2.1.8 AAA Model — Authentication, Authorization, Accounting

A widely deployed operational security model in **public and private sector networks**.

- **Authentication** — *"Who are you?"* (passwords, biometrics, certificates)
- **Authorization** — *"What are you allowed to do?"* (ACLs, RBAC)
- **Accounting** — *"What did you do?"* (logs, audit trails)

**Protocols Implementing AAA:** RADIUS (Remote Authentication Dial-In User Service) and TACACS+ (Terminal Access Controller Access-Control System Plus).

### 2.1.9 Defense in Depth Model

A layered security strategy in which multiple independent security controls are stacked. Even if one layer fails, others provide protection.

**Layers (from outermost to innermost):**
1. Perimeter security (firewalls)
2. Network security (IDS/IPS, segmentation)
3. Host security (antivirus, patching)
4. Application security (secure coding, WAF)
5. Data security (encryption, DLP)
6. Human/process security (training, policies)

### 2.1.10 NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) — Governance Model

A **risk-based** model with five core functions:
1. **Identify** — Asset and risk inventory
2. **Protect** — Implement safeguards
3. **Detect** — Identify incidents
4. **Respond** — Contain and mitigate
5. **Recover** — Restore capabilities

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Model | Core Rule | Key Property Protected | Year |
|---|-------|-----------|------------------------|------|
| 1 | **Bell-LaPadula** | No Read Up, No Write Down | Confidentiality | 1973 |
| 2 | **Biba** | No Read Down, No Write Up | Integrity | 1977 |
| 3 | **Clark-Wilson** | Well-formed transactions, Separation of duties | Integrity | 1987 |
| 4 | **Brewer-Nash** | Same-company or different-class access | Conflict-of-Interest (Confidentiality) | 1989 |
| 5 | **DAC** | Owner decides | Flexibility | 1970s |
| 6 | **MAC** | System policy decides | Confidentiality | 1970s |
| 7 | **RBAC** | Role decides | Scalability | 1996 |
| 8 | **Zero Trust** | Never trust, always verify | All (CIA+) | 2010s |
| 9 | **STRIDE** | Categorize threats | Threat identification | 1999 |
| 10 | **AAA** | Authenticate → Authorize → Account | Accountability | 1990s |
