| 11 | **Defense in Depth** | Layer multiple controls | Resilience | Cold War era |
| 12 | **NIST CSF** | Identify-Protect-Detect-Respond-Recover | Holistic risk mgmt | 2014 |
| 13 | **Parker Hexad** | 6 attributes (CIA + Auth/Poss/Util) | Loss prevention | 1998 |

### Mathematical Notation of Key Properties

For **Bell-LaPadula** on subjects $S$, objects $O$, with security levels $L$:

$$\text{ss-property: } \forall s \in S, \forall o \in O, \text{ read}(s, o) \Rightarrow L(s) \geq L(o)$$

$$\text{* - property: } \forall s \in S, \forall o \in O, \text{ write}(s, o) \Rightarrow L(s) \leq L(o)$$

For **Biba** with integrity levels $I$:

$$\text{Simple Integrity: } \text{read}(s, o) \Rightarrow I(s) \leq I(o)$$

$$\text{* - Integrity: } \text{write}(s, o) \Rightarrow I(s) \geq I(o)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **BLP** → Enforced in SELinux, NSA-grade systems.
> - **Biba/Clark-Wilson** → Enforced in financial databases (e.g., Oracle Label Security).
> - **RBAC** → Used in Windows Active Directory, AWS IAM, SAP.
> - **Zero Trust** → Foundation of Google BeyondCorp, Zscaler, Microsoft 365.
> - **STRIDE** → Used in every Microsoft SDL (Security Development Lifecycle) project.

---

## 2.3 Mapping Models to Sectors

| Sector | Most Relevant Model(s) | Reason |
|--------|------------------------|--------|
| **Defense / Government** | Bell-LaPadula, MAC | Classification hierarchy |
| **Banking / Finance** | Clark-Wilson, Biba, RBAC | Integrity, separation of duties, audit |
| **Healthcare** | RBAC, HIPAA-mapped AAA | Role-based clinician access |
| **Consulting / Auditing** | Brewer-Nash | Conflict-of-interest |
| **Cloud / SaaS** | Zero Trust, RBAC, NIST CSF | Distributed, no perimeter |
| **Software Development** | STRIDE, Defense in Depth | Threat modeling + layered testing |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: Why "No Write Down" Prevents Leaks in BLP

This derivation demonstrates, with explicit steps, why the **\*-property** of Bell-LaPadula is necessary and sufficient to prevent unauthorized information flow from High to Low.

### Step 1 — Define the System Components

Let:
- $S = \{ s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_m \}$ be the set of subjects (users/processes).
- $O = \{ o_1, o_2, \ldots, o_n \}$ be the set of objects (files/records).
- $L : S \cup O \rightarrow \mathcal{L}$ be a labeling function mapping each entity to a security level from a lattice $\mathcal{L}$.
- $f_s : S \rightarrow \mathcal{L}$ be the current security level of subject $s$ (also called *current classification*).

The lattice ordering $\leq$ is partial. For simplicity, assume a linear chain:

$$\mathcal{L} = \{ \text{Unclassified} < \text{Confidential} < \text{Secret} < \text{Top Secret} \}$$

### Step 2 — Formal Statement of the Threat

We want to prove: **No information can flow from a higher-classified object to a lower-classified object via any subject.**

Equivalently:

$$\nexists \text{ a sequence of access operations that results in } L(o_{\text{high}}) \text{ becoming readable at level } \ell_{\text{low}} \text{ where } \ell_{\text{low}} < L(o_{\text{high}})$$

### Step 3 — Suppose the \* - Property is Removed

Assume we drop the \*-property and allow a subject $s$ at Secret level to write to an Unclassified object $o_u$.

**Initial State:**

$$f_s(s) = \text{Secret}, \quad L(s) = \text{Secret}, \quad L(o_h) = \text{Secret}, \quad L(o_u) = \text{Unclassified}$$

**Action 1 — Read (allowed by ss-property since Secret ≥ Secret):**

$$s \text{ reads } o_h \Rightarrow s \text{ now possesses the content of } o_h$$

**Action 2 — Write (assumed allowed by dropping \*):**

$$s \text{ writes the content of } o_h \text{ to } o_u \Rightarrow L(o_u) = \text{Unclassified (unchanged label), but content is now Secret}$$

**Step 3 — Final State:**

A subject $s_{\text{low}}$ at Unclassified level can now read $o_u$ (since Unclassified ≥ Unclassified), thereby accessing Secret information — a **confidentiality violation**.

### Step 4 — Apply the \* - Property to Block the Leak

Enforce:

$$\text{write}(s, o) \Rightarrow f_s(s) \leq L(o)$$

In our scenario, $f_s(s) = \text{Secret}$ and $L(o_u) = \text{Unclassified}$. Therefore:

$$f_s(s) = \text{Secret} \not\leq \text{Unclassified} = L(o_u)$$

Hence the write operation is **denied by the \*-property**. The information flow path is broken.

### Step 5 — Conclude

By induction over all possible operation sequences, any attempt to push information down the lattice is rejected. Therefore:

$$\forall s \in S, \forall o \in O, \forall \text{ operation sequences } \pi: \text{ secure}(s_0) \wedge \pi \text{ respects ss \& *-property} \Rightarrow \text{secure}(s_{\text{final}})$$

This is the **Basic Security Theorem (BST)** of Bell-LaPadula.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Property-by-Property Model Comparison

The following table provides the analytical core for answering **"Compare X and Y"** questions, a KTU favorite.

| Comparison Axis | Bell-LaPadula | Biba | Clark-Wilson | Brewer-Nash |
|-----------------|---------------|------|--------------|-------------|
| **Primary Goal** | Confidentiality | Integrity | Integrity (commercial) | Conflict-of-Interest |
| **Core Construct** | Security lattice | Integrity lattice | CDIs, TPs, IVPs | Company dataset, COI class |
| **Read Rule** | No Read Up (NRU) | No Read Down (NRD) | Only via TP | Same-dataset only |
| **Write Rule** | No Write Down (NWD) | No Write Up (NWU) | Only via TP, certified | Same as read |
| **Key Innovation** | Tranquility principle | Dual lattice for integrity | Well-formed transactions | Dynamic access history |
| **Limitation** | Ignores integrity; no covert channels handled | Ignores confidentiality | Complex to implement | Limited to consulting/finance domain |
| **Sector Fit** | Military, government | Embedded, financial | Banking, ERP | Auditing, law, consulting |

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Analysis: Zero Trust Decision Flow

A typical Zero Trust access request undergoes the following decision pipeline:

**Step 1 — Identity Verification:**
- User presents credentials → MFA challenge.
- Identity Provider (IdP) returns a signed SAML/OIDC token.

**Step 2 — Device Posture Check:**
- EDR agent reports OS patch level, antivirus status, encryption state.
- Non-compliant devices are denied.

**Step 3 — Contextual Risk Evaluation:**
- Geolocation, time-of-day, IP reputation, behavioral analytics.
- A score is computed:

$$R_{\text{score}} = w_1 \cdot \text{IdentityConfidence} + w_2 \cdot \text{DeviceTrust} + w_3 \cdot \text{ContextRisk} + w_4 \cdot \text{BehaviorAnomaly}$$

where $\sum_{i=1}^{4} w_i = 1$.

**Step 4 — Policy Decision:**
- Policy Decision Point (PDP) compares $R_{\text{score}}$ to threshold $\tau$.

$$\text{Access} = \begin{cases} \text{Allow} & \text{if } R_{\text{score}} \geq \tau \text{ and policy match} \\ \text{Allow + MFA step-up} & \text{if } R_{\text{score}} \in [\tau - \delta, \tau) \\ \text{Deny} & \text{if } R_{\text{score}} < \tau - \delta \end{cases}$$

**Step 5 — Continuous Verification:**
- Throughout the session, telemetry is monitored. Trust can be **revoked mid-session** if behavior changes.

This derivation shows that **Zero Trust is not a product, but a policy-engine architecture** — a key KTU answer point.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of "Two-Person Rule" in Clark-Wilson

The Clark-Wilson model enforces **separation of duties** mathematically as follows:

Let $T_1, T_2$ be two transformation procedures such that:
- $T_1 : \text{CDI}_A \rightarrow \text{CDI}_B$ (e.g., "Create PO")
- $T_2 : \text{CDI}_B \rightarrow \text{CDI}_C$ (e.g., "Approve PO")

A user $u_1$ is authorized for $T_1$ but not $T_2$, while $u_2$ is authorized for $T_2$ but not $T_1$.

**Enforcement rule (E) states:**

$$\text{For all CDIs } c, \text{ there exists a TP } T \text{ such that } c \text{ can be modified only by } T$$

Therefore, $u_1$ cannot complete the full cycle (Create + Approve) alone. This is the **mathematical embodiment of "no single person can both initiate and approve a financial transaction"** — the cornerstone of SOX and banking regulations.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Reference (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):**
> - Part A: Short-answer questions of 3 marks each (no choice).
> - Part B: Long-answer questions of 14 marks each with internal choice (typically two sub-parts of 7 marks each).

---

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define the Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (CIA) triad. Why is it considered the foundation of information security models?**

**Model Answer:**

The **CIA Triad** is the foundational model of information security comprising three core properties:

1. **Confidentiality** — Ensures that information is accessible only to those authorized to view it. It protects against unauthorized *disclosure*. Example: encryption of patient health records.
2. **Integrity** — Ensures that information is accurate and has not been tampered with. It protects against unauthorized *modification*. Example: digital signatures on bank transactions.
3. **Availability** — Ensures that information and systems are accessible to authorized users when needed. It protects against *denial of service*. Example: redundant servers in a data center.

It is considered the **foundation** because every security model — whether Bell-LaPadula, Biba, Clark-Wilson, or Zero Trust — is ultimately evaluated against how well it preserves these three properties (or their extended versions in Parker's Hexad).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often *list* the three terms without explaining *what they protect against* (disclosure, modification, denial). Always state the threat each property counters.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]**
**What is the difference between Discretionary Access Control (DAC) and Mandatory Access Control (MAC)? Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | DAC | MAC |
|--------|-----|-----|
| **Decision Authority** | Resource owner decides who accesses | System policy decides based on classification |
| **Flexibility** | High | Low |
| **Granularity** | Per-object (per file) | Per-label (per classification level) |
| **Enforcement** | Access Control Lists (ACLs) | Security lattice enforced by OS kernel |

- **DAC Example:** A Google Drive file owner shares a document with specific email addresses.
- **MAC Example:** In a defense system, a file marked "Top Secret" can be read only by users with "Top Secret" clearance, regardless of the file's owner.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not confuse "Mandatory" with "Compulsory." It refers to *policy-imposed* access, not user choice.

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the Bell-LaPadula Model in detail. State its two main security properties and explain how the "No Write Down" rule prevents information leakage.**

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain the Biba Model and show how it complements Bell-LaPadula. Give a real-world scenario where Biba is more suitable than BLP.**

---

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Bell-LaPadula (BLP) Model — Detailed Explanation:**

The BLP model was developed by **David Elliott Bell and Leonard J. LaPadula in 1973** for the U.S. Department of Defense as part of the Multics secure operating system design. It is the **most influential confidentiality model** in information security.

**Core Components:**
- **Subjects** — Active entities (users, processes).
- **Objects** — Passive entities (files, records, memory).
- **Security Levels** — A lattice of labels such as *Unclassified < Confidential < Secret < Top Secret*.
- **Clearance (L(s))** — The maximum level a subject may access.
- **Current Classification (f(s))** — The level at which a subject is currently operating.

**The Two Main Security Properties:**

> 1. **Simple Security Property (ss-property) — "No Read Up":**
>    A subject $s$ may read object $o$ only if:
>    $$L(s) \geq L(o)$$
>    That is, a subject cannot read an object at a higher classification than its own clearance.

> 2. **Star (*) Property — "No Write Down":**
>    A subject $s$ may write to object $o$ only if:
>    $$L(s) \leq L(o)$$
>    A subject cannot write information to an object at a lower classification level.

**How "No Write Down" Prevents Leakage:**

Suppose a Secret-cleared user reads a Secret document. If they were then allowed to write that data to an Unclassified file, the *content* of the Secret file would be readable by Unclassified users — a confidentiality breach. The \*-property mathematically blocks this downward information flow, ensuring that data can only flow upward or laterally within the lattice, never down.

**Strengths:**
- Mathematically provable (Basic Security Theorem).
- Simple, lattice-based, easy to audit.

**Weaknesses:**
- Ignores integrity.
- Vulnerable to **covert channels** (e.g., timing channels).
- Does not handle dynamic policy changes well (addressed by the *tranquility principle*).

---

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Biba Model — Detailed Explanation:**

The Biba model was proposed by **Kenneth J. Biba in 1977** as the **integrity counterpart** of BLP. It addresses a key weakness of BLP: BLP protects secrecy but does not prevent unauthorized modification of data.

**Core Construct — Integrity Levels:**
Subjects and objects are assigned integrity levels (e.g., *Untrusted < Slightly Trusted < Trusted < Highly Trusted*). The lattice ordering is reversed from BLP: **higher integrity means more trustworthy**.

**The Two Integrity Axioms:**

> 1. **Simple Integrity Axiom — "No Read Down":**
>    A subject $s$ may read object $o$ only if:
>    $$I(s) \leq I(o)$$
>    This prevents a high-integrity process from being *contaminated* by low-integrity (untrusted) data.

> 2. **Star (*) Integrity Axiom — "No Write Up":**
>    A subject $s$ may write to object $o$ only if:
>    $$I(s) \geq I(o)$$
>    This prevents a low-integrity process from *polluting* high-integrity data.

**How Biba Complements BLP:**

| Dimension | Bell-LaPadula | Biba |
|-----------|---------------|------|
| Protects | Confidentiality | Integrity |
| Direction blocked | Information leak downward | Information contamination upward |
| Use when | Secrecy is paramount | Trustworthiness of data is paramount |

The two models are **orthogonal**: an organization can deploy both — BLP to enforce "no secrets leak out" and Biba to enforce "no untrusted data gets in."

**Real-World Scenario Where Biba is More Suitable:**

Consider a **banking loan approval system**. The data on "approved loans" must be of high integrity — we cannot allow unverified or low-trust data to overwrite it. A junior clerk (low integrity level) processing a customer complaint should **not** be able to write into the master loan ledger (high integrity). Here, Biba's "No Write Up" rule ensures the ledger's integrity, while BLP's confidentiality rules are largely irrelevant.

Other examples: **medical dosage databases, aircraft control software, financial trading ledgers.**

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (Internal Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the Zero Trust security model. List its core principles and explain how it differs from the traditional perimeter-based model.**

**(b) [7 Marks] Describe the STRIDE threat model. Map each of the six threat categories to the security property it violates, with one real-world example for each.**

---

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Zero Trust Security Model — Detailed Explanation:**

The **Zero Trust model** was formalized by **John Kindervag of Forrester Research in 2010** and later codified by **NIST in Special Publication 800-207 (2020)**. It has become the **de facto security architecture for modern public and private sector IT environments**, especially cloud and hybrid workforces.

**Core Definition (NIST SP 800-207):**
> "Zero Trust is a security model that assumes no implicit trust is granted to assets or user accounts based solely on their physical or network location. Zero Trust focuses on protecting resources, not network segments."

**Core Principles:**

1. **Verify Explicitly** — Every access request is authenticated and authorized using *all* available context: identity, device, location, workload, data classification, and behavioral anomalies.

2. **Least Privilege Access** — Access is granted using **Just-In-Time (JIT)** and **Just-Enough-Access (JEA)** principles. Users get only the minimum permissions required for the current task, for the shortest duration needed.

3. **Assume Breach** — The architecture is designed as if the attacker is **already inside** the network. Segmentation, micro-segmentation, and continuous verification become mandatory.

**Architectural Components:**
- **Policy Engine (PE)** + **Policy Administrator (PA)** = **Policy Decision Point (PDP)**.
- **Policy Enforcement Point (PEP)** — the gatekeeper that admits or denies traffic.
- **Continuous Diagnostics and Mitigation (CDM)** systems.
- **Industry compliance:** FedRAMP, DoD Zero Trust Reference Architecture.

**Comparison with Traditional Perimeter Model:**

| Aspect | Traditional Perimeter (Castle-and-Moat) | Zero Trust |
|--------|----------------------------------------|------------|
| **Trust Assumption** | Inside network = trusted | Inside network = untrusted |
| **Authentication** | Once at perimeter | Continuously, per request |
| **Access Granularity** | Coarse (network segment) | Fine (per-resource, per-session) |
| **Lateral Movement** | Easy once inside | Severely restricted via micro-segmentation |
| **Remote Work Support** | Weak (VPN needed) | Native |
| **Failure Mode** | Single firewall breach = catastrophic | Compromise of one resource ≠ compromise of all |

**Example:** In a Zero Trust deployment, when a doctor accesses a patient's MRI scan from home, the system verifies: (1) the doctor's MFA token, (2) that the laptop has up-to-date antivirus, (3) the IP is not on a threat-intelligence blacklist, (4) behavioral analytics do not flag anomalous download volume — *before* granting access, and continues to monitor during the session.

---

#### Model Solution for (b)

**STRIDE Threat Model — Detailed Explanation:**

The **STRIDE model** was developed by **Microsoft** (Praerit Garg and Loren Kohnfelder, 1999) as part of the **Security Development Lifecycle (SDL)**. It is a **threat classification framework** used during the design phase of software and systems to identify what could go wrong.

**The acronym STRIDE stands for six threat categories:**

| Letter | Threat | Property Violated | Real-World Example |
|--------|--------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **S** | **Spoofing** | Authenticity | An attacker sends an email pretending to be the CEO (phishing) to trick an employee into wiring money. |
| **T** | **Tampering** | Integrity | An attacker modifies the destination bank account number in an online wire-transfer form via a man-in-the-browser attack. |
| **R** | **Repudiation** | Accountability / Non-repudiation | A user denies having made a purchase, and the system has no log to prove they did — because audit logging was disabled. |
| **I** | **Information Disclosure** | Confidentiality | SQL injection on a university website exposes the personal data of 50,000 students. |
| **D** | **Denial of Service** | Availability | A botnet floods a government e-services portal with traffic, preventing citizens from filing tax returns on the deadline. |
| **E** | **Elevation of Privilege** | Authorization | A buffer overflow in a web server lets a guest user execute commands as the system administrator. |

**Mapping to Security Properties:**

$$f : \text{STRIDE} \rightarrow \text{Security Property}$$

$$\text{Spoofing} \rightarrow \text{Authenticity}, \quad \text{Tampering} \rightarrow \text{Integrity}, \quad \text{Repudiation} \rightarrow \text{Accountability}$$

$$\text{Information Disclosure} \rightarrow \text{Confidentiality}, \quad \text{DoS} \rightarrow \text{Availability}, \quad \text{Elevation} \rightarrow \text{Authorization}$$

**Usage in Practice:**
1. Create a **Data Flow Diagram (DFD)** of the system.
2. For each DFD element (process, data store, data flow, external entity), enumerate applicable STRIDE threats.
3. Rate each threat by **likelihood × impact**.
4. Design mitigations (e.g., digital signatures for S, input validation for T, audit logs for R, encryption for I, rate-limiting for D, sandboxing for E).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mark Losers):**
> 1. **Confusing BLP and Biba directions:** Students often write "Biba says no read up" — the correct rule is **No Read Down**. Memorize the orientation!
> 2. **Mixing Policy and Model:** A model is formal/mathematical; a policy is a high-level statement. Examiners deduct marks for treating them as synonyms.
> 3. **Forgetting Parker's Hexad:** If the question says "list security attributes," listing only CIA is incomplete for KTU 2024. Always mention **Authenticity, Possession, Utility** as Parker extensions.
> 4. **Zero Trust ≠ VPN replacement:** Zero Trust is a *philosophy and architecture*, not just a product. Saying "use Zero Trust software" loses marks.
> 5. **STRIDE misuse:** STRIDE is for *threat modeling*, not access control. Students who say "use STRIDE to grant access" lose 2-3 marks.
> 6. **Skipping sector mapping:** When asked "which model for a bank?" the answer should explicitly name Clark-Wilson/Biba, not just say "use a good model."

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

### ✅ Must-Know Definitions
- [ ] **Security Model** = formal statement of security policy.
- [ ] **CIA Triad** = Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability.
- [ ] **Parker's Hexad** = CIA + Authenticity, Possession, Utility.
- [ ] **BLP** = No Read Up, No Write Down (confidentiality).
- [ ] **Biba** = No Read Down, No Write Up (integrity).
- [ ] **Clark-Wilson** = Well-formed transactions + separation of duties.
- [ ] **Brewer-Nash** = Same-company / different-class access (Chinese Wall).
- [ ] **Zero Trust** = Never trust, always verify (NIST SP 800-207).
- [ ] **STRIDE** = Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, DoS, Elevation.
- [ ] **AAA** = Authentication, Authorization, Accounting.

### ✅ Must-Know Dates / Authors
- [ ] BLP — 1973, Bell & LaPadula, DoD.
- [ ] Biba — 1977, Kenneth Biba.
- [ ] Clark-Wilson — 1987, Clark & Wilson (commercial integrity).
- [ ] Brewer-Nash — 1989, David Brewer & Michael Nash.
- [ ] RBAC formalization — 1996, Ferraiolo & Kuhn.
- [ ] STRIDE — 1999, Microsoft.
- [ ] Zero Trust — 2010, John Kindervag (Forrester).
- [ ] NIST CSF — 2014, NIST.
- [ ] NIST SP 800-207 (Zero Trust) — 2020.

### ✅ Must-Know Mnemonics
- **"Read Up is bad" for BLP, "Read Down is bad" for Biba** → flip the direction.
- **"Write Down leaks secrets" (BLP) vs. "Write Up pollutes trust" (Biba).**
- **STRIDE order: S-T-R-I-D-E** = Authenticity, Integrity, Accountability, Confidentiality, Availability, Authorization.

### ✅ Must-Know Sector Mappings
- **Defense / Government** → BLP, MAC.
- **Banking / Finance** → Clark-Wilson, Biba, RBAC.
- **Healthcare** → RBAC, AAA, HIPAA-aligned.
- **Consulting** → Brewer-Nash.
- **Cloud / Modern IT** → Zero Trust, NIST CSF, STRIDE.

### ✅ Must-Know Equations
- [ ] BLP read: $L(s) \geq L(o)$.
- [ ] BLP write: $L(s) \leq L(o)$.
- [ ] Biba read: $I(s) \leq I(o)$.
- [ ] Biba write: $I(s) \geq I(o)$.
- [ ] Zero Trust risk: $R_{\text{score}} = \sum w_i \cdot \text{factor}_i$.

### ✅ High-Yield Exam Tips
1. Always **define** the model **before** explaining rules.
2. Always **map** the model to a **real sector** (bank, hospital, defense) — KTU examiners reward practical relevance.
3. For 14-mark questions, structure as: **Definition → Components → Rules → Properties → Example → Strengths/Weaknesses**.
4. For comparison questions (e.g., DAC vs MAC), use a **table** with at least 4 comparison axes.
5. Mention **Parker's Hexad** at least once in any "explain security attributes" question — it is a KTU 2024 favorite.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trusted O.S Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT753 - INFORMATION SECURITY IN PUBLIC AND PRIVATE SECTORS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Trusted O.S Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Trusted Operating System (Trusted OS)** is an operating system that has been formally verified, evaluated, and certified to provide a sufficient level of security guarantees in protecting the **confidentiality**, **integrity**, and **availability** of system resources and user data. It is built upon a well-defined **security policy**, a **security model**, and a **Trusted Computing Base (TCB)** that enforces the policy through hardware and software mechanisms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECCT753):**
> A Trusted OS is one that has been verified to enforce the security policy of its **Trusted Computing Base (TCB)** completely, correctly, and efficiently, satisfying the requirements specified under the **Trusted Computer System Evaluation Criteria (TCSEC / Orange Book)** and modern equivalents such as the **Common Criteria (ISO/IEC 15408)**.

The fundamental distinction between a *secure* system and a *trusted* system:

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Trusted** | The system has been subjected to formal evaluation and is *known* to behave correctly under all circumstances. |
| **Secure** | The system is *believed* to enforce the security policy but has not necessarily been formally verified. |

Every secure system **must be** trusted, but not every trusted system may be fully secure in every possible attack scenario.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Bank Vault vs. the Home Locker**
>
> Imagine two storage systems:
>
> - **Home Locker (Ordinary OS):** You *believe* it keeps your valuables safe. You put a lock on it, but you never tested it against a professional locksmith. It looks secure, but there is no formal proof.
> - **Bank Vault (Trusted OS):** The vault has been engineered by certified architects, stress-tested, certified by independent agencies (UL rating, ISO), and its design principles are *publicly documented* and *mathematically proven* to resist known attacks.
>
> The **Trusted OS** is the digital equivalent of the Bank Vault. It doesn't merely *appear* secure — it has been **provably engineered** to satisfy a documented security policy, and its enforcement mechanisms form an unbroken chain called the **Reference Monitor**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield)

- **TCB (Trusted Computing Base):** The totality of protection mechanisms (hardware, firmware, software) responsible for enforcing the security policy.
- **Reference Monitor:** The conceptual, always-active, tamper-proof access-control mediator within the TCB.
- **Security Kernel:** The hardware + software implementation of the Reference Monitor.
- **Multilevel Security (MLS):** Operating environments where information at different sensitivity levels (e.g., *Unclassified*, *Confidential*, *Secret*, *Top Secret*) is processed simultaneously.
- **Least Privilege:** Each subject is granted the minimum privileges necessary to perform its task.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Trusted Computing Base (TCB)

The **TCB** is the *heart* of a Trusted OS. It is the only part of the system that we *must* trust, because if it fails, the entire security policy collapses. The TCB typically includes:

- **Security Kernel** (memory protection, process isolation, I/O control)
- **Trusted Software** (login authenticators, audit daemons)
- **Hardware Root-of-Trust** (TPM chips, secure boot ROM)
- **Trusted Documentation** (security policy documents, reference manuals)

### 2.1.1 TCB Operational Logic

The TCB enforces the security policy through a sequence of decisions:

1. A **Subject** (user/process) issues an operation request.
2. The request is intercepted by the **Reference Monitor**.
3. The Reference Monitor consults the **Access Control Database**.
4. The decision is enforced by the **Security Kernel** (allow/deny).
5. The outcome is logged in the **Audit Trail**.

## 2.2 The Reference Monitor — Three Mandatory Properties

According to **Anderson's Report (1972)** and reinforced in the **Orange Book (TCSEC)**, the Reference Monitor must be:

1. **Complete Mediation** — *Every* access to *every* object must be checked. No bypass path.
2. **Tamper-Proof** — The Reference Monitor and its database must be protected from unauthorized modification.
3. **Verifiable** — The implementation must be small enough to be exhaustively analyzed and proven correct.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** The three properties of the Reference Monitor are frequently asked as a 3-mark question.

## 2.3 Saltzer & Schroeder's 8 Design Principles (1975)

These foundational principles govern all Trusted OS design. They are *theoretically tested in every KTU question on OS security.*

| # | Principle | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Least Privilege** | Grant the minimum privileges needed. |
| 2 | **Separation of Privileges** | Require multiple conditions to grant access. |
| 3 | **Least Common Mechanism** | Minimize shared resources between users. |
| 4 | **Open Design** | Security must not depend on secrecy of mechanism. |
| 5 | **Complete Mediation** | Every access must be validated. |
| 6 | **Psychological Acceptability** | Security UI must be user-friendly. |
| 7 | **Work Factor** | Cost of attack must exceed the value of the asset. |
| 8 | **Defense in Depth** | Layered security controls. |

## 2.4 Mandatory Access Control (MAC) vs Discretionary Access Control (DAC)

| Aspect | DAC | MAC |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Control Authority | Owner of the object | Central security policy / OS |
| Flexibility | High | Low |
| Granularity | Per-user | Per-label / per-clearance |
| Used In | Linux/Windows standard | Trusted OS (SELinux, Trusted Solaris) |
| Susceptible to Trojan Horse | Yes | No |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations and notational conventions are mandatory for answering numerical/model questions in Trusted OS design.

$$
\text{TCB} = \text{SK} \cup \text{TS} \cup \text{HRT} \cup \text{TD}
$$

$$
\text{where } \text{SK} = \text{Security Kernel},\ \text{TS} = \text{Trusted Software},\ \text{HRT} = \text{Hardware Root-of-Trust},\ \text{TD} = \text{Trusted Docs}
$$

$$
\text{BLP Property (No Read Up)}: \quad \lambda(s) \ge \lambda(o)
$$

$$
\text{BLP Property (No Write Down)}: \quad \lambda(o') \ge \lambda(s)
$$

$$
\text{Biba Integrity Property (No Read Down)}: \quad i(s) \le i(o)
$$

$$
\text{Biba Integrity Property (No Write Up)}: \quad i(o') \le i(s)
$$

$$
\text{Trusted Path Requirement} = \text{End-to-End Authenticated Channel}
$$

$$
\text{Assurance Level (TCSEC)}: \quad A1 > B3 > B2 > B1 > C2 > C1 > D
$$

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of Trusted OS Design

Trusted OS principles are not academic — they are deployed in:

- **Government & Defense** — Multilevel secure systems handling Top Secret + Unclassified data simultaneously.
- **Banking & Financial Systems** — Separation of duties, audit trails for compliance.
- **Cloud Multi-Tenancy** — Hypervisor-level isolation.
- **Mobile Platforms** — Android SELinux MAC, iOS Secure Enclave.
- **IoT & Critical Infrastructure** — SCADA systems, medical devices.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Verifying Bell-LaPadula (BLP) Confidentiality

The **Bell-LaPadula Model (1973)** is the cornerstone mathematical model behind multilevel Trusted OS. It enforces *confidentiality* using a lattice of security labels.

### 3.1.1 Notation & Setup

Let:
- $S$ = Set of subjects $\{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_n\}$
- $O$ = Set of objects $\{o_1, o_2, \dots, o_m\}$
- $\lambda : S \cup O \to L$ = Security-level mapping function
- $L$ = Poset of security labels $\{$Unclassified, Confidential, Secret, Top Secret$\}$ with $\le$ as the dominance relation.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Mathematical Walkthrough

**Step 1:** Define the lattice of security levels.

$$
L = \{U, C, S, TS\} \quad \text{with} \quad U < C < S < TS
$$

**Step 2:** Define the access matrix $M$ where $M[s, o]$ denotes the current access mode (read, write, append, execute).

$$
M = \begin{bmatrix} m_{11} & m_{12} & \cdots & m_{1m} \\ m_{21} & m_{22} & \cdots & m_{2m} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ m_{n1} & m_{n2} & \cdots & m_{nm} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the **Simple Security Property (No Read Up — NRU).**

$$
\forall s \in S,\ \forall o \in O : (\text{read} \in M[s,o]) \implies \lambda(s) \ge \lambda(o)
$$

> **Logic:** A subject can only *read* objects at its own level or lower. A Secret clearance subject cannot read Top Secret files.

**Step 4:** Apply the **Star Property (No Write Down — NWD).**

$$
\forall s \in S,\ \forall o \in O : (\text{write} \in M[s,o]) \implies \lambda(s) \le \lambda(o)
$$

> **Logic:** A subject can only *write* to objects at its own level or higher. A Secret subject can write to Secret or Top Secret files but never *down* to Unclassified.

**Step 5:** Apply the **Discretionary Security Property (DSP).**

$$
\forall s \in S,\ \forall o \in O : (\text{access} \in M[s,o]) \implies (s, o) \in \text{ACL}(o)
$$

**Step 6:** Define the **Tranquility Principle.**

$$
\text{Strong Tranquility:} \quad \lambda(s), \lambda(o) = \text{constant during system operation}
$$

$$
\text{Weak Tranquility:} \quad \lambda(s), \lambda(o) \text{ may change only if BLP is preserved}
$$

**Step 7:** Prove **Basic Security Theorem (BST).**

$$
\text{If a system } \Sigma \text{ satisfies NRU, NWD, DSP, and starts in a secure state,}
$$

$$
\text{then every reachable state of } \Sigma \text{ is also secure.}
$$

$$
\Sigma \models \text{Secure} \iff (\forall \text{ reachable state } \sigma : \sigma \models \text{NRU} \land \text{NWD} \land \text{DSP})
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Biba Integrity Model

The **Biba Model (1977)** mirrors BLP but for *integrity* (preventing unauthorized modification).

| BLP Rule | Biba Rule | Effect |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| No Read Up (NRU) | No Read Down (NRD) | Prevents reading low-integrity (untrusted) data |
| No Write Down (NWD) | No Write Up (NWU) | Prevents corrupting high-integrity data |

$$
i : S \cup O \to \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4\} \quad \text{(Integrity Levels)}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Question:** A Trusted OS has four security levels: Unclassified (U), Confidential (C), Secret (S), Top Secret (TS). User Alice has clearance level S. Determine whether the following accesses are permitted under BLP.

- (i) Alice reads a C document.
- (ii) Alice writes to a C document.
- (iii) Alice reads a TS document.
- (iv) Alice writes to a TS document.

**Solution:**

(i) $\lambda(\text{Alice}) = S$, $\lambda(o) = C$. Since $S \ge C$, **read is permitted.**

(ii) $\lambda(\text{Alice}) = S$, $\lambda(o) = C$. We need $\lambda(\text{Alice}) \le \lambda(o)$, i.e., $S \le C$. This is **false**, so **write is denied** (No Write Down).

(iii) $\lambda(\text{Alice}) = S$, $\lambda(o) = TS$. We need $\lambda(\text{Alice}) \ge \lambda(o)$, i.e., $S \ge TS$. This is **false**, so **read is denied** (No Read Up).

(iv) $\lambda(\text{Alice}) = S$, $\lambda(o) = TS$. We need $S \le TS$. This is **true**, so **write is permitted.**

## 3.4 Clark-Wilson Commercial Integrity Model (For Completeness)

Clark-Wilson (1987) defines two categories:
- **CDI** — Constrained Data Items (well-formed)
- **UDI** — Unconstrained Data Items (untrusted input)

Five certification rules (C1–C5) and four enforcement rules (E1–E4) ensure integrity via **Transformation Procedures (TPs)**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Trusted OS Layered Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TB
    userA[End User Application]
    userB[System Administrator]
    appLayer[Application Layer]
    svcLayer[System Services Layer]
    secKernel[Security Kernel Reference Monitor]
    accessCtrl[Access Control Database]
    auditLog[Audit Log Facility]
    cryptoMod[Cryptographic Module]
    hwRoot[Hardware Root of Trust TPM]
    isolation[Memory and Process Isolation MMU]
    
    userA --> appLayer
    userB --> appLayer
    appLayer --> svcLayer
    svcLayer --> secKernel
    secKernel --> accessCtrl
    secKernel --> auditLog
    secKernel --> cryptoMod
    secKernel --> hwRoot
    secKernel --> isolation
```

## 4.2 Reference Monitor Mediation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sub1[Subject Process A]
    sub2[Subject Process B]
    obj1[Object File 1]
    obj2[Object File 2]
    rm[Reference Monitor]
    acdb[(Access Control DB)]
    allow1[Allow]
    deny1[Deny]
    audit1[Audit Log]
    
    sub1 -->|Request Access| rm
    sub2 -->|Request Access| rm
    rm <-->|Query| acdb
    rm -->|Permitted| allow1
    rm -->|Rejected| deny1
    rm -->|Record| audit1
    allow1 --> obj1
    allow1 --> obj2
```

## 4.3 TCB Composition Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TB
    sys[Total System]
    tcb[Trusted Computing Base TCB]
    nonTCB[Non TCB Software]
    
    sk[Security Kernel]
    ts[Trusted Software]
    hrt[Hardware Root of Trust]
    td[Trusted Documentation]
    
    sys --> tcb
    sys --> nonTCB
    
    tcb --> sk
    tcb --> ts
    tcb --> hrt
    tcb --> td
    
    sk --> mm1[Memory Protection]
    sk --> mm2[Process Isolation]
    sk --> mm3[I/O Mediation]
    sk --> mm4[CPU Privilege Rings]
```

## 4.4 Bell-LaPadula State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SecureInitial: System Initialization
    SecureInitial --> MediatedRequest: Subject Requests Access
    MediatedRequest --> NRUCheck: Check Read Permission
    NRUCheck --> Allowed: lambda S greater or equal lambda O
    NRUCheck --> Denied: Violation of Simple Security
    Allowed --> NWDCheck: Check Write Permission
    NWDCheck --> Granted: lambda S less or equal lambda O
    NWDCheck --> Denied: Violation of Star Property
    Granted --> AuditLog: Record Access
    Denied --> AuditLog: Record Violation
    AuditLog --> SecureNext: New Secure State
    SecureNext --> MediatedRequest: Continue Operation
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**Define a Trusted Operating System. How does it differ from a Secure Operating System?**

**Model Answer:**

A Trusted Operating System is an OS whose **Trusted Computing Base (TCB)** has been formally evaluated and verified to enforce the security policy completely, correctly, and efficiently. It satisfies evaluation criteria such as **TCSEC (Orange Book)** or **Common Criteria (ISO 15408)**.

A *Secure* OS is one that is *believed* to provide security but has not undergone formal verification. The distinction is that **every Trusted system has a security policy and a provable enforcement mechanism**, whereas a merely *secure* system relies on trust by reputation or design intent. In KTU terminology: *Trust is verifiable; security is observational.*

**(3 Marks — Key terms: TCB, verification, security policy, evaluation criteria)**

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**List and explain the three mandatory properties of a Reference Monitor.**

**Model Answer:**

According to Anderson's Report (1972), a Reference Monitor must be:

1. **Complete Mediation** — Every access by every subject to every object must be validated by the Reference Monitor. There must be no bypass path (no "back doors").
2. **Tamper-Proof** — The Reference Monitor and its protected database must be shielded from unauthorized modification, both by external attackers and by malicious insiders.
3. **Verifiable** — The Reference Monitor must be small, modular, and simple enough to be subjected to rigorous mathematical verification (formal proofs, code audits, testing).

These properties form the foundation of the **Security Kernel** implementation in any Trusted OS.

**(3 Marks — Mandatory for KTU Board)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **Trusted Computing Base (TCB)** of an operating system. Explain the role of the **Security Kernel** and the **Reference Monitor** in enforcing the security policy. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Bell-LaPadula (BLP) Model** with its security rules and prove the **Basic Security Theorem (BST)**. Demonstrate with an example how the model enforces confidentiality in a multilevel secure system. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A (a):**

**Definition:** The TCB is the totality of protection mechanisms within a computer system that are responsible for enforcing the security policy. It is the *only* portion of the system that we need to *trust absolutely*.

**Components of TCB:**

- **Security Kernel** — The hardware + software implementing the Reference Monitor. It enforces:
  - Memory Protection (segmentation, paging, MMU)
  - Process Isolation
  - I/O Mediation
  - CPU Privilege Rings (Ring 0 to Ring 3)
  - Mandatory Access Control checks

- **Trusted Software** — Login authenticators, password verifiers, audit daemons, network firewalls operating within the kernel.

- **Hardware Root-of-Trust** — TPM chips, secure boot ROM, hardware cryptographic accelerators.

- **Trusted Documentation** — Security policy, evaluation evidence, administrator manuals.

**Role of the Reference Monitor:**

The Reference Monitor is a *conceptual* element within the Security Kernel that mediates every access. Its three properties (Complete Mediation, Tamper-Proof, Verifiable) ensure that no subject can bypass the access control mechanism. In practice, it is realized through system calls, traps into kernel mode, and the access control matrix.

**Working Mechanism:**

1. Subject invokes a system call (e.g., `open()`, `read()`, `write()`).
2. The CPU traps to kernel mode (Ring 0).
3. The Reference Monitor checks the subject's clearance against the object's sensitivity.
4. If MAC, DAC, and BLP constraints are satisfied → access is granted.
5. The access is logged in the audit subsystem.
6. Control returns to user mode (Ring 3).

**(7 Marks — Awarded for: definition 2M, components 3M, working 2M)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A (b):**

**Introduction to BLP:**

The Bell-LaPadula Model (1973) is a formal state-machine model developed by David Bell and Leonard LaPadula for the U.S. Department of Defense. It enforces *confidentiality* in multilevel secure (MLS) systems.

**Formal Definition:**

Let $S$ be the set of subjects and $O$ the set of objects. Define the security level function:

$$
\lambda : S \cup O \to L
$$

where $L$ is a lattice of security labels (e.g., Unclassified $\le$ Confidential $\le$ Secret $\le$ Top Secret).

**Three Security Rules:**

1. **Simple Security Property (No Read Up — NRU):**

$$
\forall s \in S,\ \forall o \in O : (\text{read} \in M[s,o]) \implies \lambda(s) \ge \lambda(o)
$$

2. **Star Property (No Write Down — NWD):**

$$
\forall s \in S,\ \forall o \in O : (\text{write} \in M[s,o]) \implies \lambda(s) \le \lambda(o)
$$

3. **Discretionary Security Property (DSP):**

$$
(\text{access} \in M[s,o]) \implies (s, o) \in \text{ACL}(o)
$$

**Basic Security Theorem (BST):**

A system $\Sigma$ is *secure* if and only if:

$$
\Sigma \models \text{Secure} \iff (\forall\ \text{reachable state } \sigma : \sigma \models \text{NRU} \land \text{NWD} \land \text{DSP})
$$

**Proof Sketch:**

- **Base Case:** Initial state $\sigma_0$ is assumed secure (by definition of system bootstrap).
- **Inductive Step:** Suppose state $\sigma_i$ is secure. The next state $\sigma_{i+1}$ is reached by a transition $T$. Since $T$ is checked by the Reference Monitor against NRU, NWD, DSP, and these are enforced atomically, $\sigma_{i+1}$ must also be secure.
- **Conclusion:** By induction on $i$, all reachable states are secure.

**Worked Example:**

Consider Alice (clearance = Secret) requesting:

- Read a Confidential file: $S \ge C$ → **Allowed**
- Write to a Confidential file: $S \not\le C$ → **Denied** (NWD violated)
- Read a Top Secret file: $S \not\ge TS$ → **Denied** (NRU violated)
- Write to a Top Secret file: $S \le TS$ → **Allowed**

**(7 Marks — Awarded for: rules 3M, BST proof 2M, example 2M)**

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **Saltzer and Schroeder's eight design principles** for the protection of information in computer systems. Why is *Open Design* considered the most important principle for a Trusted OS? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Mandatory Access Control (MAC)** and **Discretionary Access Control (DAC)**. Explain how **SELinux** implements MAC in modern Linux systems. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B (a):**

The eight principles by Saltzer and Schroeder (1975) are:

1. **Least Privilege:** Each subject is granted only the privileges it requires to accomplish its task. Minimizes damage from compromise.

2. **Separation of Privileges:** Two or more conditions must be satisfied to grant access (e.g., password + smart card).

3. **Least Common Mechanism:** Minimize shared state between users to limit covert channels.

4. **Open Design:** Security should not depend on secrecy of mechanism. Kerckhoffs' principle: *the enemy knows the system*. Open designs allow public scrutiny and cryptanalysis, producing stronger systems.

5. **Complete Mediation:** Every access must be checked. Caching is dangerous — design must invalidate stale permissions.

6. **Psychological Acceptability:** Security UIs must be intuitive. If users find them annoying, they will bypass them.

7. **Work Factor:** The cost to attack should exceed the value of the asset.

8. **Defense in Depth:** Layered security — if one fails, others stand.

**Why Open Design is paramount for Trusted OS:**

A Trusted OS must be **verifiable**. A closed-source design cannot be independently audited. Open designs (e.g., SELinux, OpenBSD) allow mathematicians, cryptographers, and security researchers worldwide to test the system, thereby strengthening it. Closed designs hide flaws and create *security through obscurity* — universally rejected in modern security engineering.

**(7 Marks — Awarded for: 8 principles 4M, Open Design explanation 3M)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B (b):**

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | DAC | MAC |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Authority | Resource owner | Central policy / system |
| Flexibility | High | Low |
| Vulnerable to Trojan | Yes | No |
| Auditability | Weak | Strong |
| Example | Unix file permissions (rwx) | SELinux, Trusted Solaris |

**SELinux Implementation:**

Security-Enhanced Linux (SELinux), developed by NSA and Red Hat, implements **Flux Advanced Security Kernel (FLASK)** architecture:

1. Every subject (process) and object (file, socket, port) is assigned a **security context** (label), e.g., `user:role:type:level`.

2. The **Type Enforcement (TE)** module governs access based on subject type and object type, using a centralized `policy.conf` file.

3. The **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)** module restricts subjects to authorized roles.

4. The **Multi-Level Security (MLS)** module optionally enforces Bell-LaPadula constraints.

5. Decisions are made by the **Access Vector Cache (AVC)** for performance and stored persistently in the **SELinux Policy Database**.

When a system call is made, the LSM (Linux Security Module) hook invokes the SELinux Enforcement Engine, which queries the AVC and grants or denies access.

**(7 Marks — Awarded for: comparison 3M, SELinux mechanism 4M)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Trusted OS Design Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing TCB with Security Kernel:** The TCB is the *entire* trusted set; the Security Kernel is only *one component* of the TCB. Writing "TCB is the security kernel" loses 2 marks.
>
> 2. **Inverting BLP Rules:** Many students write "No Write Down" as "cannot write to lower" — which is correct — but then confuse it with "cannot read lower." Memorize: *No Read Up (NRU), No Write Down (NWD)*.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Tranquility Principle:** When asked about BLP, examiners award 1 mark for mentioning **Strong vs Weak Tranquility**. Missing this loses an easy mark.
>
> 4. **Open Design Misquote:** Students often say "Open Design means source code is open." The correct interpretation is *"security must not depend on the secrecy of the mechanism"* — Kerckhoffs' principle.
>
> 5. **BST Proof Skipping:** Examiners expect the **inductive base case** + **inductive step**. Writing only the statement of BST without proof structure loses 2 of the 2 marks allotted.
>
> 6. **Mixing Biba and BLP:** Biba enforces *integrity*, BLP enforces *confidentiality*. Writing "Biba prevents unauthorized read" is incorrect — it prevents reading *low-integrity* data, not low-classification data.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Trusted OS** = formally verified OS that enforces a security policy via TCB.
- **TCB** = Security Kernel + Trusted Software + Hardware Root of Trust + Trusted Documentation.
- **Reference Monitor** = conceptual access-control mediator; must be *Complete, Tamper-Proof, Verifiable*.
- **Saltzer & Schroeder's 8 Principles** = Least Privilege, Separation, Least Common Mechanism, Open Design, Complete Mediation, Psychological Acceptability, Work Factor, Defense in Depth.
- **BLP Model** = No Read Up (NRU), No Write Down (NWD), DSP, plus Basic Security Theorem.
- **Biba Model** = No Read Down, No Write Up — for *integrity*, not confidentiality.
- **Clark-Wilson** = CDI/UDI with Transformation Procedures — for *commercial* integrity.
- **MAC vs DAC** = MAC is system-controlled, DAC is owner-controlled.
- **SELinux** = modern implementation of FLASK MAC architecture in Linux.
- **Tranquility** = Strong (labels never change) vs Weak (labels may change if BLP is preserved).
- **Common Criteria (CC)** = modern replacement for TCSEC Orange Book (EAL1–EAL7).
- **Mandatory memorization:** Bell-LaPadula rules, Reference Monitor properties, Saltzer's principles.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assurance in Trusted O.S. Implementation Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT753 - INFORMATION SECURITY IN PUBLIC AND PRIVATE SECTORS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Assurance in Trusted O.S. Implementation Examples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Assurance** in the context of a Trusted Operating System (TOS) is defined as the *measure of confidence* that the security mechanisms of the OS correctly enforce the stated security policy, and that they continue to do so under all operational conditions, throughout the system lifecycle.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, assurance is achieved through:
- **Specification** — formally defining what the security policy *is*.
- **Implementation** — building the OS such that the policy is *enforced*.
- **Evaluation** — independent verification that the implementation *matches* the specification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trusted Computing Base (TCB)** — the totality of protection mechanisms (hardware, firmware, software) responsible for enforcing the security policy. A trusted OS is essentially a *minimally* implemented TCB where the reference monitor concept is central.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reference Monitor Concept (Saltzer & Schroeder, 1975)** — three mandatory properties:
> 1. **Complete Mediation** — every access is checked.
> 2. **Tamper-Proof** — the monitor cannot be modified.
> 3. **Small Enough to be Verified** — minimal in size and complexity.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bank Vault Analogy

Imagine a bank vault protecting customer money:

| Bank Vault Element | Trusted OS Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The thick steel door | **Security Kernel** (inner protection layer) |
| The CCTV cameras & logs | **Audit Subsystem** |
| The guard checking every entry | **Reference Monitor** |
| The rule book "who can enter" | **Security Policy** |
| Annual vault inspection by auditors | **Assurance Evaluation (TCSEC/CC)** |
| Confidence the vault actually works | **Assurance** itself |

> **The deeper the inspection (evaluation), and the smaller/cleaner the inspection target (minimal TCB), the higher the assurance.** This is the *heart* of trusted OS engineering.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Levels (KTU-Highlight)

The two principal assurance frameworks in the syllabus are:

- **TCSEC (Trusted Computer System Evaluation Criteria, 1983)** — the U.S. "Orange Book" with assurance classes **A1, B3, B2, B1, C2, C1** (A1 = highest).
- **Common Criteria (CC) — ISO/IEC 15408 (1999 onwards)** — with Evaluation Assurance Levels **EAL1 → EAL7**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always quote assurance levels in **bold** in your KTU answer scripts — examiners award extra credit for using canonical acronyms correctly.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Assurance Triad

Assurance in trusted OS implementations rests on three pillars, structured as a logic chain:

- **Step 1 — Policy (What?)**
  - Defines the *security goals*: confidentiality, integrity, availability, accountability.
  - Common formal models: Bell-LaPadula (BLP, *confidentiality*), Biba (*integrity*), Clark-Wilson, Chinese Wall.
  - *Why this step?* Without a written, verifiable policy, evaluation is impossible.

- **Step 2 — Mechanism (How?)**
  - Implements policy via a **Security Kernel** + **Reference Monitor** + **Audit** + **Identification/Authentication (I&A)**.
  - *How?* The kernel is isolated in its own hardware-protected address space, often using **rings of protection** (Ring 0 = kernel, Ring 3 = user).

- **Step 3 — Assurance (Prove it!)**
  - Provides evidence — formal proofs, design verification, code review, penetration testing, configuration management.
  - *Why this step?* A mechanism that cannot be proven correct is merely *believed* to enforce policy; assurance is *evidence-based* confidence.

## 2.2 Key Implementation Examples (KTU High-Yield List)

The following trusted OS implementations are repeatedly tested in KTU examinations:

| # | System | Vendor/Year | Assurance Target | Key Design Feature |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Multics** | MIT/Bell Labs (1965) | B2 (conceptual) | **Ring-segmentation** (up to 64 rings), segmented virtual memory |
| 2 | **SCOMP** | Honeywell (1983) | **A1** (Orange Book) | Minimal 4-ring kernel, formally verified, "good for the next 20 years" |
| 3 | **Trusted XENIX** | IBM/Microsoft (late 1980s) | **B2 / C2** | Covert channel analysis, mandatory access control (MAC) layered on UNIX |
| 4 | **Trusted Solaris** | Sun Microsystems (1992–2017) | **B1 / Labeled Security** | Sensitivity labels, mandatory access control, least privilege |
| 5 | **SELinux** | NSA + Red Hat (2000–present) | **EAL4+** (CC evaluated) | Flask architecture, Type Enforcement (TE) + RBAC + MLS |
| 6 | **GEMSOS** | Gemini Computers (1985) | **A1** | UNIX-compatible, formally verified security kernel |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SCOMP and GEMSOS are the only two systems that ever achieved the A1 (highest TCSEC) rating.** This is a very high-probability KTU two-mark question.

## 2.3 SCOMP — The Gold Standard (KTU Favourite)

**SCOMP (Secure Communications Processor)** by Honeywell (1983) is the textbook exemplar of a trusted OS implementation. Its architecture directly embodies the reference monitor concept:

- **Hardware layer**: Custom hardware (SCE — Security Control Electronics) implements four protection rings:
  - Ring 0 — Security Kernel (highest privilege)
  - Ring 1 — Trusted Software
  - Ring 2 — Untrusted software (still inside TCB boundary)
  - Ring 3 — User applications
- **Software layer**: A minimal, formally specified security kernel (~10,000 LOC).
- **Assurance evidence**:
  - Complete **Formal Top-Level Specification (FTLS)**.
  - **Formal verification** of the kernel against the security policy model.
  - **Covert channel analysis** (both timing and storage channels).
  - **Trusted Distribution** via cryptographic signatures.
- **Use case**: Process-isolated message handling for U.S. government multi-level secure (MLS) systems.

## 2.4 SELinux — Modern Reference Implementation

**SELinux (Security-Enhanced Linux)** is the most widely deployed trusted OS extension in production today. It was designed to demonstrate the Flask architecture, which separates *policy* from *enforcement*:

- **Policy Logic** lives in userspace (loaded into the kernel at boot).
- **Enforcement Mechanism** lives in **LSM (Linux Security Modules)** hooks inside the kernel.
- **Access decisions** are based on:
  - **Type Enforcement (TE)** — every subject/object has a *type*, and rules govern type interactions.
  - **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)** — users are assigned roles.
  - **Multi-Level Security (MLS)** — sensitivity labels with Bell-LaPadula semantics.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Fact Cheat Sheet

> Because this is a *theoretical* security topic (not a numerate one), the "formula sheet" is replaced by a **Canonical Facts Table** that examiners love to test.

| Concept | Canonical Statement | KTU Marks Weight |
|---|---|---|
| Reference Monitor | Saltzer & Schroeder, 1975; 3 properties | 3 marks (favourite) |
| TCSEC Highest Level | **A1 — Verified Design** | 2 marks (one-liner) |
| TCSEC B1 → A1 Levels | C1, C2, B1, B2, B3, A1 | 3 marks (list) |
| CC EAL Range | EAL1 (lowest) → EAL7 (highest) | 2 marks |
| Only A1 systems | **SCOMP, GEMSOS** | 2 marks (favourite) |
| Biba Model | Protects *integrity* (No Read Down, No Write Up) | 3 marks |
| Bell-LaPadula | Protects *confidentiality* (No Read Up, No Write Down) | 3 marks |
| TCB | Total of all protection mechanisms in a trusted system | 2 marks |
| SELinux Architecture | Flask = Policy (user) + Enforcement (LSM hooks) | 5 marks |
| Trusted Solaris | B1, sensitivity labels, MAC | 3 marks |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** Trusted OS assurance is not academic — it underpins **smart-card operating systems, military command systems, cloud hypervisors (e.g., SEV-SNP, Intel SGX enclaves), and certified ICS/SCADA platforms** used in critical infrastructure.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Reasoning Walkthroughs

> Since this is a non-numerate theory topic, "derivations" are replaced by **structured logical walkthroughs** of the design and assurance process — exactly the kind examiners mark when awarding full 14 marks.

## 3.1 Walkthrough A — How Assurance is Built into SCOMP (Step-by-Step)

**Problem context:** Demonstrate how SCOMP achieves A1 assurance. This is a typical 7-mark sub-question in KTU ESE.

**Step 1 — Establish the security policy mathematically**
The security policy of SCOMP is specified using the **Bell-LaPadula** model. Formally:

$$
S = \langle S, O, \mathcal{L}, \lambda, \leq, \text{read}, \text{write} \rangle
$$

where:
- $S$ = set of subjects (active entities, e.g., processes).
- $O$ = set of objects (files, devices, memory segments).
- $\mathcal{L}$ = lattice of security levels (e.g., Unclassified $<$ Confidential $<$ Secret $<$ Top Secret).
- $\lambda : S \cup O \rightarrow \mathcal{L}$ = the *level function* assigning a clearance/classification to each subject/object.
- $\leq$ = the dominance (partial order) relation on $\mathcal{L}$.
- $\text{read}, \text{write}$ = access modes.

**Step 2 — Define the access control axioms**

For every access request $\langle s, o, m \rangle$ where $s \in S$, $o \in O$, $m \in \{\text{read}, \text{write}\}$:

$$
\text{Simple Security Property (no read up):} \quad m = \text{read} \Rightarrow \lambda(s) \geq \lambda(o)
$$

$$
\text{*-Property (no write down):} \quad m = \text{write} \Rightarrow \lambda(o) \geq \lambda(s)
$$

*Conversion logic:* the first equation forbids a low-clearance subject from reading a high-classification object (preserves confidentiality); the second forbids a high-clearance subject from writing to a low-classification object (prevents leakage).

**Step 3 — Embed the policy in a Security Kernel**
The security kernel of SCOMP is the *only* software that is permitted to evaluate the equations above. It is the **reference monitor**. All higher-level OS services (file system, networking) are forced to call into the kernel for every access decision.

**Step 4 — Isolate the kernel in hardware rings**
SCOMP hardware enforces four rings $(R_0, R_1, R_2, R_3)$ with the following privilege ordering:

$$
\Pr(R_0) > \Pr(R_1) > \Pr(R_2) > \Pr(R_3)
$$

The security kernel lives in $R_0$; user applications are confined to $R_3$. Attempts to execute privileged instructions from $R_3$ trigger a hardware trap routed back to the kernel — thereby *tamper-proofing* the reference monitor.

**Step 5 — Provide formal specification (FTLS)**
A **Formal Top-Level Specification** is written in a mathematical notation (e.g., Z, VDM, or a custom HDL) describing *what* the kernel does at its interface. This is mandatory at A1.

**Step 6 — Formal verification**
The FTLS is mathematically proven to satisfy the BLP axioms from Step 2. This is the **verified design** assurance requirement that distinguishes A1 from B3.

**Step 7 — Covert channel analysis**
SCOMP designers systematically enumerate and rate covert channels (storage and timing) using information-theoretic measures. For any channel of bandwidth $C$ bits/sec, the impact rating is:

$$
\text{Impact}(C) = f(\text{Channel Bandwidth}, \text{Reachable Subjects}, \text{Channel Type})
$$

Channels exceeding the documented bandwidth threshold must be *audited* or *mitigated* (e.g., via noise injection, kernel scheduling jitter).

**Step 8 — Trusted distribution**
The binary image of the kernel is cryptographically signed. Every installed copy is verified at boot, ensuring that no attacker can replace the kernel with a trojaned version — closing the *tamper-proof* property end-to-end.

> **Conclusion of Walkthrough A:** SCOMP achieves A1 because every assurance requirement of the Orange Book is met with *formal evidence*, not merely *documentation*.

## 3.2 Walkthrough B — Assurance Reasoning in SELinux

**Step 1 — Architecture separation (Flask)**
SELinux splits decision-making from enforcement:

$$
\text{Decision} = \text{AVC}(\text{Subject SID}, \text{Object SID}, \text{Class}, \text{Permission})
$$

where `SID` = Security Identifier (a packed label encoding *user : role : type : MLS range*).

**Step 2 — Policy loading**
At boot, the **policy compiler (`checkpolicy`)** validates the source policy against type rules, role transition rules, and MLS constraints. Invalid policies cause boot failure — this is an *early* assurance gate.

**Step 3 — Enforcement via LSM hooks**
When a process performs `open()`, `read()`, `execve()`, etc., the kernel LSM framework queries the **Access Vector Cache (AVC)**:

$$
\text{AVC lookup} : (\text{ssid}, \text{tsid}, \text{tclass}) \longrightarrow \{\text{allow}, \text{deny}, \text{bad}\}
$$

If a decision is `bad`, an audit event is emitted and access is denied — *complete mediation* in action.

**Step 4 — Continuous enforcement**
Unlike discretionary systems, even the **root user** is bound by SELinux policy. This is the *principle of least privilege* and *separation of duty* applied to the highest-privilege subject.

**Step 5 — Evaluation**
SELinux has been evaluated at **EAL4+** under Common Criteria with augmentations for *flaw remediation*, *developer environment*, and *life-cycle support* — corresponding roughly to **B3** in TCSEC terms.

> **Conclusion of Walkthrough B:** SELinux shows that assurance can be layered onto an existing monolithic kernel (Linux) using LSM hooks, achieving CC EAL4+ in production.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Assurance vs Implementation

| Assurance Property | SCOMP (A1) | SELinux (EAL4+) | Trusted Solaris (B1) | Multics (Pre-Orange) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Formal Security Policy | ✔ BLP | ✔ (configurable) | ✔ (labels) | ✔ (early BLP) |
| Reference Monitor | ✔ 4-ring | ✔ LSM | ✔ 2-privilege | ✔ 64-ring |
| Covert Channel Analysis | ✔ Full | ✔ Documented | ✔ Partial | ✘ |
| Formal Verification | ✔ Kernel | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ |
| Trusted Distribution | ✔ Crypto | ✔ RPM sigs | ✔ Pkg sigs | ✘ |
| Production Use | ✘ (defunct) | ✔ Huge | ✘ (sunset) | ✘ (historical) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Trusted OS Assurance Reference Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[User Applications Ring 3] --> B[Untrusted OS Services Ring 2]
    B --> C[Trusted OS Services Ring 1]
    C --> D[Security Kernel Ring 0 Reference Monitor]
    D --> E[Hardware Protection Rings MMU TLB]
    E --> F[Formal Security Policy BLP Biba Clark Wilson]
    F --> G[Assurance Evidence FTLS Covert Channel Trusted Distribution]
    G --> H[Independent Evaluation TCSEC Common Criteria EAL]

    classDef ring3 fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef ring0 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef policy fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    classDef eval fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#990000,color:#000

    class A,B,C ring3
    class D,E ring0
    class F,G policy
    class H eval
```

## 4.2 SELinux Flask Architecture (Policy + Enforcement)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Policy Source TE RBAC MLS] --> PC[checkpolicy compiler]
    PC --> PI[Policy binary in kernel]
    PI --> AVC[Access Vector Cache]
    KS[Kernel Subsystem open read write exec] --> LSM[Linux Security Module Hooks]
    LSM --> AVC
    AVC --> DEC{Decision}
    DEC -->|allow| KS
    DEC -->|deny| AUD[Audit Log AVC Denial]
    DEC -->|bad| AUD

    classDef policy fill:#e1f5e1,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    classDef kernel fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000

    class P,PC,PI policy
    class KS,LSM kernel
    class AVC,DEC,AUD decision
```

## 4.3 TCSEC Assurance Class Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[Minimal Assurance] --> L1[C1 Discretionary Security Protection]
    L1 --> L2[C2 Controlled Access Protection]
    L2 --> L3[B1 Labeled Security]
    L3 --> L4[B2 Structured Protection]
    L4 --> L5[B3 Security Domains]
    L5 --> L6[A1 Verified Design]
    L6 --> L7[Beyond A1 Trusted Network Interpretation TNI]

    classDef low fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef mid fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000
    classDef high fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000

    class L0,L1,L2 low
    class L3,L4 mid
    class L5,L6,L7 high
```

## 4.4 Reference Monitor Concept & TCB Boundary

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TCB[Trusted Computing Base TCB Boundary]
        RM[Reference Monitor]
        SP[Security Policy]
        AR[Audit Records]
        IA[Identification and Authentication]
    end
    US[Untrusted Subject User Process] -->|access request| RM
    RM -->|consult| SP
    RM -->|grant or deny| US
    RM --> AR
    US --> IA
    IA --> RM

    classDef tcb fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    classDef user fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#990000,color:#000

    class RM,SP,AR,IA tcb
    class US user
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define the term "Reference Monitor" and list its three mandatory properties.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A Reference Monitor is an abstract concept that mediates all access of subjects to objects in a trusted system, ensuring that every access is evaluated against the security policy.
> Its three mandatory properties (Saltzer & Schroeder, 1975) are:
> 1. **Complete Mediation** — every access is checked, not just a sample.
> 2. **Tamper-Proof** — the reference monitor and its database cannot be modified.
> 3. **Verifiable** — it is small and simple enough to be exhaustively tested and verified.
>
> *(Award 1 mark each for the definition and the three properties.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sample Paper]`
**List any three trusted operating systems and state the highest TCSEC assurance level achieved by each.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> | # | System | Highest TCSEC Level |
> |---|---|---|
> | 1 | SCOMP (Honeywell) | **A1** |
> | 2 | GEMSOS (Gemini) | **A1** |
> | 3 | Trusted XENIX (IBM/Microsoft) | **B2** |
> | 4 | Trusted Solaris (Sun) | **B1** |
>
> *(Award 1 mark per correct row, up to 3 rows.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (1 × 14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Explain in detail the assurance methodology used in trusted operating systems, using SCOMP as a case study. Discuss how SCOMP satisfies the A1 level of TCSEC.**

#### Part (a) — Assurance Methodology (7 Marks)

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Step 1 — Security Policy Definition (2 marks)**
> The first step in trusted OS assurance is to formally specify the security policy. SCOMP uses the **Bell-LaPadula (BLP) model** to enforce *confidentiality* through:
> - Simple Security Property: $\lambda(s) \geq \lambda(o)$ for read.
> - *-Property: $\lambda(o) \geq \lambda(s)$ for write.
>
> **Step 2 — Reference Monitor Implementation (2 marks)**
> The security kernel of SCOMP is the *only* code permitted to evaluate BLP. It mediates every access between subjects (processes) and objects (files, devices), satisfying **complete mediation**.
>
> **Step 3 — Tamper-Proofing (1.5 marks)**
> The kernel is placed in hardware Ring 0 of a 4-ring hierarchy. Hardware traps prevent user code (Ring 3) from modifying the kernel, ensuring **tamper-proofness**.
>
> **Step 4 — Minimisation & Verification (1.5 marks)**
> The SCOMP kernel is intentionally small (~10,000 LOC) so it can be exhaustively code-reviewed and **formally verified** against the FTLS. This satisfies the **verifiable** property.

#### Part (b) — SCOMP at A1 (7 Marks)

> **Model Answer:**
>
> A1 is the highest assurance class in TCSEC. SCOMP satisfies it as follows:
>
> | A1 Requirement | How SCOMP Satisfies It | Marks |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Verified Design** | Kernel is formally verified against the BLP model | 1.5 |
> | **Formal Top-Level Specification (FTLS)** | Complete FTLS in formal mathematical notation | 1 |
> | **Informal / Formal Covert Channel Analysis** | Documented analysis of storage and timing channels with bandwidth ratings | 1 |
> | **Trusted Configuration Management** | Strict change control over the kernel source | 0.5 |
> | **Trusted Distribution** | Cryptographically signed kernel binaries | 0.5 |
> | **Trusted Facility Management** | Controlled development environment, background checks for developers | 0.5 |
> | **Trusted Recovery** | Documented procedures for safe restart after compromise | 0.5 |
> | **Covert Channel Auditing** | Periodic audit of channels, not just one-time analysis | 0.5 |
> | **Trusted Design Documentation** | Complete DESIGN document, DESCRIPTION document | 0.5 |
> | **Implementation must be the FTLS** | Code matches the formal specification (proved) | 0.5 |
>
> **Conclusion (1 mark):**
> SCOMP remains one of only two systems (with **GEMSOS**) to ever attain the A1 rating under the Orange Book, and is therefore the canonical reference for assurance engineering.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative* `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Discuss the architecture of SELinux and explain how it provides assurance under the Common Criteria framework. Compare it briefly with SCOMP.**

#### Part (a) — SELinux Architecture (7 Marks)

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Step 1 — Flask Architecture (2 marks)**
> SELinux implements the Flask architecture, which decouples the *security policy logic* (userspace, pluggable) from the *enforcement mechanism* (kernel LSM hooks). The policy is compiled by `checkpolicy` and loaded into the kernel at boot.
>
> **Step 2 — Three Pillars of Policy (2 marks)**
> - **Type Enforcement (TE):** every subject/object has a *type*; allow rules govern type pairs.
> - **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC):** users are assigned roles, and roles define which types they may transition to.
> - **Multi-Level Security (MLS):** sensitivity labels (`s0`–`s15`) implement BLP-style constraints.
>
> **Step 3 — Access Vector Cache (AVC) (1.5 marks)**
> The AVC caches recent policy decisions for performance. The query
> $$\text{avc\_has\_perm}(\text{ssid}, \text{tsid}, \text{tclass}, \text{requested}) \in \{\text{allow}, \text{deny}\}$$
> is invoked at every LSM hook (e.g., `file_open`, `inode_permission`).
>
> **Step 4 — Complete Mediation Evidence (1.5 marks)**
> LSM hooks are placed in *every* kernel subsystem that performs access control. This ensures *complete mediation* across files, networking, IPC, capabilities, and process exec.

#### Part (b) — Assurance under Common Criteria & Comparison with SCOMP (7 Marks)

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Common Criteria Evaluation (3 marks):**
> SELinux has been evaluated at **EAL4+** (methodically designed, tested, and reviewed). The augmentations include *ALC_FLR.3* (flaw remediation), *AVA_VAN.3* (vulnerability analysis), and *ASE_INT* / *ASE_TSS* (security target evaluation). This corresponds roughly to TCSEC **B3**.
>
> **Comparison with SCOMP (4 marks):**
>
> | Dimension | SCOMP | SELinux |
> |---|---|---|
> | Assurance Level | **A1** (highest TCSEC) | **EAL4+** (CC) |
> | Formally Verified Kernel | ✔ Yes | ✘ No (practical, not verified) |
> | Hardware Rings | 4 custom rings | Standard Linux rings (0/3) |
> | Policy Model | Hard-coded BLP | Pluggable Flask (BLP, Biba, custom) |
> | Production Deployment | Defunct (1980s) | Massive (RHEL, Fedora, Android SE) |
> | Covert Channel Analysis | Full formal | Documented, not formally audited |
> | Engineering Focus | *Maximum* assurance | *Practical* assurance at scale |
>
> **Conclusion (1 mark):** SCOMP is the *theoretical* gold standard; SELinux is the *practical* gold standard. Both demonstrate that **assurance = evidence + simplicity + formal reasoning** — only the trade-off between strictness and deployability differs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing TCSEC levels and CC EALs** — never equate EAL4 with B1; EAL4 is roughly **B3**. Mapping is approximate, not exact.
> 2. **Forgetting the three Reference Monitor properties** — examiners often award 1 mark per property; missing even one costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Writing only "SCOMP is secure"** — must cite *evidence* (FTLS, formal verification, covert channel analysis, trusted distribution).
> 4. **Confusing Biba and BLP** — Biba protects *integrity* (no read **down**, no write **up**); BLP protects *confidentiality* (no read **up**, no write **down**). Many students invert them.
> 5. **Omitting "Trusted Distribution"** for A1 questions — this is a frequently tested bullet that students overlook.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Assurance = Confidence backed by Evidence**, not just a feature list.
- **TCB** = Hardware + Firmware + Software enforcing security policy.
- **Reference Monitor** = Complete Mediation + Tamper-Proof + Verifiable (Saltzer & Schroeder, 1975).
- **TCSEC Levels (ascending):** C1 → C2 → B1 → B2 → B3 → A1.
- **Common Criteria EALs (ascending):** EAL1 → EAL2 → EAL3 → EAL4 → EAL5 → EAL6 → EAL7.
- **A1 systems ever certified:** Only **SCOMP** and **GEMSOS**.
- **BLP** = confidentiality (no read up, no write down).
- **Biba** = integrity (no read down, no write up).
- **SCOMP highlights:** 4 rings, ~10k LOC, FTLS, formal verification, covert channel analysis, trusted distribution.
- **SELinux = Flask architecture** = Policy (user) + Enforcement (LSM hooks).
- **SELinux pillars:** Type Enforcement + RBAC + MLS.
- **SELinux CC level:** EAL4+ (≈ TCSEC B3).
- **Trusted Solaris** = B1, sensitivity labels, MAC.
- **Multics** = historical 64-ring segmented system, precursor to BLP.
- **Covert channels** = storage (file lock) + timing (CPU usage). Must be analysed for high assurance.
- **Trusted Distribution** = crypto-signed binaries prevent kernel tampering in supply chain.
- **Engineering applications:** military MLS systems, smart-card OS, cloud TEE (SGX, SEV-SNP), ICS/SCADA, Android SE.
- **Memory aid:** "**SC**OMP achieves A**1** = **S**uper **C**ertified **O**S at **M**ilitary level **P**lus" *(mnemonic only — not in syllabus)*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT753 - INFORMATION SECURITY IN PUBLIC AND PRIVATE SECTORS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Security Requirements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Security Requirements** are precise, testable statements of security functionality that an information system **must** provide, or security constraints that it **must** satisfy, in order to protect the **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (CIA Triad)** of information assets from identified threats and vulnerabilities across public and private sector operational environments.

In the context of **PECCT753 (Information Security in Public and Private Sectors)**, security requirements bridge the gap between an organisation's **risk profile** and the **technical, procedural, and legal controls** implemented to safeguard information assets. They are derived from multiple sources — legal mandates, regulatory frameworks, organisational policies, threat intelligence, and business objectives.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction (KTU Board Favourite):**
> - **Security Requirement (SR)** → A *need* that must be satisfied (e.g., "The system shall enforce multi-factor authentication for privileged users").
> - **Security Control** → A *mechanism* that satisfies the requirement (e.g., RSA SecurID tokens, biometric scanners).
> - **Security Policy** → The *high-level governance document* that mandates the requirement.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bank Vault Model

Imagine an organisation as a **bank branch** holding customer valuables (data assets):

| Bank Element | Information Security Analogue |
|---|---|
| Vault Door | Access Control Mechanism (Authentication) |
| Customer's Locker | Encrypted Database (Confidentiality) |
| Ledger Book | Audit Logs (Integrity + Non-Repudiation) |
| CCTV Cameras | Intrusion Detection Systems (Monitoring) |
| Fire Insurance | Disaster Recovery / Business Continuity |
| RBI Banking Regulations | Legal/Regulatory Compliance (e.g., IT Act 2000, GDPR) |
