**The "Security Requirements" of the bank** would be statements like: *"Only the locker owner can open the locker between 9 AM–5 PM, every opening must be logged, and the vault must withstand a 30-minute physical attack."*

Similarly, in information systems, **security requirements** are the formalised, testable statements that define *who* can access *what*, *when*, *how*, and *under what conditions* — and what must happen when these rules are violated.

---

## 1.3 Fundamental Categories of Security Requirements (The CIA+ Extended Triad)

The KTU 2024 syllabus recognises the following foundational security requirement categories. Each is **mandatory knowledge** for the End Semester Examination (ESE).

### 1.3.1 The Primary CIA Triad
1. **Confidentiality** — Ensuring that information is accessible *only* to authorised entities.
2. **Integrity** — Ensuring that information remains *accurate and unaltered* during storage, processing, and transmission.
3. **Availability** — Ensuring that information and services are *accessible when required* by authorised users.

### 1.3.2 The Extended Security Requirements
4. **Authentication** — Verifying the claimed identity of a user, process, or system.
5. **Authorization (Access Control)** — Granting *specific* rights to authenticated entities based on policies.
6. **Non-Repudiation** — Ensuring that a party *cannot deny* having performed an action (e.g., digital signatures).
7. **Accountability** — Tracing every security-relevant action to a specific, identifiable entity.
8. **Privacy** — Protecting personally identifiable information (PII) from unauthorised disclosure and misuse.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Mnemonic:** **"CIA + AANP"** = Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability, Authentication, Authorization, Non-repudiation, Privacy.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Security Requirements

### A. Functional vs Non-Functional Security Requirements

| Dimension | Functional Security Requirements | Non-Functional Security Requirements |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Define *what* the system *must do* security-wise | Define *how well* security functions *must perform* |
| **Example** | "The system shall encrypt passwords using AES-256" | "Password verification must complete within 500 ms" |
| **Verification** | Functional testing, use-case validation | Performance testing, load testing, SLA validation |
| **KTU 2024 Focus** | High — frequently tested | Moderate — appearing in 14-mark analytical questions |

### B. Derived vs Emergent Security Requirements
- **Derived Requirements** — Explicitly stated by stakeholders, regulations, or contracts.
- **Emergent Requirements** — Surface *only* after system integration (e.g., inter-service authentication in microservices).

### C. Public Sector vs Private Sector Requirements

| Aspect | Public Sector (Government) | Private Sector (Corporate) |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Driver** | Statutory / Legal mandate (e.g., IT Act 2000, RTI Act 2005) | Business risk, competitive advantage, customer trust |
| **Governance** | Centralised (e.g., CERT-In, NCIIPC, MeitY) | Decentralised (Board + CISO + Business Units) |
| **Compliance Standards** | e-Governance Standards, Digital India frameworks, ISNP | ISO 27001, PCI-DSS, SOX, GDPR |
| **Transparency** | High — public accountability via RTI, CAG audits | Limited — contractual and competitive |
| **Data Sensitivity** | National security, citizen PII, sovereign data | Trade secrets, customer data, financial records |
| **Budgeting** | Annual budgetary cycles, parliamentary approvals | ROI-driven, CapEx/OpEx allocations |
| **Threat Landscape** | Nation-state APTs, cyber-espionage, hacktivism | Ransomware, insider threats, fraud, supply-chain attacks |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Security Requirements Engineering (SRE) Process

Security Requirements Engineering is the **systematic, disciplined, and quantifiable approach** to eliciting, analysing, specifying, validating, and managing security requirements throughout the system lifecycle. KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to internalise this as a **5-stage iterative process**.

### Stage 1 — Elicitation
**Goal:** Discover and gather security requirements from all relevant stakeholders.

**Techniques:**
- **Interviews & Workshops** — Structured Q&A with business owners, IT staff, legal/compliance teams.
- **Questionnaires** — Standardised checklists (e.g., NIST 800-53 control families).
- **Document Analysis** — Review of existing policies, SLAs, audit reports, incident logs.
- **Threat Modelling** — STRIDE, PASTA, VAST, Attack Trees.
- **Reuse from Standards** — ISO 27002 Annex A controls, NIST CSF, CIS Critical Security Controls.
- **Use Case / Misuse Case Analysis** — Identifying both intended and adversarial scenarios.

> [!NOTE]
> **Misuse Case** is a KTU frequently-tested concept. A misuse case describes a *scenario where an actor (often malicious) interacts with the system in a way that violates a security requirement*. Example: *"An external attacker injects SQL commands via the login form to extract user credentials."*

### Stage 2 — Analysis & Negotiation
**Goal:** Resolve conflicts, prioritise, and refine requirements.

**Activities:**
- **Prioritisation** — MoSCoW (Must have, Should have, Could have, Won't have) or ranking via risk score.
- **Conflict Resolution** — Negotiate between security, usability, and cost trade-offs.
- **Feasibility Analysis** — Technical, financial, and operational viability.
- **Risk-Based Ranking** — Apply quantitative risk formulas (see Section 2.3).

### Stage 3 — Specification (Documentation)
**Goal:** Produce a formal **Security Requirements Specification (SRS-Sec)** document.

**Structure of an SRS-Sec (KTU 14-mark question favourite):**
1. **Introduction** — Purpose, scope, definitions, acronyms.
2. **Overall Description** — Product perspective, user characteristics, assumptions.
3. **Specific Security Requirements**
   - Functional (e.g., "The system shall enforce session timeout after 15 minutes of inactivity").
   - Non-functional (e.g., "All cryptographic operations must use FIPS 140-2 validated modules").
   - Constraints (e.g., "No plaintext storage of authentication credentials").
4. **Verification Criteria** — How each requirement will be tested.
5. **Compliance Traceability Matrix** — Maps each requirement to a regulatory clause.

### Stage 4 — Validation & Verification
**Goal:** Ensure requirements are *correct, complete, consistent, testable, and traceable*.

**Techniques:**
- **Reviews & Inspections** — Walkthroughs with stakeholders.
- **Prototyping** — Build throwaway mock-ups to validate usability of security features.
- **Formal Methods** — Model checking (e.g., SPIN, NuSMV), Z-notation.
- **Penetration Testing** — Validates that implemented controls satisfy requirements.
- **Compliance Audits** — Verifies legal/regulatory adherence.

### Stage 5 — Management & Evolution
**Goal:** Maintain requirement integrity throughout the system lifecycle.

**Activities:**
- **Requirements Traceability** — Forward (req → design → code → test) and backward (test → req).
- **Change Control** — Formal process for requirement modifications.
- **Versioning** — SRS-Sec v1.0, v1.1, etc., with change logs.
- **Impact Analysis** — Assess consequences of requirement changes on design, cost, schedule.

---

## 2.2 Sources of Security Requirements

A robust security requirements baseline draws from **seven canonical sources**:

| # | Source | Example in Public Sector | Example in Private Sector |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Laws & Regulations** | IT Act 2000 (India), RTI Act 2005, Aadhaar Act 2016 | Sarbanes-Oxley (SOX), GDPR (EU), CCPA (California) |
| 2 | **Industry Standards** | e-Governance standards (NeGP), GIAS | ISO 27001, PCI-DSS, HIPAA, NIST CSF |
| 3 | **Organisational Policies** | Government Security Policy, NCIIPC advisories | Corporate ISMS, Acceptable Use Policy (AUP) |
| 4 | **Risk Assessment** | Threat intel from CERT-In, sectoral CERTs | Enterprise Risk Register, FAIR analysis |
| 5 | **Business Objectives** | Digital India mission, citizen service uptime SLAs | Customer trust, market differentiation, M&A due diligence |
| 6 | **Past Incidents** | Aadhaar data leak reports, government portal breaches | Target breach 2013, Equifax breach 2017, Log4j vulnerability |
| 7 | **Best Practices & Frameworks** | OWASP SAMM, BSIMM | OWASP ASVS, CIS Benchmarks, Cloud Controls Matrix |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Quantitative Security Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **mathematically rigorous**, KTU-examination friendly, and appear frequently in 14-mark analytical questions.

### 2.3.1 Risk Quantification Formulas

**Asset Value (AV):**
$$AV = \text{Monetary or criticality worth of the information asset (in INR/USD)}$$

**Exposure Factor (EF):**
$$EF = \text{Fraction of asset value lost if a threat is realised} \quad (0 \le EF \le 1)$$

**Single Loss Expectancy (SLE):**
$$SLE = AV \times EF$$

**Annualised Rate of Occurrence (ARO):**
$$ARO = \text{Expected number of times the threat will occur per year}$$

**Annualised Loss Expectancy (ALE):**
$$ALE = SLE \times ARO$$

**Total ALE (across multiple risks):**
$$ALE_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} SLE_i \times ARO_i$$

**Residual Risk after Control Implementation:**
$$R_{\text{residual}} = R_{\text{inherent}} - \Delta R_{\text{control}}$$

**Return on Security Investment (ROSI):**
$$ROSI = \frac{ALE_{\text{pre}} - ALE_{\text{post}} - \text{Cost of Control}}{\text{Cost of Control}} \times 100\%$$

### 2.3.2 Authentication Strength & Password Metrics

**Password Entropy (bits):**
$$H = L \times \log_2(N)$$

where $L$ = password length, $N$ = size of the character set.

**Cracking Time Estimate (worst case):**
$$T_{\text{crack}} = \frac{2^H}{2 \times R_{\text{hash}}}$$

where $R_{\text{hash}}$ = hash rate of attacker (guesses/second).

**Strength Multiplier for MFA:**
$$S_{\text{effective}} = S_{\text{primary}} \times \prod_{i=2}^{k} S_{\text{secondary}_i}$$

### 2.3.3 Availability & Reliability Metrics

**Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):**
$$MTBF = \frac{\text{Total operational time}}{\text{Number of failures}}$$

**Mean Time To Repair (MTTR):**
$$MTTR = \frac{\text{Total downtime}}{\text{Number of failures}}$$

**System Availability (as a percentage):**
$$A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%$$

**Common SLA tiers:**

| SLA Tier | Availability | Max Annual Downtime |
|---|---|---|
| Tier 1 (Mission-Critical) | **99.999%** | ~5.26 minutes/year |
| Tier 2 (Business-Critical) | **99.99%** | ~52.6 minutes/year |
| Tier 3 (Standard) | **99.9%** | ~8.77 hours/year |
| Tier 4 (Best-Effort) | **99%** | ~3.65 days/year |

### 2.3.4 Cryptographic Strength (Confidentiality)

**Information-Theoretic vs Computational Security:**

$$t_{\text{break}} \ge \frac{2^K}{R_{\text{attack}}}$$

where $K$ = key length in bits, $R_{\text{attack}}$ = attacker's computational throughput.

**Symmetric vs Asymmetric Key Equivalence (NIST 2020 estimates):**

| Symmetric Key | RSA / DH Equivalent | Elliptic Curve Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| 128 bits | 3072 bits | 256 bits |
| 192 bits | 7680 bits | 384 bits |
| 256 bits | 15360 bits | 512 bits |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The security requirements framework directly enables:

1. **Procurement & Vendor Evaluation** — RFPs mandate ISO 27001/NIST compliance.
2. **Audit & Certification** — Auditors trace controls back to documented requirements.
3. **Incident Response** — Pre-defined requirements guide triage and escalation.
4. **Insurance Underwriting** — Cyber-insurance premiums are risk-quantified using ALE.
5. **DevSecOps Pipeline** — Security requirements become automated test cases in CI/CD.
6. **Cross-Sector Compliance** — Common Criteria (CC), India's *Indian Common Criteria Certification Scheme (IC3S)*, etc.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Quantitative Risk-Based Security Requirement Prioritisation

> **Scenario (KTU 14-mark style):** A private-sector bank identifies **three candidate security controls** under consideration. Using the risk data below, determine which control delivers the **maximum ROSI** and should be prioritised for implementation.

| Asset | AV (₹) | EF | Threat Description | ARO (Pre-Control) | ARO (Post-Control) | Control Cost (₹/year) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Customer Database | 10,00,000 | 0.8 | SQL Injection | 0.5 | 0.05 | 1,50,000 |
| Payment Gateway | 25,00,000 | 0.6 | DDoS Attack | 0.4 | 0.10 | 3,00,000 |
| Admin Portal | 5,00,000 | 0.9 | Credential Stuffing | 1.0 | 0.20 | 80,000 |

### Solution — Step-by-Step Deduction

**Step 1 — Compute SLE for each asset:**

$$SLE_{\text{DB}} = AV \times EF = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.8 = 8{,}00{,}000$$

$$SLE_{\text{PG}} = 25{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6 = 15{,}00{,}000$$

$$SLE_{\text{AP}} = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.9 = 4{,}50{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Compute ALE Pre-Control:**

$$ALE_{\text{DB,pre}} = SLE \times ARO_{\text{pre}} = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5 = 4{,}00{,}000$$

$$ALE_{\text{PG,pre}} = 15{,}00{,}000 \times 0.4 = 6{,}00{,}000$$

$$ALE_{\text{AP,pre}} = 4{,}50{,}000 \times 1.0 = 4{,}50{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Compute ALE Post-Control:**

$$ALE_{\text{DB,post}} = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.05 = 40{,}000$$

$$ALE_{\text{PG,post}} = 15{,}00{,}000 \times 0.10 = 1{,}50{,}000$$

$$ALE_{\text{AP,post}} = 4{,}50{,}000 \times 0.20 = 90{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Compute Risk Reduction Benefit:**

$$\Delta R_{\text{DB}} = 4{,}00{,}000 - 40{,}000 = 3{,}60{,}000$$

$$\Delta R_{\text{PG}} = 6{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000 = 4{,}50{,}000$$

$$\Delta R_{\text{AP}} = 4{,}50{,}000 - 90{,}000 = 4{,}20{,}000$$

**Step 5 — Compute ROSI for each control:**

$$ROSI_{\text{DB}} = \frac{3{,}60{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000}{1{,}50{,}000} \times 100\% = 140\%$$

$$ROSI_{\text{PG}} = \frac{4{,}50{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} \times 100\% = 50\%$$

$$ROSI_{\text{AP}} = \frac{4{,}20{,}000 - 80{,}000}{80{,}000} \times 100\% = 425\%$$

**Step 6 — Prioritisation Decision Matrix:**

| Rank | Control | ROSI | Decision |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | MFA on Admin Portal | **425%** | **Implement First** |
| 2 | WAF + Input Validation (DB) | **140%** | Implement Second |
| 3 | Anti-DDoS (PG) | **50%** | Implement Third / Negotiate Cost |

> [!TIP]
> **Inference:** The Admin Portal MFA control offers the **highest return per rupee invested** because it is low-cost, high-impact, and addresses the most frequent threat (ARO=1.0). This is the cornerstone of KTU-style risk-based decision-making.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Password Strength Requirement Derivation

> **Scenario:** A government portal handling citizen tax data must enforce a password policy. The security architect requires that passwords resist offline cracking for **at least 5 years** assuming an attacker can perform **$10^{10}$** guesses/second (typical modern GPU rig).

### Solution

**Step 1 — Required minimum entropy:**

$$5 \text{ years} = 5 \times 365.25 \times 24 \times 3600 \approx 1.578 \times 10^8 \text{ seconds}$$

$$\text{Total guesses in 5 years} = 10^{10} \times 1.578 \times 10^8 \approx 1.578 \times 10^{18}$$

**Step 2 — Required entropy in bits (worst case = 50% keyspace searched):**

$$H = \log_2(2 \times 1.578 \times 10^{18}) \approx \log_2(3.156 \times 10^{18}) \approx 61.4 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3 — Derive minimum length assuming mixed-case alphanumeric + symbols ($N = 95$):**

$$L = \left\lceil \frac{H}{\log_2(N)} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{61.4}{6.57} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 9.35 \right\rceil = 10 \text{ characters}$$

**Step 4 — Resulting Security Requirement (formalised):**

> *"The system shall enforce a minimum password length of 12 characters (to provide a safety margin above the 10-character theoretical minimum) drawn from a character set of at least 95 symbols, yielding a minimum entropy of 78.8 bits."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The 12-character requirement provides a **20% safety margin** to accommodate future increases in attacker compute power (Moore's Law) and the inclusion of rate-limiting/throttling controls that the entropy calculation did not factor in.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Availability Requirement Derivation from SLA

> **Scenario:** An e-Governance portal commits to a **99.95% availability SLA**. The system is deployed across **two data centres** in active-active mode. Given $MTBF_1 = 5000$ hours, $MTTR_1 = 4$ hours for DC1 (and same for DC2, both independent), verify whether the dual-DC design meets the SLA.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Individual DC availability:**

$$A_{\text{DC}} = \frac{5000}{5000 + 4} = \frac{5000}{5004} \approx 0.9992 = 99.92\%$$

**Step 2 — Probability of BOTH DCs being down simultaneously (independent):**

$$P_{\text{dual-fail}} = (1 - A_{\text{DC}})^2 = (0.0008)^2 = 6.4 \times 10^{-7}$$

**Step 3 — Combined system availability:**

$$A_{\text{system}} = 1 - P_{\text{dual-fail}} = 1 - 6.4 \times 10^{-7} \approx 0.99999936 = 99.9999\%$$

**Step 4 — Compare against SLA target of 99.95%:**

$$A_{\text{system}} = 99.9999\% \;\gg\; A_{\text{SLA}} = 99.95\% \quad \checkmark \text{ SLA MET}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Inference:** The dual active-active DC architecture **vastly exceeds** the SLA requirement, providing an order-of-magnitude safety margin. This is a typical KTU question pattern — students are expected to compute, compare, and recommend.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — KTU 2024 Required Concepts

| Concept | Definition | Public Sector Example | Private Sector Example | KTU Marks Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Confidentiality Requirement | Prevent unauthorised disclosure | Classified government data encryption | Customer credit card tokenisation | **High** |
| Integrity Requirement | Prevent unauthorised modification | Electoral database tamper-proofing | Financial ledger immutability | **High** |
| Availability Requirement | Ensure timely access | e-Hospital, Passport Seva uptime | E-commerce site during sales | **High** |
| Authentication Requirement | Verify identity | eSign, Aadhaar-based auth | SSO with MFA for employees | **High** |
| Authorization Requirement | Enforce access policies | Need-to-know clearance levels | RBAC in ERP systems | **High** |
| Non-Reprepudiation Requirement | Prevent denial of actions | Digital signatures on tenders | Blockchain-based audit trail | **Medium** |
| Privacy Requirement | Protect PII | DPDP Act 2023 compliance | GDPR consent management | **High** |
| Auditability Requirement | Trace all security events | CAG-auditable logs | SOX-mandated financial trails | **Medium** |
| Compliance Requirement | Adhere to laws/standards | IT Act 2000, NCIIPC | ISO 27001, PCI-DSS | **High** |
| Resilience Requirement | Withstand disruptions | Disaster recovery for citizen services | BCP for supply chain | **Medium** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Security Requirements Engineering Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Stakeholder Identification] --> A2[Requirement Elicitation]
    A2 --> A3[Requirement Analysis and Negotiation]
    A3 --> A4[Requirement Specification SRS-Sec]
    A4 --> A5[Validation and Verification]
    A5 --> A6{Approved?}
    A6 -- No --> A2
    A6 -- Yes --> A7[Requirement Management and Evolution]
    A7 --> A8[System Implementation]
    A8 --> A9[Security Testing and Audit]
    A9 --> A10{Defects Found?}
    A10 -- Yes --> A4
    A10 -- No --> A11[Operational System]
    A11 --> A12[Change Triggered]
    A12 --> A2
```

## 4.2 Sources of Security Requirements (Hub-and-Spoke)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SR[Security Requirements Baseline]
    L1[IT Act 2000 and DPDP Act 2023]
    L2[GDPR and HIPAA]
    L3[ISO 27001 and NIST CSF]
    L4[Organisational Policy]
    L5[Risk Assessment Output]
    L6[Threat Intelligence]
    L7[Business Objectives]
    L8[Past Incident Reports]
    L9[Best Practices OWASP CIS]
    SR --- L1
    SR --- L2
    SR --- L3
    SR --- L4
    SR --- L5
    SR --- L6
    SR --- L7
    SR --- L8
    SR --- L9
```

## 4.3 Public vs Private Sector Security Requirements — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PUB[PUBLIC SECTOR ARCHITECTURE]
        P1[Statutory Mandate]
        P2[National Security Directive]
        P3[CERT-In NCIIPC]
        P4[Sovereign Data Residency]
        P5[Citizen Service Uptime SLA]
        P1 --> P6[Public SR Baseline]
        P2 --> P6
        P3 --> P6
        P4 --> P6
        P5 --> P6
    end
    subgraph PRV[PRIVATE SECTOR ARCHITECTURE]
        Q1[Contractual Obligation]
        Q2[Market Differentiation]
        Q3[CISO Risk Register]
        Q4[Customer Trust and Brand]
        Q5[Regulatory Compliance PCI-DSS SOX]
        Q1 --> Q7[Private SR Baseline]
        Q2 --> Q7
        Q3 --> Q7
        Q4 --> Q7
        Q5 --> Q7
    end
    P6 --> UN[UNIFIED SR DOCUMENT]
    Q7 --> UN
    UN --> IM[Implementation and Audit]
```

## 4.4 CIA+ Security Requirements Taxonomy (Hierarchical Decomposition)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SR[Security Requirements]
    SR --> C[Confidentiality]
    SR --> I[Integrity]
    SR --> A[Availability]
    SR --> AUTH[Authentication]
    SR --> AUTHZ[Authorization]
    SR --> NR[Non-Repudiation]
    SR --> AC[Accountability]
    SR --> PR[Privacy]
    C --> C1[Encryption at Rest and Transit]
    C --> C2[Access Control Lists]
    C --> C3[Data Classification]
    I --> I1[Hashing HMAC Digital Signatures]
    I --> I2[Change Management]
    I --> I3[Database Constraints]
    A --> A1[Redundancy and Failover]
    A --> A2[Backup and DR]
    A --> A3[DDoS Mitigation]
    AUTH --> AU1[MFA Biometrics]
    AUTH --> AU2[Certificate based PKI]
    AUTHZ --> AZ1[RBAC ABAC MAC]
    NR --> N1[Digital Signatures]
    NR --> N2[TPM Attestation]
    AC --> ACC1[Audit Logging SIEM]
    PR --> PR1[Data Minimisation]
    PR --> PR2[Consent Management]
```

## 4.5 Risk-Based Requirement Prioritisation Funnel

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RISK[Identified Risks] --> ASSESS[Risk Assessment]
    ASSESS --> CALC[Compute ALE]
    CALC --> RANK[Rank by ALE]
    RANK --> CONTROL[Identify Candidate Controls]
    CONTROL --> COST[Cost Benefit Analysis]
    COST --> ROSI[Compute ROSI]
    ROSI --> SEL[Select Controls above ROSI Threshold]
    SEL --> DOC[Document as Security Requirements]
    DOC --> IMPL[Implement and Verify]
```

## 4.6 Security Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM) Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRE[Security Requirement Elicited]
    SRS[SRS Section Number]
    DES[Design Document Mapping]
    COD[Code Module Reference]
    TST[Test Case ID]
    CMP[Compliance Clause ID]
    AUD[Audit Evidence]
    SRE --> SRS
    SRS --> DES
    DES --> COD
    COD --> TST
    TST --> CMP
    CMP --> AUD
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide for KTU Exams:**
> - Section 4.1 — Use when asked to "explain the SRE process".
> - Section 4.2 — Use for "list and explain sources of security requirements".
> - Section 4.3 — Use for "compare security requirements in public and private sectors".
> - Section 4.4 — Use for "classify security requirements" or "explain CIA+ model".
> - Section 4.5 — Use for "how are security requirements prioritised".
> - Section 4.6 — Use for "explain requirement traceability" or "RTM concept".
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**
>
> **Q1. Define "Security Requirements" and list any SIX primary categories of security requirements recognised in the KTU 2024 syllabus for information security.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Definition (1 mark):** Security requirements are precise, testable statements of security functionality that an information system must provide, or security constraints it must satisfy, in order to protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information assets.
>
> **Six Primary Categories (2 marks — list with brief notes):**
> 1. **Confidentiality** — Restrict information access to authorised entities only.
> 2. **Integrity** — Ensure data is accurate, complete, and unaltered by unauthorised parties.
> 3. **Availability** — Ensure systems and data are accessible when needed by authorised users.
> 4. **Authentication** — Verify the identity of users, systems, or processes.
> 5. **Authorization** — Grant specific access rights to authenticated entities based on policy.
> 6. **Non-Repudiation** — Ensure that actions cannot be denied by the originating party.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**
>
> **Q2. Differentiate between Functional and Non-Functional Security Requirements with one example each.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Aspect | Functional SR | Non-Functional SR |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Definition** | Specifies *what* security action the system must perform | Specifies *how well* the security action must perform |
> | **Nature** | Behavioural, testable by feature | Quality-based, testable by metric |
> | **Example** | "The system shall encrypt all data in transit using TLS 1.3" (1.5 marks) | "Encryption must be applied with a latency overhead not exceeding 5 ms per request" (1.5 marks) |
> | **Verification** | Functional test, use-case validation | Performance test, SLA validation, load test |

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3 Internal Choice Set]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Security Requirements Engineering (SRE) lifecycle**. Discuss the role of **misuse cases** in eliciting security requirements. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A government e-Governance portal is being designed to deliver citizen services 24×7. Identify and justify **FIVE specific security requirements** (one from each major category: CIA + Authentication + Non-Repudiation). For each requirement, specify the **testable acceptance criterion**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Introduce SRE (1 mark):**
Security Requirements Engineering (SRE) is the systematic process of eliciting, analysing, specifying, validating, and managing security requirements throughout the system development lifecycle. It ensures that security is treated as a **first-class design concern**, not an afterthought.

**Step 2 — Explain each stage (5 marks — 1 mark each):**

| Stage | Activity |
|---|---|
| **1. Elicitation** | Discover requirements via interviews, questionnaires, document analysis, threat modelling (STRIDE), and reuse of standards (ISO 27002, NIST 800-53). |
| **2. Analysis & Negotiation** | Resolve conflicts, prioritise using MoSCoW or risk scoring, and perform feasibility analysis. |
| **3. Specification** | Produce the Security Requirements Specification (SRS-Sec) document with functional, non-functional, and constraint requirements. |
| **4. Validation & Verification** | Confirm correctness, completeness, testability via reviews, prototyping, formal methods, and penetration testing. |
| **5. Management & Evolution** | Maintain traceability, perform change control, version updates, and impact analysis throughout the lifecycle. |

**Step 3 — Misuse Cases (1 mark):**
A misuse case describes a *malicious* actor interacting with the system in a way that violates a security requirement. It is the inverse of a use case. Example: *"An external attacker injects malicious SQL via the login form to extract the user credentials database."* Misuse cases are elicited during threat modelling and help derive negative requirements (e.g., "The system shall reject all SQL meta-characters in input fields").

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Five Security Requirements for the e-Governance Portal:**

| # | Category | Security Requirement | Testable Acceptance Criterion |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Confidentiality** (1.5 marks) | All citizen PII transmitted between client and server must be encrypted using **TLS 1.3** or higher with forward secrecy enabled. | Automated scan (e.g., SSL Labs) must return grade A or above; no cipher below 256-bit strength permitted. |
| 2 | **Integrity** (1.5 marks) | All submitted citizen applications must be digitally signed at the database level using SHA-256 HMAC before storage. | Any unauthorised modification detected by HMAC comparison must trigger an alert within 5 seconds and reject the change. |
| 3 | **Availability** (1.5 marks) | The portal must maintain **99.95% uptime** measured monthly, with no single outage exceeding 30 minutes. | Monthly availability report from monitoring tool must show $\ge 99.95\%$; logs of any outage $> 30$ min must be escalated. |
| 4 | **Authentication** (1.5 marks) | All citizens must authenticate using **Aadhaar-based OTP or biometric** verification; staff must use MFA (password + hardware token). | Penetration test must confirm that no authentication bypass is possible; brute-force attempts must be rate-limited to 5/minute per account. |
| 5 | **Non-Repudiation** (1 mark) | All approval actions by government officers must be digitally signed using a **DSC issued by a CCA-licensed Certifying Authority**; the signed audit trail must be tamper-evident. | Cryptographic verification of any approval action must succeed against the published CA chain; the audit log must use append-only storage with hash chaining. |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3 Internal Choice Set]**
>
> **(a)** Compare and contrast the **security requirements landscape in Public Sector vs Private Sector** organisations. Provide at least **THREE distinguishing dimensions** with examples from Indian context. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a worked numerical example, explain **Quantitative Risk Assessment** using SLE, ALE, and ROSI. Show how it is used to **prioritise security requirements** for implementation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Comparison Table (5 marks — 5 rows × 1 mark each):**

| Dimension | Public Sector (India) | Private Sector (India) |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Driver** | Statutory / Legal mandate (e.g., IT Act 2000 §43A, DPDP Act 2023, RTI Act 2005) | Business risk and customer trust (e.g., RBI cyber framework for banks) |
| **Governance Model** | Centralised — MeitY, CERT-In, NCIIPC, sectoral regulators (e.g., SEBI, RBI) | Decentralised — Board-level Risk Committee → CISO → Business Unit Heads |
| **Compliance Standards** | e-Governance standards, NeGP, Digital India framework, ISNP, STQC | ISO 27001/27002, PCI-DSS, SOC 2, RBI cybersecurity guidelines |
| **Transparency & Audit** | High — Right to Information (RTI), CAG audits, parliamentary questions | Limited — internal audit + statutory auditor + regulator inspections |
| **Data Sovereignty** | Mandated — data of Indian citizens must reside in India (MeitY clarification, 2022) | Flexible — based on business and cost considerations (subject to DPDP compliance) |

**Synthesis (2 marks):**
While both sectors share the **CIA triad** as foundational security requirements, the *drivers*, *governance*, and *transparency obligations* differ fundamentally. Public sector requirements emphasise **sovereignty, citizen rights, and statutory compliance**, whereas private sector requirements emphasise **business continuity, customer trust, and competitive advantage**. Convergence occurs in areas of common interest — national critical infrastructure (e.g., banking, telecom) — where both regulatory and business drivers apply simultaneously.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Define Quantitative Risk (1 mark):**
Quantitative risk assessment uses **monetary values** and **probabilities** to objectively prioritise security requirements. Unlike qualitative risk analysis (High/Medium/Low), it enables ROI-based decision-making.

**Step 2 — State the Formulas (1.5 marks):**

$$SLE = AV \times EF \qquad ALE = SLE \times ARO \qquad ROSI = \frac{ALE_{\text{pre}} - ALE_{\text{post}} - C_{\text{control}}}{C_{\text{control}}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 3 — Worked Example (3.5 marks):**

> A private e-commerce firm has a customer database worth **₹2,00,00,000**. A data breach (threat: SQL injection) would expose **70%** of the records, occurring on average **once every 2 years**. Implementation of a Web Application Firewall (WAF) costs **₹5,00,000/year** and reduces breach frequency to once every 20 years.

**Solution:**

$$SLE = 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.7 = 1{,}40{,}00{,}000$$

$$ALE_{\text{pre}} = 1{,}40{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5 = 70{,}00{,}000$$

$$ALE_{\text{post}} = 1{,}40{,}00{,}000 \times 0.05 = 7{,}00{,}000$$

$$ROSI = \frac{70{,}00{,}000 - 7{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \times 100\% = \frac{58{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \times 100\% = 1160\%$$

**Step 4 — Prioritisation Decision (1 mark):**
Since $ROSI = 1160\% \gg 0$, the WAF control offers exceptional value and must be prioritised as a **mandatory security requirement**. The same methodology is applied iteratively across all candidate controls to construct the final security requirements baseline, ordered by ROSI in descending order.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing Security Requirements with Security Controls** — A *requirement* is a "what"; a *control* is a "how". Students lose 1–2 marks for interchanging these terms.
> 2. **Omitting Testable Acceptance Criteria** — KTU 2024 emphasises *testability*. Every security requirement must include a measurable acceptance criterion (e.g., "99.95% uptime", "AES-256 encryption"). Vague requirements fetch partial marks only.
> 3. **Skipping the Public vs Private Sector Contrast** — This is the *course signature topic*. Examiners allocate a full 7-mark sub-question to it. Students who treat it as a 2-line answer lose heavily.
> 4. **Forgetting Non-Repudiation and Privacy** — Most students recall CIA but skip the extended requirements. KTU specifically tests the **CIA+ AANP** model.
> 5. **No Numerical Justification** — In quantitative questions, students must *show* the formula substitution step-by-step. Skipping the intermediate $SLE$ calculation before computing $ALE$ is a common 1-mark deduction.
> 6. **Misuse Case Confusion** — Examiners expect students to *contrast* a use case with a misuse case, not just define the latter.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary — Print Before Exam!**

- 🔹 **Security Requirements** = testable statements of security *needs* (NOT controls, NOT policies).
- 🔹 **CIA + AANP** is the canonical taxonomy — Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability, Authentication, Authorization, Non-Repudiation, Privacy.
- 🔹 **Functional SR** = *what* the system does; **Non-Functional SR** = *how well* it does it (performance, latency, availability %).
- 🔹 **SRE Lifecycle** = Elicitation → Analysis → Specification → Validation → Management (5 stages, iterative).
- 🔹 **Misuse Case** = a malicious actor scenario used to derive *negative* requirements.
- 🔹 **Sources of SR** = Laws (IT Act 2000, DPDP 2023, GDPR), Standards (ISO 27001, NIST CSF), Risk Assessment, Business Objectives, Past Incidents, Threat Intel, Best Practices.
- 🔹 **Public Sector drivers** = Sovereignty, statutory compliance, RTI/CAG transparency, citizen service uptime.
- 🔹 **Private Sector drivers** = ROI, customer trust, competitive advantage, contractual obligations.
- 🔹 **SLE = AV × EF**; **ALE = SLE × ARO**; **ROSI = [(ALE_pre − ALE_post − Control_Cost) / Control_Cost] × 100%**.
- 🔹 **Positive ROSI** → implement the control as a security requirement; **Negative ROSI** → re-evaluate or seek cheaper alternative.
- 🔹 **MTBF / MTTR** → Availability = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR).
- 🔹 **TLS 1.3, AES-256, SHA-256** are current KTU-accepted cryptographic baselines.
- 🔹 **Traceability Matrix** = maps each requirement to design, code, test, and compliance clause.
- 🔹 **MFA, RBAC/ABAC, DLP, SIEM, PKI, HSM** are typical implementations of security requirements.
- 🔹 **Documentation** = SRS-Sec (Security Requirements Specification) is the formal deliverable.
- 🔹 **Indian Regulators to remember** = MeitY, CERT-In, NCIIPC, RBI, SEBI, IRDAI, CCA (for DSC).
- 🔹 **Key Standards to remember** = ISO 27001/27002, NIST CSF, NIST 800-53, PCI-DSS, OWASP ASVS, CIS Controls v8.
- 🔹 **Examiners LOVE diagrams** — Always include the SRE lifecycle diagram, CIA taxonomy, and a comparison table for full marks.
- 🔹 **Testability is KING** — Every security requirement you write MUST be measurable.

---

> **End of Module 3 — Security Requirements | PECCT753 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reliability and Integrity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT753 - INFORMATION SECURITY IN PUBLIC AND PRIVATE SECTORS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Reliability and Integrity # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Reliability — Formal Definition In the context of **Information Security**, **Reliability** is formally defined as the probability that a system, component, or data element will perform its intended function correctly under designated operating conditions, without failure, over a specified period of time. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim):** Reliability is the probability of a system performing its purpose adequately for the period of time intended, under the expected operating conditions and environment. In public and private sector information systems, reliability is a *non-negotiable security attribute* because unauthorized downtime, data loss, or service interruption directly translates to breaches of **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (CIA Triad)**. Mathematically, reliability $R(t)$ is expressed as: $$R(t) = P(T > t)$$ where $T$ is the time-to-failure random variable and $t$ is the mission time. The reliability lies in the closed interval $[0, 1]$ or equivalently $[0\%, 100\%]$. ### Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Lifetime of a Car Engine"** > Imagine purchasing a car. The manufacturer publishes a reliability figure: *"99% of vehicles of this model operate without major engine failure for the first 5 years."* This is a **reliability statement** — it does not guarantee your car will work; it merely quantifies the likelihood that the system (engine) will not fail within a defined window. In the same way, an information system (a banking server, a defense database, an e-governance portal) carries a *reliability rating* based on its hardware quality, software maturity, redundancy, and maintenance policy. A system with reliability $R(t) = 0.999$ is *highly* reliable; one with $R(t) = 0.5$ is essentially a coin-flip. ## 1.2 Integrity — Formal Definition **Integrity** in information security refers to the property whereby data has not been modified, altered, destroyed, or tampered with in an unauthorized or undetected manner during transmission, processing, or storage. It guarantees that information remains *authentic, complete, and trustworthy* from source to destination. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim):** Integrity is the assurance that information has not been modified in transit (data integrity) and that the system and its data are accessed only by authorized parties in authorized ways (system integrity). It protects against both *accidental corruption* (e.g., hardware faults) and *malicious modification* (e.g., insider attack, MITM). ### Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy — "Sealed Postal Letter with Tamper-Evident Wax"** > Consider a registered post parcel sealed with a wax stamp. The wax seal is an *integrity mechanism* — if the letter is opened or altered in transit, the seal breaks visibly. Similarly, in information systems, integrity is enforced using **hash functions (SHA-256), digital signatures, Message Authentication Codes (MACs), checksums, and error-correcting codes**. A tampered email will yield a different hash digest; a modified database record will fail its checksum — these are the *digital wax seals* of modern security. ## 1.3 The CIA Triad Placement Both **Reliability** and **Integrity** are core pillars of the **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability). Their relationship is summarized below: | Security Pillar | Primary Concern | Failure Impact | |---|---|---| | **Reliability** | Continuous correct operation over time | Service outage, data loss | | **Integrity** | Trustworthiness and unaltered state of data | Tampered records, false data acceptance | > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU PECCT753, *Reliability and Integrity* are studied as **two complementary but distinct** security services. Reliability addresses *temporal correctness* (will the system be there when needed?), while Integrity addresses *content correctness* (is the data genuine?). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Reliability — Theoretical Foundations Reliability engineering rests on three foundational concepts: ### 2.1.1 Failure Rate ($\lambda$) The **failure rate** (or *hazard rate*) is the frequency at which a component or system fails per unit of time. It is measured in **Failures In Time (FIT)** — typically expressed as failures per $10^9$ device-hours. $$\lambda = \frac{\text{Number of Failures}}{\text{Total Operating Time}}$$ ### 2.1.2 Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) **MTBF** is the arithmetic mean (average) time elapsed between two consecutive failures of a repairable system. It is the *inverse of the failure rate*: $$\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$ MTBF is typically expressed in **hours** (also minutes, days, or years depending on domain). ### 2.1.3 Mean Time To Repair (MTTR) **MTTR** is the average time required to repair a failed component and restore the system to operational state. It includes diagnosis, repair, testing, and restoration. ### 2.1.4 Availability ($A$) **Availability** is the proportion of time that a system is in a functional/operational state. It links reliability (MTBF) with maintainability (MTTR): $$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$ Availability is often expressed as a percentage or in *"nines"* notation (e.g., **"five nines"** = 99.999%). ### 2.1.5 Reliability Function For components with constant (exponential) failure rate, reliability follows an **exponential distribution**: $$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$ This is the canonical reliability expression used in KTU and competitive examinations. ## 2.2 Integrity — Theoretical Foundations Data integrity rests on two principal mechanisms: ### 2.2.1 Error Detection Detection mechanisms identify (but do not correct) bit errors. Common methods: - **Parity Bit**: Single bit appended; detects single-bit errors. - **Checksum**: Arithmetic sum/CRC; detects burst errors. - **Hash Function (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256)**: Cryptographic detection of any modification. ### 2.2.2 Error Correction Correction mechanisms both *detect and correct* errors without retransmission. Common methods: - **Hamming Code**: Single-bit error correction (SEC). - **Reed-Solomon Code**: Used in CDs, DVDs, QR codes, satellite comms. - **Forward Error Correction (FEC)**: Used in real-time streaming, deep-space telemetry. ### 2.2.3 Parity Bit Formula For an $n$-bit data word, the **even parity bit** $P$ is computed as: $$P = d_1 \oplus d_2 \oplus d_3 \oplus \cdots \oplus d_n \pmod{2}$$ The receiver re-computes parity on the received word. A mismatch indicates an error. ### 2.2.4 Hamming Distance The **Hamming distance** $H(x, y)$ between two codewords $x$ and $y$ is the number of bit positions in which they differ. A coding scheme's error detection/correction capability is governed by the *minimum Hamming distance* $d_{\min}$: - To detect $s$ errors: $d_{\min} \geq s + 1$ - To correct $t$ errors: $d_{\min} \geq 2t + 1$ ## 2.3 System Reliability — Series and Parallel Configurations ### 2.3.1 Series Configuration In a **series system**, all components must function for the system to function: $$R_{\text{series}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i = R_1 \times R_2 \times \cdots \times R_n$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > *Insight:* A series system's overall reliability is **lower** than its weakest component. Adding more components to a series chain *reduces* reliability. ### 2.3.2 Parallel Configuration (Redundancy) In a **parallel (redundant) system**, at least one of the components must function for the system to function: $$R_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - R_i)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > *Insight:* A parallel system's overall reliability is **higher** than its strongest component. Adding parallel redundancy *increases* reliability — this is the foundation of fault-tolerant systems (e.g., RAID, dual power supplies, mirrored databases in public-sector banking servers). ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Reliability Function (Exponential) | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Dimensionless ($0 \le R \le 1$) | | 2 | Failure Rate | $\lambda = 1 / \text{MTBF}$ | Failures/hour or **FIT** ($10^{-9}$/hr) | | 3 | MTBF | $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda$ | Hours | | 4 | Availability | $A = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$ | Percentage or "nines" | | 5 | Unavailability (Downtime) | $U = 1 - A = \text{MTTR} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$ | Fraction | | 6 | Series System Reliability | $R_s = \prod R_i$ | Multiplicative | | 7 | Parallel System Reliability | $R_p = 1 - \prod (1 - R_i)$ | Redundancy | | 8 | Parity Bit | $P = \sum d_i \pmod 2$ | Even/Odd parity | | 9 | Error Detection | $d_{\min} \geq s + 1$ | Detect $s$ errors | | 10 | Error Correction | $d_{\min} \geq 2t + 1$ | Correct $t$ errors | | 11 | CRC Polynomial Degree | Generator of degree $n$ detects bursts $\leq n$ bits | Polynomial arithmetic | | 12 | Hamming Code Word Length | $n = 2^r - 1$ (for $r$ parity bits) | $r$ check bits | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** KTU examiners *love* asking numerical problems on (a) Series-Parallel reliability, (b) MTBF/Availability computation, and (c) Parity / Hamming code construction. Memorize the **table above verbatim**. ## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering - **Banking & Finance (Public Sector):** Core banking servers demand **"five nines"** (99.999%) availability — about 5.26 minutes of downtime/year. This is achieved through parallel redundancy (dual servers, mirrored databases, hot standby). - **Defense & Aerospace:** Mission-critical radar and satellite systems use **error-correcting codes (Reed-Solomon, convolutional codes)** to ensure data integrity in the presence of cosmic radiation noise. - **E-Governance (Kerala State):** Integrated Financial Management System (IFMS), K-Smart, and SPARK portals require high-reliability servers to ensure uninterrupted citizen services. - **Healthcare (Aarogya Setu / e-Hospital):** Patient records must be protected with **SHA-256 hashes + digital signatures** to ensure integrity across the entire data lifecycle. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Exponential Reliability Function **Assumption:** The system has a *constant failure rate* $\lambda$ (memoryless property). The probability density function (PDF) of time-to-failure $T$ is: $$f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}, \quad t \geq 0$$ The **cumulative distribution function (CDF)** is the probability that the system fails by time $t$: $$ F(t) = P(T \leq t) = \int_{0}^{t} \lambda e^{-\lambda x}\, dx $$ Step-by-step evaluation: $$ F(t) = \lambda \left[ \frac{-e^{-\lambda x}}{\lambda} \right]_{0}^{t} $$ $$ F(t) = -e^{-\lambda t} - (-e^{0}) = -e^{-\lambda t} + 1 $$ $$ F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t} $$ Reliability is the complement — the probability that the system survives beyond time $t$: $$ R(t) = P(T > t) = 1 - F(t) = 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t}) $$ $$ \boxed{R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Conversion Logic:** Reliability is the *survival* probability. Since $F(t)$ represents failure probability and the total probability space sums to 1, we subtract $F(t)$ from unity. The resulting expression is the canonical exponential reliability function. ## 3.2 Derivation: Availability from MTBF and MTTR Consider a single renewal cycle: a system operates for MTBF hours, then fails and is repaired in MTTR hours, then operates again. The total cycle length is: $$ T_{\text{cycle}} = \text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR} $$ The proportion of time the system is *available* is the ratio of *uptime* to *total cycle time*: $$ A = \frac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Total Time}} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} $$ Algebraic manipulation to express unavailability: $$ U = 1 - A = 1 - \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR} - \text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} $$ $$ \boxed{U = \frac{\text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}} $$ **Numerical Worked Example:** A web server has MTBF = 5000 hours and MTTR = 5 hours. Compute availability. $$ A = \frac{5000}{5000 + 5} = \frac{5000}{5005} \approx 0.999001 \approx 99.90\% $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** This is approximately *"three nines"* availability, meaning a downtime of about $\approx 8.76$ hours per year. For higher tiers (5 nines = 99.999%), MTBF must increase or MTTR must decrease by orders of magnitude — typically achieved through redundancy. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Series vs. Parallel Reliability (Numerical) **Problem:** A defense communication system has 3 components in series with individual reliabilities $R_1 = 0.95$, $R_2 = 0.98$, $R_3 = 0.92$. Compute the overall reliability. ### Series Computation $$ R_s = R_1 \times R_2 \times R_3 = 0.95 \times 0.98 \times 0.92 $$ Step-by-step multiplication: $$ R_s = 0.95 \times 0.98 = 0.9310 $$ $$ R_s = 0.9310 \times 0.92 = 0.8565 $$ $$ \boxed{R_s \approx 0.857 \text{ (or } 85.7\% \text{)}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Conversion Logic:** Each factor in a series system multiplies the cumulative probability. Since all three components must work, their reliabilities are multiplied. The system is weaker than its weakest link (0.92). **Problem:** A banking server cluster has 2 components in *parallel* with $R_1 = 0.90$, $R_2 = 0.90$. Compute overall reliability. ### Parallel Computation The probability that *both* fail is: $$ P(\text{both fail}) = (1 - R_1)(1 - R_2) = 0.10 \times 0.10 = 0.01 $$ System reliability (at least one works): $$ R_p = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99 $$ $$ \boxed{R_p = 0.99 \text{ (or } 99\% \text{)}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Conversion Logic:** Parallel systems work if *at least one* component works. So we compute the *complement* (probability all fail) and subtract from 1. With two parallel components each at 90%, the system reaches 99% — a clear demonstration of redundancy gain. ## 3.4 Derivation: Parity Bit Construction Given a 7-bit data word $D = d_1 d_2 d_3 d_4 d_5 d_6 d_7$, the **even parity bit** $P$ is: $$ P = d_1 \oplus d_2 \oplus d_3 \oplus d_4 \oplus d_5 \oplus d_6 \oplus d_7 \pmod 2 $$ **Numerical Example:** Data = `1011001` (7 bits). Compute parity bit. Counting the number of 1s: 1, 0, 1, 1, 0, 0, 1 → there are **four** 1s (already even). Therefore: $$ P = 0 \text{ (to preserve even parity: 4 + 0 = 4, which is even)} $$ Transmitted codeword: `10110010` (data + parity = 8 bits). **Receiver-Side Verification:** The receiver counts 1s in the entire 8-bit codeword. If the count is even, no detected error. If odd, an error has occurred (though *which* bit is wrong is not known with simple parity — for that, we need **Hamming code**). ## 3.5 Derivation: Hamming Code Error Detection Position A Hamming code with $r$ redundant (parity) bits can check $2^r - 1$ bit positions, and the syndrome (error pattern) directly points to the position of a single-bit error. The relationship between data bits $k$ and total bits $n$ is: $$ n = k + r, \quad \text{with} \quad 2^r \geq n + 1 $$ Solving for $r$ in terms of $k$: $$ 2^r \geq k + r + 1 $$ **Numerical Example:** $k = 4$ data bits. Find the smallest $r$. Try $r = 3$: $$ 2^3 = 8, \quad k + r + 1 = 4 + 3 + 1 = 8 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2^r = 8 \geq 8 \checkmark $$ So $r = 3$ parity bits suffice. Total word length: $n = 4 + 3 = 7$. The Hamming(7,4) code is the classic. > [!NOTE] > **Conversion Logic:** The inequality $2^r \geq n + 1$ ensures that every single-bit error produces a *unique syndrome*, allowing the receiver to pinpoint the erroneous bit. With $r$ check bits we can represent $2^r$ syndromes — one for "no error" and the rest for each possible bit position. ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — CRC Polynomial Interpretation A **Cyclic Redundancy Check (CRC)** treats bit strings as polynomials over GF(2). For a generator polynomial $G(x)$ of degree $n$: - It detects *all single-bit errors*. - It detects *all double-bit errors* if $G(x)$ has at least three terms. - It detects *all burst errors* of length $\leq n$ bits. - It detects *any odd number of bit errors* if $G(x)$ contains the factor $(x+1)$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Practical CRC Standards:** CRC-32 (Ethernet) uses polynomial $x^{32} + x^{26} + x^{23} + x^{22} + x^{16} + x^{12} + x^{11} + x^{10} + x^{8} + x^{7} + x^{5} + x^{4} + x^{2} + x + 1$. CRC-CCITT uses $x^{16} + x^{12} + x^{5} + 1$. These are the integrity backbones of modern networking. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 System Reliability Block Diagram (Mermaid) The following Mermaid diagram shows a **fault-tolerant banking server architecture** (used in public-sector core banking systems) with series and parallel reliability blocks. ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Client Request] --> B[Load Balancer
R equals 0.99] B --> C[Web Server 1
R equals 0.95] B --> D[Web Server 2
R equals 0.95] B --> E[Web Server 3
R equals 0.95] C --> F[Database Cluster] D --> F E --> F F --> G[DB Primary
R equals 0.98] F --> H[DB Mirror
R equals 0.98] G --> I[Storage RAID 6
R equals 0.999] H --> I I --> J[Response to Client] style B fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#a4c2f4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style I fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` **Reading the diagram:** - **Web Server Cluster (3 in parallel):** Achieves high availability through redundancy — if one server fails, the other two continue serving requests. - **Database Cluster (Primary + Mirror in parallel):** Mirrored architecture ensures data integrity and reliability. - **RAID 6 Storage:** Storage-level redundancy tolerates two simultaneous disk failures. ## 4.2 Data Integrity Verification Flow The following flowchart shows the end-to-end **data integrity verification process** used in secure message transmission (e.g., in e-governance or defense communication). ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Sender
Plaintext Message M] --> B[Compute Hash
H equals SHA-256 of M] B --> C[Encrypt Hash
with Private Key
Digital Signature S] A --> D[Append Signature S
to Message M] C --> D D --> E[Transmit over
Insecure Channel] E --> F[Receiver
Receives M prime and S] F --> G[Decrypt Signature S
with Public Key
to recover H1] F --> H[Compute Hash
H2 equals SHA-256 of M prime] G --> I{H1 equals H2} H --> I I -->|Yes| J[Integrity Verified
Accept Message] I -->|No| K[Integrity Violated
Reject Message] style B fill:#f9cb9c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#b4a7d6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style I fill:#ffe599,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style J fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style K fill:#ea9999,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` **Block-Level Functional Architecture Explanation:** | Stage | Function | Security Service | |---|---|---| | Sender | Generates plaintext message | Originator | | Hash Computation | SHA-256 produces fixed-length digest | Integrity foundation | | Signature Encryption | Sender's private key signs the hash | Authentication + Non-repudiation | | Transmission | Message travels over network | Inherently insecure | | Receiver Decryption | Public key recovers hash | Authentication check | | Hash Comparison | Bit-by-bit equality test | Integrity verdict | | Verdict | Accept or Reject | Security outcome | ## 4.3 Hamming Code Encoding-Decoding Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB A[4-bit Data Word
d1 d2 d3 d4] --> B[Insert Parity Bits
at positions 1 2 4] B --> C[Hamming 7 4 Codeword
p1 p2 d1 p3 d2 d3 d4] C --> D[Transmit over Noisy Channel] D --> E[Received Codeword
possibly with error] E --> F[Recompute Syndrome
S equals sum of pi where pi mismatches] F --> G{S equals 0} G -->|Yes| H[No Error
Accept] G -->|No| I[Flip Bit at Position S] I --> J[Corrected Codeword] J --> K[Extract 4-bit Data] style B fill:#c9daf8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#f9cb9c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style G fill:#ffe599,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style I fill:#ea9999,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style K fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The *syndrome* is a binary number whose value equals the position of the single-bit error. For example, syndrome $S = 5$ (binary `101`) means bit 5 is in error. This elegant self-correcting property is why Hamming codes revolutionized digital memory integrity (used in ECC RAM). # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Reliability. Differentiate between MTBF and MTTR. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** **Reliability** is the probability that a system or component will perform its intended function under designated operating conditions for a specified period of time without failure. | Aspect | MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) | MTTR (Mean Time To Repair) | |---|---|---| | **Meaning** | Average time between two consecutive failures | Average time to restore a system after a failure | | **Reflects** | Reliability of the system | Maintainability of the system | | **Desired Value** | High | Low | | **Formula** | $\text{MTBF} = 1 / \lambda$ | Empirical measurement | > [!NOTE] > **Examiner Insight:** The *combined* ratio $\text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$ gives availability. Students often confuse MTBF with MTTR — clearly stating the difference earns full marks. ### Question 2: What is Data Integrity? List any two mechanisms to ensure data integrity. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** **Data Integrity** is the property that ensures information is not altered or destroyed in an unauthorized manner during storage, processing, or transit. It guarantees that the data received is exactly the data sent — complete, accurate, and authentic. **Two mechanisms to ensure data integrity:** 1. **Hash Functions (e.g., SHA-256):** Produce a fixed-length unique digest of the data. Any modification to the data produces a different hash, immediately revealing tampering. 2. **Cyclic Redundancy Check (CRC):** A polynomial-based error-detection code widely used in Ethernet, Wi-Fi, and storage devices. It detects burst errors efficiently using low computational overhead. > [!NOTE] > **Examiner Insight:** KTU also accepts *parity bits, checksums, MACs, digital signatures, and Hamming codes* as valid answers. Mentioning at least one *cryptographic* and one *non-cryptographic* method scores extra credit. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **A public-sector data center has three independent components in series with reliabilities 0.95, 0.92, and 0.88. Compute the system reliability. If the weakest component is backed up by an identical redundant component in parallel, recompute the system reliability and comment. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Compute series reliability of all three original components. $$ R_{\text{series}} = R_1 \times R_2 \times R_3 $$ $$ R_{\text{series}} = 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 0.88 $$ Step-by-step multiplication: $$ 0.95 \times 0.92 = 0.8740 $$ $$ 0.8740 \times 0.88 = 0.7691 $$ $$ \boxed{R_{\text{series}} \approx 0.7691 \text{ (or } 76.91\% \text{)}} $$ **Step 2:** Identify the weakest component. $R_3 = 0.88$ is the lowest. **Step 3:** Compute parallel reliability of two identical components each with $R_3 = 0.88$. $$ R_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - (1 - R_3)^2 = 1 - (0.12)^2 $$ $$ R_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - 0.0144 = 0.9856 $$ **Step 4:** Recompute the new system reliability with the strengthened weakest link. $$ R_{\text{new}} = R_1 \times R_2 \times R_{\text{parallel}} $$ $$ R_{\text{new}} = 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 0.9856 $$ Step-by-step: $$ 0.95 \times 0.92 = 0.8740 $$ $$ 0.8740 \times 0.9856 = 0.8614 $$ $$ \boxed{R_{\text{new}} \approx 0.8614 \text{ (or } 86.14\% \text{)}} $$ **Step 5: Comment.** The original system reliability was $76.91\%$. By adding a single parallel redundant component at the weakest point, reliability rose to $86.14\%$ — an improvement of nearly **$9.23$ percentage points**. This demonstrates the engineering principle that *parallel redundancy at the bottleneck yields maximum reliability gain per unit cost*. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often mistakenly compute $R_{\text{parallel}} = R_3 \times 2$ or $R_3 + R_3$. Always use $R_p = 1 - \prod (1-R_i)$, the probability that *at least one* works. The complementary formulation is non-negotiable. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Explain the Hamming code mechanism for single-bit error detection and correction. A 4-bit data word `1011` is to be transmitted using a Hamming(7,4) code. Construct the codeword with even parity. If bit 5 of the transmitted codeword is flipped in transit, show how the receiver detects and corrects the error. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Background.** A Hamming(7,4) code has 4 data bits and 3 parity bits. Parity bits occupy positions that are powers of 2: **1, 2, 4**. The remaining positions (3, 5, 6, 7) hold data bits. **Step 2: Position layout.** | Position | 1 (P1) | 2 (P2) | 3 (D1) | 4 (P3) | 5 (D2) | 6 (D3) | 7 (D4) | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| **Step 3: Insert data bits `1011` into positions 3, 5, 6, 7.** | Position | 1 (P1) | 2 (P2) | 3 (D1=1) | 4 (P3) | 5 (D2=0) | 6 (D3=1) | 7 (D4=1) | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| **Step 4: Compute parity bits using even parity.** - **P1** (position 1) checks positions 1, 3, 5, 7: bits = `_, _, 1, _, 0, _, 1` → sum = 2 (even) → **P1 = 0** - **P2** (position 2) checks positions 2, 3, 6, 7: bits = `_, _, 1, _, _, 1, 1` → sum = 3 (odd) → **P2 = 1** - **P3** (position 4) checks positions 4, 5, 6, 7: bits = `_, _, _, _, 0, 1, 1` → sum = 2 (even) → **P3 = 0** **Transmitted Codeword: `0 1 1 0 0 1 1`** (positions 1 to 7). **Step 5: Error injection.** Suppose bit 5 (currently `0`) is flipped to `1` in transit. **Received Codeword: `0 1 1 0 1 1 1`** **Step 6: Receiver recomputes syndrome.** - **C1** (check positions 1, 3, 5, 7): bits = `0, _, 1, _, 1, _, 1` → sum = 3 (odd) → **C1 = 1** - **C2** (check positions 2, 3, 6, 7): bits = `1, _, 1, _, _, 1, 1` → sum = 4 (even) → **C2 = 0** - **C3** (check positions 4, 5, 6, 7): bits = `0, _, _, _, 1, 1, 1` → sum = 3 (odd) → **C3 = 1** **Step 7: Form syndrome.** $\text{Syndrome} = C_3 C_2 C_1 = 1\,0\,1_2 = 5_{10}$. **Step 8: Correction.** Syndrome = 5 indicates that bit at position 5 is in error. The receiver **flips bit 5** back to `0`, restoring the original codeword `0110011`. **Step 9: Extract data.** Strip parity bits to recover original 4-bit data: positions 3, 5, 6, 7 → `1, 0, 1, 1` = `1011` ✓. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Common mistakes include: (i) placing data bits in wrong positions, (ii) confusing which positions each parity bit covers, (iii) writing syndrome in wrong order. Always lay out the 7-bit table first, then compute. The syndrome in *descending* bit order (C3 C2 C1) directly gives the decimal position of the error. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Explain the concept of Data Integrity in information security. With a neat diagram, describe the digital signature mechanism for ensuring authentication, integrity, and non-repudiation. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Data Integrity Definition.** Data Integrity is the assurance that data has not been modified, altered, or destroyed in an unauthorized manner during transit, processing, or storage. It is a core pillar of the **CIA Triad** and is enforced through cryptographic and non-cryptographic mechanisms. **Step 2: Digital Signature Mechanism.** A **digital signature** is a cryptographic mechanism that provides *authentication* (proof of sender), *integrity* (proof data is unaltered), and *non-repudiation* (sender cannot deny sending). **Sender Side:** 1. The sender computes a hash $H(M)$ of the message $M$ using a secure hash algorithm (SHA-256). 2. The sender encrypts this hash with their **private key** $K_{pr}$ to produce the digital signature $S = E_{K_{pr}}(H(M))$. 3. The signature $S$ is appended to the message $M$ and transmitted. **Receiver Side:** 1. The receiver decrypts the signature $S$ using the sender's **public key** $K_{pu}$ to recover $H_1 = D_{K_{pu}}(S)$. 2. The receiver independently computes $H_2 = \text{SHA-256}(M')$ on the received message $M'$. 3. If $H_1 = H_2$, integrity is verified and the sender is authenticated. **Step 3: Diagram.** ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Original Message M] --> B[Hash Function
SHA 256] B --> C[Hash Digest H] C --> D[Encrypt with
Sender Private Key] D --> E[Digital Signature S] A --> F[Append S to M] E --> F F --> G[Transmit M plus S] G --> H[Receiver] H --> I[Decrypt S with
Sender Public Key
yields H1] H --> J[Hash M prime
yields H2] I --> K{H1 equals H2} J --> K K -->|Yes| L[Integrity OK
Authentic] K -->|No| M[Tampered or
Not Authentic] style B fill:#c9daf8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#f9cb9c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style K fill:#ffe599,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style L fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style M fill:#ea9999,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` **Step 4: Explanation of Security Services.** | Service | How Digital Signature Provides It | |---|---| | **Authentication** | Only the sender possesses the private key; successful decryption by public key proves the sender. | | **Integrity** | Any modification to $M$ changes $H_2$; mismatch with $H_1$ reveals tampering. | | **Non-Repudiation** | The sender cannot later deny signing because only they hold the private key. | > [!NOTE] > **Examiner Insight:** KTU examiners expect students to clearly state *why* a digital signature is *stronger* than a MAC — namely, the *asymmetry of keys* provides non-repudiation, which a symmetric MAC cannot. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **A web server has MTBF = 8000 hours and MTTR = 4 hours. Compute the availability, the annual downtime in hours, and the number of "nines" of availability achieved. If a parallel redundant server (identical) is added, compute the new MTBF and availability of the combined system. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Initial Availability.** $$ A_1 = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{8000}{8000 + 4} = \frac{8000}{8004} \approx 0.999500 $$ $$ \boxed{A_1 \approx 99.95\% \text{ (about "three-and-a-half nines")}} $$ **Step 2: Annual Downtime.** $$ U_1 = 1 - A_1 = \frac{4}{8004} \approx 0.0004998 $$ Hours per year = $365 \times 24 = 8760$ hours. $$ \text{Downtime} = U_1 \times 8760 = 0.0004998 \times 8760 \approx 4.378 \text{ hours/year} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Annual Downtime} \approx 4.38 \text{ hours/year}} $$ **Step 3: Number of "Nines".** $$ -\log_{10}(1 - A_1) = -\log_{10}(0.0005) \approx 3.30 $$ So this is approximately **3.3 nines** of availability. **Step 4: Parallel Redundancy — System MTBF.** For two parallel components with identical exponential reliability $R_1(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ where $\lambda = 1/8000$: $$ R_p(t) = 2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t} $$ The effective failure rate of the *parallel pair* is found by considering the system failure probability. A simpler engineering approximation uses: $$ \text{MTBF}_{\text{parallel}} = \text{MTBF} \times \frac{3}{2} = 8000 \times 1.5 = 12000 \text{ hours} $$ (This holds for two identical parallel components with constant failure rate.) > [!NOTE] > **Conversion Logic:** The exact closed-form MTBF for a parallel pair is $\int_0^{\infty} R_p(t)\,dt = \int_0^{\infty} (2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t})\,dt = 2/\lambda - 1/(2\lambda) = 3/(2\lambda) = 1.5 \times \text{MTBF}$. The factor $1.5$ reflects the average life extension. **Step 5: New Availability with MTTR unchanged.** $$ A_2 = \frac{12000}{12000 + 4} = \frac{12000}{12004} \approx 0.999667 $$ $$ \boxed{A_2 \approx 99.967\% \text{ (about "three-and-a-half-plus nines")}} $$ **Step 6: Comment.** The parallel redundancy improved MTBF from 8000 to 12000 hours (50% gain) and reduced annual downtime. To reach *true five-nines* (99.999%), additional measures like geographic redundancy, hot-standby failover, and predictive maintenance are required. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often forget to convert MTTR for parallel systems. In this problem, MTTR is assumed unchanged (same repair crew for either failed unit). For *complex* parallel systems, MTTR may also change due to shared maintenance resources. Always state your assumption explicitly. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Reliability and Integrity** ### Core Concepts (Must Memorize) - **Reliability** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ — probability of survival beyond time $t$. - **Failure Rate** $\lambda$ — constant for exponential distribution. - **MTBF** $= 1/\lambda$ — mean time between failures (reliability metric). - **MTTR** — mean time to repair (maintainability metric). - **Availability** $A = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$. - **"Five Nines"** = 99.999% uptime ≈ 5.26 minutes downtime/year. ### System Reliability Formulas - **Series:** $R_s = \prod R_i$ — chain weakens with each component. - **Parallel:** $R_p = 1 - \prod (1 - R_i)$ — redundancy strengthens. ### Integrity Mechanisms - **Parity Bit:** $P = \sum d_i \pmod 2$ — single-bit error *detection*. - **Hamming Code:** $n = 2^r - 1$ — single-bit error *correction* via syndrome. - **CRC:** Polynomial division — burst error detection (Ethernet, ZIP). - **SHA-256:** Cryptographic hash — collision-resistant integrity. - **Digital Signature:** Asymmetric cryptography — integrity + authentication + non-repudiation. ### Hamming Distance Rules - Detect $s$ errors: $d_{\min} \geq s + 1$. - Correct $t$ errors: $d_{\min} \geq 2t + 1$. ### Real-World Mapping (Public & Private Sector) - **Banking core systems:** High-availability clusters (parallel redundancy). - **Defense communications:** ECC memory + convolutional codes. - **E-Governance portals:** SHA-256 hashing + digital signatures for citizen data. - **Aadhaar / DigiLocker:** Integrity via cryptographic hashes and signed documents. ### Common KTU Exam Traps - Mixing up MTBF and MTTR. - Forgetting to take the *complement* in parallel reliability. - Wrong syndrome bit order in Hamming code. - Not specifying *which* integrity mechanism (cryptographic vs. non-cryptographic). ### Key Takeaway > [!NOTE] > **Reliability ensures the system is *there* and *working*; Integrity ensures the *data inside* is *correct and untampered*. Together, they form the operational backbone of secure public and private sector information systems.**

topics.title: Sensitive Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT753 - INFORMATION SECURITY IN PUBLIC AND PRIVATE SECTORS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sensitive Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sensitive Data** refers to any digital or physical information that, if exposed, altered, destroyed, or accessed without authorization, could cause significant harm to an individual, organization, or nation. It includes data that is protected by law, regulation, contractual obligation, or corporate policy, and which requires a higher degree of security control than ordinary business data.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** curriculum, sensitive data is formally classified as:
> Any data element — whether **Personally Identifiable Information (PII)**, **Protected Health Information (PHI)**, **financial records**, **intellectual property**, or **government-classified material** — whose confidentiality, integrity, or availability (the **CIA Triad**) is governed by statutory, regulatory, or organizational mandates and which triggers mandatory protection protocols upon collection, processing, storage, or transmission.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECCT753 – Module 3):**
> Sensitive data is the foundational construct upon which information classification, access control policies, encryption strategies, and breach notification obligations are built. Mastering its categories, sensitivity levels, and handling lifecycle is essential for understanding the broader domain of **public and private sector information security governance**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large office building with multiple rooms:

- The **lobby** (public data) — anyone can walk in and look around. Posters, reception desk, general information.
- The **regular office floors** (internal data) — only employees with basic ID cards can enter. Spreadsheets, internal memos.
- The **executive suite** (confidential/sensitive data) — only senior management with biometric access can enter. Salary records, strategic plans.
- The **high-security vault** (restricted/top-secret data) — requires multiple keys, armed guards, and constant surveillance. Nuclear codes, classified intelligence.

**Sensitive data is the executive suite and the vault combined.** It is information that, if leaked, can cause real-world damage — identity theft, financial fraud, legal liability, reputational collapse, or even national security threats. Just as you wouldn't leave the vault door open, sensitive data must be guarded with layered technical, administrative, and physical controls.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Harm Test"** — A widely accepted academic heuristic to determine if data is sensitive: *If unauthorized disclosure of this data would foreseeably cause harm to the data subject or the data controller, the data is sensitive.* This test is embedded in regulations such as the **GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation)** and the **IT Act, 2000 (India)**.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Taxonomies (Bold Highlights)

- **Sensitivity Levels (Industry Standard):** **Public**, **Internal**, **Confidential**, **Restricted**.
- **Regulatory Frameworks Governing Sensitive Data:** **GDPR**, **HIPAA**, **PCI-DSS**, **IT Act 2000 (India)**, **DPDP Act 2023 (India)**, **SOX**, **GLBA**, **FERPA**.
- **Key Performance Metric:** **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** and **Mean Time to Respond (MTTR)** for sensitive data breaches.
- **De-identification Standard:** **NIST SP 800-188 (De-identification of Personal Information)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Sensitive Data Classification

Sensitive data is governed by a **3-pillar classification framework** in modern enterprise and governmental systems:

### Pillar I — Sensitivity Tier
The level of harm that would result from unauthorized disclosure.

| Tier | Description | Example |
|------|-------------|---------|
| **Tier 0 – Public** | No impact if disclosed | Marketing brochures, public website text |
| **Tier 1 – Internal** | Minor embarrassment or operational impact | Org charts, internal phone directory |
| **Tier 2 – Confidential** | Significant competitive, legal, or financial harm | Source code, salary data, customer lists |
| **Tier 3 – Restricted** | Severe legal, financial, or safety harm | Cardholder data (PCI-DSS), medical records (PHI), classified intelligence |

### Pillar II — Data Type
The *nature* of the data, which determines which laws apply.

- **PII (Personally Identifiable Information):** Name, Aadhaar, SSN, passport, phone, email.
- **PHI (Protected Health Information):** Diagnoses, prescriptions, insurance claims (governed by HIPAA).
- **PCI (Payment Card Information):** Card number (PAN), CVV, expiry date (governed by PCI-DSS v4.0).
| **SPI (Sensitive Personal Information – DPDP Act 2023, India):** Passwords, financial data, health data, sexual orientation, biometric data, genetic data. |
| **Intellectual Property:** Trade secrets, patents, copyrighted source code. |
| **Government Classified:** "Restricted", "Confidential", "Secret", "Top Secret" (per National Classification Policy). |

### Pillar III — Lifecycle State
Where the data resides, which dictates the control set.

- **Data at Rest** — stored in databases, file systems, cloud object stores.
- **Data in Motion** — transmitted over networks (TLS, IPsec, VPN).
- **Data in Use** — actively processed in CPU registers, RAM, or application memory.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" of Sensitive Data Protection

- **Why protect?** Breach costs average **\$4.88 million per incident globally** (IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report 2024). Reputational damage, regulatory fines (up to **4% of global turnover under GDPR Article 83**), and criminal liability under the **IT Act §43A and §72A (India)**.
- **How to protect?** A **Defense-in-Depth** strategy combining:
  1. **Administrative controls** — Policies, training, audits.
  2. **Logical/Technical controls** — Encryption (AES-256, RSA-2048), Access Control Lists (ACL), DLP (Data Loss Prevention), IAM (Identity & Access Management).
  3. **Physical controls** — Locked server rooms, CCTV, biometric access.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Description |
|---------|----------------|-------------|
| **Risk Score (qualitative)** | $R = P \times I$ | $P$ = Probability of breach, $I$ = Impact (sensitivity tier weight) |
| **Sensitivity Weight (typical)** | Tier 3 → 1.0, Tier 2 → 0.7, Tier 1 → 0.3, Tier 0 → 0.0 | Used to prioritize DLP alerts |
| **Encryption Strength (AES)** | $2^{k}$ where $k$ = key length in bits | AES-256 → $2^{256}$ possible keys |
| **Hash Chain (Merkle)** | $H_{root} = H(H_{L} \parallel H_{R})$ | Used in blockchain for integrity of sensitive transaction records |
| **MTTD (Mean Time to Detect)** | $\text{MTTD} = \frac{\sum (t_{detect} - t_{occur})}{n}$ | Average detection time across $n$ breach events |
| **MTTR (Mean Time to Respond)** | $\text{MTTR} = \frac{\sum (t_{resolve} - t_{detect})}{n}$ | Average remediation time |
| **GDPR Fine Cap** | $\max(\text{€20M}, 4\% \text{ of annual global turnover})$ | Article 83(5) – highest tier penalties |
| **Data Minimization Principle** | $\text{Data Collected} \subseteq \text{Data Necessary for Purpose}$ | GDPR Article 5(1)(c) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember:** For KTU board examinations, the qualitative **Risk = Probability × Impact** formulation is the most frequently tested. Be prepared to compute a numerical risk score and justify the sensitivity tier assignment.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Sensitive data handling is the **backbone** of:

- **Banking & FinTech:** Card tokenization, PCI-DSS audits.
- **Healthcare:** EHR (Electronic Health Record) privacy under HIPAA / DISHA (India).
