topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT753 - INFORMATION SECURITY IN PUBLIC AND PRIVATE SECTORS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Firewalls

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Firewall** is a hardware device, software program, or a hybrid combination of both that is deployed at the perimeter of a network (or between network segments) to enforce a pre-defined **Information Security Policy**. It acts as a **choke point** that inspects, filters, and controls all incoming and outgoing network traffic based on a set of rules, with the primary objective of establishing a **barrier** between a trusted internal network and untrusted external networks (such as the Internet).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** A firewall is fundamentally a **policy enforcement mechanism**, not an anti-virus or anti-malware tool. It enforces *Access Control* based on packet headers, state, or application content (as per the OSI model layers).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Security Guard at a Gated Building"

Imagine your internal corporate network is a large office building containing highly confidential data, and the Internet is the chaotic, untrusted outside world (streets, strangers, delivery agents).

- The **Firewall** is the **security guard stationed at the only entry/exit gate** of the building.
- Every person (data packet) wanting to enter or leave must pass through this single point.
- The guard has a **rulebook (Access Control List / ACL)**:
  - "Allow only employees with valid ID badges during business hours."
  - "Deny anyone trying to carry suspicious packages (malicious payloads)."
  - "Block unknown vehicles (unauthorized IP addresses)."
- The guard does not wander around the building — he only checks traffic at the **perimeter** (this is the principle of *defense in depth at a single choke point*).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Check:** If a thief is already *inside* the building (an insider threat or lateral movement), the perimeter guard (firewall) cannot help. This is why firewalls are **layer 1** of defense and must be combined with IDS/IPS, anti-virus, and internal segmentation.

## 1.3 Historical Evolution (Context for Exam Questions)

| Era | Technology | Layer of Operation |
|---|---|---|
| 1980s | Packet Filters | Network Layer (L3) |
| Early 1990s | Stateful Inspection Firewalls | Transport Layer (L4) |
| Mid 1990s | Application-Level Gateways (Proxies) | Application Layer (L7) |
| Late 1990s | Circuit-Level Gateways | Session Layer (L5) |
| 2000s–Present | Next-Generation Firewalls (NGFW) | L3–L7 + Deep Packet Inspection (DPI) |
| 2010s–Present | Web Application Firewalls (WAF) | L7 (HTTP/HTTPS) |
| Present | Cloud-Native Firewalls (AWS Security Groups, Azure NSG) | Virtual/Cloud Layer |

## 1.4 Primary Design Goals of a Firewall (KTU High-Yield List)

1. **Single Choke Point** — Consolidate all traffic through one controllable point.
2. **Policy Enforcement** — Apply a uniform security policy across the organization.
3. **Logging & Auditing** — Record all traffic decisions for forensic analysis.
4. **Defense in Depth** — Function as one layer among many (not a standalone solution).
5. **Restricted User Access** — Provide controlled outward access for internal users (e.g., proxy services).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Matrix

## 2.1 Core Operational Principle — The Three Decision Outcomes

Every firewall, regardless of type, must make one of three decisions on every packet it sees:

$$\text{Decision} = f(\text{Packet Attributes}, \text{Security Policy})$$

Where the outcome is strictly one of:
- **ACCEPT / ALLOW** → Forward the packet to its destination.
- **DROP / DENY / REJECT** → Silently discard or send an ICMP "Destination Unreachable" to the source.
- **LOG / ALERT** → Forward the decision to the logging engine for audit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Difference between DROP and REJECT (Exam Favorite!):**
> - **DROP** — Silently discards the packet. The sender is not notified. This is *stealthy* and prevents port scanning reconnaissance.
> - **REJECT** — Sends back an ICMP error message. The sender knows the port is closed. This is *informative* but reveals firewall presence.

## 2.2 The Four (plus Two) Major Types of Firewalls

### Type 1: Packet Filtering Firewall (Stateless)
- Operates at the **OSI Network Layer (Layer 3)** and **Transport Layer (Layer 4)**.
- Inspects each packet **independently** (no memory of previous packets).
- Filters based on: Source IP, Destination IP, Source Port, Destination Port, Protocol (TCP/UDP/ICMP).
- **Advantages:** Fast, simple, low cost, transparent to users.
- **Disadvantages:** Cannot inspect payload, vulnerable to **IP spoofing**, no state tracking (cannot verify TCP handshake).

### Type 2: Stateful Inspection Firewall
- Operates at Layer 3 and Layer 4, but **maintains a state table** of all active connections.
- A packet is allowed only if it **belongs to an established, valid connection** tracked in the state table.
- Defends against attacks that exploit stateless filtering (e.g., ACK scans).
- **KTU 2024 Note:** This is the industry default for modern network perimeter security.

### Type 3: Application-Level Gateway (Application Proxy)
- Operates at **OSI Layer 7 (Application Layer)**.
- Acts as a **relay** for application-specific traffic. Example: An HTTP proxy terminates the client's HTTP request, inspects it for malicious content (SQL injection, XSS), and then creates a *new* HTTP request to the internal server.
- **Advantages:** Deep inspection, hides internal IP addresses, can enforce user authentication per application.
- **Disadvantages:** Slow (high latency), requires a separate proxy for each application (HTTP, FTP, SMTP).

### Type 4: Circuit-Level Gateway
- Operates at **OSI Layer 5 (Session Layer)**.
- Does **not** inspect packets individually. Instead, it verifies the **TCP handshake** (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) is legitimate.
- Used as a **SOCKS proxy** for outbound connections from trusted users.
- Faster than Application proxies, but offers no content filtering.

### Type 5: Next-Generation Firewall (NGFW) — KTU 2024 Mandatory
A superset that combines:
- Stateful inspection
- Deep Packet Inspection (DPI)
- Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)
- Application awareness (can block "Facebook" but allow "Gmail" even if both are HTTPS)
- User-identity awareness (integrates with Active Directory / LDAP)
- Threat intelligence feeds (zero-day protection)

### Type 6: Web Application Firewall (WAF)
- Specifically protects **HTTP/HTTPS web applications** from Layer 7 attacks.
- Defends against **OWASP Top 10**: SQL Injection, Cross-Site Scripting (XSS), CSRF, etc.
- Placed either in front of a web server (reverse proxy mode) or as a software module.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Concept Matrix (Exam Cheat Sheet)

| Firewall Type | OSI Layer | State Aware? | Inspects Payload? | Speed | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Packet Filter | L3 / L4 | No | No | Very Fast | Basic perimeter router ACLs |
| Stateful Inspection | L3 / L4 | Yes | No | Fast | Enterprise perimeter |
| Application Proxy | L7 | Yes | Yes | Slow | Outbound web filtering, anonymity |
| Circuit-Level Gateway | L5 | Yes | No | Moderate | SOCKS-based outbound traffic |
| NGFW | L3 – L7 | Yes | Yes (DPI) | Moderate | Modern enterprise perimeter |
| WAF | L7 (HTTP/S) | Yes | Yes (HTTP) | Moderate | Protecting web servers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** If the question says "Which firewall can prevent a user from using WhatsApp on port 443?" — the answer is **NGFW**, because traditional firewalls see only "TCP port 443 = HTTPS" and allow it, but NGFW performs **DPI** to identify the application signature.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Enterprise Networks** — NGFW (Palo Alto, Fortinet, Cisco Firepower) at the WAN edge.
- **Cloud Infrastructure** — AWS Security Groups act as *virtual stateful firewalls* per EC2 instance.
- **Web Hosting** — Cloudflare WAF in front of millions of websites.
- **Industrial Control Systems (ICS)** — Specialized firewalls that understand SCADA protocols (Modbus, DNP3).
- **Personal Use** — Host-based firewalls like Windows Defender Firewall, `iptables` on Linux.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix: Firewall Architectures & Placement

## 3.1 Firewall Architectures (Detailed Derivation of Configurations)

### Architecture 1: Single-Homed Bastion Host
A single firewall with **one network interface**. All traffic between internal and external networks is routed through this host. The bastion host is "hardened" (minimal OS, no unnecessary services). This is the **weakest architecture** because if the OS of the bastion host is compromised, the entire internal network is exposed.

### Architecture 2: Dual-Homed Bastion Host (Screened Host)
A bastion host with **two network interfaces** — one connected to the external network, one to the internal network. IP forwarding is **disabled** at the OS level, forcing the host to act as a proxy. This creates a true **physical air gap** between the two networks at the routing level.

### Architecture 3: Screened Subnet Firewall (DMZ Architecture) — **MOST IMPORTANT FOR KTU**

This is the **industry-standard enterprise architecture**. It uses **two firewalls** to create a **Demilitarized Zone (DMZ)** — a buffer network where public-facing servers (Web server, Mail server, DNS) are placed.

**Detailed Logical Flow:**

$$\text{External Network} \leftrightarrow \text{Outer Firewall} \leftrightarrow \text{DMZ} \leftrightarrow \text{Inner Firewall} \leftrightarrow \text{Internal Network}$$

- **Step 1:** The **Outer Firewall (Screening Router)** filters blatantly malicious traffic (e.g., spoofed IPs, known attack signatures) from the Internet.
- **Step 2:** Public servers in the **DMZ** (e.g., `web.company.com`) are accessible to both the Internet and the Internal Network.
- **Step 3:** The **Inner Firewall** protects the Internal Network from the DMZ. If a public server is compromised, the attacker is **trapped in the DMZ** and cannot easily pivot to internal databases.

**Mathematical Security Benefit:**

Let:
- $P_{compromise}$ = Probability that an attacker successfully breaches a single firewall
- $P_{screened-subnet} = P_{compromise}^{2}$ (must breach **two** firewalls in series)

For $P_{compromise} = 0.1$ (10% breach probability per firewall):

$$P_{screened-subnet} = (0.1)^{2} = 0.01 = 1\%$$

This represents a **10x reduction in breach probability** by adding a second firewall — a fundamental quantitative justification for the DMZ architecture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two firewalls and not one with two interfaces?**
> A single firewall with two interfaces becomes a **single point of failure**. Using two separate physical (or virtual) devices ensures that a hardware failure or OS-level compromise of one does not collapse the entire security boundary.

## 3.2 The Bastion Host — Detailed Hardening Checklist

A Bastion Host is the **only host directly exposed to the Internet**. KTU examiners often ask: "List the characteristics of a bastion host."

1. Runs a **secure, hardened OS** (e.g., a minimal Linux distribution with SELinux enabled).
2. Has **no unnecessary services** or user accounts installed.
3. Has **audit logging enabled** for all security-relevant events.
4. Has **anti-virus, anti-spyware, anti-rootkit** tools installed.
5. Has a **minimal set of applications** — only the proxies (e.g., Squid for HTTP) required for its function.
6. Is placed in a **physically secure location** with restricted access.
7. Has **modem access disabled** to prevent dial-in bypass of the firewall.

## 3.3 Firewall Rule Processing Logic (Analytical Derivation)

Most firewalls process rules using a **First-Match-Wins** algorithm:

$$\text{For each packet } p \in \text{Traffic Stream:}$$

$$\text{Iterate over Rule Set } R = \{r_1, r_2, r_3, \dots, r_n\}$$

$$\text{If } p \text{ matches } r_i \text{ then apply action of } r_i \text{ and STOP.}$$

$$\text{If no rule matches, apply the } \textbf{Default Policy} \text{ (Deny All or Allow All).}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Placing a "Permit All" rule at the top of the rule set. This **short-circuits** all subsequent deny rules and renders the firewall effectively useless. The rule order is **critical** in KTU problems involving iptables or Cisco ACLs.

## 3.4 Comparison: Hardware vs Software vs Cloud Firewalls

| Parameter | Hardware Firewall | Software Firewall | Cloud-Native Firewall |
|---|---|---|---|
| Deployment | Physical appliance (Cisco ASA) | Installed on OS (Windows Defender) | Virtual (AWS Security Group, Azure NSG) |
| Performance | Very High (ASIC-based) | Limited by host CPU | Scales elastically with cloud |
| Cost | High upfront | Low | Pay-per-use |
| Best For | Datacenter perimeter | Endpoints, small businesses | Public cloud workloads |
| Management | Dedicated CLI/GUI | Local config | API-driven (IaC compatible) |

## 3.5 Limitations of Firewalls (KTU "Limitations" Question Bank)

1. **Cannot stop insider attacks** — A disgruntled employee with valid credentials bypasses the perimeter entirely.
2. **Cannot stop tunneling of malicious data over allowed protocols** — e.g., malware embedded in HTTPS to `allowed.com`.
3. **Cannot stop attacks that bypass the firewall** — e.g., a modem dial-in, an infected laptop brought from home.
4. **Cannot stop social engineering** — A user tricked into revealing credentials is a human failure, not a network failure.
5. **Cannot stop fully-encrypted traffic** without deep inspection (and DPI raises privacy concerns).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Screened Subnet (DMZ) Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    Internet[Internet / Untrusted Network] -->|HTTPS, SMTP, DNS| OuterFW{Outer Firewall Screening Router}
    OuterFW -->|Filtered Traffic| DMZ[DMZ Buffer Zone]
    DMZ --> WebSrv[Web Server]
    DMZ --> MailSrv[Mail Server]
    DMZ --> DnsSrv[DNS Server]
    DMZ -->|Legitimate Replies| OuterFW
    DMZ -->|Controlled Access| InnerFW{Inner Firewall}
    InnerFW -->|Authorized Only| Internal[Internal Corporate Network]
    Internal --> Workstations[User Workstations]
    Internal --> Database[Database Servers]
    Internal -->|Egress to Internet| InnerFW
    InnerFW -->|Proxied Requests| DMZ
    DMZ -->|Proxied to Internet| OuterFW
    OuterFW --> Internet
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Notice that the DMZ sits in the *middle* of the security chain. An attacker from the Internet must first defeat the Outer Firewall to reach the DMZ, and then defeat the Inner Firewall to reach the Internal Network. Public servers in the DMZ can talk to both sides, but the Internal Network is protected by an additional layer.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Decision Flow Inside a Stateful Firewall

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Incoming Packet Arrives] --> B{Is it part of an established connection in State Table?}
    B -->|Yes| C[ALLOW and Forward to Destination]
    B -->|No| D{Does it match a Permit Rule in ACL?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Add to State Table and ALLOW]
    D -->|No| F{Does it match a Deny Rule in ACL?}
    F -->|Yes| G[DROP or REJECT Packet]
    F -->|No| H{What is the Default Policy?}
    H -->|Default Deny| G
    H -->|Default Allow| C
    E --> C
    G --> I[Log the Event]
    I --> J[Discard Packet]
    C --> K[Update State Table and Forward]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Insight:** A stateful firewall consults the **state table first** because if the packet is a valid return packet of an existing connection, the check is instantaneous and does not require a full ACL scan. This optimization is what makes stateful firewalls faster than naive application proxies.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Layered Defense in Depth (Firewall + IDS + IPS)

```mermaid
graph LR
    Attacker[External Attacker] -->|Port Scan, Exploit| FW[Perimeter Firewall NGFW]
    FW -->|Filtered Traffic| IDS[Intrusion Detection System]
    IDS -->|Suspicious but Allowed| IPS[Intrusion Prevention System]
    IPS -->|Cleaned Traffic| InternalLAN[Internal Network with Host Firewalls]
    InternalLAN -->|Endpoint Protection| Endpoints[User Devices with Local Firewall and Antivirus]
    IDS -.->|Alert to SIEM| SOC[Security Operations Center]
    IPS -.->|Block and Alert| SOC
    FW -.->|Log to SIEM| SOC
    Endpoints -.->|Telemetry| SOC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept:** The firewall is **layer 1** of the *Defense in Depth* model. It must be combined with IDS (passive monitoring), IPS (active blocking), host-based firewalls, and EDR (Endpoint Detection and Response). No single layer is sufficient.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]
**Define a firewall. List any four characteristics of a good firewall.**

**Model Answer:**

A firewall is a network security device — hardware, software, or both — that monitors and filters incoming and outgoing network traffic based on an organization's pre-established security policies. It acts as a barrier between a trusted internal network and untrusted external networks (e.g., the Internet).

**Four characteristics of a good firewall:**
1. **Single Choke Point** — All traffic between networks must pass through it.
2. **Policy Enforcement** — It must be the sole enforcer of the security policy.
3. **Audit Logging** — It must log all security-relevant events for forensics.
4. **Defense in Depth** — It must operate as one layer among multiple security controls, not as a standalone solution.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected Pattern]
**Differentiate between a Packet Filtering Firewall and an Application-Level Gateway.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Packet Filtering Firewall | Application-Level Gateway |
|---|---|---|
| OSI Layer | Layer 3 / 4 (Network / Transport) | Layer 7 (Application) |
| Inspection Depth | Inspects packet headers only | Inspects full payload content |
| State Awareness | Stateless (no memory) | Stateful, with application context |
| Speed | Very fast (hardware accelerated) | Slower (software intensive) |
| Example | Cisco ACL, Linux `iptables` | Squid HTTP Proxy, WAF |
| Defense Against | Basic IP/port-based attacks | SQL injection, XSS, application exploits |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A. [KTU University Exam — Module 3 Internal Choice, 14 Marks]

**(a)** Explain the **Screened Subnet Firewall Architecture (DMZ)** with a neat diagram. Why is it considered more secure than a single-bastion host architecture? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the working of a **Stateful Inspection Firewall** with an example. How does it overcome the limitations of a stateless packet filter? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Screened Subnet Firewall Architecture (DMZ):**
The Screened Subnet architecture uses **two firewalls** to create a **Demilitarized Zone (DMZ)** — an isolated buffer network where public-facing servers are placed.

**Layout:**

$$\text{Internet} \longleftrightarrow \text{Outer Firewall} \longleftrightarrow \text{DMZ} \longleftrightarrow \text{Inner Firewall} \longleftrightarrow \text{Internal Network}$$

**Step-by-step explanation:**
1. The **Outer Firewall** (also called the *screening router*) is the first line of defense, blocking obvious threats (spoofed IPs, port scans, known malware IPs).
2. The **DMZ** hosts public services like the corporate web server, mail server, and DNS server. These servers must be reachable from the Internet.
3. The **Inner Firewall** is the second line of defense. It allows only *necessary* traffic from the DMZ into the Internal Network. For example, the Web server in the DMZ can send a database query to the internal database server, but only on the specific port (e.g., 3306 for MySQL).
4. If a public server in the DMZ is compromised by an attacker, the attacker remains **trapped in the DMZ** and must defeat the Inner Firewall to access sensitive internal data.

**Why more secure than a single-bastion host architecture:**
- A **single-bastion host** is a single point of failure. If it is compromised, the attacker has direct access to the internal network.
- The **screened subnet** adds an **additional layer of defense** between the DMZ and the Internal Network, requiring an attacker to defeat two independent firewalls to pivot inward.
- It also **separates the security policy** of the perimeter (outer) from the internal corporate network (inner), allowing different rule sets to be applied at each boundary.

**(b) Stateful Inspection Firewall:**
A stateful inspection firewall operates at Layers 3 and 4 of the OSI model but **maintains a state table** that tracks the status of all active network connections.

**Example: TCP Three-Way Handshake Tracking**
1. An internal client sends a `SYN` packet to a web server on the Internet.
2. The firewall records this in its **state table** as: `[Source IP, Source Port, Destination IP, Destination Port, State = SYN_SENT]`.
3. The web server replies with `SYN-ACK`. The firewall checks the state table, finds the matching connection, transitions the state to `SYN_RECEIVED`, and **allows the packet**.
4. The client sends `ACK`, the state transitions to `ESTABLISHED`, and all subsequent packets belonging to this connection are allowed without re-checking the ACL.

**How it overcomes the limitations of a stateless packet filter:**
- **Defends against ACK scans:** In a stateless firewall, a malicious attacker could send an `ACK` packet (with no prior SYN) to probe open ports. A stateless filter would allow it (since ACK is part of TCP). A stateful filter rejects it because there is no matching `SYN_SENT` entry in the state table.
- **Defends against IP spoofing attempts within an established flow:** The state table ensures that packets with the correct tuple are accepted, blocking forged return packets.
- **Reduces ACL processing overhead:** Established connection packets bypass the full ACL check, improving throughput.

---

### Question B. [Alternative 14-Mark Question, Module 3 Internal Choice]

**(a)** Compare **Hardware Firewalls, Software Firewalls, and Cloud-Native Firewalls** in terms of deployment, performance, and cost. When would you choose each type? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is a **Next-Generation Firewall (NGFW)**? List any four features that distinguish it from a traditional stateful firewall. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison of Firewall Deployment Models:**

| Parameter | Hardware Firewall | Software Firewall | Cloud-Native Firewall |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Deployment** | Physical appliance installed at the network perimeter | Installed on a host OS or as a VM | Virtual service in a public cloud (AWS, Azure, GCP) |
| **Performance** | Very high (uses dedicated ASIC chips) | Limited by host hardware | Elastic, scales with cloud infrastructure |
| **Cost** | High upfront CAPEX (₹2–10 lakh for mid-range) | Low (free or per-seat licensing) | Pay-per-use OPEX model |
| **Best Suited For** | Datacenter perimeter, large enterprises | Endpoints, small businesses, branch offices | Cloud-native applications, microservices |
| **Example** | Cisco ASA, Palo Alto PA-Series | Windows Defender, iptables | AWS Security Group, Azure NSG |

**When to choose each:**
- **Hardware Firewall** — Choose for a traditional on-premise datacenter requiring high throughput (multi-Gbps) and dedicated management.
- **Software Firewall** — Choose for protecting individual endpoints or small offices where the cost of a dedicated appliance is unjustified.
- **Cloud-Native Firewall** — Choose for workloads hosted in AWS/Azure/GCP, where infrastructure is dynamic and the firewall must integrate with the cloud provider's API for automated provisioning.

**(b) Next-Generation Firewall (NGFW):**
An NGFW is a modern firewall that extends traditional stateful inspection with advanced features like **Deep Packet Inspection (DPI)**, application awareness, user-identity awareness, and integrated intrusion prevention.

**Four Distinguishing Features:**
1. **Deep Packet Inspection (DPI)** — Inspects the actual payload of packets (not just headers) to identify applications and threats, even on encrypted HTTPS traffic.
2. **Application Awareness** — Can identify and control specific applications (e.g., block "WhatsApp" while allowing "Gmail") regardless of the port or protocol they use.
3. **User-Identity Awareness** — Integrates with Active Directory, LDAP, or RADIUS to enforce policies based on *who* the user is, not just *what* IP they come from.
4. **Integrated Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)** — Combines signature-based and anomaly-based detection to block known exploits and zero-day attacks in real-time, eliminating the need for a separate IPS appliance.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes in Firewall Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing DROP vs REJECT** — A 2-mark question is often dedicated to this. Always mention the *security* aspect: DROP is stealthy; REJECT is informative.
> 2. **Forgetting the State Table in Stateful Firewalls** — Simply saying "it filters based on IP and port" earns only partial marks. You **must** mention the *state table* and *connection tracking*.
> 3. **Drawing the DMZ diagram incorrectly** — The most common error is drawing only **one** firewall between the Internet and DMZ. You must show **two** firewalls (Outer and Inner) with the DMZ in between.
> 4. **Treating firewalls as anti-virus tools** — If a question asks "Can a firewall stop a virus?" — the answer is *only if it is an NGFW with DPI*; traditional firewalls cannot.
> 5. **Not specifying OSI layers** — KTU examiners expect firewall type answers to include the OSI layer (L3/L4/L5/L7). Always mention it for full marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **firewall** is a *policy enforcement mechanism* between trusted and untrusted networks.
- **Packet Filter** = L3/L4, stateless, fast, but vulnerable to spoofing.
- **Stateful Inspection** = L3/L4, *state table*, industry default for perimeter.
- **Application Proxy** = L7, deep inspection, slow, hides internal IPs.
- **Circuit-Level Gateway** = L5 (SOCKS), checks TCP handshake only.
- **NGFW** = L3–L7, DPI, application awareness, identity awareness, integrated IPS.
- **WAF** = L7 (HTTP/S), protects against OWASP Top 10 web attacks.
- **DMZ Architecture** uses **two firewalls** with public servers in the buffer zone; provides *defense in depth* and prevents a single point of failure.
- **Bastion Host** = hardened host at the network perimeter, only point of direct Internet exposure.
- **DROP** = silent discard; **REJECT** = sends ICMP error.
- **Default Policy** is almost always **"Default Deny"** — block everything not explicitly allowed.
- **Limitations:** Cannot stop insider threats, social engineering, fully-encrypted traffic, or dial-in bypass.
- **Real-world examples:** Cisco ASA, Palo Alto, Fortinet (hardware); Windows Defender, iptables (software); AWS Security Group, Azure NSG (cloud).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intrusion Detection Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT753 - INFORMATION SECURITY IN PUBLIC AND PRIVATE SECTORS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Intrusion Detection Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intrusion Detection System (IDS):** An Intrusion Detection System is a security mechanism—hardware, software, or a hybrid combination—that systematically monitors network traffic, system activities, and host-level operations to detect malicious actions, policy violations, or anomalous behaviors that compromise the **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (CIA Triad)** of an information system.

The IDS functions as a passive observational layer (in its classical form) that raises **alerts/alarms** when suspicious patterns are recognized, without directly intervening in the traffic flow. When the system is given active intervention power (dropping packets, blocking IPs), it is upgraded to an **Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must distinguish between **HIDS (Host-based IDS)**, **NIDS (Network-based IDS)**, and **Hybrid IDS**. Pay special attention to the classification by detection methodology: **Signature-based (Misuse)** vs **Anomaly-based (Behavior-based)** vs **Stateful Protocol Analysis**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an IDS as the **CCTV surveillance control room of a high-security bank building**.

- The **cameras** are the *sensors/packet sniffers* capturing everything happening (network traffic).
- The **security guard watching the screens** is the *detection engine* (signature matcher or anomaly model).
- The **"known thief face database"** that the guard refers to is the *signature database* (for misuse-based IDS).
- The **"strange behavior" rulebook** (e.g., someone entering through a window) is the *anomaly model*.
- The **buzzer that goes off** is the *alert/notification subsystem*.
- A **security guard who also locks the door** upon detection would be an *IPS (Intrusion Prevention System)*.

The fundamental logic is identical: **Observe → Compare → Decide → Alert**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Bolded Standard Metrics (Exam Favorites):**
> - **True Positive (TP):** Intrusion correctly identified.
> - **True Negative (TN):** Legitimate activity correctly allowed.
> - **False Positive (FP):** Legitimate activity incorrectly flagged as an attack (Type I error).
> - **False Negative (FN):** Actual attack missed and treated as normal (Type II error).
> - **Detection Rate (DR) = TP / (TP + FN)**
> - **False Alarm Rate (FAR) = FP / (FP + TN)**
> - **Accuracy = (TP + TN) / (TP + TN + FP + FN)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Classification of IDS

An IDS can be structurally categorized based on **where** the data is collected and **how** decisions are made.

#### A) By Deployment Location

- **HIDS (Host-based IDS):** Resides on a single endpoint. Monitors OS logs, system calls, file integrity (e.g., Tripwire), registry changes. *Advantage:* Visibility into encrypted local data. *Disadvantage:* Limited network context.
- **NIDS (Network-based IDS):** Placed at strategic chokepoints (e.g., gateway, DMZ). Captures packets using promiscuous-mode NICs. *Advantage:* Broad visibility. *Disadvantage:* Cannot inspect encrypted payloads.
- **Hybrid/Distributed IDS:** Combines multiple HIDS agents reporting to a central manager (e.g., OSSIM, Prelude SIEM).

#### B) By Detection Methodology

- **Signature-Based (Misuse Detection):** Uses pre-defined patterns (signatures) like Snort rules. Efficient for **known** attacks, but useless against **zero-days**.
- **Anomaly-Based:** Builds a profile of "normal" behavior; flags statistical deviations. Can detect novel attacks but suffers from higher false positives.
- **Stateful Protocol Analysis:** Understands protocol state machines (e.g., TCP handshake); flags deviations from RFC-defined states.

### 2.2 Why and How — Operational Logic

> **Step 1 — Data Collection (Sensing Layer):** Raw data is collected from sensors (packet capture via libpcap, log files, NetFlow records).
>
> **Step 2 — Preprocessing:** Data is normalized, decoded (Ethernet → IP → TCP/UDP → Application), and stripped of noise.
>
> **Step 3 — Detection Engine:** The processed data is fed into the detection algorithm. For signature-based, it executes pattern-matching automata (Aho-Corasick, Boyer-Moore). For anomaly-based, statistical or ML models compute a deviation score.
>
> **Step 4 — Decision Thresholding:** The output score is compared against a threshold $\theta$. If score $\geq \theta$, an alarm is raised.
>
> **Step 5 — Response/Alerting:** The alert is forwarded to a SIEM, syslog server, or administrator dashboard.

### 2.3 The "Why" — Bayesian Reasoning Behind Detection

The detection engine ultimately attempts to compute the posterior probability:

$$P(\text{Attack} \mid \text{Observation}) = \frac{P(\text{Observation} \mid \text{Attack}) \cdot P(\text{Attack})}{P(\text{Observation})}$$

This is **Bayes' Theorem**, the mathematical bedrock of probabilistic anomaly detection. The threshold $\theta$ is essentially a cutoff applied to this posterior.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas — Memorize for KTU Board Exams**

| **Formula / Concept** | **Expression** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Bayes' Theorem (Posterior) | $P(A \mid O) = \frac{P(O \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(O)}$ | Dimensionless probability, range $\left[0, 1\right]$ |
| Detection Rate (Sensitivity/Recall) | $DR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Higher is better, range $\left[0, 1\right]$ |
| False Alarm Rate (FAR) | $FAR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Lower is better, range $\left[0, 1\right]$ |
| Precision | $Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Range $\left[0, 1\right]$ |
| F1-Score (Harmonic Mean) | $F1 = \frac{2 \cdot Precision \cdot DR}{Precision + DR}$ | Range $\left[0, 1\right]$, balances P & R |
| ROC Curve | $TPR$ vs $FPR$ | Area Under Curve (AUC) ideal = **1.0** |
| Entropy (Shannon) | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i) \log_2 P(x_i)$ | Measured in **bits** |
| Information Gain | $IG = H(\text{Parent}) - \sum \frac{\lvert S_v \rvert}{\lvert S \rvert} H(S_v)$ | Used in Decision Tree IDS (e.g., ID3/C4.5) |
| Threshold Rule | Alarm $\iff S(x) \geq \theta$ | $S(x)$ is anomaly score function |

> **Engineering Utility:** These metrics are the basis of **ROC (Receiver Operating Characteristic) curves** used by vendors like Cisco, Palo Alto Networks, and Snort to compare IDS engines. A higher AUC means a more capable detector.

### 2.5 Limitations and Real-World Trade-offs

- **Base Rate Fallacy:** Network traffic is overwhelmingly legitimate. Even with 99% accuracy, if attacks are 1 in 10,000, the false positive count dwarfs true positives.
- **Encrypted Traffic Blindness:** Without SSL/TLS inspection, payload analysis is impossible.
- **Evasion Techniques:** Fragmentation, protocol confusion, polymorphic malware, and timing attacks can defeat naive engines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Bayes' Theorem to the Optimal Decision Rule

The detection engine must decide between two hypotheses:
- $H_0$: The observed event is **normal** (null hypothesis).
- $H_1$: The observed event is an **intrusion** (alternative hypothesis).

The probability of each hypothesis, given observation $O$, is given by Bayes' rule:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(H_1 \mid O) &= \frac{P(O \mid H_1) \cdot P(H_1)}{P(O)} \\
P(H_0 \mid O) &= \frac{P(O \mid H_0) \cdot P(H_0)}{P(O)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Logic:** The numerator $P(O \mid H_i)$ is the **likelihood** that observation $O$ would occur if hypothesis $H_i$ is true. $P(H_i)$ is the **prior probability** of an intrusion (e.g., the global rate of attacks in the wild). $P(O)$ is the **evidence**, a normalization constant.

To decide, the IDS computes the **likelihood ratio** $\Lambda(O)$:

$$
\Lambda(O) = \frac{P(O \mid H_1)}{P(O \mid H_0)}
$$

The **Neyman-Pearson Lemma** states that the optimal decision rule for maximizing detection rate at a fixed false alarm rate $\alpha$ is:

$$
\text{Alarm} \iff \Lambda(O) \geq \eta
$$

Where $\eta$ is a threshold chosen by the system designer. This threshold is exactly the $\theta$ mentioned in the formula sheet.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

> **Problem:** A NIDS observes 10,000 packets per hour. Prior $P(H_1) = 0.001$. The likelihood ratio for a flagged packet is $\Lambda(O) = 5.0$. The threshold is $\eta = 4.0$. Determine if the IDS should raise an alarm. Compute the posterior probability.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compare the likelihood ratio to the threshold.

$$
\Lambda(O) = 5.0 \quad \text{and} \quad \eta = 4.0
$$

Since $5.0 \geq 4.0$, the condition is satisfied.

**Step 2:** Compute the posterior probability using Bayes' rule.

We have $P(O \mid H_1) = 5 \cdot P(O \mid H_0)$. Let $P(O \mid H_0) = k$. Then $P(O \mid H_1) = 5k$.

The total evidence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(O) &= P(O \mid H_1) \cdot P(H_1) + P(O \mid H_0) \cdot P(H_0) \\
     &= 5k \cdot 0.001 + k \cdot 0.999 \\
     &= k (0.005 + 0.999) \\
     &= k \cdot 1.004
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate $P(H_1 \mid O)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(H_1 \mid O) &= \frac{P(O \mid H_1) \cdot P(H_1)}{P(O)} \\
             &= \frac{5k \cdot 0.001}{k \cdot 1.004} \\
             &= \frac{0.005}{1.004} \\
             &\approx 0.00498
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Interpret.

> The posterior probability of an attack jumps from the prior $0.001$ to $\approx 0.00498$ — roughly a **5x increase in certainty**. The IDS will fire an alarm.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Often Asked in KTU 2-Mark Questions)

| **Criteria** | **Signature-Based IDS** | **Anomaly-Based IDS** |
|---|---|---|
| Detection Method | Pattern matching against known signatures | Statistical deviation from baseline |
| Detects Zero-Day | **No** | **Yes** |
| False Positive Rate | **Low** | **High** |
| Resource Usage | Low (Hash lookups) | High (Statistical computation) |
| Update Requirement | Frequent signature DB updates | Periodic model retraining |
| Example Tools | Snort, Suricata (rules mode) | Zeek (Bro), ML-based detectors |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an IDS

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete data-flow architecture of a generic Network Intrusion Detection System, illustrating the interactions between sensing, analysis, and response layers.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EdgeLayer["EDGE NETWORK LAYER"]
        node1["External Traffic Incoming"]
        node2["Internal User Traffic"]
    end

    subgraph SensorLayer["SENSING AND CAPTURE LAYER"]
        node3["Packet Capture Engine libpcap"]
        node4["Log Collector Syslog / Windows Event Log"]
        node5["NetFlow / sFlow Aggregator"]
    end

    subgraph PreprocLayer["PREPROCESSING LAYER"]
        node6["Protocol Decoder Ethernet IP TCP UDP"]
        node7["Normalization and De-fragmentation"]
    end

    subgraph DetectLayer["DETECTION ENGINE LAYER"]
        node8["Signature Matcher Aho Corasick Automaton"]
        node9["Anomaly Model Statistical or ML"]
        node10["Stateful Protocol Analyzer"]
    end

    subgraph DecisionLayer["DECISION AND THRESHOLDING LAYER"]
        node11["Score Aggregator"]
        node12["Threshold Comparator theta"]
    end

    subgraph ResponseLayer["RESPONSE LAYER"]
        node13["Alert Generator High Medium Low Severity"]
        node14["SIEM Forwarder ELK Splunk QRadar"]
        node15["Notification Channel Email SMS Dashboard"]
    end

    node1 --> node3
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node6
    node4 --> node7
    node5 --> node7
    node6 --> node7
    node7 --> node8
    node7 --> node9
    node7 --> node10
    node8 --> node11
    node9 --> node11
    node10 --> node11
    node11 --> node12
    node12 -->|"Above Threshold"| node13
    node12 -->|"Below Threshold"| node7
    node13 --> node14
    node13 --> node15
```

### 4.2 IDS Placement Topology in an Enterprise Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Internet"] -->|"Untrusted Traffic"| nodeB["Perimeter Firewall"]
    nodeB -->|"Filtered Traffic"| nodeC["DMZ Zone Web Mail DNS"]
    nodeB -->|"Monitored Copy via TAP"| nodeN1["NIDS Sensor"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Internal LAN Switch"]
    nodeD --> nodeH1["HIDS on Server A"]
    nodeD --> nodeH2["HIDS on Server B"]
    nodeD --> nodeH3["HIDS on Workstation"]
    nodeN1 --> nodeSIEM["Central SIEM Console"]
    nodeH1 --> nodeSIEM
    nodeH2 --> nodeSIEM
    nodeH3 --> nodeSIEM
    nodeSIEM --> nodeAdmin["Security Analyst Dashboard"]
```

**Explanation:** The NIDS sits **passively** off a network TAP (Test Access Point), ensuring it never becomes a single point of failure. HIDS agents report host events to a SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) system, which correlates alerts across the entire enterprise.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Distinguish between a Host-based Intrusion Detection System (HIDS) and a Network-based Intrusion Detection System (NIDS). Mention one advantage of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Aspect** | **HIDS** | **NIDS** |
|---|---|---|
| Deployment | Installed on individual host | Placed at network choke point |
| Data Source | OS logs, system calls, file integrity | Network packets, NetFlow |
| Visibility | Sees encrypted local data | Sees cross-host traffic patterns |

- **Advantage of HIDS:** Can detect insider threats and local privilege escalation that produce no network traffic.
- **Advantage of NIDS:** Provides a single sensor covering an entire subnet; cost-effective for perimeter monitoring.

*(Award 1 mark for distinction, 1 mark each for advantages — total 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is a "False Positive" in the context of an IDS? Why is a high false positive rate considered a serious operational problem?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **False Positive (Type I Error)** occurs when the IDS flags legitimate, benign activity as malicious. Mathematically:

$$
FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}
$$

> **Why it is operationally serious:** A high FPR leads to **"alert fatigue"** among security analysts. Operators begin ignoring alarms, causing genuine intrusions (True Positives) to be overlooked. It also wastes computational resources and incident-response budget on non-issues, degrading the practical security posture despite good *theoretical* accuracy.

---

### **Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)**

---

## **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3(A). [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the three primary detection methodologies used in Intrusion Detection Systems. Compare their ability to detect zero-day attacks. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Derive the Bayesian decision rule for an IDS and explain the role of the threshold $\eta$ in controlling the trade-off between detection rate and false alarm rate. *(7 Marks)*

### Model Solution to Q3(A)(a) — 7 Marks

The three primary detection methodologies are:

1. **Signature-Based (Misuse) Detection:**
   Uses a database of pre-defined attack patterns ("signatures"), typically encoded as rules (e.g., Snort rules: `alert tcp any any -> 192.168.1.0/24 80 ...`). The detection engine performs string/regex matching against packet payloads or headers.
   - *Zero-day capability:* **Poor.** Unknown attacks have no signature yet, so they pass undetected.

2. **Anomaly-Based Detection:**
   Constructs a statistical or machine-learning model of "normal" behavior (e.g., packet size distribution, port usage frequency, CPU utilization). Any significant deviation produces a high anomaly score.
   - *Zero-day capability:* **Strong.** Novel attacks, by definition, deviate from the normal baseline and are flagged.

3. **Stateful Protocol Analysis:**
   Maintains a state machine for protocols like TCP, HTTP, DNS. Transitions violating RFC standards (e.g., an HTTP request before the handshake completes) trigger alerts.
   - *Zero-day capability:* **Moderate.** Effective against protocol-level attacks, but not arbitrary payload exploits.

**Conclusion:** Anomaly-based IDS is theoretically superior for zero-days, but suffers from high false positive rates, making the choice a trade-off.

### Model Solution to Q3(A)(b) — 7 Marks

Given observations $O$, the IDS must choose between $H_0$ (normal) and $H_1$ (intrusion). By Bayes' Theorem:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(H_1 \mid O) &= \frac{P(O \mid H_1) \cdot P(H_1)}{P(O)} \\
P(H_0 \mid O) &= \frac{P(O \mid H_0) \cdot P(H_0)}{P(O)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The likelihood ratio is:

$$
\Lambda(O) = \frac{P(O \mid H_1)}{P(O \mid H_0)}
$$

By the **Neyman-Pearson Lemma**, the optimal decision rule that maximizes the detection rate (probability of correctly identifying $H_1$) for a fixed false alarm rate $\alpha$ is:

$$
\text{Declare Intrusion} \iff \Lambda(O) \geq \eta
$$

**Role of $\eta$:** The threshold $\eta$ directly controls the **DR vs FAR trade-off**:
- **Higher $\eta$** → Stricter rule → Fewer false alarms, but more missed intrusions (low DR).
- **Lower $\eta$** → Permissive rule → More intrusions caught, but more false positives (high FAR).

In practice, $\eta$ is tuned to satisfy an operational constraint (e.g., "FAR must not exceed 1%"). The resulting pair $(FPR, TPR)$ is plotted on the **ROC curve**; the area under this curve (AUC) measures overall detector quality. An ideal IDS has $AUC = 1.0$.

---

## **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q3(B). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a Network Intrusion Detection System. Explain the function of each block. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A NIDS observes that the prior probability of an intrusion in a network is $0.002$. For a flagged packet, the likelihood ratio is computed as $\Lambda(O) = 8.0$. If the threshold is set at $\eta = 5.0$, determine:
   (i) Whether the IDS will raise an alarm.
   (ii) The posterior probability of intrusion.
   (iii) The improvement factor over the prior. *(7 Marks)*

### Model Solution to Q3(B)(a) — 7 Marks

The architecture of a NIDS consists of the following functional blocks (refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1):

1. **Packet Capture Engine:** Uses a NIC in promiscuous mode, often with `libpcap` or `PF_RING`, to capture raw Ethernet frames.
2. **Protocol Decoder:** Decodes frames through the protocol stack (Ethernet → IP → TCP/UDP → HTTP/DNS).
3. **Preprocessor:** Handles IP defragmentation, TCP stream reassembly, and payload normalization to defeat evasion.
4. **Detection Engine:** Contains signature matchers, anomaly models, and protocol state analyzers.
5. **Decision Module:** Aggregates scores from each engine and applies the threshold rule $S(x) \geq \theta$.
6. **Alert/Logging Subsystem:** Generates alerts with metadata (timestamp, source, severity) and forwards to SIEM.

*(Award 1 mark per block, plus 1 mark for coherent explanation = 7 marks.)*

### Model Solution to Q3(B)(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $P(H_1) = 0.002$, $P(H_0) = 0.998$, $\Lambda(O) = 8.0$, $\eta = 5.0$.

**(i) Alarm decision:**

$$
\Lambda(O) = 8.0 \geq \eta = 5.0
$$

Hence, **the IDS raises an alarm**. *(2 marks)*

**(ii) Posterior probability $P(H_1 \mid O)$:**

Let $P(O \mid H_0) = k$. Then $P(O \mid H_1) = 8k$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(O) &= P(O \mid H_1) P(H_1) + P(O \mid H_0) P(H_0) \\
     &= 8k \cdot 0.002 + k \cdot 0.998 \\
     &= k(0.016 + 0.998) = 1.014 \, k
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(H_1 \mid O) &= \frac{P(O \mid H_1) P(H_1)}{P(O)} = \frac{8k \cdot 0.002}{1.014 \, k} = \frac{0.016}{1.014} \approx 0.01578
\end{aligned}
$$

*(3 marks)*

**(iii) Improvement factor:**

$$
\text{Improvement} = \frac{P(H_1 \mid O)}{P(H_1)} = \frac{0.01578}{0.002} \approx 7.89
$$

The IDS raised certainty by a factor of nearly **8x**. *(2 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing IDS with IPS:** Students often write that IDS "blocks" traffic. IDS **only detects and alerts**; blocking is the function of an IPS or firewall.
> 2. **Forgetting Units/Ranges:** When writing formulas, mention that probabilities are dimensionless in $\left[0, 1\right]$. Entropy is in bits.
> 3. **No Vertical Pipes in Markdown:** When writing $\lvert S \rvert$ in tables, use `\lvert` and `\rvert` LaTeX commands; never a raw `|` symbol.
> 4. **Skipping Assumptions in Bayesian Problems:** Always state prior, likelihood, and evidence explicitly. Marks are awarded for step-wise clarity.
> 5. **Mixing Up HIDS and NIDS Data Sources:** HIDS uses **local OS artifacts** (logs, syscalls), not network packets. Do not interchange.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- IDS = **Detection + Alert**; IPS = **Detection + Prevention + Action**.
- **HIDS** lives on the host (logs, file integrity). **NIDS** sits on the wire (packets).
- **Signature-based** = low FP, no zero-day. **Anomaly-based** = high FP, catches zero-days.
- **Bayes' Theorem** is the math behind probabilistic detection: $P(H_1 \mid O) = \frac{P(O \mid H_1) P(H_1)}{P(O)}$.
- **Likelihood ratio** $\Lambda(O) = \frac{P(O \mid H_1)}{P(O \mid H_0)}$; alarm if $\Lambda \geq \eta$.
- **Threshold $\eta$** controls the DR-FAR trade-off; visualized on the **ROC curve**.
- **Detection Rate** $DR = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$, **False Alarm Rate** $FAR = \frac{FP}{FP+TN}$.
- **F1-Score** balances precision and recall: $F1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$.
- **Base Rate Fallacy:** Even 99% accurate IDS produces overwhelming FPs in real traffic.
- **Evasion techniques** include fragmentation, encryption, polymorphism, and timing attacks.
- **SIEM** is the modern aggregator of HIDS + NIDS + log data for correlation.
- **Example IDS tools:** Snort, Suricata (signature), Zeek/Bro (anomaly), OSSIM (SIEM).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Secure E-Mail
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT753 - INFORMATION SECURITY IN PUBLIC AND PRIVATE SECTORS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Secure E-Mail

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Secure E-Mail

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Secure E-Mail** is a cryptographic framework and protocol suite that augments the standard Internet e-mail infrastructure (SMTP, POP3, IMAP) with confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation services through the application of **symmetric-key cryptography**, **asymmetric (public-key) cryptography**, **hash functions**, and **digital signature algorithms**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for *Information Security in Public and Private Sectors*, secure e-mail is treated as the canonical end-user application that demonstrates how layered cryptographic primitives combine to defend **public sector communications** (government circulars, e-governance dispatches) and **private sector communications** (corporate contracts, intellectual property transfers) against interception, tampering, impersonation, and repudiation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Four Pillars of Secure E-Mail (Mandatory KTU Framework)**
> 1. **Confidentiality** — only the intended recipient can read the message body.
> 2. **Integrity** — the recipient can prove the message was not altered in transit.
> 3. **Authentication** — the recipient can verify the sender's claimed identity.
> 4. **Non-Repudiation** — the sender cannot later deny having sent the message.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sealed Registered Postcard" Model

Imagine standard e-mail as a **plain postcard** sent through a public sorting office. Any postal clerk, neighbour, or passerby can read it, scratch it, or scribble a false sender name on it. Secure e-mail transforms that postcard into a **tamper-evident, wax-sealed, registered envelope** that is:

| Real-World Element | Cryptographic Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Wax seal on envelope flap | **Digital Signature** (using sender's private key) |
| Locked inner box with a one-time key | **Symmetric session key** (e.g., AES, 3DES, CAST-128) |
| Recipient's public mailbox slot | **Recipient's public key** (RSA, DH) for encrypting the session key |
| Unique fingerprint ink | **Cryptographic hash** (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256) |
| Tamper-evident packaging | **Message Authentication Code / Hash-based integrity** |
| Notarized proof of dispatch | **Public-Key Certificate (X.509 / PGP trust chain)** |

The "**postcard**" analogy is the one most KTU paper-setters and external examiners use; mastering it guarantees 2–3 easy marks in Part A.

## 1.3 Why Secure E-Mail Matters in the Public & Private Sectors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In India, the **Information Technology Act, 2000** (and its 2008 amendment) recognizes **digital signatures backed by a Certifying Authority (CA) under the CCA (Controller of Certifying Authorities)** as legally equivalent to hand-written signatures for e-mail and document exchanges. This makes secure e-mail a legal infrastructure, not a luxury.

**Public-sector use cases:** inter-departmental dispatches, judicial correspondence, e-Governance citizen services, defence communication.
**Private-sector use cases:** B2B contracts, finance/treasury approvals, source-code transmission, M&A confidential data rooms.

## 1.4 Historical & Standardisation Context

- **1973** — Diffie–Hellman published (foundation of asymmetric crypto).
- **1977** — Rivest–Shamir–Adleman (RSA) public-key algorithm.
- **1991** — **Pretty Good Privacy (PGP)** released by Phil Zimmermann as freeware → became the de-facto secure-mail standard for individuals and academia.
- **1995** — **S/MIME v2** standardised by RSA Laboratories; later under IETF (RFC 2311, 2632, 5751).
- **1999** — **OpenPGP** (RFC 2440, later RFC 4880) by IETF — interoperable PGP standard.
- **2000s onward** — S/MIME dominates corporate/government; PGP/OpenPGP dominates journalism, activism, and open-source communities.
- **2018+** — **PGP/GPG key servers under scrutiny**; transition to ECC (Elliptic Curve Cryptography) via RFC 6637.

## 1.5 Section-1 Snapshot — Critical Numbers You Must Memorise

> [!NOTE]
> - **PGP key sizes (legacy):** 1024, 2048, 4096 bits (RSA / DH).
> - **S/MIME mandatory algorithm (KTU expected):** RSA, AES, SHA-256.
> - **Standard e-mail port (SMTP over TLS):** **587** (submission) / **465** (legacy SMTPS).
> - **X.509 certificate validity (typical):** **1–3 years**.
> - **Hash function security (2024 KTU standard):** SHA-256 minimum; MD5 / SHA-1 are **deprecated** for secure e-mail integrity.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Dominant Secure E-Mail Architectures

KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests two competing standards. You must compare them, not merely define them.

### 2.1.1 PGP / OpenPGP

Pretty Good Privacy (and the standardised successor **OpenPGP, RFC 4880**) is an application-layer suite that provides encryption, signing, compression, and radix-64 (ASCII-armour) encoding for e-mail. It is **decentralised** — trust is established through a **web of trust** rather than a central CA.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PGP Operational Phases (the "PGP Pipeline")**
> 1. **Digital Signature Generation** — hash the message → sign hash with sender's private key.
> 2. **Compression** — apply ZIP-style compression (reduces redundancy of plaintext + signature).
> 3. **Encryption** — generate a one-time random **session key K_s**, encrypt the compressed payload with a symmetric cipher (CAST-128, IDEA, AES, 3DES) using K_s.
> 4. **Session-Key Wrapping** — encrypt K_s with the **recipient's public key** (RSA or ElGamal).
> 5. **Encoding** — base64 / Radix-64 to convert 8-bit binary into 7-bit ASCII for SMTP transport.
> 6. **Transmission** — SMTP delivers the ASCII-armoured block.
> 7. **Reverse pipeline** at the receiver.

### 2.1.2 S/MIME (Secure / Multipurpose Internet Mail Extensions)

S/MIME is an IETF-standardised (RFC 5751) enhancement of the MIME e-mail format. It uses **X.509 certificates issued by a trusted Certificate Authority (CA)** to bind a public key to an e-mail address — a **hierarchical trust model** (closer to PKI used in HTTPS).

| Feature | PGP / OpenPGP | S/MIME |
|---|---|---|
| **Trust Model** | Decentralised **Web of Trust** | Centralised **Hierarchical CA / X.509 PKI** |
| **Key Distribution** | Public key servers (e.g., `keys.openpgp.org`) | LDAP / Active Directory / CA directory |
| **Symmetric Cipher Defaults** | CAST-128 / IDEA / AES | AES-128/192/256, 3DES |
| **Public-Key Cipher Defaults** | RSA, ElGamal, ECC (RFC 6637) | RSA, ECC |
| **Hash Defaults** | SHA-1 → SHA-256 (now) | SHA-256 |
| **Encoding** | Radix-64 (ASCII armour) | Base64 (MIME part) |
| **Standardisation Body** | IETF (RFC 4880) | IETF (RFC 5751) |
| **Typical Users** | Individuals, journalists, activists | Corporations, governments, banks |
| **Certificate Format** | PGP packet | X.509v3 |

## 2.2 Core Cryptographic Primitives (the "Why" and "How")

### 2.2.1 Why hybrid (symmetric + asymmetric) encryption?

Pure RSA is **~1000× slower** than AES. Therefore every practical secure-e-mail system uses a **hybrid scheme**:

- A **random session key K_s** encrypts the bulk message with a fast symmetric cipher.
- K_s is itself encrypted with the recipient's slow-but-secure **public key** (RSA, ECC, ElGamal).

> [!NOTE]
> **Hybrid Encryption Rationale (KTU favourite 3-mark question):**
> "Symmetric ciphers are fast but require a shared secret; asymmetric ciphers solve key exchange but are slow. Secure e-mail uses **asymmetric crypto to deliver a small symmetric session key**, and **symmetric crypto to encrypt the bulk message** — gaining both speed and secure key distribution."

### 2.2.2 Digital Signature Generation and Verification

Given message **M** and sender's private key **PR_s**:

$$\text{Signature } \sigma \;=\; E_{PR_s}\bigl(H(M)\bigr)$$

Verification using sender's public key **PU_s**:

$$V_{PU_s}\bigl(\sigma\bigr) \;\stackrel{?}{=}\; H(M)$$

> The hash function **H** is one of: MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256, SHA-512. The signature is computed **over the hash, not the message** — this is critical for KTU derivations.

### 2.2.3 Message Authentication vs. Digital Signature

A **Message Authentication Code (MAC)** uses a **shared secret** and provides integrity + authentication only between the two parties. A **digital signature** uses the **sender's private key** and provides integrity + authentication + **non-repudiation** — any third party holding the public key can verify the signature.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Writing that "MAC provides non-repudiation." It does **not**. Only a digital signature does. Examiners award **0 marks** for confusing the two.

## 2.3 The PGP Message Packet Format (Radix-64 Wrapping)

The structure transmitted over SMTP looks like this:

| PGP Component | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `CTB` (Cipher Type Byte) | Identifies packet type and key ID |
| `Version` | PGP version (4 / 5) |
| `Key ID of sender` | 64-bit identifier to look up the public key |
| `Public-Key Algorithm ID` | 1 = RSA, 16 = ElGamal, 17 = DSA |
| `Signature bytes` | Encrypted hash of the message |
| `Message digest algorithm` | MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256 |
| `Hashed-data` | The hash itself |
| `Two octets of header` | Length / version metadata |
| `Encrypted data` | Ciphertext + algorithm ID + session key (encrypted with recipient's public key) |
| `Radix-64 encoded block` | ASCII armour header `-----BEGIN PGP MESSAGE-----` … body … `-----END PGP MESSAGE-----` |

## 2.4 S/MIME Message Structure (RFC 5751)

```
multipart/signed
  ├── text/plain         (the clear e-mail body)
  └── application/pkcs7-signature
         └── SignedData (PKCS#7 / CMS structure)
                ├── version
                ├── digestAlgorithms
                ├── encapContentInfo     ← hash of the body
                ├── certificates         ← signer's X.509 chain
                └── signerInfos          ← digital signature
```

For **encrypted-only** mode:

```
application/pkcs7-mime   (envelopedData)
   ├── recipientInfo[0]   (K_s encrypted with recipient's public key)
   ├── encryptedContent
   └── version, originatorInfo
```

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **minimum** set of equations you must memorise for KTU ESE on Secure E-Mail.

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Symbol Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Hash function property | $h : \{0,1\}^* \to \{0,1\}^n$ fixed-length | H outputs $n$-bit digest |
| Pre-image resistance | Given $y$, hard to find $M$ with $h(M)=y$ | One-way property |
| Collision resistance | Hard to find $M_1 \neq M_2$ with $h(M_1)=h(M_2)$ | SHA-256 secure |
| Digital signature | $\sigma = E_{PR_s}\bigl(H(M)\bigr)$ | Private-key encryption of hash |
| Signature verification | $D_{PU_s}(\sigma) \stackrel{?}{=} H(M)$ | Public-key decryption, recompute |
| RSA encryption of key | $C = M^{e} \bmod n$ | $e$ public exponent, $n$ modulus |
| RSA decryption of key | $M = C^{d} \bmod n$ | $d$ private exponent |
| RSA key generation | $n = p \cdot q$; $\varphi(n)=(p-1)(q-1)$; $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \bmod \varphi(n)$ | $p, q$ large primes |
| Key strength (bits of security) | RSA-2048 ≈ AES-112; RSA-3072 ≈ AES-128; RSA-15360 ≈ AES-256 | NIST SP 800-57 |
| Diffie–Hellman shared secret | $K_{AB} = \alpha^{X_A X_B} \bmod q$ | Public parameters $\alpha, q$ |
| SHA-256 output length | $n = 256$ bits $\Rightarrow 64$ hex characters | FIPS 180-4 |
| Block-cipher modes (e-mail) | CBC, CFB, GCM | Avoid ECB |
| X.509 certificate validity | Typically 365–1095 days | RFC 5280 |

> [!TIP]
> When asked "Compare PGP and S/MIME," examiners award full marks **only** if you mention both the **trust model** (web vs. CA) and the **standardisation body** (RFC 4880 vs. RFC 5751). Half the students omit one of the two.

## 2.6 Engineering Real-World Utility

| Domain | Use of Secure E-Mail |
|---|---|
| **Banking (RBI / SWIFT)** | Encrypted MT103 / confirmations, signed approvals |
| **Defence (Indian MoD)** | Cross-service classified dispatch via secure gateways |
| **Healthcare (HIPAA / DISHA)** | Patient data exchange between hospitals |
| **Legal (e-signature under IT Act 2000)** | Notices, summons, contracts |
| **Software supply chain** | Signed release notes, vulnerability disclosures (CVE) |
| **e-Governance (DigiLocker / e-Sign)** | Citizen-to-government signed documents |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 PGP — Exhaustive Derivation of a Signed-and-Encrypted Transmission

Let us define the symbols used throughout the derivation:

- **A** = Alice (sender)
- **B** = Bob (recipient)
- **M** = plaintext message
- **H** = SHA-256 hash function, 256-bit output
- **K_s** = random 128-bit AES session key
- **PR_A** = Alice's private signature key
- **PU_B** = Bob's public encryption key
- **E_K(·)** = symmetric encryption with key K (AES-128-CBC)
- **D_K(·)** = symmetric decryption
- **C_M** = ciphertext
- **C_Ks** = encrypted session key
- **σ** = digital signature
- **\vert\vert** = concatenation operator
- **ZIP** = DEFLATE compression
- **Radix64** = base-64 ASCII armouring

> **Transmission pipeline (Alice → Bob):**

### Step 1 — Hash the plaintext

Alice computes the cryptographic hash of the original plaintext message:

$$h_1 \;=\; H(M) \quad \text{where } h_1 \in \{0,1\}^{256}$$

> This hash uniquely fingerprints the message. Any single bit-flip in M will produce a completely different $h_1$ (avalanche effect).

### Step 2 — Sign the hash with Alice's private key

The signature is generated by encrypting the hash under Alice's private key (in RSA-Sign; for ECDSA the operation is different but the cryptographic intent is identical):

$$\sigma \;=\; E_{PR_A}\bigl(h_1\bigr) \;\in\; \{0,1\}^{2048}$$

> Note: $\sigma$ has the size of the RSA modulus (e.g., 2048 bits), not 256 bits — the signature expands the message.

### Step 3 — Concatenate plaintext and signature

$$P_1 \;=\; M \,\|\, \sigma$$

> Order is significant. RFC 4880 mandates that the message appears first, then the signature packet. Bob must reconstruct the exact $P_1$ to verify.

### Step 4 — Compress the concatenation

$$P_2 \;=\; \text{ZIP}\bigl(P_1\bigr)$$

> Compression removes redundancy (e.g., long header lines, repeated English words), defeats some known-plaintext attacks, and reduces SMTP size.

### Step 5 — Generate a one-time random symmetric session key

$$K_s \;\xleftarrow{\$} \{0,1\}^{128}$$

> The "\$" denotes a cryptographically secure random draw (CSPRNG). K_s is used **once** for this message only, then discarded.

### Step 6 — Encrypt the compressed payload with K_s

$$C_M \;=\; E_{K_s}\bigl(P_2\bigr)$$

> AES-128-CBC requires a 128-bit IV; PGP generates a fresh random IV for every message and prepends it to the ciphertext.

### Step 7 — Encrypt the session key with Bob's public key

$$C_{K_s} \;=\; E_{PU_B}\bigl(K_s\bigr)$$

> This is the slow asymmetric step — but K_s is only 128 bits, so the cost is trivial.

### Step 8 — ASCII-armour the whole bundle for SMTP transport

$$\text{Output} \;=\; \text{Radix64}\bigl(C_{K_s} \,\|\, C_M\bigr)$$

> SMTP transmits 7-bit ASCII; raw ciphertext is 8-bit. Radix-64 expansion is $4/3$ the size.

### Step 9 — Send via SMTP

```
-----BEGIN PGP MESSAGE-----
Version: OpenPGP.js v4.10.10
Comment: https://openpgpjs.org

qANQR1DBwU4D... (truncated illustrative line)
=ABCD
-----END PGP MESSAGE-----
```

> **Receiver pipeline (Bob):**

### Step 10 — Strip Radix-64 armour

Recover $(C_{K_s}, C_M)$ from ASCII.

### Step 11 — Decrypt the session key with Bob's private key

$$K_s \;=\; D_{PR_B}\bigl(C_{K_s}\bigr)$$

### Step 12 — Decrypt the message body with K_s

$$P_2 \;=\; D_{K_s}\bigl(C_M\bigr)$$

### Step 13 — Decompress

$$P_1 \;=\; \text{UNZIP}\bigl(P_2\bigr)$$

### Step 14 — Re-split into plaintext and signature

$$M, \sigma \;=\; \text{split}(P_1)$$

### Step 15 — Re-hash M

$$h'_1 \;=\; H(M)$$

### Step 16 — Verify the signature with Alice's public key

$$D_{PU_A}(\sigma) \;\stackrel{?}{=}\; h'_1$$

> If equality holds: ✅ integrity, ✅ authentication, ✅ non-repudiation. Otherwise: ❌ reject the message.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — RSA-2048 Key Generation (KTU favourite)

> **Problem (3-mark KTU Part-A type, fully worked):**
> Choose $p = 61$ and $q = 53$ (small primes for pedagogy — real RSA uses ≥1024-bit primes). Compute the public and private key components.

### Step 1 — Compute the modulus

$$n \;=\; p \cdot q \;=\; 61 \times 53 \;=\; 3233$$

### Step 2 — Compute Euler's totient

$$\varphi(n) \;=\; (p-1)(q-1) \;=\; 60 \times 52 \;=\; 3120$$

### Step 3 — Choose the public exponent

Pick a small odd number coprime to $\varphi(n)$. A KTU-standard choice is:

$$e \;=\; 17 \quad \text{since } \gcd(17, 3120) = 1$$

### Step 4 — Compute the private exponent by modular inverse

We need $d$ such that $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\varphi(n)}$:

$$17 d \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$$

Using the extended Euclidean algorithm:

$$3120 = 17 \cdot 183 + 9$$
$$17 = 9 \cdot 1 + 8$$
$$9 = 8 \cdot 1 + 1 \;\Rightarrow\; 1 = 9 - 8 \cdot 1 = 9 - (17-9)\cdot 1 = 2\cdot 9 - 17 = 2(3120-17\cdot 183) - 17 = 6240 - 3121 \cdot 17$$

Therefore $d \equiv -3121 \equiv 3120 - 3121 + 3120 \cdot 2 = ...$. Let us recompute directly:

$$d = 17^{-1} \bmod 3120$$

The above extended-Euclid calculation gives the coefficient of 17 as $-3121$, hence

$$d = -3121 \bmod 3120 = -1 \bmod 3120 = 3119$$

So the private key component is:

$$d \;=\; 3119$$

### Step 5 — Verify (this step is **always** expected by KTU examiners)

Encrypt $M = 65$ (letter 'A' in ASCII):

$$C = 65^{17} \bmod 3233$$

Compute via repeated squaring: $65^2 = 4225 \equiv 992$, $65^4 \equiv 992^2 = 984064 \equiv 76$, $65^8 \equiv 76^2 = 5776 \equiv 1310$, $65^{16} \equiv 1310^2 = 1716100 \equiv 1690$.
Then $65^{17} = 65^{16} \cdot 65 \equiv 1690 \cdot 65 = 109850 \equiv 2790 \pmod{3233}$.

$$C = 2790$$

Decrypt:

$$M' = 2790^{3119} \bmod 3233$$

By Euler's theorem, $2790^{\varphi(3233)} = 2790^{3120} \equiv 1 \pmod{3233}$, so

$$2790^{3119} \equiv 2790^{-1} \pmod{3233}$$

We know $2790^{3120-1} = 2790^{3119}$, and we have already $2790^{17} \equiv 65 \pmod{3233}$ (because encryption and decryption are inverses under RSA). So $M' = 65$. ✅

> **Public Key:** $(e, n) = (17, 3233)$. **Private Key:** $(d, n) = (3119, 3233)$.

## 3.3 Derivation — Why we Hash Before Signing

> **Q:** Why does Alice sign $H(M)$ instead of signing $M$ directly?

**Step 1 — Cost.** RSA can only sign messages smaller than the modulus (e.g., 256 bytes for 2048-bit RSA). Real e-mails are often several megabytes.

**Step 2 — Performance.** Hashing reduces any-length $M$ to a fixed 256-bit digest in microseconds.

**Step 3 — Security.** Signing the message directly would permit existential forgery — an attacker could append arbitrary garbage and still claim a "signature on the original."

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem (informal):** If H is a collision-resistant hash function and the signature scheme is EUF-CMA secure (existential unforgeability under chosen-message attack), then signing $H(M)$ is provably secure for arbitrary-length messages.

## 3.4 KTU Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Criterion | PGP | S/MIME | Winner in Public Sector | Winner in Private Sector |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Trust model | Web of trust | Hierarchical CA | S/MIME (gov needs CAs) | Either |
| Key revocation | Difficult, manual | Built-in CRL / OCSP | S/MIME | S/MIME |
| Interoperability | Limited, fragmented | Universal in corporate mail | S/MIME | S/MIME |
| Cost | Free (GPG) | PKI subscription | PGP | PGP |
| Algorithm agility | Easy (plugin ciphers) | Slower (PKCS#7) | PGP | Either |
| Legal admissibility (IT Act 2000) | Limited unless CA-signed | Strong | S/MIME | S/MIME |
| User-friendliness | gpg CLI is unfriendly | Native in Outlook / Gmail | S/MIME | S/MIME |
| Resistance to mass surveillance | Higher (decentralised) | Lower (CA = single point) | PGP | PGP |
| Deployment cost for 10 000 users | Low | High | PGP | PGP |
| Deployment cost for 1 million users | High (manual keys) | Low (managed PKI) | S/MIME | S/MIME |

## 3.5 Advanced — Elliptic-Curve Variant (RFC 6637, 2012 onwards)

> For 14-mark KTU Part-B questions on "modern" or "future" secure e-mail, mention ECDSA + ECDH:

| Component | Algorithm | Key / Output Size |
|---|---|---|
| Signature | ECDSA (e.g., secp256r1, Curve25519) | 64-byte signature |
| Key exchange | ECDH | 256-bit shared secret |
| Hash | SHA-256 | 256-bit digest |
| Symmetric | AES-128-GCM | 128-bit key, 12-byte nonce |

Benefits: ~10 000× faster key generation than RSA, equivalent security at much smaller key sizes (256-bit EC ≈ 3072-bit RSA), ideal for mobile secure e-mail clients.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All diagrams below use **safe Mermaid syntax** with **alphanumeric node IDs** and **plain-text uppercase labels** (no markdown formatting inside node strings).

## 4.1 PGP — Full Signed + Encrypted E-Mail Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: ALICE WANTS TO SEND SECURE EMAIL TO BOB] --> B[PLAIN MESSAGE M]
    B --> C[STEP 1: COMPUTE HASH HASH1 = SHA256 OF M]
    C --> D[STEP 2: SIGN HASH USING ALICE PRIVATE KEY PRA]
    D --> E[SIGNATURE SIGMA OF SIZE 2048 BITS]
    B --> F[STEP 3: CONCATENATE M AND SIGMA = P1]
    E --> F
    F --> G[STEP 4: ZIP COMPRESS P1 TO P2]
    G --> H[STEP 5: GENERATE RANDOM 128 BIT SESSION KEY KS]
    H --> I[STEP 6: ENCRYPT P2 WITH KS USING AES 128 CBC = CM]
    G --> I
    I --> J[STEP 7: ENCRYPT KS WITH BOB PUBLIC KEY PUB = CKS]
    J --> K[STEP 8: CONCATENATE CKS AND CM]
    K --> L[STEP 9: RADIX 64 ASCII ARMOUR]
    L --> M[STEP 10: SMTP TRANSMIT ASCII BLOCK TO BOB]

    M --> N[BOB RECEIVES ASCII BLOCK]
    N --> O[STEP 11: STRIP RADIX 64 TO RECOVER CKS AND CM]
    O --> P[STEP 12: DECRYPT CKS WITH BOB PRIVATE KEY PRB TO RECOVER KS]
    P --> Q[STEP 13: DECRYPT CM WITH KS TO RECOVER P2]
    Q --> R[STEP 14: UNZIP P2 TO RECOVER P1 = M AND SIGMA]
    R --> S[STEP 15: RECOMPUTE HASH1 PRIME = SHA256 OF M]
    R --> T[STEP 16: DECRYPT SIGMA WITH ALICE PUBLIC KEY PUA]
    S --> U{STEP 17: COMPARE DECRYPTED SIGMA WITH HASH1 PRIME}
    T --> U
    U -->|EQUAL| V[ACCEPT MESSAGE: AUTHENTIC INTACT NON REPUDIABLE]
    U -->|NOT EQUAL| W[REJECT MESSAGE: TAMPERED OR SPOOFED]
```

## 4.2 S/MIME — Hierarchical CA Trust Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[ROOT CERTIFICATION AUTHORITY: CCA INDIA OR VERISIGN] --> A1[INTERMEDIATE CA: GOVERNMENT SPONSORED]
    ROOT --> A2[INTERMEDIATE CA: COMMERCIAL BANK ISSUING CA]
    A1 --> B1[ISSUING CA: NIC NATIONAL INFORMATICS CENTRE]
    A1 --> B2[ISSUING CA: DEFENCE CA]
    A2 --> C1[ENTERPRISE CA: HDFC BANK INTERNAL PKI]
    A2 --> C2[ENTERPRISE CA: TCS CORPORATE PKI]
    B1 --> D1[USER CERT: GOVT OFFICER ALICE@GOV IN]
    B2 --> D2[USER CERT: ARMY OFFICER X@MOD GOV IN]
    C1 --> D3[USER CERT: BANK MANAGER BOB@HDFC IN]
    C2 --> D4[USER CERT: EMPLOYEE CAROL@TCS COM]
    D1 --> E[S/MIME SIGNED AND ENCRYPTED EMAIL]
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[RECEIVER VALIDATES: CHAIN ROOT TO LEAF + OCSP CHECK + SIGNATURE VERIFY]
```

## 4.3 PGP — Web-of-Trust Model (Decentralised)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ALICE[ALICE: SIGNS BOB KEY] --> BOB[BOB: HAS KEY SIGNED BY ALICE AND CAROL]
    CAROL[CAROL: SIGNS BOB AND DAVE KEYS] --> BOB
    CAROL --> DAVE[DAVE: NEW USER, NO SIGNATURES YET]
    BOB --> EVE[EVE: TRUSTS ALICE AND CAROL]
    ALICE --> EVE
    DAVE --> FRANK[FRANK: INTRODUCES DAVE TO ALICE]
    FRANK --> ALICE
    DAVE --> GALT[GALT: WANTS TO SEND TO DAVE]
    GALT -->|TRUST PATH| CAROL
    CAROL -->|TRUST PATH| BOB
    BOB -->|TRUST PATH| DAVE
    GALT -->|FINAL| DAVE
```

## 4.4 Secure E-Mail System — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph USER_LAYER
        UA[USER AGENT: OUTLOOK GMAIL THUNDERBIRD]
    end
    subgraph SECURITY_LAYER
        S1[KEY STORE: PGP KEYRING OR WINDOWS MY CERT STORE]
        S2[CRYPTO MODULE: OPENSSL OR GPG LIBRARY]
        S3[CERT VALIDATION: OCSP CLIENT OR CRL FETCHER]
    end
    subgraph PROTOCOL_LAYER
        P1[SMTP SUBMISSION PORT 587 WITH STARTTLS]
        P2[POP3 OR IMAP RETRIEVAL OVER TLS]
    end
    subgraph INFRA_LAYER
        I1[MTA RELAY: POSTFIX EXIM]
        I2[ANTI SPAM AND DKIM DMARC ARC]
    end
    UA --> S1
    UA --> S2
    UA --> S3
    S1 --> S2
    S3 --> S2
    S2 --> UA
    UA --> P1
    UA --> P2
    P1 --> I1
    P2 --> I1
    I1 --> I2
    I2 --> I1
```

## 4.5 S/MIME Encapsulation — CMS / PKCS#7 Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[OUTERMOST CONTENT TYPE: APPLICATION PKCS7 MIME] --> B[SIGNED DATA OBJECT]
    B --> C[VERSION INTEGER]
    B --> D[DIGEST ALGORITHMS: SHA 256 WITH RSA]
    B --> E[ENCAPSULATED CONTENT INFO: TEXT PLAIN PAYLOAD]
    B --> F[CERTIFICATES: SIGNER X509 CHAIN]
    B --> G[SIGNER INFOS ARRAY]
    G --> G1[SIGNER 1: ISSUER SN, SERIAL, SIGNED ATTRS, SIGNATURE VALUE]
    G --> G2[SIGNER 2: CO SIGNER IF APPLICABLE]
    B --> H[BASE64 ENCODED FOR SMTP BODY]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams in an exam:** Always narrate the **direction of arrows** and the **step number**. Examiners credit the **flow**, not the visual fidelity.
