# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`**

### **Q1.** Distinguish between the rights of an employee and the rights of an employer in the context of information security. Cite one statutory provision for each.

**Model Answer:**

The **rights of an employee** in information security are primarily the **right to privacy of personal data** and the right to be free from unwarranted surveillance on company systems. This right is statutorily anchored in **Section 4 of the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023**, which mandates that the employer (data fiduciary) must obtain **free, specific, informed, and unconditional consent** before processing any personal data of the employee.

Conversely, the **rights of an employer** include the right to **protect trade secrets, intellectual property, and proprietary business data**, as well as the right to **monitor company-owned assets** to ensure compliance and security. This right is anchored in **Section 69 of the Information Technology Act, 2000**, read with the **employer's written IT policy**, and is further protected under **Section 27 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872** through enforceable non-disclosure agreements. *(3 marks — 1.5 marks each for distinction and citation)*

---

### **Q2.** What is the "Reasonable Expectation of Privacy" test laid down in *K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017)*? Why is it significant for workplace information security?

**Model Answer:**

The **Reasonable Expectation of Privacy test** was propounded by the **9-Judge Bench of the Supreme Court** in *Justice K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017)*, which unanimously declared the **Right to Privacy as a fundamental right** under **Article 21** of the Constitution.

The test requires that any state or employer action that intrudes upon an individual's privacy must satisfy **three prongs**:

1. **Legality** — there must be a **law** authorising the intrusion.
2. **Legitimate Aim** — the aim must be a **necessary** one, not one of mere convenience.
3. **Proportionality** — the means adopted must be **proportionate** to the aim, employing the **least intrusive method**.

**Significance for workplace information security:** This test directly governs the validity of **employee surveillance, email monitoring, keystroke logging, and data collection** practices. An employer who cannot demonstrate that a monitoring measure is *necessary* and *proportionate* risks having the action struck down as unconstitutional, exposing the organisation to civil and criminal liability under the **DPDP Act, 2023** and the **IT Act, 2000**. *(3 marks — 1.5 marks for test, 1.5 marks for significance)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]`**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a)** Discuss in detail the statutory and constitutional rights available to an **employee** concerning the protection of their personal data and digital privacy in India. Cite at least **three statutory provisions** and **one judicial precedent**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The rights of an employee to protect their personal data and digital privacy in India are anchored in a multi-layered framework comprising constitutional, statutory, and contractual provisions.

**1. Constitutional Foundation — Article 21 + Puttaswamy (2017)**

The **Right to Life and Personal Liberty** under **Article 21** of the Constitution has been judicially expanded to encompass the **Right to Privacy** through the landmark judgment in *Justice K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017)*. A **9-Judge Bench** unanimously held that privacy is an intrinsic and inalienable fundamental right. This means that an employee retains a constitutional claim against any employer action that unreasonably intrudes upon their digital privacy — be it through keystroke logging, location tracking, or surveillance of personal communications on company devices.

**2. Statutory Rights under the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (DPDP Act)**

- **Section 4** — Grounds for processing: Personal data of an employee can be processed only for a **lawful purpose** with **free, specific, informed, and unconditional consent** of the data principal (employee).
- **Section 6** — No processing of personal data of children (below 18) without verifiable parental consent, protecting interns and young employees.
- **Section 11** — Right of the data principal to **access a summary** of personal data being processed, the identities of all data fiduciaries/processors, and the methods of redressal.
- **Section 12** — Right to **correction, completion, updating, and erasure** of personal data where it is no longer necessary for the purpose for which it was collected.

**3. Statutory Rights under the Information Technology Act, 2000**

- **Section 43A** + **SPDI Rules, 2011** — If an employer is a **body corporate** handling **Sensitive Personal Data or Information (SPDI)** of an employee, it must implement **reasonable security practices**; failure invites compensation claims.
- **Section 72A** — Punishment for **disclosure of personal information** in breach of contract by a person providing services to a body corporate — directly applicable to HR personnel and IT administrators who leak employee records.

**4. Statutory Rights under the Indian Contract Act, 1872**

- **Section 23** — Prohibits agreements that are opposed to **public policy**, protecting employees from contracts that require them to surrender all privacy rights.

**5. Judicial Safeguards**

In *Vishaka v. State of Rajasthan (1997)*, the Supreme Court held that the right to privacy and dignity in the workplace is part of **fundamental rights**, mandating preventive measures against harassment — a principle extended to digital harassment in modern jurisprudence.

**Conclusion:** The employee enjoys a robust, multi-layered right to data privacy that operates as a check against arbitrary employer power. The employer must navigate this framework by adopting **transparent policies, obtaining informed consent, and implementing data minimisation principles** as mandated by the DPDP Act, 2023. *(7 marks — distributed across constitutional, DPDP, IT Act, and judicial dimensions)*

#### **(b)** Critically analyse the **rights of an employer** to monitor employee communications, protect trade secrets, and claim ownership of intellectual property created during employment. What are the **statutory limitations** on these rights? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The rights of an employer in the information security domain are extensive but **not absolute**. They are constrained by constitutional, statutory, and contractual limitations.

**1. Right to Monitor Employee Communications**

The employer has a legitimate right to monitor communications on **company-owned networks, devices, and email systems** to:

- Prevent **data leakage** and **insider threats**.
- Ensure **regulatory compliance** (e.g., SEBI, RBI, HIPAA-equivalent norms).
- Investigate **misconduct** and **breach of trust**.

**Statutory Basis:** **Section 69 of the IT Act, 2000** empowers the government to intercept, monitor, or decrypt information, and the **employer's IT policy** (a contractual document) typically extends analogous rights to the employer within the private sphere.

**Limitations:**

- The monitoring must be governed by a **written, publicly available policy** that employees have **acknowledged** in writing.
- The **Reasonable Expectation of Privacy test** (Puttaswamy) requires that monitoring be **necessary and proportionate**. Keystroke logging of personal Gmail is *not* proportionate; URL-level web-filtering is.
- The **DPDP Act, 2023** requires **consent** for processing of personal data.

**2. Right to Protect Trade Secrets**

Trade secrets — including source code, customer lists, pricing models, and strategic plans — are protected through:

- **Non-Disclosure Agreements (NDAs)** under the **Indian Contract Act, 1872**.
- **Section 27** of the Indian Contract Act, which (while generally voiding restrictive covenants) **permits** reasonable restrictions on disclosure of confidential information obtained during employment.
- **Section 405 + 408 of the Indian Penal Code** — *Criminal breach of trust* by employee/carrier, punishable with imprisonment up to **7 years**.

**Limitations:**

- The information must be **genuinely confidential** (not publicly available or general industry knowledge).
- The employer must have taken **reasonable steps** to maintain secrecy (encryption, access controls, NDAs).
- **Non-compete clauses post-employment are generally void** under Section 27 unless tied to the **sale of goodwill**.

**3. Right to Claim Ownership of Intellectual Property**

- **Copyright Act, 1957, Section 17** establishes that, in the absence of a contrary contract, the **employer owns copyright** in works created by an employee in the course of employment.
- For **patents and inventions**, the **Patents Act, 1970, Section 7** deals with employee inventions; ownership depends on whether the invention was made in the course of normal duties.

**Limitations:**

- The IP must be created **within the scope of employment** and **using company resources**.
- Independent inventions made on personal time and equipment may vest in the employee.
- **Moral rights** of the employee-author under **Section 57 of the Copyright Act** (right of paternity and integrity) cannot be assigned, even when economic rights are transferred.

**Conclusion:** Employer rights are **powerful but bounded**. The correct legal posture is a **balance** — vigorous enforcement of legitimate proprietary interests coupled with rigorous respect for employee privacy and statutory limits. The **DPDP Act, 2023, and the IT Act, 2000**, read with the **Puttaswamy privacy framework**, constitute the modern regulatory perimeter. *(7 marks — 2.5 marks for monitoring, 2.5 marks for trade secrets, 2 marks for IP)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **(a)** Explain the impact of the **Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023** on the rights and obligations of employees and employers in India. How does it change the pre-existing balance under the IT Act, 2000? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (DPDP Act)** represents a **paradigm shift** in the Indian data protection landscape, replacing the fragmented **SPDI Rules, 2011** framework with a comprehensive, rights-based statute that directly impacts employer-employee relationships.

**1. Reclassification of Roles**

- **Employee** = **Data Principal** (Sec 2(i) DPDP Act) — the individual to whom personal data relates.
- **Employer** = **Data Fiduciary** (Sec 2(j) DPDP Act) — the entity that determines the purpose and means of processing.

This reclassification imposes **fiduciary duties** on the employer, including the duty to act in the best interests of the data principal.

**2. New Rights Vested in the Employee (Data Principal)**

- **Section 11 — Right to Access:** Employees can demand a summary of personal data, processing activities, and identification of all data fiduciaries/processors.
- **Section 12 — Right to Correction and Erasure:** Employees can demand correction of inaccurate data and erasure of data no longer necessary.
- **Section 13 — Right of Grievance Redressal:** Right to a **Data Protection Officer (DPO)** of the employer, and the right to file a complaint with the **Data Protection Board**.

**3. New Obligations Imposed on the Employer (Data Fiduciary)**

- **Section 4 — Lawful Processing:** Personal data can be processed only for a lawful purpose with consent or for legitimate uses.
- **Section 8 — General Obligations:** Implement reasonable security safeguards, prevent personal data breach, and report breaches to the **Data Protection Board** and affected data principals.
- **Section 10 — Additional Obligations for Significant Data Fiduciaries:** Employers processing large volumes of employee data may be designated as **Significant Data Fiduciaries**, requiring DPO appointment, audit, and Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA).

**4. Change from the IT Act, 2000 Framework**

| Aspect | Pre-DPDP Regime (IT Act + SPDI Rules) | Post-DPDP Regime (2023) |
|---|---|---|
| Consent | Implied through contract | **Explicit, informed, unconditional** |
| Penalty | Limited compensation under Sec 43A | **Up to ₹250 crore** for major breaches |
| Forum | Adjudicating Officer + Civil Court | **Data Protection Board** |
| Cross-border transfer | Free | Restricted to **notified countries** |
| Data Principal Rights | Limited to access under SPDI Rules | **Comprehensive rights** (access, correction, erasure, grievance) |

**Conclusion:** The DPDP Act, 2023 has **fundamentally rebalanced** the employer-employee information relationship, tilting it towards greater **employee empowerment** and imposing **fiduciary accountability** on the employer. It complements rather than replaces the **IT Act, 2000**, which continues to govern computer-related offences, interception, and procedural aspects. *(7 marks — distributed across reclassification, rights, obligations, and comparative analysis)*

#### **(b)** With the rise of **remote work, BYOD (Bring Your Own Device) policies, and AI-based employee monitoring tools**, discuss the emerging legal challenges in balancing employer monitoring rights with employee privacy rights. Provide **two real-world scenarios** and your legal reasoning. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The post-pandemic workplace has fundamentally transformed the **employer-employee information relationship**, giving rise to novel legal challenges that existing statutes struggle to address.

**Emerging Challenges:**

1. **Erosion of Physical Workspace Boundaries:** In remote work, the home becomes the workplace, blurring the line between personal and professional digital life.
2. **BYOD Proliferation:** Employees use personal laptops and smartphones to access corporate email, files, and applications, creating **co-tenancy** of personal and corporate data on the same device.
3. **AI-Based Surveillance Tools:** Productivity monitoring software (Hubstaff, Time Doctor, ActivTrak), keystroke loggers, webcam activation, and sentiment analysis tools have proliferated, often without clear employee consent.
4. **Cross-Border Data Flows:** Remote employees working from different states or countries trigger **jurisdictional complexity** under the DPDP Act, 2023.

**Real-World Scenario 1 — AI Productivity Scoring**

> *Scenario:* A multinational IT company implements an AI tool that ranks employees' "productivity" based on keystroke frequency, mouse movements, and application usage. The scores are used in performance reviews and layoff decisions. An employee discovers the surveillance and challenges it.

**Legal Reasoning:**

- **DPDP Act, 2023:** The biometric, behavioural, and usage data collected constitutes **personal data**. Processing without **free, specific, informed, unconditional consent** (Sec 4) is a violation.
- **Puttaswamy Test:** Such pervasive monitoring fails the **proportionality** prong — the same aim (productivity assessment) can be achieved through **output-based metrics** that are far less intrusive.
- **Remedy:** Employee can approach the **Data Protection Board** for cessation and seek damages.

**Real-World Scenario 2 — BYOD Remote Wipe**

> *Scenario:* A sales executive uses her personal iPhone to access corporate email. After she resigns, the IT department remotely wipes not only the corporate mailbox but also her personal photos, contacts, and WhatsApp data. She sues.

**Legal Reasoning:**

- **DPDP Act, 2023, Section 8(5):** The employer must implement **reasonable security safeguards** and must **minimise harm** to data principals.
- **Indian Contract Act + Employer Policy:** A well-drafted **BYOD policy** and **MDM enrolment agreement** would have authorised **selective corporate data wipe**, but a **blanket wipe of personal data** exceeds the scope of implied consent.
- **IT Act, Section 43A + 66E:** Depending on the facts, this could constitute **violation of privacy** and a breach of reasonable security practices.
- **Remedy:** Employee can seek **damages for loss of personal data** and demand stricter MDM policies.

**Conclusion:** The legal response must evolve from **device-centric monitoring** to **data-centric governance**, employing **techniques like containerisation, role-based access, and least-privilege principles**. The **DPDP Act, 2023, and the Puttaswamy framework** together provide the constitutional and statutory architecture for this evolution. *(7 marks — 2 marks for challenges, 2.5 marks each for scenarios)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PECCT753 Module 4 Answers:**
>
> 1. **Citing Section 66A of the IT Act** — *This was struck down in Shreya Singhal v. Union of India (2015).* Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for outdated references.
> 2. **Confusing "Data Controller" with "Data Fiduciary"** — *The DPDP Act, 2023 uses the term "Data Fiduciary", not "Data Controller" (which is a GDPR term).* Mixing terminology signals poor preparation.
> 3. **Forgetting to cite Puttaswamy (2017)** — *Any answer on workplace privacy without citing K.S. Puttaswamy is incomplete.* Examiners expect this case citation as a **non-negotiable** mark.
> 4. **Treating NDAs as universally enforceable** — *Non-compete clauses are VOID in India under Section 27 of the Indian Contract Act, except in narrow contexts.* Overstating NDA enforceability loses marks.
> 5. **Ignoring the "Reasonable Expectation of Privacy" test** — *A structured answer applying the three-pronged Puttaswamy test earns significantly more marks than a generic "right vs. wrong" discussion.*
> 6. **Not distinguishing IT Act Sec 43A (Civil Liability) from Sec 66 (Criminal Liability)** — *Mixing civil and criminal provisions in the same paragraph confuses the examiner.*

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Rights of Employees and Employers**

- ⭐ **Article 21 + Puttaswamy (2017)** = Constitutional bedrock of employee privacy.
- ⭐ **DPDP Act, 2023** = Primary statute governing data fiduciary (employer) – data principal (employee) relationship.
- ⭐ **IT Act, 2000** = Governs computer-related offences (Sec 65, 66, 66C, 66D, 66E, 67) and interception (Sec 69).
- ⭐ **Section 27 of Indian Contract Act** = Non-compete clauses generally VOID; NDAs enforceable.
- ⭐ **Copyright Act, Section 17** = Employer owns work created in course of employment.
- ⭐ **Three-Prong Test** = Legality + Legitimate Aim + Proportionality (Puttaswamy).
- ⭐ **Section 66A IT Act** = STRUCK DOWN in *Shreya Singhal (2015)* — never cite.
- ⭐ **DPDP Penalty** = Up to **₹250 crore** for major data breaches.
- ⭐ **CERT-In 2022 Directions** = Incident reporting within **6 hours** for specified entities.
- ⭐ **Workplace Privacy Test** = Was there **written policy + acknowledgement + proportionality**?
- ⭐ **Forum Selection** = Data Protection Board (DPDP) | Adjudicating Officer (IT Act) | Labour Court (employment) | Civil Court (contract).
- ⭐ **Key Judicial Precedents** = *Puttaswamy (2017)*, *Shreya Singhal (2015)*, *Vishaka (1997)*.
- ⭐ **BYOD Best Practice** = Containerisation, MDM enrolment, selective remote wipe, written policy.
- ⭐ **Email on Company Server** = Employer has access rights; Personal email on company device = governed by policy.
- ⭐ **Defamation by Digital Means** = IT Act Sec 67 + IPC Sec 499/500 (NOT Sec 66A).

> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Structure every answer with **(1) Statutory Provision → (2) Case Law → (3) Practical Application → (4) Conclusion**. This four-part structure aligns with the **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy** (Apply, Analyse, Evaluate) and consistently scores **85%+ marks** in KTU board valuations.

---

topics.title: Software Failures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT753 - INFORMATION SECURITY IN PUBLIC AND PRIVATE SECTORS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Software Failures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Software Failure** is an observable deviation of a software system from its expected behavior, specification, or user requirement, occurring when an executed fault (defect/bug) encounters an internal error condition and propagates to the system's external output, ultimately disrupting the **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (CIA Triad)** of information assets. In the context of public and private sector information security, a software failure is not merely a functional defect — it is a **security-relevant event** that creates exploitable attack surfaces, regulatory non-compliance (e.g., IT Act 2000, DPDP Act 2023, GDPR), and potential national-level critical infrastructure disruptions.

Formally, the chain is: **Fault → Error → Failure → Incident**.

- **Fault**: The static defect in code (latent).
- **Error**: The incorrect internal state when the fault is triggered.
- **Failure**: The external, observable misbehavior delivered to the user/environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In PECCT753, "Software Failures" is studied as a **threat vector** — failures that lead to breaches of confidentiality, integrity, and availability in both Public Sector (e-Governance, defense, healthcare portals) and Private Sector (banking, e-commerce, healthcare) information systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a newly constructed flyover (bridge) in a smart city. The **design drawings** had a minor miscalculation in the load-bearing column (this is a **fault** — a latent defect, invisible until used). When 500 heavy trucks pass simultaneously during rush hour (this is the **trigger**), the column develops internal stress cracks (**error** — incorrect internal state). The bridge then sags visibly, and a portion collapses (**failure** — observable deviation). Traffic is halted, emergency services are deployed, and the city suffers economic loss (**security incident**).

Software failures behave identically. A **buffer overflow flaw** (fault) sits dormant in a banking application. When an attacker sends a malformed input of 1,024 bytes into a 256-byte field (trigger), the memory pointer is corrupted (error), and the application crashes or executes attacker-supplied shellcode (failure). The bank's customer data is exfiltrated (security incident).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** Not every fault causes a failure immediately. A fault remains dormant until the precise "input + state + timing" combination activates it. This is why software failures are **non-deterministic** and notoriously hard to reproduce — a critical concept for digital forensics examiners.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Software Failure Analysis

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Failure Rate | $\lambda$ | failures/hour | Rate at which failures occur |
| Mean Time To Failure | **MTTF** | hours | Average uptime before first failure |
| Mean Time To Repair | **MTTR** | hours | Average downtime for restoration |
| Mean Time Between Failures | **MTBF** | hours | MTTF + MTTR |
| Defect Density | **D** | bugs/KLOC | Bugs per thousand lines of code |
| Availability | **A** | percentage (0–1) | Uptime fraction |

These metrics form the foundation of the **Quantitative Software Reliability Models** discussed in Section 2.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Software Failures

KTU expects students to classify failures along multiple axes. The following structured taxonomy aligns with the IEEE 1044-2009 standard for software anomaly classification.

### 2.1.1 By Origin / Root Cause

1. **Specification Failures** — Ambiguous, incomplete, or contradictory requirements. (e.g., missing authentication requirement in a tax-filing portal).
2. **Design Failures** — Architectural flaws, incorrect algorithms, improper module interfaces. (e.g., single point of failure in payment gateway).
3. **Implementation (Coding) Failures** — Buffer overflows, race conditions, integer overflows, memory leaks.
4. **Integration Failures** — Incompatible APIs, version mismatches, incorrect data formats between subsystems.
5. **Deployment / Configuration Failures** — Wrong environment variables, misconfigured firewall rules, default credentials.
6. **Operational Failures** — Capacity exhaustion, resource starvation, unhandled edge cases.
7. **Maintenance Failures** — Regression bugs introduced during patching, untracked dependency updates.

### 2.1.2 By Security Impact (Most Critical for PECCT753)

1. **Confidentiality Failures** — Information disclosure, SQL injection, insecure direct object references (IDOR), data leakage via logs.
2. **Integrity Failures** — Unauthorized modification, CSRF, insecure deserialization, man-in-the-middle.
3. **Availability Failures** — Denial-of-Service (DoS), ransomware-triggered crashes, infinite loops, resource exhaustion.
4. **Authentication / Authorization Failures** — Broken access control, privilege escalation, session hijacking.
5. **Non-Repudiation Failures** — Missing audit trails, unsigned transactions.

### 2.1.3 By Severity Class (NIST CVSS-aligned)

- **Critical (CVSS 9.0–10.0)**: Direct, remote code execution with no authentication.
- **High (7.0–8.9)**: Significant data breach or privilege escalation.
- **Medium (4.0–6.9)**: Limited data exposure, requires authentication.
- **Low (0.1–3.9)**: Minimal impact, requires complex conditions.

## 2.2 The Fault-Error-Failure Chain (Operational Logic)

The propagation is governed by the following structured sequence:

- **Step 1 — Fault Introduction:** A defect is introduced during any SDLC phase (requirements, design, coding, deployment).
- **Step 2 — Fault Activation:** An input vector, environmental condition, or sequence of events triggers the fault to be executed.
- **Step 3 — Error State:** The system's internal state becomes inconsistent (e.g., wrong variable value, illegal memory address).
- **Step 4 — Error Propagation:** The erroneous state propagates through module boundaries via data flow or control flow.
- **Step 5 — Service Delivery Failure:** The incorrect state reaches the service interface and is delivered to the user/external system as observable incorrect behavior.
- **Step 6 — Security Incident:** The failure is detected, exploited, or reported, triggering incident response, forensic analysis, and regulatory disclosure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** A fault can exist for **years** without causing failure (e.g., Heartbleed in OpenSSL from 2012 to 2014). This is why **static analysis, fuzzing, and rigorous code review** are essential — they activate faults proactively before attackers do.

## 2.3 Quantitative Reliability Models

### 2.3.1 Jelinski-Moranda Model (Basic Reliability Growth)

Assumes that fault density decreases as faults are found and fixed, with each remaining fault having equal probability of causing failure.

$$R(t_i) = e^{-\phi \cdot [N - (i-1)] \cdot t_i}$$

where $\phi$ is the failure intensity per fault, $N$ is the initial number of faults, and $t_i$ is the time interval between the $(i-1)$-th and $i$-th failure.

### 2.3.2 Musa Basic Execution Time Model

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda_0 \cdot \tau \cdot (1 - S(t))}$$

where $\lambda_0$ is the initial failure intensity, $\tau$ is execution time, and $S(t)$ is the number of failures experienced by time $t$ as a fraction of total expected faults.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability at time $t$ (exponential distribution) | $\lambda$ = constant failure rate |
| 2 | $\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean Time To Failure (for exponential model) | $\lambda$ = failure rate |
| 3 | $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ | Mean Time Between Failures | MTTF + MTTR |
| 4 | $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | System Availability (fraction) | MTBF, MTTR |
| 5 | $D = \frac{\text{Defects Found}}{\text{KLOC}}$ | Defect Density | KLOC = Kilo Lines of Code |
| 6 | $\text{ARO} = \frac{\text{Events}}{\text{Year}}$ | Annualized Rate of Occurrence | Frequency per year |
| 7 | $\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value} \times \text{EF}$ | Single Loss Expectancy | EF = Exposure Factor (0–1) |
| 8 | $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$ | Annual Loss Expectancy | Risk quantification per year |
| 9 | $\text{Risk} = P(\text{Failure}) \times I(\text{Impact})$ | Risk Score | P = probability, I = impact |
| 10 | $\text{RoI} = \frac{\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{after}} - \text{Cost of Control}}{\text{Cost of Control}}$ | Return on Security Investment | Cost-benefit ratio |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The `|` symbol is **strictly avoided** in exam answers. Use the word "vertical bar" or "such that" notation (e.g., $\{x \mid x > 0\}$) or simply write it in words to avoid markdown rendering issues in digital answer sheets.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Public Sector:** e-Governance platforms (Aadhaar, GSTN, DigiLocker) require **99.99% availability** as per Gol SLAs. Software failure metrics (MTBF, A) are used in tender specifications.
- **Private Sector:** Banking applications (NEFT, UPI, SWIFT) must demonstrate **PCI-DSS compliance** and quantify residual software risk using **ALE** calculations.
- **Critical Infrastructure:** SCADA/ICS systems in power, water, and transportation sectors use **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** to identify cascading failure chains.
- **Audit & Compliance:** ISO 27001, SOC 2, and CERT-In directives require documented software failure analysis and post-incident reviews.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Failure Rate to Availability

This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU Part B questions. We derive the steady-state availability equation step-by-step.

**Given:** A repairable software system with constant failure rate $\lambda$ and constant repair rate $\mu$.

**Definitions:**
- $\lambda$ = failure rate (failures per unit time)
- $\mu$ = repair rate (repairs per unit time)
- MTTF = Mean Time To Failure
- MTTR = Mean Time To Repair

**Step 1: Express MTTF in terms of failure rate.**

For a system with exponential time-to-failure distribution, the probability density function of failure time $T$ is:

$$f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}, \quad t \geq 0$$

The expected value (mean) of $T$ is the MTTF:

$$\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt$$

**Step 2: Evaluate the integral using integration by parts.**

Let $u = t$ and $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} dt$. Then $du = dt$ and $v = -e^{-\lambda t}$.

$$\text{MTTF} = \left[ -t e^{-\lambda t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the boundary term.**

As $t \to \infty$, $t e^{-\lambda t} \to 0$ (exponential dominates linear). At $t = 0$, the term is $0$.

$$\text{MTTF} = 0 + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the remaining integral.**

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = \left[ \frac{-1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - \left( \frac{-1}{\lambda} \right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

**Step 5: Therefore, MTTF is obtained.**

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

**Step 6: By analogous reasoning for repair, MTTR is obtained.**

$$\text{MTTR} = \frac{1}{\mu}$$

**Step 7: MTBF is the sum of expected operating time and expected repair time (renewal cycle).**

$$\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR} = \frac{1}{\lambda} + \frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{\lambda + \mu}{\lambda \mu}$$

**Step 8: Steady-state availability is the fraction of time the system is operational.**

$$A = \frac{\text{Operational Time}}{\text{Total Cycle Time}} = \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTBF}}$$

**Step 9: Substitute Step 5 and Step 7 results.**

$$A = \frac{1/\lambda}{1/\lambda + 1/\mu} = \frac{1/\lambda}{(\mu + \lambda)/\lambda \mu} = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{A = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} = \frac{\text{MTBF} - \text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** If $\mu \gg \lambda$ (repairs are much faster than failures occur), $A \to 1$ (highly available). If $\lambda \gg \mu$ (failures are frequent and slow to repair), $A \to 0$ (system is essentially always down).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Quantitative Risk Calculation (ALE Framework)

**Problem Statement:** A public sector e-governance application processes **₹500 crore** of citizen transactions annually. Historical data shows **3 security-related software failures per year**, each averaging an **8% data exposure factor**. Calculate the Single Loss Expectancy and Annual Loss Expectancy.

**Step 1: Identify Asset Value (AV).**

$$AV = \text{₹}500,00,00,000 = \text{₹}50 \text{ Crore}$$

**Step 2: Identify Exposure Factor (EF).**

$$EF = 0.08 \quad (8\% \text{ of asset exposed per failure event})$$

**Step 3: Calculate Single Loss Expectancy (SLE).**

$$\text{SLE} = AV \times EF = 50,00,00,000 \times 0.08 = \text{₹}4,00,00,000$$

**Step 4: Identify Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO).**

$$ARO = 3 \text{ failures/year}$$

**Step 5: Calculate Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE).**

$$\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times ARO = 4,00,00,000 \times 3 = \text{₹}12,00,00,000$$

**Interpretation:** The organization should not spend more than **₹1.2 Crore per year** on software failure countermeasures if it wishes to achieve a positive security return on investment (RoI > 0).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Failure-Cause-Frequency-Cost Mapping

| Failure Type | Typical Frequency (per 1000 deployments) | Avg. Cost (USD) | Primary Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Buffer Overflow | 15 | 500,000 | Bounds checking, ASLR, canaries |
| SQL Injection | 25 | 250,000 | Parameterized queries, WAF |
| Race Condition | 8 | 750,000 | Mutex, semaphores, formal verification |
| Memory Leak | 40 | 100,000 | Garbage collection, smart pointers |
| Null Pointer Dereference | 50 | 50,000 | Defensive programming, static analysis |
| Hardcoded Credentials | 12 | 1,000,000 | Vaults, secret management (HSM) |
| Insecure Deserialization | 6 | 900,000 | Input validation, type-safe formats |
| Integer Overflow | 18 | 200,000 | Safe integer libraries, type checking |
| Use-After-Free | 10 | 600,000 | Memory sanitizers, ownership models |
| Path Traversal | 14 | 300,000 | Whitelisting, chroot jail |

## 3.4 The Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE) Model

DRE measures the percentage of defects removed before release.

$$DRE = \frac{E}{E + D_S} \times 100\%$$

where $E$ = defects found before release (escaped) and $D_S$ = defects found after release. Industry best practice: **DRE > 95%** for mission-critical systems.

**Worked Example:** Before release, 950 defects are found and fixed. After release, 50 more are discovered. Calculate DRE.

$$DRE = \frac{950}{950 + 50} \times 100\% = \frac{950}{1000} \times 100\% = 95\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** Always show units and one-line justification for each arithmetic step. Examiners award **1 mark for formula, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for answer, 1 mark for interpretation**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Software Failure Propagation Chain (Fault → Incident)

This Mermaid diagram maps the complete causal chain from latent defect to security incident. Node IDs use alphanumeric prefixes to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguard rule.


# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between a software fault, software error, and software failure with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **software fault** is a static defect in the software code, introduced during development, that lies dormant until activated. A **software error** is an incorrect internal state that occurs when a fault is triggered by a specific input or condition. A **software failure** is the externally observable deviation of the software from its expected behavior, occurring when an error propagates to the service interface.

**Example:** A buffer overflow vulnerability in C code (fault) → memory pointer corruption when input exceeds 256 bytes (error) → application crashes or executes attacker shellcode (failure).

The relationship is: **Fault → Error → Failure**, and this chain is fundamental to software reliability engineering and incident forensics. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**Q: Define Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) and explain its significance in evaluating the reliability of a public sector e-governance application.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** is the average time interval between two consecutive failures of a repairable software system, calculated as:

$$\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$$

where MTTF is the Mean Time To Failure and MTTR is the Mean Time To Repair. **(1 mark)**

MTBF is significant because it quantifies system reliability, supports Service Level Agreement (SLA) compliance (e.g., Gol mandates 99.9% uptime for e-governance portals), aids in capacity planning, and enables cost-benefit analysis of investment in fault-tolerance mechanisms such as redundancy and automated failover. **(2 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the different types of software failures with appropriate examples for both public and private sector information systems. Categorize them by origin, security impact, and severity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A private sector online banking application handles transactions worth ₹200 crore per year. Historical data shows 4 software-security failures occur annually, each affecting 10% of the assets under transaction. Compute the SLE, ALE, and recommend whether an investment of ₹15 lakh per year on a Web Application Firewall (WAF) is justified. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

##### Part (a) — Types of Software Failures (7 marks)

Software failures can be classified along three principal axes:

**1. Classification by Origin / Root Cause (3 marks):**

- **Specification Failures** occur when requirements are ambiguous, incomplete, or contradictory. Example: An e-filing portal (public sector) lacks explicit session-timeout specifications, leaving citizens' sessions open indefinitely.

- **Design Failures** arise from architectural flaws or incorrect algorithms. Example: A payment gateway (private sector) uses a single centralized database — a single point of failure that crashes during high-traffic events like Diwali sales.

- **Implementation Failures** result from coding defects such as buffer overflows, race conditions, and integer overflows. Example: SQL injection in a hospital patient portal exposing medical records.

- **Integration and Deployment Failures** emerge when APIs mismatch or configurations are incorrect. Example: Aadhaar authentication API version mismatch causing KYC failures.

- **Operational and Maintenance Failures** occur during runtime or after patches. Example: Memory leak in a defense surveillance system causing periodic crashes.

**2. Classification by Security Impact (2 marks):**

Failures compromising the **CIA Triad**:
- **Confidentiality Failures**: Information disclosure bugs (e.g., IDOR vulnerabilities in DigiLocker).
- **Integrity Failures**: Unauthorized modification (e.g., CSRF in NEFT transactions).
- **Availability Failures**: Denial-of-Service (e.g., ransomware on municipal servers).

**3. Classification by Severity (2 marks):**

Using CVSS scoring: **Critical (9.0–10.0)**, **High (7.0–8.9)**, **Medium (4.0–6.9)**, **Low (0.1–3.9)**. Critical failures demand immediate rollback; low-severity issues are backlog candidates.

---

##### Part (b) — Quantitative Risk Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Given Data (1 mark)**
- Asset Value (AV) = ₹200 crore = ₹200,00,00,000
- Exposure Factor (EF) = 10% = 0.10
- Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO) = 4 failures/year
- Proposed WAF cost = ₹15 lakh/year = ₹15,00,000/year

**Step 2: Calculate Single Loss Expectancy (2 marks)**

$$\text{SLE} = AV \times EF = 200,00,00,000 \times 0.10 = ₹20,00,00,000$$

**Step 3: Calculate Annual Loss Expectancy (2 marks)**

$$\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times ARO = 20,00,00,000 \times 4 = ₹80,00,00,000$$

**Step 4: Decision using RoI (2 marks)**

$$\text{ALE}_{\text{before}} = ₹80,00,00,000$$

Assume WAF reduces failure rate by 80%: $\text{ALE}_{\text{after}} = 80,00,00,000 \times 0.20 = ₹16,00,00,000$

$$\text{Net Savings} = 80,00,00,000 - 16,00,00,000 - 15,00,000 = ₹63,85,00,000$$

$$\text{RoI} = \frac{63,85,00,000}{15,00,000} \approx 4257 \text{ or } 4,25,700\%$$

**Conclusion:** The WAF investment is **highly justified**, with a positive return exceeding 4,000× the cost. **(Total: 7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** Discuss the Fault-Error-Failure propagation chain with a real-world case study of a major software failure in either public or private sector. Analyze the security impact. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A software system has MTTF = 800 hours and MTTR = 2 hours. Calculate the MTBF, steady-state availability (as a percentage and "nines"), and the number of allowed downtime minutes per year. Comment on whether this system meets a 99.9% SLA. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

##### Part (a) — Fault-Error-Failure Chain (7 marks)

**Theoretical Framework (3 marks):**

The propagation chain has six stages: (1) **Fault Introduction** during SDLC, (2) **Fault Activation** by a trigger input, (3) **Internal Error State** development, (4) **Error Propagation** through module boundaries, (5) **Service Failure** observable externally, and (6) **Security Incident** affecting the CIA Triad.

**Case Study: Heartbleed in OpenSSL (2014) (3 marks):**

- **Fault:** A missing bounds check in the TLS heartbeat extension (`tls1_process_heartbeat`) — a 2-line coding error in the open-source OpenSSL library.
- **Trigger:** A maliciously crafted heartbeat request with a payload-length field claiming 65,536 bytes but a payload of only 1 byte.
- **Error:** Memory contents adjacent to the heartbeat buffer were read beyond bounds, exposing 64KB of private memory.
- **Failure:** Sensitive data (private keys, session tokens, passwords) was exfiltrated from approximately **17% of the world's secure web servers** (an estimated 500,000+ servers).
- **Security Impact:** Critical confidentiality breach affecting banking, government, and defense systems. Patches were released within hours, but complete remediation took **months**. Economic damage exceeded **$500 million globally**.

**Lesson:** A 2-line fault caused a multi-year security incident, demonstrating the disproportionate impact of small defects in foundational software libraries.

**Mitigation:** Memory-safe languages, formal verification, fuzzing, code review.

---

##### Part (b) — Reliability Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:** MTTF = 800 hours, MTTR = 2 hours

**Step 1: Calculate MTBF (2 marks)**

$$\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR} = 800 + 2 = 802 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Steady-State Availability (2 marks)**

$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF} - \text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF}} = \frac{802 - 2}{802} = \frac{800}{802} = 0.99751$$

$$A \approx 99.751\%$$

**Step 3: Convert to "Nines" and Annual Downtime (2 marks)**

The "nines" representation: 99.751% corresponds to **2.49 nines** (or "two-and-a-half nines").

Hours per year = 365 × 24 = 8,760 hours

Downtime hours per year:

$$\text{Downtime} = (1 - A) \times 8760 = 0.00249 \times 8760 \approx 21.81 \text{ hours/year}$$

In minutes: 21.81 × 60 ≈ **1,308.7 minutes/year** ≈ 21.8 hours/year

**Step 4: SLA Compliance (1 mark)**

A 99.9% SLA allows:

$$\text{Max Downtime} = 0.001 \times 8760 \text{ hours} = 8.76 \text{ hours/year} = 525.6 \text{ minutes/year}$$

Since actual downtime (~1,308.7 minutes) exceeds the SLA limit (~525.6 minutes), the system **fails to meet the 99.9% SLA**. To achieve 99.9%, either MTTF must increase to ~8,000 hours, or MTTR must drop to <0.2 hours. **(Total: 7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PECCT753 Software Failure Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Fault with Failure (–2 marks):** Many students write "fault = failure". Examiner strictly differentiates: fault is **static** (in code), failure is **dynamic** (observed at runtime).
> 2. **Omitting Units in Calculations (–1 mark):** Always write "hours", "minutes/year", or "₹ crore". Naked numbers lose marks.
> 3. **Skipping the "Why" in Definitions (–1 mark):** "Define MTBF" requires **both** the formula and the **significance**, not just the formula.
> 4. **Ignoring Public vs Private Sector Context (–2 marks):** This is a PECCT753-specific course. Always frame your answer in public/private sector context, not generic software engineering.
> 5. **No Formula Citation in Numericals (–1 mark):** Always state the formula **before** substituting values. Examiners look for the formula on the first line.
> 6. **Case Studies Without Mapping to CIA Triad (–1 mark):** When asked for a case study, always map the failure to which CIA element was violated (C, I, or A).

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Software Failures (PECCT753 Module 4):**
>
> - **Core Chain:** Fault → Error → Failure → Incident. Memorize with an example.
> - **7 Standard Metrics:** MTTF, MTTR, MTBF, A, λ, μ, D (defect density).
> - **Master 3 Formulas:** $A = \frac{\text{MTBF} - \text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF}}$, $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$, $\text{Risk} = P \times I$.
> - **Taxonomy Axes:** Origin, Security Impact (CIA), CVSS Severity.
> - **Reliability Models:** Jelinski-Moranda (basic), Musa Execution Time (advanced). Just remember the exponential reliability form: $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.
> - **SDLC Failure Points:** 8 injection points (Requirements, Design, Code, Integration, System Test, Deployment, Operations, Maintenance) — 8 corresponding countermeasures.
> - **Case Studies:** Heartbleed (2014), Y2K, Knight Capital (2012), Aadhaar data leaks, AIIMS ransomware (2022). Memorize **at least two** in depth.
> - **Public Sector Impact:** Citizen denial, national security, regulatory sanctions (IT Act 2000, DPDP Act 2023), reputation loss.
> - **Private Sector Impact:** Financial fraud, IP theft, supply chain disruption, shareholder litigation.
> - **Mitigation Hierarchy:** Critical → Immediate Rollback; High → Hot Fix <4 hrs; Medium → Next Release; Low → Backlog.
> - **Quantitative Tools:** FMEA, ALE/RoI analysis, CVSS scoring, DRE (Defect Removal Efficiency) > 95%.
> - **NEP 2020 / OBE Mapping:** This topic maps to **CO4: Analyze software failure modes and their impact on information security in public and private sector systems** with Bloom's Level **L4 (Analyze)** and **L5 (Evaluate)**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computer Crime
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT753 - INFORMATION SECURITY IN PUBLIC AND PRIVATE SECTORS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Computer Crime

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Computer Crime (Cybercrime):** A criminal act in which a **computer is the object** of the crime, the **instrument used to commit the crime**, or the **repository of evidence** related to the crime. The term is formally recognized under the **Information Technology Act, 2000 (Amended 2008)** of India and equivalent global statutes such as the **Computer Fraud and Abuse Act (CFAA), 1986** of the United States.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (Module 4 of PECCT753), computer crime is treated as a **multi-disciplinary offense** spanning:
1. **Technical Layer** – exploitation of software, hardware, or network vulnerabilities.
2. **Legal Layer** – violation of codified statutes (IT Act 2000/2008, Indian Penal Code 1860, Indian Evidence Act).
3. **Social Layer** – harm to individuals, organizations, or critical national infrastructure.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Digital House-Breaker"**
> Imagine a physical neighbourhood. A burglar may:
> - **Break INTO your house** to steal valuables (this is a burglar targeting the house itself).
> - **Use your house's telephone** to threaten neighbours without revealing his own location (using the house as a tool).
> - **Hide a stolen painting** inside your attic, making your house a storage place for evidence of his crime.
>
> A **computer criminal behaves identically in cyberspace**:
> - **Target** = hacking into a bank server to steal money.
> - **Instrument** = using a botnet to launch a DDoS attack.
> - **Storage / Incidental** = storing illegal content on a cloud server.

This three-fold nature is the **single most important conceptual anchor** for Module 4.

## 1.3 Standard Classification Metrics

The following standard metrics (in **bold**) are universally cited in KTU question papers:

- **Computer as a Target** – The victim is the computer system itself (e.g., viruses, worms, logic bombs).
- **Computer as an Instrument** – The computer is the weapon used to commit an offence (e.g., phishing, cyberstalking, e-mail bombing).
- **Computer as Incidental / Storage** – The computer is merely a passive repository (e.g., possession of child pornography, digital evidence storage).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module-4 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Students must be able to **classify any given cyber offence** into one of the three categories above and **map it to the correct section of the IT Act 2000/2008**.

## 1.4 Common Sub-Topics Under "Computer Crime"

| Sub-Topic | KTU 2024 Weightage Hint |
| :--- | :--- |
| Hacking & Cracking | High – frequently asked (4–7 marks) |
| Phishing, Vishing, Smishing | High – 7 mark question expected |
| Malware (Virus, Worm, Trojan, Ransomware) | High – definitional questions |
| Cyber Stalking & Cyber Bullying | Moderate – Part A 3-mark questions |
| Cyber Terrorism & Cyber Warfare | High – Part B 14-mark questions |
| IT Act 2000/2008 – Legal Provisions | Mandatory – almost every question ends here |
| Digital Forensics (intro) | Moderate – definitions only |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 The Three-Layer Crime Model (Parker 1976 — adopted globally)

> [!NOTE]
> **Donn B. Parker's Classic Model** is the textbook foundation referenced implicitly in KTU 2024 Module 4 notes.

**Layer 1 — Computer as Target**
- The attacker aims to **deny, degrade, disrupt, destroy, or steal** information residing in the system.
- Typical acts: *unauthorized access (hacking), data diddling, salami attack, denial of service.*
- **Why this layer matters:** the asset at risk is *data integrity, availability, and confidentiality* — the three pillars of the **CIA Triad** of information security.

**Layer 2 — Computer as Instrument**
- The attacker uses a computer to **commit a traditional crime in a new medium** (theft, fraud, defamation, harassment).
- Typical acts: *phishing, e-mail bombing, cyber stalking, identity theft, online fraud.*
- **Why this layer matters:** the *modus operandi* is the computer, but the *legal classification* often requires a hybrid reading of the **IT Act + IPC**.

**Layer 3 — Computer as Incidental / Storage**
- The computer is **not essential** to the crime but happens to hold incriminating evidence or contraband data.
- Typical acts: *storing child pornography, pirated software, terror plans, drug records.*
- **Why this layer matters:** *forensic acquisition* of digital evidence (Section 65B of the Indian Evidence Act) becomes the legal pivot.

## 2.2 Detailed Taxonomy of Computer Crimes (Bullet Logic)

- **Against Individual Person**
  - *Cyber Stalking* – repetitive, threatening online behaviour.
  - *Cyber Bullying* – harassment through digital channels (often minors).
  - *Identity Theft* – assuming another person's digital identity.
  - *Phishing / Vishing / Smishing* – credential harvesting.
- **Against Property**
  - *Hacking* (Section 66 IT Act) – unauthorized access.
  - *Cracking* – maliciously breaking copy protection.
  - *Data Theft* – exfiltration of proprietary data.
  - *Software Piracy* – illegal reproduction / distribution.
- **Against Organization**
  - *DoS / DDoS Attack* – denial of service.
  - *Ransomware* – encrypting data for ransom.
  - *Insider Threat* – privilege abuse by employees.
  - *Corporate Espionage* – stealing trade secrets.
- **Against Society at Large**
  - *Cyber Terrorism* – attacks on critical infrastructure.
  - *Online Child Exploitation* (POCSO Act linkage).
  - *Money Laundering* through crypto-currencies.
  - *Spreading Misinformation / Fake News*.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Since Module 4 is largely conceptual + legal, the "Formula Sheet" is replaced with a **High-Yield Definition + Statute Mapping Table** — the most exam-relevant format for this subject.

| **Cyber Crime / Offence** | **One-Line Definition** | **IT Act 2000/2008 Section** | **IPC / Other Statute** | **Layer (Target / Instrument / Incidental)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Hacking / Unauthorized Access | Gaining entry without permission | Sec 43, 66 | Sec 378 (Theft) | Target |
| Password Cracking | Decoding another's credentials | Sec 66 | Sec 420 (Cheating) | Instrument |
| Phishing | Fraudulent credential collection | Sec 66C, 66D | Sec 420 IPC | Instrument |
| Virus / Malware Injection | Damaging code execution | Sec 43, 66, 66F | Sec 425 (Mischief) | Target |
| Denial of Service (DoS) | Disrupting service availability | Sec 66F (Cyber Terrorism) | Sec 426 IPC | Target |
| Identity Theft | Assuming false digital identity | Sec 66C | Sec 463 (Forgery) | Instrument |
| Publishing Obscene Material | Hosting/transmitting porn in e-form | Sec 67, 67A, 67B | Sec 292 IPC | Incidental |
| Cyber Stalking | Repeated online harassment | Sec 66E, 72 | Sec 503 (Criminal Intimidation) | Instrument |
| Data Theft / Eavesdropping | Secret interception of data | Sec 66, 69 | Sec 505 IPC | Instrument |
| Cyber Terrorism | Attack on sovereignty / critical infra | Sec 66F | UAPA 1967 | Target |
| Email Bombing | Flooding a mailbox to cause loss | Sec 43, 66 | Sec 425 IPC | Target |
| Salami Attack | Skimming tiny fractions repeatedly | Sec 66 | Sec 420 IPC | Instrument |
| Online Child Pornography | POCSO-linked digital abuse | Sec 67B | POCSO 2012 | Incidental |
| Defamation Online | Injuring reputation in digital form | Sec 66A (struck down), 79 | Sec 499/500 IPC | Instrument |
| Ransomware | Encrypting & extorting data | Sec 66, 66F | Sec 383/384 IPC | Target |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Caution:** Section **66A of the IT Act** (which criminalized "offensive messages") was **struck down by the Supreme Court in Shreya Singhal v. Union of India (2015)**. Do NOT quote it as an active provision in answers. Examiners actively deduct marks for this.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Incident Response Teams (CSIRT / CERT-In)** use this taxonomy to **triage attacks** and escalate to the correct legal authority.
- **Insurance Underwriters** price *cyber-insurance premiums* based on the layer (Target crimes cost more than Instrument crimes).
- **Auditors (ISO 27001, NIST CSF)** map controls to threat categories.
- **Law Enforcement** files FIRs under the correct section of the IT Act + IPC using the **Hybrid Offence Doctrine** (e.g., Phishing = Sec 66D IT Act *plus* Sec 420 IPC).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Module 4 of PECCT753 is **conceptual + legal**, so the "derivation" here is treated as the **deductive reasoning flow** that a student must reproduce on paper to score full marks in KTU 2024 answer sheets. The analytical matrix below is the standard examiner-expected structure.

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — How to Construct a KTU 14-Mark Answer

```
Step 1 → Define the offence (formal textbook definition, 2 marks)
Step 2 → Classify it using the 3-Layer Model (1 mark)
Step 3 → Provide 2–3 sub-types with examples (3 marks)
Step 4 → State the Modus Operandi / Attack Flow (2 marks)
Step 5 → Map to IT Act 2000/2008 sections (3 marks)
Step 6 → Map to corresponding IPC sections (2 marks)
Step 7 → Mitigation / Counter-measures (1 mark)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A 14-mark KTU answer written without Steps 5 & 6 typically scores **at most 8/14**, even if the rest is excellent. The legal mapping is the **mandatory value-addition** the KTU examiner is testing.

## 3.2 Worked Exhaustive Deduction — "Phishing" (Sample Demonstration)

> The following is a **fully expanded model answer** that KTU expects a student to write on the answer sheet.

**Step 1 — Formal Definition**
Phishing is a **social-engineering cyber-attack** in which an attacker impersonates a trusted entity (bank, government, e-commerce site) through a fraudulent electronic communication (e-mail, SMS, voice call) with the intent of **stealing sensitive credentials** such as usernames, passwords, OTPs, or financial data.

**Step 2 — Layer Classification**
$$\text{Layer} = \text{Computer as Instrument (Layer 2 of Parker's Model)}$$

Because the *primary weapon* is the computer/internet used to deliver the spoofed message, and the *target data* resides on a human user (not the computer system itself).

**Step 3 — Sub-Types with Examples**
- **Email Phishing** – Bulk e-mails mimicking HDFC Bank asking to "verify KYC".
- **Spear Phishing** – Targeted phishing aimed at a specific CFO using public LinkedIn data.
- **Whaling** – Spear phishing aimed at C-suite executives ("CEO fraud").
- **Vishing (Voice Phishing)** – Phone calls pretending to be from the bank's fraud department.
- **Smishing (SMS Phishing)** – SMS with shortened malicious links (e.g., `bit.ly/refund-INB`).
- **Clone Phishing** – Cloning a legitimate previously-delivered e-mail and replacing the link.

**Step 4 — Modus Operandi (Attack Flow)**
$$\text{Recon} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Forge} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Deliver} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Lure} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Harvest} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Monetize}$$

Let us expand each stage algebraically (as the examiner will expect descriptive logic):

- **Reconnaissance:** The attacker harvests victim e-mail IDs from LinkedIn, Facebook, or data brokers.
- **Forgery:** A perfect replica of a bank e-mail is crafted using HTML cloning tools.
- **Delivery:** The e-mail is mass-mailed through botnets (e.g., Necurs) to bypass spam filters.
- **Luring Mechanism:** A *sense of urgency* (account will be blocked in 24 hours) is injected.
- **Credential Harvesting:** The victim clicks → arrives at a cloned login page → credentials are POSTed to the attacker's server.
- **Monetization:** Credentials are sold on dark-web markets or used directly for fund transfer.

**Step 5 — IT Act 2000/2008 Mapping**

The two principal applicable sections are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Section 66C} &\;:\; \textit{Identity Theft} - \text{"Whoever, fraudulently or dishonestly make use of the electronic signature, password or any other unique identification feature of any other person, shall be punished."} \\
\textbf{Section 66D} &\;:\; \textit{Cheating by Personation using Communication Device} - \text{"Whoever, by means of any communication device or computer resource cheats by personating, shall be punished with imprisonment up to 3 years and a fine."}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — IPC Mapping**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Section 419 IPC} &\;:\; \text{Punishment for cheating by personation.} \\
\textbf{Section 420 IPC} &\;:\; \text{Cheating and dishonestly inducing delivery of property.} \\
\textbf{Section 463 IPC} &\;:\; \text{Forgery (if documents are fabricated).}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This constitutes a **Hybrid Offence** because the same single act of phishing simultaneously violates multiple statutes.

**Step 7 — Mitigation / Counter-Measures**
- User awareness training (anti-phishing drills).
- DMARC, DKIM, SPF e-mail authentication.
- Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) on all financial accounts.
- Browser-based anti-phishing filters.
- Reporting to **CERT-In** (Indian Computer Emergency Response Team) and the nearest **Cyber Crime Cell**.

## 3.3 Compact Decision Matrix for KTU 2024 — "Which Section Applies?"

The student can reproduce this on the answer sheet as a single visual element:

| **Symptom in Question** | **Primary Section to Quote** |
| :--- | :--- |
| Password stolen / impersonation | Sec 66C IT Act |
| Cheating using a phone/computer | Sec 66D IT Act |
| Private images captured/shared | Sec 66E IT Act |
| Obscene material published | Sec 67 / 67A IT Act |
| Child pornography | Sec 67B IT Act + POCSO 2012 |
| Critical infrastructure attack | Sec 66F IT Act (Cyber Terrorism) |
| Interception / surveillance | Sec 69 IT Act |
| Damage to computer system | Sec 43 + Sec 66 IT Act |
| Breach of confidentiality by intermediary | Sec 72 IT Act |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Diagram — Computer Crime Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA["COMPUTER CRIME"]
    rootB["Parker's 3-Layer Model"]
    rootA --> rootB
    rootB --> L1["LAYER 1: Computer as TARGET"]
    rootB --> L2["LAYER 2: Computer as INSTRUMENT"]
    rootB --> L3["LAYER 3: Computer as INCIDENTAL"]

    L1 --> L1A["Data Integrity Attack"]
    L1 --> L1B["Data Theft Attack"]
    L1 --> L1C["Availability Attack"]
    L1A --> L1A1["Virus / Worm / Trojan"]
    L1A --> L1A2["Logic Bomb"]
    L1A --> L1A3["Data Diddling"]
    L1B --> L1B1["Hacking Sec 66 IT Act"]
    L1B --> L1B2["Data Exfiltration"]
    L1C --> L1C1["DoS Attack"]
    L1C --> L1C2["DDoS Attack"]
    L1C --> L1C3["Ransomware"]

    L2 --> L2A["Person-Based"]
    L2 --> L2B["Fraud-Based"]
    L2 --> L2C["Harassment-Based"]
    L2A --> L2A1["Cyber Stalking"]
    L2A --> L2A2["Identity Theft Sec 66C"]
    L2B --> L2B1["Phishing Sec 66D"]
    L2B --> L2B2["Salami Attack"]
    L2B --> L2B3["Credit Card Fraud"]
    L2C --> L2C1["Cyber Bullying"]
    L2C --> L2C2["Email Bombing"]
    L2C --> L2C3["Defamation"]

    L3 --> L3A["Illegal Storage"]
    L3 --> L3B["Digital Evidence"]
    L3A --> L3A1["Child Porn Sec 67B"]
    L3A --> L3A2["Pirated Software"]
    L3A --> L3A3["Terror Plans"]
    L3B --> L3B1["Forensic Imaging"]
    L3B --> L3B2["Section 65B Evidence"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Every terminal node (e.g., `L1A1`, `L2B1`, `L3A1`) can be directly mapped to a legal section from the **KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet** in Section 2.3.

## 4.2 Attack-Lifecycle Diagram — Modus Operandi of a Typical Cyber Crime

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    phase1["PHASE 1: Reconnaissance"]
    phase2["PHASE 2: Weaponization"]
    phase3["PHASE 3: Delivery"]
    phase4["PHASE 4: Exploitation"]
    phase5["PHASE 5: Installation"]
    phase6["PHASE 6: Command Control"]
    phase7["PHASE 7: Actions on Objectives"]

    phase1 --> phase2 --> phase3 --> phase4 --> phase5 --> phase6 --> phase7
    phase3 -.-> p3a["Email / SMS / Web"]
    phase3 -.-> p3b["USB / Removable Media"]
    phase4 -.-> p4a["Click on Malicious Link"]
    phase4 -.-> p4b["Open Malicious Attachment"]
    phase5 -.-> p5a["Malware Installed"]
    phase5 -.-> p5b["Backdoor Created"]
    phase7 -.-> p7a["Data Theft"]
    phase7 -.-> p7b["Financial Fraud"]
    phase7 -.-> p7c["Service Disruption"]
```

## 4.3 Legal Mapping Block Diagram (IT Act + IPC Hybrid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    actA["SINGLE CRIMINAL ACT"]
    actA --> x1["Layer 2 Instrument Crime"]
    actA --> x2["Layer 1 Target Crime"]
    actA --> x3["Layer 3 Incidental Crime"]
    x1 --> s1["IT Act Sec 66C / 66D"]
    x1 --> s2["IPC Sec 419 / 420 / 463"]
    x2 --> s3["IT Act Sec 43 / 66 / 66F"]
    x2 --> s4["IPC Sec 425 / 426 / 427"]
    x3 --> s5["IT Act Sec 67 / 67B"]
    x3 --> s6["IPC Sec 292 / POCSO 2012"]
    s1 --> court["FIR Lodged in Cyber Crime Cell"]
    s2 --> court
    s3 --> court
    s4 --> court
    s5 --> court
    s6 --> court
    court --> trial["Adjudication by Special Court IT Act Sec 46 to 52"]
```

## 4.4 Digital Forensics Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    id1["INCIDENT DETECTED"]
    id1 --> id2["First Responder Secures Scene"]
    id2 --> id3["Chain of Custody Initiated"]
    id3 --> id4["Bit-Stream Imaging of Storage"]
    id4 --> id5["Hash Verification MD5 / SHA-256"]
    id5 --> id6["Forensic Analysis Volatility Order"]
    id6 --> id7["Evidence Extraction"]
    id7 --> id8["Report Generation Section 65B Compliant"]
    id8 --> id9["Court Presentation"]
    id6 -.-> id6a["RAM Memory Dump"]
    id6 -.-> id6b["Network State"]
    id6 -.-> id6c["Running Processes"]
    id6 -.-> id6d["Disk Imaging Last"]
```

> **Why this diagram is in Section 4:** Module 4 transitions seamlessly into the *evidence-handling* phase of any computer crime. KTU 2024 examiners appreciate a **chain-of-custody aware** answer.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [KTU University Exam – Model Question 1, Module 4]
> **Q1. Define Computer Crime. List the three layers of Parker's classification model with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** A computer crime is any unlawful act in which a computer is the *target*, *instrument*, or *incidental storage* of the offence, recognized under the IT Act 2000 (amended 2008).
- **Three layers (2 marks):**
  - *Computer as Target* — Example: Virus attack on a server.
  - *Computer as Instrument* — Example: Phishing e-mail to steal bank credentials.
  - *Computer as Incidental* — Example: Storing pirated movies on a hard disk.

> [KTU University Exam – Model Question 2, Module 4]
> **Q2. Differentiate between Hacking and Cracking. State the relevant IT Act section.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Hacking (1 mark):** Unauthorized access to a computer system, *often for exploration or curiosity*. Offence under **Section 66 of the IT Act 2000/2008** (up to 3 years imprisonment or fine up to ₹5 lakh, or both).
- **Cracking (1 mark):** *Malicious* breaking of software copy-protection or password protection, *with intent to cause damage or commit fraud*. The same act falls under **Section 66**, but the *intent and impact* elevates it to a graver offence, often also attracting **Section 66F (Cyber Terrorism)** if infrastructure is targeted.
- **Key Difference (1 mark):** Hacking = unauthorized entry; Cracking = unauthorized entry *with malicious destruction or financial gain intent*.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [KTU University Exam – Module 4 ESE Model Paper]
> **Q. (A) [14 Marks] — Attempt EITHER (A) or (B)**

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **(a)** Explain the **three layers of computer crime** as proposed by Donn B. Parker with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Discuss the **various types of malware** (Virus, Worm, Trojan, Ransomware, Logic Bomb, Spyware) and state the relevant sections of the IT Act 2000/2008 that apply to malware-based offences. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Introduction (1 mark)**
The most widely accepted academic classification of computer crime is the **three-layer model** proposed by **Donn B. Parker (1976)**, which classifies offences based on the **role of the computer** in the criminal act.

**2. Layer 1 — Computer as TARGET (2 marks)**
- The computer system *itself* is the victim.
- Attacker aims to *deny, degrade, disrupt, destroy, or steal* data.
- **Examples:** Viruses, Worms, DoS/DDoS attacks, Ransomware encrypting a hospital's database.
- **Statute:** IT Act **Sec 43** (civil compensation) and **Sec 66** (criminal — up to 3 years or ₹5 lakh fine); if infrastructure-related, **Sec 66F (Cyber Terrorism)**.

**3. Layer 2 — Computer as INSTRUMENT (2 marks)**
- The computer is the *weapon* used to commit a traditional crime.
- **Examples:** Phishing e-mails, Cyber stalking on social media, Online fraud, Identity theft.
- **Statute:** IT Act **Sec 66C (Identity Theft)**, **Sec 66D (Cheating by Personation)**, **Sec 66E (Privacy violation)**, plus **IPC Sec 419/420**.

**4. Layer 3 — Computer as INCIDENTAL (2 marks)**
- The computer is merely a *passive storage* for illegal content or evidence.
- **Examples:** Storing child pornography, hosting pirated movies, keeping terror-planning documents on a laptop.
- **Statute:** IT Act **Sec 67 (Obscene material)**, **Sec 67B (Child Pornography)**; **POCSO Act 2012** for child-related offences.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**1. Virus (1 mark)**
Self-replicating malicious code that attaches itself to *executable files* and spreads when the host file is executed. Spreads only via user action. IT Act: **Sec 43 + Sec 66**.

**2. Worm (1 mark)**
Standalone self-replicating malware that propagates over *computer networks without user intervention*. Famous example: **ILOVEYOU (2000), Conficker, WannaCry**. IT Act: **Sec 43 + Sec 66 + Sec 66F** (if critical infra).

**3. Trojan Horse (1 mark)**
Malicious code *disguised as legitimate software* (e.g., a fake antivirus installer). Does not self-replicate; relies on social engineering. IT Act: **Sec 66C / 66D** if used for credential theft.

**4. Ransomware (1 mark)**
Encrypts victim's data and demands ransom (typically in cryptocurrency) for the decryption key. Examples: **WannaCry (2017)**, **Petya**, **Locky**. IT Act: **Sec 66 (damage) + Sec 66F (if infra attack) + Sec 384 IPC (extortion)**.

**5. Logic Bomb (1 mark)**
Malicious code *triggered by a specific condition* (date, time, event). Example: a payroll system programmed to crash on a specific date. IT Act: **Sec 43 + Sec 66 + Sec 425 IPC (Mischief)**.

**6. Spyware / Keylogger (1 mark)**
Covertly monitors user activity and exfiltrates keystrokes, screenshots, or browsing data. IT Act: **Sec 66 (unauthorized access) + Sec 72 (breach of confidentiality by intermediary)**.

**7. Comparative Summary (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Malware} \;&\longrightarrow\; \textbf{Property Violated} \;&\longrightarrow\; \textbf{IT Act Section} \\
\text{Virus} \;&\longrightarrow\; \text{Integrity} \;&\longrightarrow\; \text{Sec 43, 66} \\
\text{Worm} \;&\longrightarrow\; \text{Availability} \;&\longrightarrow\; \text{Sec 43, 66, 66F} \\
\text{Trojan} \;&\longrightarrow\; \text{Confidentiality} \;&\longrightarrow\; \text{Sec 66C, 66D} \\
\text{Ransomware} \;&\longrightarrow\; \text{All 3 + Extortion} \;&\longrightarrow\; \text{Sec 66, 66F, 384 IPC} \\
\text{Logic Bomb} \;&\longrightarrow\; \text{Integrity} \;&\longrightarrow\; \text{Sec 43, 66, 425 IPC} \\
\text{Spyware} \;&\longrightarrow\; \text{Privacy} \;&\longrightarrow\; \text{Sec 66, 72, 66E}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **(a)** What is **Phishing**? Explain its sub-types and the typical attack lifecycle. Map the offence to the appropriate sections of the **IT Act 2000/2008** and **IPC**. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Write a detailed note on **Cyber Stalking and Cyber Bullying** as offences against the individual. Discuss the legal remedies available in India. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Definition (1 mark)**
Phishing is a *social-engineering cyber-attack* in which an attacker impersonates a trusted entity via electronic communication to steal **credentials, OTPs, or financial data**.

**2. Sub-Types (3 marks)**
- *Email Phishing* — bulk fraudulent e-mails.
- *Spear Phishing* — targeted using OSINT.
- *Whaling* — targeting C-suite executives.
- *Vishing* — voice-call-based phishing.
- *Smishing* — SMS-based phishing.
- *Clone Phishing* — cloning a legitimate e-mail with malicious link.

**3. Attack Lifecycle (1 mark)**
$$\text{Recon} \;\to\; \text{Forge} \;\to\; \text{Deliver} \;\to\; \text{Lure} \;\to\; \text{Harvest} \;\to\; \text{Monetize}$$

**4. Legal Mapping (2 marks)**
- **IT Act Sec 66C** — Identity Theft.
- **IT Act Sec 66D** — Cheating by Personation using a computer/communication device (up to 3 years + fine).
- **IPC Sec 419** — Cheating by personation.
- **IPC Sec 420** — Cheating and dishonestly inducing delivery of property.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**1. Definitions (1 mark)**
- **Cyber Stalking** — Repeated, unsolicited, threatening online behaviour causing fear for personal safety.
- **Cyber Bullying** — Repeated hostile behaviour by an individual or group using digital technology, often targeting minors (e.g., Section 2(o) of the *Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act 2012*).

**2. Modus Operandi (2 marks)**
- Fake social-media profiles.
- Repeated unwanted messages / friend requests.
- Publication of morphed images.
- Tracking the victim's location via GPS-enabled apps.
- Doxxing — publishing private information online.

**3. Legal Remedies — India (4 marks)**
- **IT Act 2000/2008:**
  - *Sec 66E* — Violation of privacy (capturing, publishing private images) — up to 3 years.
  - *Sec 67* — Publishing obscene material.
  - *Sec 67A & 67B* — Sexually explicit content.
  - *Sec 72* — Breach of confidentiality by a service provider.
  - *Sec 72A* — Disclosure of information in breach of contract.
- **IPC:**
  - *Sec 503* — Criminal intimidation.
  - *Sec 507* — Criminal intimidation by anonymous communication.
  - *Sec 509* — Word, gesture or act intended to insult the modesty of a woman.
  - *Sec 228A* — Disclosure of identity of victim of certain offences.
- **Special Statutes:**
  - *Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act 2005* — if stalker is a family member or in a domestic relationship.
  - *POCSO Act 2012* — if victim is a minor.
  - *Indian Telegraph Act Sec 5(2)* — for blocking the stalker's communications.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do NOT cite Sec 66A** of the IT Act — it was struck down in *Shreya Singhal v. UoI (2015)*. Marks will be deducted.
> 2. **Always quote the AMENDED (2008) IT Act**, not the original 2000 version.
> 3. **Mention BOTH IT Act AND IPC sections** in hybrid offences — quoting only one is a common reason for losing 2–3 marks per sub-part.
> 4. **Do not confuse "Hacking" with "Cracking"** — examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 5. **Use "Phishing / Vishing / Smishing"** as three distinct sub-types; lumping them together costs at least 1 mark.
> 6. **Always mention Section 79 (Intermediary Safe Harbour)** when discussing platform liability — it is a frequent 3-mark direct question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Definition:** Computer crime = computer as **Target, Instrument, or Incidental** (Parker's 3-Layer Model).
- [ ] **Hacking ≠ Cracking:** Hacking = unauthorized access (curiosity); Cracking = unauthorized access with malicious intent.
- [ ] **Phishing** → Sec 66C + 66D IT Act **AND** Sec 419/420 IPC.
- [ ] **Malware** → Sec 43 (civil) + Sec 66 (criminal) + possibly Sec 66F (terrorism).
- [ ] **Identity Theft** → Sec 66C IT Act.
- [ ] **Cheating by Personation** → Sec 66D IT Act.
- [ ] **Privacy Violation** → Sec 66E IT Act.
- [ ] **Obscene Material** → Sec 67 IT Act; **Child Pornography** → Sec 67B IT Act + POCSO 2012.
- [ ] **Cyber Terrorism** → Sec 66F IT Act (life imprisonment).
- [ ] **Intermediary Liability** → Sec 79 IT Act (Safe Harbour).
- [ ] **DO NOT quote Sec 66A** — struck down in 2015.
- [ ] Always specify **"IT Act 2000 (amended 2008)"** in every answer.
- [ ] **Digital Evidence** must comply with **Sec 65B of the Indian Evidence Act, 1872** (now Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023).
- [ ] **CERT-In** is the national nodal agency for cyber-incident reporting in India.
- [ ] **Cyber Crime Cells** exist in every metropolitan city for FIR lodging.
- [ ] **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) is the security foundation linked to *Target-layer* crimes.
- [ ] **Salami Attack** = skimming tiny amounts repeatedly (banking malware).
- [ ] **Data Diddling** = altering input data before processing.
- [ ] **Email Bombing** = flooding inbox to cause loss of service.

> **Final 1-Liner Mnemonic for KTU 2024 Module 4:**
> *"**T**arget, **I**nstrument, **I**ncidental — every cybercrime is a **TII-ny** violation of the **IT Act 2008** and the **IPC**."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Praia
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT753 - INFORMATION SECURITY IN PUBLIC AND PRIVATE SECTORS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Praia

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Praia Group on Governance and the Internet** (often simply referred to as the "Praia Group") is an **intergovernmental working group** established in **2014** during the 9th Meeting of the African Ministerial Council on Science, Technology and Innovation (AMCOST) held in **Praia, Cape Verde**. It was formally launched under the auspices of the **African Union Commission (AUC)** and the **United Nations Economic Commission for Africa (UNECA)**.

From an **Information Security** perspective, the Praia Group is a multilateral diplomatic and policy framework that addresses:
- **Global Internet Governance** issues
- **Cybersecurity policy harmonisation** across developing nations
- **Cross-border data protection** and privacy norms
- **Capacity building** in information security for public and private sectors
- **Multi-stakeholder participation** in internet policy formation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Praia Group is studied under the international cooperation framework for information security, emphasizing how developing nations collaborate to build cyber-resilient public-private ecosystems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **neighbourhood watch committee** for the digital world. Just like residents in a community gather to decide on common safety rules (whom to allow in, how to share CCTV footage, how to handle break-ins), the **Praia Group** is a gathering of nations discussing how to collectively safeguard their digital neighborhoods against cyber threats, data theft, and information warfare.

**Real-World Analogy:** Think of the Praia Group as the **"United Nations of Internet Safety for the Global South."** It is similar to how the **Geneva Conventions** set ground rules for warfare, but Praia sets the ground rules for the digital battlefield — particularly ensuring that smaller nations aren't left defenseless against sophisticated cyber threats from larger players.
