# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution (as per KTU 2024 PECCT753 ESE pattern):** Each elective is a 14-mark module-end internal-choice question. The questions below are calibrated to the *exact* KTU 2024 ESE pattern: **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**, with **one full alternative**.

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]**
> **Q1. Define "Information Ethics" and list Mason's four pillars.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Information Ethics is the branch of applied ethics that examines the moral responsibilities of individuals and organizations in the creation, collection, processing, and dissemination of digital information.
> Mason's four pillars are:
> 1. **P**rivacy — control over personal information.
> 2. **A**ccuracy — duty to ensure data correctness.
> 3. **P**roperty — recognition of intellectual ownership.
> 4. **A**ccessibility — right to access entitled information.
> *(Award 1 mark for definition; 2 marks for the four pillars.)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]**
> **Q2. Differentiate between utilitarianism and deontological ethics with one information-security example each.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Utilitarianism** judges actions by their *consequences* — the action producing the greatest net good is ethically correct. *Example:* Mass surveillance may be justified if it prevents a major terror attack and saves many lives.
> - **Deontological ethics** judges actions by their *inherent rightness* — certain acts (lying, theft) are wrong regardless of outcome. *Example:* A security professional must not lie to a regulator about a breach, even if the lie protects the company's reputation.
> *(1 mark per correct definition + example; 1 mark for distinction clarity.)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Pattern: 7 + 7 Marks, Internal Choice]**

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks (CHOICE 1)**

> **(a) Analyse the Enron scandal using any two ethical frameworks. What information-security governance reforms resulted from it? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Background (1 mark):**
Enron Corporation, a U.S. energy major, collapsed in December 2001 after hidden debts of $1 billion and overstated earnings were revealed. Shareholders lost $74 billion; employees lost pensions. Auditor Arthur Andersen obstructed justice by shredding documents.

**Step 2 — Utilitarian Analysis (2 marks):**
From a utilitarian view, executives' actions failed the *greatest-good test* catastrophically. The benefit to a small group of insiders (retained bonuses, inflated stock) was vastly outweighed by the harm to millions of employees, retirees, and the public trust in capital markets. The action was *demonstrably immoral* under consequentialist analysis.

**Step 3 — Deontological Analysis (2 marks):**
From a deontological view, the actions were inherently wrong. Lying to investors, breaching fiduciary duty, manipulating SPEs to deceive, and obstructing justice violate Kant's categorical imperative — such actions, if universalized, would destroy the very possibility of honest commerce. Therefore the actions were *impermissible irrespective of outcome*.

**Step 4 — Resulting Reforms (2 marks):**
The U.S. Congress enacted the **Sarbanes-Oxley Act (SOX) 2002**, mandating:
- Section 302 — CEO/CFO personal certification of financial reports.
- Section 404 — **Internal Controls over Financial Reporting (ICFR)**, directly tying information-systems security to financial accuracy.
- Section 409 — Real-time disclosure of material changes.
- Creation of the **Public Company Accounting Oversight Board (PCAOB)** to oversee auditors.
- Whistleblower protections (Section 806).

> **(b) Discuss the Wells Fargo fake-accounts case as a failure of organizational ethics culture. Suggest three controls from the NIST 800-53 framework that could have prevented it. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — What Happened (1 mark):**
Between 2002 and 2016, Wells Fargo employees opened **3.5 million unauthorized bank and credit-card accounts** in customers' names to meet aggressive sales targets. Approximately 5,300 employees were fired. Whistleblowers were retaliated against. The bank paid $3 billion in settlements.

**Step 2 — Failure of Culture (2 marks):**
The case is a textbook example of a *toxic goal-driven culture* overriding individual ethical judgement. The "eight is great" target (eight accounts per customer) created **perverse incentives**. Senior management knew of the issue for over a decade. This reflects the **"tone at the top"** failure documented in COSO and OECD governance principles.

**Step 3 — Three NIST 800-53 Controls (3 marks):**
1. **AC-2 (Account Management):** Automated lifecycle management would have flagged abnormal account creation patterns in real time.
2. **PM-22 (Whistleblower Protection):** Formal, anonymous, retaliation-proof channels would have surfaced concerns much earlier.
3. **AT-2 (Awareness Training) + AT-3 (Role-Based Training):** Ethics training tailored to retail-banking roles would have clarified acceptable vs. unacceptable sales practices.

**Step 4 — Synthesis (1 mark):**
The Wells Fargo case shows that technical controls alone are insufficient; without ethical culture, training, and whistleblower protection, even the best technical safeguards can be circumvented by employees acting under pressure.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (CHOICE 2 — ALTERNATIVE)

> **(a) Explain the Cambridge Analytica–Facebook case using Mason's eight principles of information ethics. Which principle was violated most severely, and why? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Recap Mason's Eight Principles (2 marks):**
1. **Privacy** — Informational self-determination.
2. **Accuracy** — Truthfulness of information.
3. **Integrity** — Consistency and trustworthiness.
4. **Proprietary** — Protection of intellectual property.
5. **Ownership** — Recognition of data ownership.
6. **Openness** — Free flow of information.
7. **Legality** — Compliance with law.
8. **Accessibility** — Right to access.

**Step 2 — Mapping the Case to Each Principle (3 marks):**

| Principle | Violation in Cambridge Analytica Case |
|---|---|
| Privacy | Personal data harvested without consent via API loophole |
| Accuracy | Psychographic profiles were scientifically dubious |
| Integrity | Data was used contrary to stated purpose (research) |
| Proprietary | Facebook's platform rules and IP were circumvented |
| Ownership | Users had no meaningful control over "their" data |
| Openness | Data flows were hidden from regulators and users |
| Legality | Violated FTC consent decree and pre-GDPR data laws |
| Accessibility | Democratic discourse was *distorted* by targeted manipulation |

**Step 3 — Most Severe Violation (2 marks):**
The **Privacy** and **Ownership** principles were most severely violated. Consent was obtained from only ~270,000 users, but data of 87 million was harvested. This was a systemic, deliberate, profit-driven breach of the foundational principle that *individuals own their personal data*. The downstream political manipulation amplifies the harm from individual to societal scale.

> **(b) "The Equifax breach was ethically worse than technically sophisticated." Critically evaluate this statement. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — The Statement (1 mark):**
This statement argues that the ethical negligence — not the attack sophistication — is the true cause of harm in the 2017 Equifax breach, which exposed 147 million Americans' PII.

**Step 2 — Technical Reality (1.5 marks):**
The exploited vulnerability (**Apache Struts CVE-2017-5638**) was a known, critical-severity (CVSS 10.0) flaw. A patch had been available for **two months** before exploitation began. The attack itself used a simple, automated scanner. There was **zero novelty** in the attack vector.

**Step 3 — Ethical Failures (3 marks):**
1. **Negligent patch management** — despite being a credit-bureau, a fundamental security hygiene practice was ignored.
2. **Inadequate encryption** — sensitive PII was stored unencrypted in some systems.
3. **Insider trading allegations** — executives sold $1.8M in stock between breach discovery and public disclosure.
4. **Delayed breach notification** — a 76-day window of concealment while executives offloaded stock.
5. **Repeated breaches** — Equifax had suffered prior breaches (2013, 2016) without remediation.

**Step 4 — Verdict (1.5 marks):**
The statement is **correct**. The technical attack was unsophisticated; the harm was entirely due to *ethical and procedural negligence*. This aligns with the **"due care" vs. "due diligence"** distinction in security governance — Equifax failed both, and paid $700 million in consequences.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not** answer a case-study question by merely *narrating events*. Examiners award marks for *ethical analysis using a named framework* (Utilitarian / Deontological / Virtue / Mason / NIST / SOX). Always **name the framework** in your opening sentence.
> 2. **Do not** write answers in a single paragraph. Use **headings and tables**. KTU examiners have ~90 seconds per answer; structured answers score higher.
> 3. **Do not** forget the *information security dimension* of ethics. A question on "Volkswagen ethics" is incomplete if you do not mention the *defeat-device software* as a privileged-access control bypass.
> 4. **Do not** misattribute facts. Mixing up Enron (SPEs) with WorldCom (capitalization) costs at least 1 mark.
> 5. **Always** end with a "lesson learned" or "control recommendation" — this is the **synthesis mark** and is frequently missed by students.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (memorize the night before ESE):**

- **Information ethics** is a *human-layer security control*, not just philosophy.
- **Mason's four pillars:** Privacy, Accuracy, Property, Accessibility.
- **Mason's eight principles:** Add Integrity, Proprietary, Ownership, Openness, Legality.
- **Three ethical frameworks:** Utilitarian (outcome), Deontological (duty), Virtue (character).
- **The 7-step decision model** must be quoted verbatim in any 14-mark ethics case answer.
- **Three Lines of Defence:** Operational (Line 1) → Risk/Compliance (Line 2) → Audit (Line 3).
- **Enron → SOX 2002** (Section 404 ICFR).
- **WorldCom → Segregation of Duties in ERP**.
- **Wells Fargo → Toxic goal culture + whistleblower suppression** ($3B fine).
- **Volkswagen → Defeat device firmware** ($30B+ fines).
- **Cambridge Analytica → Consent and API privacy** (led to GDPR enforcement; $5B FTC fine).
- **Equifax → Unpatched Apache Struts** ($700M; CVE-2017-5638, CVSS 10.0, 76-day dwell time, 147M victims).
- **Theranos → Founder fraud in tech startups** (Holmes sentenced 11+ years; wire fraud conviction).
- **Standard codes referenced in KTU:** ISACA, ACM, (ISC)², IEEE, IT Act 2000, DPDP Act 2023, GDPR.
- **DPDP Act 2023 (India)** and **IT Act 2000** are the most relevant Indian statutory frameworks for KTU.
- **One framework name + one case study + one control recommendation** is the "magic triad" for full marks on any 14-mark ethics question.
